 Universal Registration Document 2024 This Universal Registration Document has been filed on March 18, 2025, with the Autorité des marchés financiers (the "AMF") as the competent authority under Regulation (EU) 2017/1129, without prior approval pursuant to Article 9 of Regulation (EU) 2017/1129. This Universal Registration Document may be used for the purposes of an offer to the public of securities or admission of securities to trading on a regulated market if completed by a securities note and, if applicable, a summary and all amendments to the Universal Registration Document. The combined document is approved by the AMF in accordance with Regulation (EU) 2017/1129. This Universal Registration Document is a reproduction in PDF format, translated into English, of the official version of the Universal Registration Document issued in French in the xHTML format, filed with the AMF on March 18, 2025 and available on the AMF website (www.amf‑france.org). This translation is available on our website (www.axa.com). This Universal Registration Document (which we also refer to as this "Document" or "Annual Report") includes (i) the Annual Financial Report (Rapport Financier Annuel) referred to in paragraph I of Article L.451‑1‑2 of the French Monetary and Financial Code (Code monétaire et financier) as well as in Article 222‑3 of the AMF General Regulation (Règlement Général de l'AMF) (the relevant Sections of this Document corresponding to such required disclosures are referred to in the cross‑reference table in Appendix VII - Cross reference table of the Annual Financial Report), (ii) the management report (rapport de gestion) of the Board of Directors to be presented at AXA's Shareholders' Meeting to be held on April 24, 2025, prepared pursuant to Articles L.225‑100 et seq., L.232‑1 et seq. and L.22‑10‑35 et seq. of the French Commercial Code (Code de commerce) (the relevant Sections of this Document corresponding to such required disclosures are referred to in the cross‑reference table in Appendix V - Cross reference table of the Board of Directors' management report (including the corporate governance report)); and (iii) the corporate governance report (rapport sur le gouvernement d'entreprise) established pursuant to Articles L.225‑37 et seq. and L.22‑10‑8 et seq. of the French Commercial Code (the relevant sections of this Document corresponding to such required disclosures are referred to in the cross‑reference table in Appendix V - Cross reference table of the Board of Directors' management report (including the corporate governance report)). The cross‑reference table in Appendix VI - Cross reference tables - EU regulation indicates the items of Annexes 1 and 2 of Delegated Regulation (EU) 2019/980 to which the information contained herein corresponds. Certain preliminary information about this Document In this Document, unless provided otherwise, (i) the "Company", "AXA" and "AXA SA" refer to AXA, a société anonyme (a public limited company) organized under the laws of France, which is the publicly traded parent company of the AXA Group, and (ii) the "AXA Group", the "Group" and "we" refer to AXA SA together with its direct and indirect consolidated subsidiaries. The Company's ordinary shares are referred to in this Document as "shares", "ordinary shares", "AXA shares" or "AXA ordinary shares". The principal trading market for AXA's ordinary shares is the regulated market Euronext in Paris (Compartment A), which we refer to in this Document as "Euronext Paris". The Group's consolidated financial statements and related notes are prepared in accordance with International Financial Reporting Standards (IFRS) (the "Consolidated Financial Statements") and published in Euro ("Euro", "euro", "EUR" or "€"), as are all other amounts in this Document, with applicable foreign exchange rates presented in Section 2.2.1.3 - Exchange rates, and presented in million for convenience. Such amounts may have been rounded. Rounding differences may exist, including for percentages. In this Document, unless provided otherwise, "restated" refers to the comparative period that was restated following (i) the termination in May 2024 of the sale agreement of a closed life and pensions portfolio at AXA Germany, classified as held for sale in 2023 and (ii) the announcement on August 1, 2024, that AXA had entered into an exclusive negotiation to sell its asset manager AXA Investment Managers ("AXA IM") to BNP Paribas, leading to classify it as a discontinued operation in the Consolidated Financial Statements. Where reference is made to a website in this Document, the contents of such website do not form part of this Document. No information, document or material from the website of the Company (www.axa.com) or any other source shall form part of this Document, unless such information, document or material is expressly incorporated by reference into this Document.  Cautionary statement regarding forward‑looking statements and Use of Non‑GAAP and Alternative Performance Measures This Document may include statements with respect to future events, trends, plans, expectations or objectives and other forwardlooking statements relating to the Group's future business, financial condition, results of operations, performance, and strategy. Forward‑looking statements are not statements of historical fact and may contain the terms "may", "will", "should", "continue", "aims", "estimates", "projects", "believes", "intends", "expects", "plans", "seeks" or "anticipates", or words of similar meaning. Such statements are based on Management's current views and assumptions and, by nature, involve known and unknown risks and uncertainties; therefore, undue reliance should not be placed on them. Actual financial condition, results of operations, performance or events may differ materially from those expressed or implied in such forward‑looking statements, due to a number of factors including, without limitation, general economic and political conditions and competitive situations; future financial market performance and conditions, including fluctuations in exchange and interest rates; frequency and severity of insured loss events, and increases in loss expenses; mortality and morbidity levels and trends; persistency levels; changes in laws, regulations and standards; the impact of acquisitions and disposals, including related integration issues, and reorganization measures; and general competitive factors, in each case on a local, regional, national and/or global basis. Many of these factors may be more likely to occur, or more pronounced, as a result of catastrophic events, including weather‑related catastrophic events, pandemics, terrorist‑related incidents or acts of war. Please refer to Section 5 - Risk Factors and Risk Management of this Document for a description of certain important factors, risks and uncertainties that may affect AXA's business and/or results of operations. AXA specifically disclaims and undertakes no obligation to publicly update or revise any of these forward‑looking statements, whether to reflect new information, future events or circumstances or otherwise, except as required by applicable laws and regulations. In addition to the Consolidated Financial Statements, this Document refers to certain non‑GAAP financial measures, or alternative performance measures (APMs), used by Management in analyzing the Group's operating trends, financial performance and financial position and providing investors with additional information that Management believes to be useful and relevant regarding the Group's results. These non‑GAAP financial measures generally have no standardized meaning and therefore may not be comparable to similarly labelled measures used by other companies. As a result, none of these non‑GAAP financial measures should be considered in isolation from, or as a substitute for, the Consolidated Financial Statements included in Chapter 6 - Consolidated Financial Statements of this Document. The non‑GAAP financial measures used by the Group are defined in the Glossary set forth in Appendix IV to this Document. AXA | Universal Registration Document 2024

 Chairman and Chief Executive Officer's Message 2024 was a year of significant change and uncertainty marked by geopolitical tensions, divergent economic performance across geographies and climate change challenges. Amidst these challenges, the rapid pace of technological innovation is opening new avenues for innovation, offering the potential to enhance efficiency, improve decision-making, and create tailored solutions that better meet the evolving needs of consumers and businesses. In a world characterized by volatility, uncertainty, and evolving risks, there is an urgent need for robust risk management solutions. The value of insurance has never been more relevant. For businesses, insurance provides the essential protection that allows them to continue their operations with confidence, safeguarding against unforeseen events that could disrupt their activities and financial stability. For individuals, insurance offers peace of mind by ensuring that their assets are protected, their health is covered, and their retirement is secure. Insurance fosters resilience, enabling both businesses and individuals to thrive in an uncertain world. At AXA, we remain committed to addressing the needs of our customers, ensuring they have access to the coverage they require in an uncertain world. Our business model, which is balanced between commercial and retail insurance, with leading positions in our markets, continues to thrive. With strong distribution and product capabilities, we are well placed to meet the evolving needs of our customers and capitalize on the growth opportunities that we see in our markets. We remain agile to respond to changing market dynamics. Our business model is delivering consistent results for our shareholders. In 2024, AXA successfully launched its new Strategic Plan, "Unlock the Future", aimed at growing and strengthening the core insurance business. The plan is focused on driving higher organic growth and scaling technical and operational excellence across the Group. At the same time, we affirm our long-standing commitment to corporate social "Our business model… continues to thrive" responsibility including by offering adapted products and services designed to facilitate the climate transition by enhancing protection and insurability. Beyond supporting the transition to a lower carbon economy, we are also focused on fostering positive societal impact through inclusive insurance solutions for vulnerable populations and community and workforce engagement, notably through the AXA Hearts in Action initiatives. The "Unlock the Future" strategy is off to a strong start. The Group reported an 8% increase in topline growth with revenues reaching €110 billion, while our underlying earnings per share also rose by 8%. We have delivered a 15% return on equity while maintaining a strong balance sheet with a Solvency II ratio of 216%. In 2024, we also made a strategic decision to further simplify our business profile through the sale of AXA Investment Managers (IM) to BNP Paribas. This transaction will pave the way for the creation of a leading and scaled European asset manager with whom we plan to maintain a strong relationship in the years ahead. This partnership will give our clients access to a broader range of investment solutions and our colleagues at AXA IM a scaled platform to grow. As part of our ambition to strengthen our insurance franchise in our markets, we also announced the acquisition of Nobis in Italy last summer. In 2025, AXA will celebrate its 40th anniversary. This is an occasion to celebrate and an opportunity to draw inspiration from AXA's past successes. As we move forward, we remain focused on meeting the evolving needs of our customers, driving profitable growth, while also delivering value to our shareholders. Finally, this performance is a collective success, and we would like to acknowledge the exceptional work of our employees, agents, and partners. We are extremely grateful for their unwavering commitment to serving our customers. Thank you for your ongoing support and trust in our company. Antoine Gosset-Grainville Chairman of the Board of Directors Thomas Buberl Chief Executive Officer AXA | Universal Registration Document 2024
4
 Strategics Orientations In 2024, the insurance industry faced a complex landscape shaped by various macroeconomic factors: ■ Geopolitical and climate challenges: (i) High geopolitical tensions, including the ongoing war in Ukraine and instability in the Middle East, continued to pose significant global risks. (ii) The escalating competition between the US and China, along with the rise of populism in Europe, increased the likelihood of disruptive policy changes. (iii) Climate change remained a critical concern, with more frequent and severe natural disasters impacting the industry. ■ Insurance industry dynamics: (i) The sector benefited from a favorable cycle in Property & Casualty (P&C), driving premium growth, while higher interest rates supported a recovery in the Life & Health (L&H) segment. Claims cost showed signs of stabilization amid moderating general inflation. (ii) Regulators continue to focus on consumer protection, emphasizing transparency. (iii) Rapid technological advancements prompted insurers to modernize operations and enhance customer experience. ■ Growth opportunities: (i) High interest rates spurred demand for Life insurance, particularly savings and retirement products. (ii) The demand for Health and Protection rose, driven by demographic shifts and protection gaps. (iii) Significant potential emerged to leverage data analytics and artificial intelligence (AI) to enhance operational excellence in underwriting, pricing, and claims management. AXA's strong market position enabled it to navigate this challenging environment and drive strong growth, particularly in the first year of executing its 2024-2026 strategic plan, "Unlock the Future"
.
 ■ ■ ■ ■ Driving profitable organic growth: (i) P&C Commercial lines experienced robust revenue growth, with premiums increasing by +6% to EUR 34.9 billion. (ii) In Personal lines, premiums rose by 7%, reflecting disciplined pricing actions, notably in the UK and Germany. (iii) Short-term Life & Health premiums reached EUR 16 billion with continued margin expansion. In the UK, we made significant progress in claims pathway management to help customers find the right care. Laya, a leader in the Irish health market, was successfully integrated into the Group and met its 2024 business objectives. (iv) Our high- quality L&S portfolio demonstrated strong growth dynamics in Unit- Linked following successful sales campaigns; Boosting technical excellence: (i) Our P&C combined ratio improved to 91.0%, reflecting underwriting measures taken in the UK & Ireland and Germany in response to higher frequency in Motor in 2023, and continued improvement in Commercial lines. (ii) We continued to enhance technical excellence in Health by leveraging proprietary assets for pricing and claims management; Enhancing operational excellence: (i) AXA's offshore locations in India, Poland, and Morocco saw continuous growth, increasing the share of high-value services in technology, data, AI, and industrial automation. (ii) We scaled over 17 high-value data and AI use cases across entities, in addition to our disciplined execution of automation initiatives that continue to yield benefits as planned. (iii) The number of generative AI use cases increased throughout 2024 in alignment with our strategic objectives; and Expanding AXA's social role: (i) Our inclusive insurance unit, AXA EssentiALL, is addressing the significant protection gap for low-to- middle-income households and small businesses in emerging markets, expanding its geographical reach into European markets. In 2024, we launched a pan-European flagship product for microentrepreneurs and tailored offers for the retail segment. (ii) The Group continues to set targets to reduce carbon emissions and maintain its commitment to climate transition. We remain focused on simplifying our business profile and strengthening our positions in core markets: (i) The acquisition of Nobis (pending regulatory approval) is expected to enhance our position in the Italian P&C market, adding scale and diversified distribution capabilities. (ii) Partnering with the Spanish postal service, Correos, should extend our reach to new customers. (iii) The sale of AXA Investment Managers (AXA IM) to BNP Paribas aligns with our strategy to focus on our core insurance business. As part of this planned transaction, AXA and BNP Paribas would establish a long-term strategic partnership, providing AXA a broader range of products and strategies. In 2025, AXA intends to maintain its focus on executing its "Unlock the Future" strategic plan. In P&C, we aim to deliver disciplined growth and better customer retention while benefiting from the earn-through of higher pricing. In Life & Health, we intend to sustain the growth momentum in short-term business, particularly Employee Benefits, and focus on improving net flows in our savings business. As we pursue profitable organic growth, we will continue to scale technical excellence and drive operational improvements across the Group, including through the deployment of technology and AI. We expect to remain on track to meet the key financial targets outlined in AXA's strategic plan. AXA | Universal Registration Document 2024

 1.2 Business overview AXA operates in five hubs (France, Europe, AXA XL, Asia, Africa & EME‑LATAM, and AXA IM) and offers a broad range of Property & Casualty, Life & Health, and Asset Management products and expertise. AXA and its subsidiaries have established branches across various jurisdictions to support their operations. For additional information on the Group's main subsidiaries, see Note 2 of Chapter 6.6. The nature and level of competition vary among the countries where AXA operates. AXA competes with insurance companies along with banks, asset management companies, investment advisors and other financial institutions. 1.2 Business overview The principal competitive factors are as follows:
For additional information on markets, see Section 2.2 "Market environment - Market conditions" of this Document. The table set out below presents AXA's Gross Written Premiums and Other Revenues (after intercompany eliminations) by line of business. The AXA Group 1

1.2.1 France 1.2.1.1 Products and services AXA offers in France a full range of insurance products, including Property & Casualty, and Life & Health. Its offering covers a broad range of products including Motor, Household, Property and general liability insurance, Banking, savings, and other investment‑based products for both Retail/Individual and Commercial/Group customers, as well as Health, Protection, and retirement products for individual and professional customers. 1.2.1.2 New product initiatives In Life & Health, AXA France has expanded its financial offering in the context of the Green Industry Law and its product offering in the context of the Shared Value Law. AXA France has consequently renewed its offers of Mandate Management (Wealth Management), Managed Investments (for the general public), and Horizon Management (Retirement Savings Enterprise products). In these latter offerings, three non‑listed assets funds (financing of the real economy and environmental and social transitions) are now available, including private debt, a new asset class, since 2024. Simultaneously, AXA France has reaffirmed its commitment to society and its clients (e.g., strengthening of guarantees and financial education services) while continuing its innovation in structured products. This commitment is also evident in the Protection sector with the launch of the "Family Assistance" guarantee, included for all new subscriptions. AXA | Universal Registration Document 2024
13
 1.2 Business overview As a committed insurer for society, AXA France has launched an innovative and inclusive product, "Mon Pack Entrepreneur" specifically designed for micro‑entrepreneurs. This product combines several essential guarantees, such as property damage, professional liability, legal protection, and daily allowances, within a single contract. Moreover, AXA France has developed the offer "My Moto" to better catch clients' needs. Additionally, Direct Assurance developed an inclusive insurance in 2024, which facilitated access to insurance through the payment of the deductible in three installments with no fees. In terms of technology, the launch of the "YouDrive" device has enabled numerous clients to benefit from a more advantageous rate with the possibility of reducing their premiums by 50%. AXA France also has launched the "Mission Prévention" to protect its professional clients and businesses by anticipating current and future risks to offer proactive and tailored protection. Lastly, within the Credit & Lifestyle Protection (CLP) business, In collaboration with one of its partners, a predictive AI model (2024 French "Trophées de l'Assurance" award for Technological Innovation) has been deployed to further optimize and simplify the claims management process to better support our customers. In addition, Care Cover benefit was approved as an add‑on to our MPPI products in France. 1.2.1.3 Distribution channels In France, AXA distributes its insurance products through exclusive and non‑exclusive channels including exclusive agents, salaried sales forces, direct sales, banks, as well as brokers, independent financial advisors, aligned distributors or wholesale distributors and partnerships. 1.2.2 Europe The European market includes AXA's operations in eight countries (Switzerland, Germany, Belgium, Luxembourg, the United Kingdom, Ireland, Spain, and Italy). 1.2.2.1 Products and services Except for the United Kingdom & Ireland (where AXA operates only in Property & Casualty and Health), AXA offers in Europe a full range of insurance products, including Property & Casualty and Life & Health. Its offering covers a broad range of products including Motor, Household, Property and General Liability insurance, term life, whole life, universal life, endowment, deferred annuities, immediate annuities, and other investment‑based products for both Retail/Individual and Commercial/Group customers. 1.2.2.2 New product initiatives In Europe, new initiatives are regularly being launched in a consistent aspiration to deliver high value‑added services and insurance coverage in all lines of business: ● in Switzerland, AXA continuously aims at developing its product offers to effectively address customers' needs. In Individual Life AXA notably launched "SmartFlex Capital Plan", a new Unit‑Linked single premium product; ● in Germany, AXA launched new Unit‑Linked offers: a single premium product "InvestNow" based on a building block approach offering customizable solution along with a regular premium offer "JustInvest" with access to more than 100 funds including exchange traded funds and sustainable funds selection in the product line "JustGreenInvest"; ● in Belgium, AXA re‑entered the Individual savings market with a new flexible and hybrid product allowing both accumulation and decumulation. In Health, AXA launched "Ambu CareFlex" consisting in a product for outpatient care offering extra‑legal customizable benefits through a flexible plan of an employer; ●
in Italy, AXA launched "Progetto Rendimento", a single premium General Account saving product offering capital safety and regular growth over time as well as "Più Autonomia", an affordable Long‑term care solution for 50‑70‑year‑old persons, with fixed duration until 85 and low entry level. In Property & Casualty, Quixa launched "Van & Job" the first insurance product dedicated to micro‑businesses in Italy offering both Motor and business activity insurance coverage into one single contract. 1.2.2.3 Distribution channels In Europe, AXA distributes its insurance products through exclusive and non‑exclusive channels that vary from country to country, including agents (exclusive or non‑exclusive), salaried sales forces, direct sales, banks and other partnerships (e.g., car dealers), brokers, independent financial advisors and aligned or wholesale distributors. 1

 1.2.3 AXA XL 1.2.3.1 Products and services AXA XL, through its operating subsidiaries, is a leading provider of Property & Casualty insurance and reinsurance coverages to industrial, commercial and professional firms, insurance companies, and other enterprises on a worldwide basis. Through its main operations, AXA XL provides: ● insurance, with a broad range of coverages, including property, primary and excess casualty, excess and surplus lines, environmental liability, professional liability, construction, marine, energy, aviation & satellite, fine art & specie, livestock & aquaculture, accident & health and crisis management, among other risks; ● reinsurance, with casualty, property risk, property catastrophe, specialty, and other reinsurance lines on a global basis with business being written on both a proportional and non‑proportional treaty basis, as well as on a facultative basis; ● risk consulting, as clients receive customized Risk Management solutions and consulting services to understand and quantify the risks companies face or may face in the future. The objective is to help them avoid preventable losses and mitigate the impact of losses that do occur. 1.2.3.2 New product initiatives AXA XL continues to bring innovative solutions to the market by: ● working with clients to address their Generative Artificial Intelligence (Gen AI) needs, AXA XL introduced a new endorsement to its Cyber insurance coverage that specifically addresses risks associated with developing Gen AI models, including data poisoning, usage rights infringement, and regulatory violations, enabling businesses to leverage Gen AI while mitigating potential liabilities; ● supporting clients on their energy transition journeys, AXA XL launched its global Energy Transition offering, drawing on the capabilities of AXA Group, and its in‑house underwriting and risk consulting expertise to provide innovative insurance products and solutions; ● enabling conservation projects via debt conversion, AXA XL's Political Risk, Credit & Bond team provided credit insurance protection to the Government of The Bahamas, allowing the country to expand its commitments to safeguard vital habitats within the largest Marine Protected Areas in the Caribbean region. 1.2 Business overview 1.2.3.3 Distribution channels The majority of AXA XL business originates via a large number of international, national and regional brokers. This channel is supported by client and country management teams, which include sales and distribution representatives in key markets throughout the world. Underwriting authority may also be contractually delegated to selected third parties which are subject to a financial and operational due diligence review prior to any such delegation of authority, as well as ongoing reviews and audits as deemed necessary with the goal of assuring the continuing integrity of underwriting and related business operations. 1.2.4 Asia, Africa & EME‑LATAM The Asia, Africa & EME‑LATAM market includes AXA's operations in more than 15 countries, notably in Japan, Hong Kong, China, Thailand, Indonesia, Mexico, Colombia, Türkiye, and Morocco. Asia includes (i) the subsidiaries in Japan, Hong Kong, China (AXA Tianping), and South Korea which are fully consolidated, (ii) the Property & Casualty entity in Thailand, and the non‑bancassurance Life & Health entity in Indonesia which are fully consolidated, as well as (iii) the joint ventures in China (Life & Health), Philippines (Property & Casualty and Life & Health), Thailand (Life & Health), and Indonesia (Life & Health) which are consolidated under the equity method and do not contribute to Gross Written Premiums and Other Revenues. Africa & EME‑LATAM includes (i) Mexico, Colombia, Brazil, Türkiye, Morocco, Egypt and Nigeria which are fully consolidated, combined with (ii) Russia (Reso) that is consolidated under the equity method and does not contribute to Gross Written Premiums and Other Revenues. Further details on the scope of market activities are defined in the Glossary set forth in Appendix IV of this Document. 1.2.4.1 Products and services In Asia, Africa & EME‑LATAM, AXA offers insurance products, including Property & Casualty and Life & Health products. Types and specificities of the products offered by AXA vary depending on geographies and cover a broad range of products including Motor, Household, Property and General Liability insurance, Health, term life, whole life, universal life, endowment, and other investment‑based products for both Retail/Individual and Commercial/Group customers.  1.2 Business overview In addition to its product propositions, in Africa & EME‑LATAM, an integrated ecosystem between medical assets and the insurance offer is provided to customers, through onsite services in clinics in Colombia, Nigeria, Egypt and Mexico as well as through remote teleconsultation and digital services or through home services in most markets. AXA also offers other services, for example Emma, the digital assistant that, in addition to making servicing requests simpler and more efficient, provides access to a comprehensive range of related services including wellness advice and support. Emma is available in Japan, Hong Kong, China, Thailand, Indonesia and the Philippines. 1.2.4.2 New product initiatives AXA continues to strengthen its position in those geographies, while improving its customer propositions with new initiatives. In Hong Kong, AXA has launched new critical illness products as well as enhancing Savings products offering flexibility to modify policy terms based upon changing customer needs. In Japan the launch of a "Unit‑Linked Annuity" product empowering policyholders to plan for a comfortable retirement while insuring for death and major illnesses. In Asia emerging markets, AXA launched endowment products in Thailand, Indonesia and the Philippines to cater to customer needs and offered the first multiple critical illness product in the Philippines. In South Korea, AXA introduced "AXA MS‑Cancer", an enhanced insurance product specifically designed for seniors. In China, AXA continues developing solutions specifically oriented to electric vehicles in line with market development while diversifying its portfolio by transforming the product offering in Non‑Motor. In Africa & EME‑LATAM, in Commercial lines, AXA launched Environmental Liability products in Colombia and Mexico in collaboration with AXA XL. In Türkiye a "Fast Claim Assessment" system was implemented, leveraging technology to digitize and streamline the claim process to enhance customer experience. In Brazil, a customer‑centric approach to claims management was adopted to strengthen broker relationships resulting in an increase of claims closing ratio. 1.2.4.3 Distribution channels In Asia, Africa & EME‑LATAM, AXA distributes its insurance products through exclusive and non‑exclusive channels that vary from country to country, including exclusive and non‑exclusive agents (notably in Japan, Hong Kong), salaried sales forces, direct sales (notably Property & Casualty in Japan and South Korea), bancassurance with leading domestic banks (China, Hong Kong, Indonesia, the Philippines, Thailand, and Egypt) and other partnerships (e.g., car dealers), as well as brokers, independent financial advisors, aligned distributors or wholesale distributors. In Asia, the "AXA Prime" network operated across Southeast Asia to help build a strong knowledgeable salesforce improving customer services resulting in higher customer retention and satisfaction. In Africa & EME‑LATAM, AXA continues its strong traction on digitization with a focus on customer experience and digital assets for agents and brokers. For example, in Egypt, AXA and Post for Investment ("PFI"), the investment arm of Egypt Post, have decided to establish the first micro‑insurance company in Egypt with an ambitious goal to reach 12 million customers within the first decade of operation. 1.2.5 AXA Investment Managers AXA Investment Managers ("AXA IM") is a leading global asset management firm offering a comprehensive range of investment opportunities across alternative and traditional asset classes, as well as multi‑manager solutions. AXA IM is committed to delivering strong investment performance and sustainable growth by expanding product and solution offerings, broadening its client base, and enhancing its global reach.  1.4 Dividends and dividend policy The Company pays dividends in Euro. AXA's dividend policy is based on Underlying Earnings Per Share. AXA targets, in line with its "Unlock the Future" strategic plan announced on February 22, 2024, to (i) pay a dividend per share of 60% of the Group's Underlying Earnings Per Share, and (ii) adhere to a dividend per share floor whereby the proposed dividend per share in any given year is targeted to be at least equal to the dividend per share paid in the prior year (subject to the absence of a significant capital event, i.e. an event which significantly deteriorates the Group's solvency). The Board of Directors has full discretion to determine the dividend to be proposed at the applicable Shareholders' Meeting, taking into 1.4 Dividends and dividend policy account a variety of factors, including AXA's earnings, financial condition, applicable capital and solvency requirements, prevailing operating and financial market conditions and the general economic environment. A dividend of €2.15 per share for the 2024 fiscal year will be proposed to the Shareholders' Meeting to be held on April 24, 2025. The Company's dividend policy is part of a wider capital management policy which targets an aggregate payout ratio (based on dividend payouts and share buy‑backs) of 75% of the Group's Underlying Earnings Per Share (see Section 2.4.5 - Group capital management objectives of this Document).  AXA entered into an exclusive negotiation to sell AXA Investment Managers to BNP Paribas On August 1, 2024, AXA announced that it had entered into an exclusive negotiation to sell its asset manager AXA Investment Managers ("AXA IM") to BNP Paribas for cash proceeds (1) of €5.1 billion. In addition, AXA would receive €0.3 billion consideration from the sale of Select (2) to AXA IM prior to the closing of the proposed transaction. The total estimated transaction value was expected to be €5.4 billion, representing a multiple of 15x 2023 earnings. Under the terms of the proposed transaction, AXA and BNP Paribas would also enter into a long‑term strategic partnership under which BNP Paribas would provide investment management services to AXA. The combination of AXA Investment Managers and BNP Paribas would create a leading European asset manager, with total assets under management of €1.5 trillion (3).
Following the completion of the information‑consultation procedure with the employee representative bodies of both AXA and BNP Paribas, AXA announced on December 21, 2024, that it had entered into a Share Purchase Agreement with BNP Paribas in relation to the transaction. The completion of the transaction is subject to customary closing conditions, including the receipt of regulatory approvals, and is expected to take place around the end of the second quarter of 2025. In FY24, AXA IM has been classified as "discontinued operations" in AXA's Consolidated Financial Statements and AXA will continue to account for the contribution of AXA IM to the Group's Underlying Earnings until the expected completion of the sale. Upon completion, the transaction is expected to result in (i) a reduction in Underlying Earnings of ca. €0.4 billion on an annualized basis for the Group and (ii) an estimated one‑off Net income gain of €2.2 billion. AXA intends to offset 2.1 Operating highlights the earnings dilution from the transaction with a share buyback of €3.8 billion, to be launched following the closing of the transaction. The transaction and the associated share buyback are expected to have a neutral impact on AXA's Solvency II ratio. The transaction is expected to have no material impact on the key financial targets (4) that were communicated as part of the "Unlock the Future" plan. AXA expands its Retail P&C operations in Italy with the acquisition of Nobis Group On August 1, 2024, AXA announced that it had entered into an agreement to acquire Gruppo Nobis ("Nobis"). Nobis is a predominantly Retail P&C insurance company in Italy, benefiting from a well‑diversified distribution network, that includes multi‑tied agents and car dealer partnerships. Nobis reported (5) €0.5 billion of gross written premiums and net income of €35 million in 2023. Under the terms of the transaction, the upfront consideration for the acquisition would amount to €423 million, with a potential earn‑out (6) of up to €55 million. Based on the full consideration, the price‑to‑earnings multiple was ca. 11x, including expected synergies. Upon closing, the transaction was expected to result in an impact of -1 point on AXA Group's Solvency II ratio. The acquisition would further diversify AXA's distribution channels, particularly through long‑term agreements with car dealerships within the Nobis network, supporting profitable growth. By leveraging Nobis' network and expertise, AXA Italy expected to improve its market position in P&C from #5 to #4, gaining ca. 1 point of market share. The completion of the transaction is subject to customary closing conditions, including the receipt of regulatory approvals, and is expected to take place before the end of the first half of 2025.  2.1 Operating highlights 2.1.3 Capital/debt operations AXA announced the successful placement of €1.5 billion Restricted Tier 1 Notes On January 10, 2024, AXA announced that it had successfully completed the placement of €1.5 billion of Reg S perpetual deeply subordinated notes (the "Notes") with institutional investors. The Notes qualify as Restricted Tier 1 capital under Solvency II. Investor demand for the issuance was strong with a book subscribed more than 5 times. The initial fixed rate was set at 6.375% per annum until the end of a 6‑month call window period (ending on January 16, 2034), when the interest rate will reset every five years thereafter at the prevailing Euro 5‑year Mid Swap rate plus a margin of 384.1 basis points. In line with the Solvency II requirements, the Notes feature a loss absorption mechanism in the form of a write‑down (1) of the nominal amount of the Notes if one of the solvency‑related triggers (2) is breached (3). Interest payments are at the full discretion of AXA unless they are mandatorily prohibited. The Notes were rated BBB+ by Standard & Poor's and Baa1(hyb) by Moody's. They are eligible to be treated as capital from a regulatory and rating agencies' perspective within applicable limits. This issuance was part of AXA Group's funding plan for 2024 and the proceeds were to be used for general corporate purposes, including the refinancing of part of AXA Group's outstanding debt. The settlement of the Notes took place on January 16, 2024. Execution of a share repurchase agreement in relation to AXA's share buy‑back program of up to €1.8 billion 2

Despite ongoing geopolitical uncertainty, global equity markets continued to benefit from strong momentum in 2024, supported by strong corporate earnings, decreasing inflation and loosening of monetary policies, although the performance was uneven across geographies. The MSCI global equity index posted another strong year of gains with an increase of 17%, following a 22% increase in the previous year. In the United States, the S&P 500 index witnessed another strong year posting a growth of 23%. The year saw some sector rotation, but technology stocks continued to perform strongly. Moreover, the appreciation was broadly even across the year and further gains occurred after the presidential elections. In Europe, markets rose in early 2024, but flatlined from Q2 as economic growth slowed down. The Eurostoxx 50 index posted gains of just 8% over the year compared to a 19% in 2023. In France, the CAC 40 underperformed other markets as stocks suffered from the political and fiscal uncertainty following the mid‑year elections. The French index fell by 2% by end of 2024 compared to the 17% gain in 2023. In the United Kingdom, the FTSE 100 index rose by 6% compared to a 4% in 2023 following the election of the new Labour government which introduced a new budget marked by fiscal loosening. In Asia, Japan's stock markets posted gains of 19% across 2024 boosted by the sharp depreciation of the Yen, which increased overseas sales and earnings. 2.2.1.2 Bond markets (Government bonds in % or basis points (bps))

 Bond markets have had different yield evolutions over 2024 across the United States, Europe, and Asia. In the United States, yields dipped to a low of 3.62% following a 50 bps rate cut in September. However, the combination of a stronger economy, inflation and the results of November's presidential elections saw yields rise, finishing the year at 4.57%. In Europe, German yields softened in early H2 2024, reflecting an economic contraction. However, this was reversed later in the year as a result of political uncertainty, finishing the year at 2.37% up 34 bps. This occurred despite the expectations of an accelerated monetary policy easing by the European Central Bank. French OAT yields rose by 64 bps to 3.20% in response to 2.2.1.3 Exchange rates
2.2 Market environment political and fiscal uncertainty, while Italian BTP yields fell by 18 bps to 3.52%. The UK gilt yields rose by 103 bps to 4.57% by year‑end due to fiscal uncertainty. In Japan, increasing confidence that the country has overcome its deflationary battle saw the Bank of Japan raise the policy rate and signal further tightening ahead. Hence, the Japanese 10‑year rose by 49 bps, finishing the year at 1.10%. Corporate spreads continued to tighten through 2024 across jurisdictions. In the United States, spreads have continued to narrow to their tightest levels since 2007, below post‑pandemic levels. European credit spreads have also narrowed sharply following a significant correction last year. Activity Report and Capital Management 2

The US Dollar had been relatively steady against a basket of currencies through most of 2024. However, starting at the end of September, it posted significant gains against most currencies thanks to a combination of a resilient economic momentum, a revision of interest rate expectations and the presidential elections. The Euro came under pressure as weaker growth and fading inflation allowed the ECB to ease policy at a faster pace. The Euro fell by 6% and closed 2024 at USD 1.04. It also fell by 5% against the British Pound Sterling (to GBB 0.83), which remained supported by expectations of reduced monetary loosening from the Bank of England.  2.2 Market environment 2.2.2 Market Conditions 2.2.2.1 Insurance activities Main developed markets In France, the savings insurance market was up by 14% compared to the previous year, with premiums reaching €173 billion. This growth was fueled by a positive trend in both Unit‑Linked (+8%) and General Account (+17%) products. The share of Unit‑Linked products slightly decreased compared to the previous year, standing at +38% (40% in 2023). In a context of economic, fiscal and political uncertainty as well as a stagnant real estate market, the saving ratio increased, supporting net flows in the Life insurance market. The level of surrenders decreased compared to the previous year in General Account funds, despite the "Livret A" rate being maintained at 3%, partially offset by an increase in Unit‑Linked surrenders. The Protection and complementary Health insurance market witnessed medical inflation that the market partly absorbed through an average tariff increase of 8% in 2024. The rising cost and frequency of claims have both been accentuated. In addition, political uncertainty around the budget for the French welfare system (the Sécurité Sociale) led some insurers to foresee that the decrease in the share of medical expenses reimbursed by the Sécurité Sociale would result in further tariff increases. In 2024, the Property & Casualty market witnessed an increase in claims in the Motor business notably due to driver behavior as well as repair costs. Several natural catastrophes marked the year, such as overseas cyclones Belal (c. €0.1 billion) and Chido (c. €0.65 - €0.8 billion), as well as numerous floods (c. €0.35 - €0.42 billion), including those resulting from the Kirk depression. In Europe (excluding France), Property & Casualty insurance markets faced economic challenges such as rising repair costs supported by persistent but moderating inflation along with evolving risks such as climate change. Insurance markets have been impacted by less severe but multiple weather events mainly from hailstorms and floods across most geographies. Insurers are responding by implementing pricing measures and reshaping product offerings as well as risk management practices. In Life & Savings, European markets are recovering from last year's challenging macroeconomic environment but are still affected by modest growth as well as economic and political uncertainties. In Health, European private insurers are facing an ageing population, claims cost inflation and strains on national healthcare systems, leading to the implementation of progressive price increases. In the meantime, private health insurance providers are focusing on digital transformation, including care pathway management and developing wellness benefits to meet changing consumer expectations. In Japan, the Life insurance market grew by 5% in terms of gross written premium, mainly from strong sales of traditional GA savings products as policyholders benefited from higher guarantees being offered as a result of the increase in interest rates in Japan. The net income of major insurers was driven by a better investment income from a favorable Japanese equity market performance. The Property & Casualty insurance market improved by 3% in terms of gross written premiums, driven by price increases in Motor and Fire insurance. In Hong Kong, Life gross written premiums increased by 8%. New business recorded significant growth of 16% derived from non‑Linked individual business. New business from Mainland China Visitors slightly decreased compared with 2023, representing 28% of total Individual business. The Property & Casualty insurance market remained stable. The United States Property insurance market rates have risen, although at a slower pace in 2024, with strict underwriting across capacity deployment, risk selection, and terms and conditions. However, the increased frequency of Nat Cat events, particularly secondary perils, along with rising reinsurance costs and economic inflation that drove up construction costs, have adversely impacted the market. Overall, the United States Casualty insurance market continues to experience rate increases due to loss trends. However, elevated casualty claims due to social inflation have impacted underwriting margins and reserves. The Reinsurance market remains well‑capitalized, with companies successfully raising capital to support growth initiatives. The property reinsurance market has experienced increased rates and attachment points, resulting in improved margins. The realignment of property catastrophe risk has allowed reinsurers to limit their losses despite active weather patterns. Strong underwriting performance along with improved Investment returns have helped mitigate adverse prior year reserves development reported in US casualty business. 2

 Main emerging markets In Asia Emerging Markets, the Property & Casualty insurance markets benefited from growth across most geographies. The market grew notably thanks to Motor business in China and the Philippines, as well as Property in Thailand and the Philippines. The Life insurance market continues showing good trends. In Thailand, new business volumes grew by 3% driven by higher sales of G/A Saving products benefiting from higher yields. Philippines volumes grew by 16% mainly driven by single premium products sales in main channels. China observed the continued expansion of Health. Life insurance markets continued to mature, shifting away from pricing wars to focus on customer needs with rapid development of participating products, while governments and regulatory authorities have also issued a series of policies to guide market players into reducing costs and increasing efficiency. Indonesia observed low persistency in Unit‑Linked products, partially offset by the launch of new Endowment and Protection products. 2.2 Market environment In Other Emerging Markets: ● ● ● the Mexican insurance market saw 18% growth, where Property & Casualty benefited from market‑wide price increases in Motor to mitigate inflation. Life insurance growth was driven by sales of Unit‑Linked products while Health insurance growth was mainly driven by price increases; in Türkiye, the Property & Casualty market grew by 67%, mainly driven by high 44% inflation. Excluding inflation, the growth was mainly linked to higher volumes in the Motor business. The Health segment grew by 97% driven by significant price increases driven by strong inflation as well as higher volumes following increased awareness; and the Colombian insurance market grew by 8%, primarily from mandatory accident insurance in P&C Protection business, Individual Life insurance, and Workers' Compensation, despite a slowdown of the banking sector, partly offset by the Motor business due to lower new car sales.  2.2.2.2 Asset Management activities In 2024, after battling inflation for two years, major central banks began lowering interest rates. Although inflation was still above target, the expectation of lower rates boosted equity markets and attracted more investment in equity ETFs. Growing excitement around artificial intelligence also contributed to this success, with the S&P 500 rising 23% by the end of the year. However, confidence in significant rate cuts from the US Federal Reserve decreased due to resilient growth and sticky inflation. Meanwhile, the European Central Bank's lower rate strategy remained steady, resulting in strong investments in euro credit. This positive economic environment and moderating inflation also boosted interest in high yield investments. The credit asset class performed well too, with most sectors yielding better returns than government bonds. High yield credit was particularly strong, achieving over 8% returns in both US dollar and Euro markets. 2.2 Market environment Private debt remains a notable alternative strategy within the alternative assets sphere, continuing to provide appealing returns for investors. Conversely, the real estate and infrastructure markets continued to experience value adjustments during the first half of 2024 – in line with their usual valuation lag; however, the more accommodative central bank policy allowed for values to stabilize in the second half of the year.  2.4 Liquidity and capital resources Information in this section should be read in conjunction with Note 4 "Financial and insurance Risk Management" in Chapter 6 "Consolidated Financial Statements" of this Document. Liquidity management is a core part of the Group's financial planning and includes the management of our debt and the maturities of our debt instruments and, more generally, the Group's capital allocation strategy. Our liquidity resources mainly come from our insurance and asset management operations, capital‑raising activities and committed bank credit facilities. 2.4 Liquidity and capital resources Over the past several years, the Group has expanded its core operations through organic growth, direct investments, and acquisitions. This expansion has been funded primarily through (i) dividends from operating subsidiaries, (ii) proceeds from debt instruments and intra‑group borrowings, (iii) issuance of shares, and (iv) proceeds from the sale of non‑core businesses and assets. The liquidity and financing needs of the Group must be managed in compliance with regulatory constraints, including minimum solvency ratios. 2.4.1 Liquidity sources and needs for Group operating entities Each major operating subsidiary of the Group is responsible for managing its own liquidity position in coordination with the Company, which, as the holding company, coordinates funding and liquidity management, and participates in financing the operations of certain of its subsidiaries. The principal sources of liquidity for the Group's (re)insurance subsidiaries and the Company's reinsurance activities are premiums, investment income, and proceeds from sales of invested assets. These funds are mainly used to pay policyholder benefits, claims, policy surrenders and operating expenses, purchase financial assets, and meet potential collateral obligations related to derivatives. The liquidity of the Group's insurance operations is affected by the quality of its investments and its ability to timely liquidate financial assets to meet policyholder benefits and claims. The Group regularly reviews asset quality to ensure adequate liquidity in stress scenarios. Information on projected payments and surrenders related to Life & Health and Property & Casualty insurance contracts is disclosed in Note 12.1.9 "Liquidity risk arising from contracts within the scope of IFRS 17" in Chapter 6 of the Consolidated financial statements in this Document. Information in this section should be read in conjunction with the Chapters 5.1.1.1 "Market‑related risks", 5.1.2.1 "Insurance and reinsurance pricing and underwriting‑related risks", and 5.1.2.2 "Operational risks" in Chapter 5.1 "Risk factors" of this Document. 2.4.1.1 Property & Casualty (including reinsurance) Liquidity needs of Property & Casualty activities can be affected by actual claims experience. These insurance activities' net cash flows are generally positive but can be negative in the event of exceptional losses or poor portfolio profitability. A portion of these cash flows is invested in liquid, short‑term bonds and other listed securities to manage liquidity risk. 2.4.1.2 Life & Health Liquidity needs of Life & Health activities can be affected by fluctuations in surrenders, withdrawals, maturities, and guarantees to policyholders. AXA's Life & Health subsidiaries design their investment strategy to match the return and maturity profile of their investments with expected cash flows from insurance contracts. Entities regularly monitor the valuation, duration, and performance of their financial assets. Financial market performance can affect surrenders and withdrawals on life insurance policies and actual and projected cash needs. Group entities adjust their investment portfolios accordingly. 2.4.1.3 Asset Management The principal sources of liquidity for AXA's Asset Management subsidiaries are operating cash flows, repurchase agreements, borrowings from credit institutions, and drawings on credit facilities. The financing needs of Asset Management subsidiaries arise principally from their activities, requiring working capital to constitute seed money for new funds at AXA IM (1).

 2.4 Liquidity and capital resources 2.4.2 Liquidity position In 2024, AXA continued to follow a prudent approach to managing its liquidity risk. At year‑end 2024, the Group had: ● ● ● a significant cash position across all business lines, with cash and cash equivalents of €18.1 billion, net of bank overdrafts of €0.9 billion; broad access to various markets via standardized debt programs, including a maximum capacity of €6.0 billion of French Negotiable European Commercial Paper, $2.0 billion of US commercial paper, €20.0 billion under a Euro Medium Term Note program (with €17.3 billion already issued), and €1.5 billion of French Negotiable European Medium Term Notes; a debt profile characterized by mostly subordinated debt with a long maturity profile. In 2024, AXA SA reimbursed €0.1 billion of debt (net of issuances), resulting in consolidated debt of €19.2 billion at year‑end. Debt gearing (1) increased by 0.2 points to 20.6% at year‑end 2024, (compared to 20.3% at year‑end 2023), driven by other comprehensive income (OCI) decrease due to decrease in swap rates and government bond spreads widening. AXA has a robust liquidity Risk Management framework, reviewed regularly through quarterly monitoring of liquidity and solvency requirements in stressed scenarios at both local and Group levels. For additional information, refer to Chapter 5.2 "Internal control and Risk Management - Consolidated Financial Statements" (2) and Chapter 5.5 "Liquidity risk" (3) of this Document. In 2024, the Liquidity Risk Management framework has been reshaped to take into account the resilience to several stress scenarios, with time horizons ranging from 1 week to 12 months, including an extreme short‑term scenario, whereby entities must ensure they can sustain a very quick and severe financial stress on collateral needs arising from derivatives and repo transactions. Each major operating subsidiary of the Group is responsible for monitoring short term liquidity risk, leveraging on a weekly Liquidity dashboard, detailing available eligible collateral before and after the extreme scenario. Excess of liquidity can then be monetized by implementing term lending transactions, generating additional investment income. At year‑end 2024, Group entities held more than €181 billion of government and related bonds, including €103 billion issued by diverse Eurozone countries, to address local liquidity needs through highly liquid assets. AXA SA maintained liquid resources consisting of cash, money market instruments, highly liquid fixed income investments, and a portfolio of investments backing its statutory reinsurance liabilities. AXA SA also maintained available credit facilities of €7.9 billion at year‑end 2024. Its liquidity requirements primarily arise from solvency needs of entities under severe stress scenarios, its central reinsurance activities, the remuneration of its shareholders and its portfolio of collateralized derivatives. For additional information on the currencies in which our cash and cash equivalents are held, refer to Chapter "5.3.3.2 Exchange‑rate risk related to the operating activities of group subsidiaries" of this Document. For information on borrowings, refer to Note 11.1.1, Note 15, and Note 16.2 in Chapter 6 "Consolidated Financial Statements" of this Document. For additional information on the use of financial instruments for hedging purposes, refer to Note 18 "Derivative instruments" in Chapter 6 "Consolidated Financial Statements" of this Document. As part of its risk control framework, the Company is vigilant regarding contractual provisions that may give lenders, securityholders or other counterparties the right to accelerate repayment, demand collateral or seek other similar remedies under circumstances that could have a material adverse effect on the Group's consolidated financial position. At year‑end 2024, AXA had no rating triggers and no financial covenants in its credit facilities. (1) Debt gearing is defined in the Glossary set forth in Appendix IV of this Document. Other debt owed to credit institutions (including bank overdrafts) At December 31, 2024, other debt owed to credit institutions amounted to €4,520 million (including €875 million of bank overdrafts), a decrease of €263 million compared to €4,783 million at the end of 2023 (including €845 million of bank overdrafts). Debt (other than financing debt) owed to credit institutions is described in Note 16 "Payables" in Chapter 6 "Consolidated Financial Statements" of this Document. On a consolidated basis, AXA's total outstanding non‑subordinated financing debt excluding derivatives amounted to €3,223 million at December 31, 2024, an increase of €812 million from €2,411 million at the end of 2023 mainly driven by the issuance of €750 million in senior unsecured notes in May 2024. Financing debt instruments are described in Note 15 "Financing debt" in Chapter 6 "Consolidated Financial Statements" of this Document. 2.4.2.3 Other debt Financing debt owed to credit institutions At December 31, 2024, the Group did not owe any financing debt to credit institutions. Other debt instruments issued At December 31, 2024, other debt instruments amounted to €444 million compared to €674 million at the end of 2023 mainly driven by a decrease of the commercial paper position of AXA SA. 2.4.2.4 Issuance of ordinary shares The AXA Group offers its employees the opportunity to subscribe for shares through a capital increase reserved for employees. In 2024, close to 13 million new shares were issued for approximatively €400 million. Newly issued shares under stock options plans amounted to approximatively 2.630 million shares in 2024. To eliminate the dilutive impact arising from employee share offerings and stock options exercises, AXA generally buys back and cancels an equivalent number of shares. 2.4.2.5 Dividends received from Group subsidiaries 2.4.3 Uses of funds Interest paid by AXA SA in 2024 amounted to €1,199 million (€1,107 million in 2023), of which interest on undated subordinated debt was €304 million (€250 million in 2023).  2.4 Liquidity and capital resources 2.4.4 Impact of regulatory requirements The Group's operations are subject to a wide variety of (re)insurance and other laws and regulations in the jurisdictions in which it operates, including regulatory capital and solvency requirements. For additional information, refer to Chapter 7.3 "Regulation and Supervision" (1) of this Document. 2.4.4.1 Regulatory capital requirements The Group's operating (re)insurance entities (including AXA SA as a reinsurance company) are subject to local regulatory capital requirements designed to protect policyholders and monitor capital adequacy. If the Group or any of its (re)insurance entities fail to meet minimum regulatory capital requirements, regulators would have broad authority to take various actions. Such a failure or severe deterioration of solvency may necessitate to inject significant new capital in the relevant Group's entities, which could adversely affect the Group's liquidity. For additional information, refer to "The Group's or its insurance or reinsurance entities' failure to meet their solvency and capital adequacy requirements" in Chapter 5.1 "Risk Factors" of this Document. The Group and AXA SA maintained Eligible Own Funds in excess of their Solvency Capital Requirement ("SCR") at all times during 2024 and monitor compliance with this requirement continuously. If the Group's Solvency II ratio were to deteriorate excessively, the AXA Group may take measures to improve it, such as being more selective on growth initiatives, increasing reinsurance, reducing its investment risk appetite or reducing its dividend. AXA's approach is to maintain its Group Solvency II ratio above the risk appetite limit of 140%. 2.4.4.2 Tiering analysis of capital Solvency II Eligible Own Funds relate to the Group's and AXA SA's (on a solo basis) available capital resources before tiering eligibility restrictions and after considering the potential non‑availability of certain capital elements. Eligible Own Funds are split into three tiers as defined under Solvency II regulations. The classification depends upon the extent to which the own fund item is immediately available to absorb losses including in case of a winding‑up (permanent availability) and subordinated to all other obligations including towards policyholders and beneficiaries. The highest‑quality capital elements are classified in Tier 1. Eligibility limits apply to these components to cover the Group's and AXA SA's (on a solo basis) SCR. For compliance with the SCR, the following quantitative limits apply: (a) the eligible amount of Tier 1 items must be at least 50% of the SCR, (b) the eligible amount of Tier 3 items must not exceed 15% of the SCR, and (c) the sum of the eligible amounts of Tier 2 and Tier 3 items must not exceed 50% of the SCR. Hybrid debt instruments eligible for Tier 1 must not exceed 20% of the total Tier 1 capital. 2.4.4.3 Dated and undated subordinated debt description Dated and undated subordinated notes issued by the Company qualify as Eligible Own Funds. Subordinated notes issued by the Company since January 18, 2015, are structured to be eligible as own funds under Solvency II regulations. Subordinated notes issued before January 17, 2015, mostly benefit from transitional provisions in Directive 2014/51/EU (Omnibus II), as they were eligible under the Solvency I regime and issued prior to the entry into force of Delegated Regulation EU 2015/35 of October 10, 2014. Such transitional provisions are ending on January 1, 2026. The Company has issued dated subordinated notes ("TSR"), undated subordinated notes ("TSDI"), and undated deeply subordinated notes ("TSS"), which include provisions designed to allow the Company to ensure continuity of activities if its financial position deteriorates. 2

 Certain TSR include clauses that permit or force the Company to defer interest payments. Additionally, redemption at maturity requires (i) prior approval by the ACPR and (ii) the absence of any event (a) causing the Company's and/or the Group's own funds to be below regulatory capital requirements, (b) triggering a requirement for the Company to take specific action regarding payments under the notes due to its financial condition, or (c) having an adverse effect on its insurance subsidiaries' ability to pay claims. Under the terms and conditions of AXA's TSDI, the Company may, and in certain circumstances must, defer interest payment. Deferred interest payment may become due in specified cases (e.g., payment of a dividend, end of a regulatory demand to restore solvency, liquidation of the Company, or redemption of the TSDI). Most of the Company's TSS and all TSS issued under the Solvency II regime (Restricted Tier 1) include loss absorption mechanisms which provide that under certain circumstances where the Company and/or the Group does not meet its regulatory capital requirements, the principal amount of each of the relevant TSS will be written down. In such event, interest will become payable on the reduced principal amount only. The principal may be reinstated when the Company and/or the Group returns to financial health, as defined under the terms and conditions of the TSS. For most of the Company's TSS issued under the Solvency I regime, the Company is required to defer interest payments upon certain events related to consolidated net earnings and shareholders' equity. Under its TSS issued under the Solvency I regime, the Company can cancel interest payments upon the deterioration of its financial position, unless certain events occurred in the preceding year, such as a dividend payment, interest payment on any TSS, share buy‑back outside the Company's share repurchase program, or a repurchase or redemption of any TSS. 2.4 Liquidity and capital resources Under its TSS issued under the Solvency II regime, the Company is required to cancel interest payments in certain circumstances, including when the Company and/or the Group does not meet (or would not meet, as a result of making such payment) its regulatory capital requirement, or when the Company distributable items are insufficient, as defined under the terms and conditions of such TSS. Furthermore, the Company has full discretion to cancel payment of interest in respect of such TSS. 2.4.4.4 Fungibility of Group capital In assessing whether fungibility restrictions on capital held by AXA's subsidiaries may limit coverage of the Group SCR, the Group considers whether: ● ● ● legal or regulatory requirements restrict the ability of own funds items to absorb all types of losses anywhere in the Group; legal or regulatory requirements restrict the transferability of assets to another insurance or reinsurance entity; and making those own funds available for covering the Group SCR is possible within 9 months. Own funds not deemed available at Group level are included in the Group's own funds if they are eligible for covering the SCR of the entity which holds them. However, subordinated debt instruments issued by Group subsidiaries are not deemed available at Group level if they cannot cover the Group's SCR, despite being eligible for the issuing entity's SCR. Currently, no subordinated debt instrument issued by a Group subsidiary is deemed available at Group level. The ACPR currently considers that AXA SA is not subject to financial conglomerate requirements. Activity Report and Capital Management AXA | Universal Registration Document 2024

 2.4 Liquidity and capital resources 2.4.5 Group capital management objectives Following the announcement of its "Unlock the Future" strategic plan on February 22, 2024, the AXA Group's capital management policy is based on the following principles: ● ● ● ● ●
AXA intends to pay annual dividends representing a payout ratio of 60% of the Group's UEPS. The dividend per share in a given year is targeted to be at least equal to the dividend paid in the prior year; AXA intends to buy back shares annually in an amount corresponding to 15% of the Group's UEPS, representing a total target payout ratio of 75% of the Group's UEPS when combined with the dividend per share; the share buy‑back component may be canceled by the Group if there is a significant earnings event i.e., significant deviation in the Group's Underlying Earnings. The dividend floor set at the prior year level may be canceled if there is a significant capital event (i.e., an event that significantly deteriorates the Group's solvency); AXA applies a strict financial discipline with the following priorities: (i) payment of the annual dividend, (ii) execution of annual share buy‑backs, (iii) and investment in inorganic growth; AXA also intends to buy back shares to neutralize dilution from employee share offerings, stock‑based compensation and loss of earnings from certain disposals and in‑force management transactions. These buy‑backs would be in addition to the annual share buy‑backs corresponding to 15% of the Group's UEPS. The Board of Directors has full discretion to set the dividend to be proposed at the relevant Shareholders' Meetings and to decide (pursuant to the relevant resolution in this respect) on share buy‑backs, based on factors such as AXA's earnings, financial condition, applicable capital and solvency requirements, prevailing operating and financial market conditions, and the economic environment. The AXA Group has implemented capital management standards to ensure competitiveness and maintain an adequate solvency ratio in accordance with local regulatory requirements. Management has also developed contingency plans, which may involve reinsurance, sales of investment portfolios and assets, or measures to reduce capital strain of new business. There can be no assurance, however, that these plans will be effective. For additional information, refer to Chapter 5.1 "Risk Factors" of this Document. 2

 2.6 Outlook 2.6 Outlook AXA is focused on executing its "Unlock the Future" plan over 2024‑2026, which is based on delivering profitable organic growth and scaling technical excellence across each of its businesses, while driving operational excellence across the organization. In P&C Personal lines, the Group aims at further accelerating volume growth while higher pricing is expected to continue to earn through. In P&C Commercial lines, pricing remains favorable in SME and mid‑market, while continuing to moderate in Large Commercial lines with conditions varying by lines. The Group will remain disciplined to ensure good cycle management and expects to maintain margins including through efficiency gains. The Group's natural catastrophe (1) load of ca. 4.5 points of combined ratio for 2025 is maintained. This includes the Group's current estimated losses from California wildfires of €0.1 billion, before tax and net of reinsurance (2). In Life & Health, in the near‑term, earnings growth should come from the Short‑Term Business reflecting attractive growth opportunities, notably from Employee Benefits, as well as continued margin expansion. New business volumes combined with improved persistency are expected to drive higher normalized CSM growth over time. Results in Holdings reflect investments in technology and growth initiatives and are expected to remain at a similar level in 2025. Considering the strong overall operating performance delivered in 2024, and assuming current operating conditions persist, Management believes AXA is on track to deliver the main financial targets of AXA's "Unlock the Future" plan: (i) Underlying Earnings Per Share growth of 6‑8% CAGR (3) target range between 2023 and 2026E, (ii) underlying Return On Equity between 14% and 16% between 2024 and 2026E, and (iii) cumulative organic cash upstream in excess of €21 billion for 2024‑2026. The Group is committed to its capital management policy (4), targeting a total payout ratio of 75% (5), comprising a 60% dividend payout ratio and an additional 15% from annual share buy‑backs .

The Board of Directors considers that AXA's corporate governance practices comply with the recommendations of the Afep‑Medef Code (revised in December 2022) as well as its implementation guidelines. The Board also takes into consideration the evolution of governance practices, both nationally and internationally, as well as the recommendations and standards of shareholders, regulators, the High Committee on Corporate Governance (Haut Comité de Gouvernement d'Entreprise), proxy advisors, rating agencies, and other stakeholders in setting its governance. 3.1.2 Presentation of AXA's governance 3

Assists the Chief Executive Officer in developing and implementing key strategic initiatives Chief Executive Officer Thomas Buberl of and represents the Company Acts in all circumstances on behalf 3.1.2.1 Separation of the roles of Chairman of the Board of Directors and Chief Executive Officer Since 2016, the roles of Chairman of the Board of Directors (the "Chairman") and Chief Executive Officer (the "CEO") have been separated. This decision is in line with the ACPR's recommendations, best governance practices, and the expectations of many shareholders, and is considered the most suitable for the Group's current situation and challenges. Thomas Buberl was appointed as CEO in 2016, at the same time as Denis Duverne, who became non‑independent Chairman. In 2022, whereas the mandate of Thomas Buberl as CEO was renewed, the Board decided to maintain this separated governance model and to appoint Antoine Gosset‑Grainville as independent Chairman, replacing Denis Duverne. In 2024, following the renewal of his mandate as director by the Shareholders' Meeting, the Board of Directors confirmed Antoine Gosset‑Grainville as Chairman. These decisions were taken in light of the complementary profiles and experiences of Antoine Gosset‑Grainville and Thomas Buberl, as well as the quality of governance in place both within the Board of Directors and during interactions with Management, as notably illustrated by the results of the annual assessment of the Board carried out in 2023. 3.1.2.2 Chairman of the Board of Directors 3.1 AXA's governance ● ● ● ● ● ● ● ● upon invitation from the CEO, he takes part in internal meetings with the Group's executives and teams to provide his opinion on strategic issues. The Chairman reports to the Board of Directors on such consultations and meetings; and upon request by the CEO, he represents the Company in its relations, on a national and international level, in particular with public bodies, institutions, regulators and shareholders as well as the Company's main strategic partners and stakeholders. As a general rule, the Chairman of the Board provides assistance and advice to the CEO and organizes his time to ensure that he is available and puts his experience to the service of the Group. His responsibilities are contributory and do not carry any executive authority. For any responsibilities that are entrusted to him other than by law, the Chairman acts in close collaboration with the CEO who is solely responsible for the Company's operational leadership and management. In this context, in 2024, the Chairman participated in: national and international events where he represented the Company; events with individual shareholders; internal meetings, at the CEO's request; and governance roadshows with institutional shareholders of the Company, in particular prior to the Shareholders' Meeting, to discuss governance, compensation and sustainability. In accordance with French law, the Chairman organizes and oversees the work of the Board of Directors. In this context, he notably (i) sets the agenda for Board meetings, (ii) maintains regular discussions with the CEO and directors, (iii) ensures that directors are able to carry out their duties, and (iv) organizes training programs for the directors. Given his experience, expertise and in‑depth knowledge of the Group, the Board has entrusted the following duties to its Chairman: he contributes to the promotion of the Company's values and culture and protects its reputation, using his best endeavors to promote the actions undertaken by the Group, in particular in relation to corporate responsibility and ethics and professional deontology; he regularly consults with the CEO and, on his initiative, on all major topics and events relating to the Company (including the Company's strategy, major acquisition or divestment projects, significant financial transactions, major community endeavors or the appointment of the principal executives of the Group); 3.1.2.3 Chief Executive Officer The CEO is entrusted with the most extensive powers to act on behalf of the Company in all circumstances and represents the Company in its dealings vis-à-vis third parties, within the limits of the corporate purpose and subject to the powers attributed to the Shareholders' Meetings and the Board of Directors. However, the Terms of Reference of the Board of Directors (the "Board's Terms of Reference") provide for specific limitations to the CEO's powers. Prior authorization of the Board is for instance required to perform certain transactions as set out in Section 3.3.1.3 - Powers of the Board of Directors of this Document. 3.1.2.4 Management Committee The CEO is supported by a Management Committee to assist him in steering the operational management of the Group. The Management Committee meets regularly to notably discuss the Group's strategic, financial, operational and sustainability matters. The Management Committee has no formal decision‑making power.
