Should the CEO no longer be in a position to perform his duties, temporarily or permanently, and unless decided otherwise by the Board, any Deputy Chief Executive Officer and, failing which, the persons who effectively run the Company (dirigeants effectifs) within the meaning of the Solvency II regulations chosen by the Board, shall manage the Company on an interim basis. Chairman The Compensation, Governance & Sustainability Committee establishes the succession plan for the Chairman with the Senior Independent Director, if any, the other Committee Chairs, as the case may be, and the CEO. Should the Chairman no longer be in a position to perform his duties, temporarily or permanently, the chairmanship of the Board shall be exercised on an interim basis by the Senior Independent Director failing which by the Chairman of the Compensation, Governance & Sustainability Committee. The succession plans for the CEO and the Chairman are reviewed at least annually by the Compensation, Governance & Sustainability Committee and are regularly examined by the Board. • Member 3.2.2 Process for appointing directors Process for appointing independent directors 1 Profile and applications 2 Interviews 3 Recommendation 4 Appointment The Compensation, Governance & Sustainability Committee: ●defines appropriate selection criteria reflecting the Board's needs, including the Board's diversity policy; ●identifies potential candidates based on predefined selection criteria potentially using external recruitment firms. The Compensation, Governance & Sustainability Committee organizes interviews between short‑listed candidates, the Chairman of the Compensation, Governance & Sustainability Committee and the CEO as well as with the Chairs of the other Board Committees, the Chairman, and any other director, as deemed appropriate. Following completion of this interview process, and after consultation with the Chairman and the CEO, the Chairman of the Compensation, Governance & Sustainability Committee presents the proposed candidates to the Compensation, Governance & Sustainability Committee for review and recommendation to the Board. The Board proposes the appointment of the selected candidate at a Shareholders' Meeting. Process for appointing the employee representatives As the Board of Directors is comprised of over eight members appointed at the Shareholders' Meeting, one director representing the employees must be appointed by the French Group Committee (Comité de Groupe France) and one by the European Works Council (Comité d'entreprise européen). The process for appointing directors representing the employees is detailed in the Company's Bylaws. The French Group Committee appointed Martine Bièvre on June 13, 2018, and decided to renew her mandate on April 28, 2022. The European Works Council appointed Bettina Cramm on June 20, 2018, and renewed her mandate on April 28, 2022. Process for appointing the employee shareholder representative As the participation of Group employees in the Company's capital exceeds the 3% threshold provided for by French law, a director representing the employee shareholders must be appointed at the Shareholders' Meeting from among the candidates proposed by the employee shareholders. The process for appointing the employee shareholder representative is detailed in the Company's Bylaws. Helen Browne was appointed as employee shareholder representative for the first time on June 30, 2020, and was reappointed at the Shareholders' Meeting held on April 23, 2024.  3.2 Composition of the Board of Directors and its Committees 3.2.5 Directors' independence In accordance with the Afep‑Medef Code, a director is independent if he or she has no relationship of any kind whatsoever with the Company, its Group or its management that may interfere with his or her freedom of judgment. Accordingly, a director is qualified as independent when he or she meets the following criteria: During its meeting held on February 26, 2025, the Board assessed the independence of each of its directors (excluding the members representing the employees and the employee shareholders, i.e. Martine Bièvre, Bettina Cramm and Helen Browne) on December 31, 2024 according to the Afep‑Medef Code criteria. The Board noted that four members do not meet all the Afep‑Medef Code criteria: Thomas Buberl, Gérald Harlin, Ramon de Oliveira and Angelien Kemna. 3

Criterion 2 - Cross‑mandates: not to be an executive officer of a company in which AXA directly or indirectly holds a directorship or in which an employee appointed as such or an executive officer of AXA (currently in office or having held such office within the last five years) holds a mandate; ‑
Criterion 3 - Significant business relationships: not to be a customer, supplier, commercial banker, investment banker, or consultant that is significant for AXA or the Group or for which AXA represents a significant portion of its activity; Criterion 4 - Family ties: not to be related by close family ties to a corporate officer of AXA; Criterion 5 - Statutory Auditor: not to have been a statutory auditor of AXA within the previous five years; Criterion 6 - Term of office exceeding 12 years: not to have been an AXA director for more than twelve years; Criterion 7 - Status of non‑executive corporate officer: a non‑executive officer cannot be considered independent if he/she receives variable compensation in cash or in the form of securities or any compensation linked to the performance of AXA or the Group; ‑
Criterion 8 - Status of major shareholder: directors representing major shareholders of AXA may be considered independent, provided those shareholders do not take part in the control of AXA. Nevertheless, beyond a 10% threshold in capital or voting rights, the Board, upon report from the Compensation, Governance & Sustainability Committee, should systematically review the qualification of a director as independent taking into account the composition of AXA's share capital and potential conflicts of interest. 64
 Regarding the specific situation of Angelien Kemna, the fact that she sits on the Board of AXA Investment Managers as non‑executive director was a point for consideration by the Board as part of its overall assessment. After discussion and in line with its previous assessments, the Board has concluded that this situation neither prevents Angelien Kemna from exercising her mandate as a director of AXA with complete independence nor impairs her independence under the Afep‑Medef Code given her past performance as a director as well as her independent mindset. This conclusion was further supported by the fact that AXA Investment Managers is a 97%- owned unlisted subsidiary of the Group and that any potential conflict could be resolved by Angelien Kemna abstaining from any discussion and/or vote on the relevant subject, in accordance with the Board's Terms of Reference. In addition, considering Angelien Kemna's experience and knowledge of the asset management business, this position provides the Board with valuable insight into the strategy, operational activities, processes, and teams of AXA Investment Managers, and more broadly into the Group's asset management strategy and activities. These skills have been particularly useful to the Board in recent years in a context in which the asset management sector has undergone significant changes and in which the Board has discussed the strategic positioning, risks, and opportunities for AXA Investment Managers on numerous occasions. Due to this, procedures that involve a regular review of the situation have been put in place to manage potential conflicts of interest that could arise from Angelien Kemna's mandate at AXA Investment Managers. The Board considered that Angelien Kemna was not in a position of conflict of interest during the preliminary discussions. However, 3.2 Composition of the Board of Directors and its Committees it was concluded that Angelien Kemna should abstain from participating in any deliberation and vote related to the sale of AXA Investment Managers to BNP Paribas and the conclusion of a long‑term strategic agreement in which BNP Paribas would provide investment management services to AXA. The Board also specifically assessed the business relationships between independent directors and the Group. To this end, the Board (1) reviewed the annual disclosures submitted by directors detailing any business relationships with the Group during the past year and confirming that these are not likely to create a conflict of interest, and if applicable, (2) qualitatively and quantitatively analyzed each business relationship, taking into account; (i) the director's position within the entity with a business relationship with the Group, the existence of direct decision‑making power over the relationship, or any compensation received by the director relating to the relationship; and (ii) the significance of the relationship, ensuring that the business relationships do not represent a significant portion of the Group's overall business in terms of revenues, nor create any potential economic dependency or exclusivity. The Board concluded that none of the independent directors had significant business relationships with the Company or the Group that could compromise their independence. In light of the foregoing, the Board of Directors concluded that nine out of twelve (1) directors should be considered independent according to the Afep‑Medef Code criteria: Clotilde Delbos, Rachel Duan, Guillaume Faury, Ramon Fernandez, Antoine Gosset‑Grainville, Isabel Hudson, Angelien Kemna, Rachel Picard and Marie‑France Tschudin. 3.2.7 Proposed changes within the Board and its Committees following the 2025 Shareholders' Meeting The mandates of three directors will expire at the end of the Shareholders' Meeting to be held on April 24, 2025: Guillaume Faury, Ramon Fernandez and Ramon de Oliveira. It will be proposed at the 2025 Shareholders' Meeting to renew the mandates of Guillaume Faury and Ramon Fernandez as directors for a four‑year term. Ramon de Oliveira, director for the past 15 years and member of the Finance & Risk Committee, will not seek the renewal of his mandate and it will not be proposed to replace him at the 2025 Shareholders' Meeting. It will also be proposed at the 2025 Shareholders' Meeting to ratify the cooptation of Ewout Steenbergen as a director, replacing Rachel Duan, for the remainder of her mandate, i.e. until the end of the 2026 Shareholders' Meeting called to approve the 2025 financial statements. Subject to shareholder approval of the renewal of the mandates of Guillaume Faury and Ramon Fernandez and the cooptation of Ewout Steenbergen, the Board of Directors, after the 2025 Shareholders' Meeting, will comprise 14 members, including (i) 8 women (57.1% including employee and employee shareholder representatives) (1) and (ii) 9 independent members (81.8%) according to the Afep‑Medef Code criteria, excluding employee and employee shareholder representatives and the composition of the Committees will be as follows: ● ● ●
Finance & Risk Committee: Ramon Fernandez (Chairman), Clotilde Delbos, Gérald Harlin, Isabel Hudson and Ewout Steenbergen, with four members out of five considered as independent by the Board in accordance with the Afep‑Medef Code criteria; Compensation, Governance & Sustainability Committee: Guillaume Faury (Chairman), Bettina Cramm, Gérald Harlin, Rachel Picard and Marie‑France Tschudin with three members out of five considered as independent by the Board in accordance with the Afep‑Medef Code criteria; Audit Committee: Isabel Hudson (Chairwoman), Clotilde Delbos, Ramon Fernandez, Angelien Kemna and Ewout Steenbergen; all members are considered as independent by the Board in accordance with the Afep‑Medef Code criteria. (1) 45.5% of women and 55.5% of men, excluding directors representing the employees and the director representing the employee shareholders, in accordance with applicable laws as of the date of this Document.  France Appointed on April 23, 2024 – Term expires at the 2028 Shareholders' Meeting First appointment on June 30, 2020 (director) – April 28, 2022 (Chairman of the Board of Directors) Number of shares: 4,268 Antoine Gosset‑Grainville Chairman of the AXA Board of Directors (independent) Partner at BDGS Associés, Law Firm Expertise and experience Antoine Gosset‑Grainville is a graduate of the Institut d'études politiques of Paris and holds a "DESS" (postgraduate degree) in banking and finance from the University of Paris IX Dauphine. He was admitted to the Paris Bar (2002) and the Brussels Bar (2002). After graduating from the French École nationale d'administration, he began his career, in 1993, at the Inspection Générale des Finances, before taking the position of Deputy General Secretary of the Economic and Financial Committee of the European Union in 1997. From 1999 to 2002, he was advisor for Economic and Industry Affairs in the office of the European Commissioner in charge of Trade. In 2002, he became partner at Gide (law firm), where he managed the Brussels office for five years before becoming Deputy Director of the Cabinet of the French Prime Minister in 2007, in charge of economic and financial issues and the management of the French State's investment portfolio. In 2010, he was appointed Deputy Chief Executive Officer (Directeur Général Adjoint) of the Caisse des Dépôts et Consignations in charge of finance, strategy, investments and international operations and interim Chief Executive Officer of the Caisse des Dépôts group from February to July 2012. In April 2013, he co‑founded BDGS Associés (law firm). On April 28, 2022, Antoine Gosset‑Grainville was appointed Chairman of the Board of Directors of AXA.  3.3 Organization and activities of the Board of Directors and its Committees 3.3 Organization and activities of the Board of Directors and its Committees 3.3.1 Operating framework The Board of Directors is supported by three Committees: the Audit Committee, the Finance & Risk Committee and the Compensation, Governance & Sustainability Committee. The Board's Terms of Reference detail the role and responsibilities of the Board and its Committees, as well as the decisions falling within the Board's exclusive competence. In addition, to promote the alignment of the directors' personal interests with those of the Company, directors (excluding directors representing the employees) shall be shareholders of the Company and are required to hold a number of shares equal to at least one year's compensation as valued each year on December 31. 3.3.1.1
 Ethics 3.3.1.2 Information and training Directors shall act in all circumstances in the corporate interest (intérêt social) of the Company and its shareholders and shall avoid carrying out activities or transactions which could give rise to a conflict of interest with the Company. Each director has the obligation to inform the Chairman and/or the Senior Independent Director, if any, of any personal situation likely to give rise to a conflict of interest with the Company or with Group companies and to abstain from engaging in discussion or voting in relation to any such potential conflict of interest. Directors must comply with the following obligations: ● ● ● ● ● a general obligation of secrecy, confidentiality and discretion; observing the rules aimed at limiting the number of mandates held in other companies in order to be able to devote the necessary time and attention to their role; having the integrity required to perform their duties, enabling them to contribute to a sound and prudent management of the Company; complying in particular with the AXA Compliance & Ethics Code, notably on trading (including black‑out periods) and conflicts of interest; and reporting to the AMF and the Company any transactions they may have performed on AXA securities (for transactions declared in 2024, please refer to Section 3.2.9.5 - Transactions reported in 2024 by directors and closely associated persons involving the Company's securities of this Document). Directors are informed by the CEO by any means and on a regular basis of the Company's financial situation, cash flows and commitments, together with any significant events or transactions relating to the Company. At least quarterly, the CEO shall report to the Board of Directors on the activities and operations of the Company. In addition, each director may request any documents that he/she deems useful for performing his/her responsibilities. Upon his/her appointment, each director receives training related to the Company's businesses, activities, the risks and challenges it faces in terms of sustainability, including climate‑related issues. Any director may also receive further training if he/she deems it appropriate. The Board collectively receives regular training adapted to the challenges facing the Company. For further information, please refer to Section 3.3.2.3 - Training of this Document. Directors representing the employees and the director representing the employee shareholders may also receive customized training. In addition, each director may attend meetings of the Audit Committee or the Finance & Risk Committee, even if he/she is not a member of that Committee.  3.3 Organization and activities of the Board of Directors and its Committees 3.3.1.3 Powers of the Board of Directors The Board of Directors exercises the powers vested in it by applicable laws and acts at all times in the corporate interest of the Company. The Board endeavors to promote long‑term value creation by considering the interests of the Company's various stakeholders and its social and environmental impacts. It notably has the power to: ● ● ● ● ● ● ● ● ● ● ● ● ● ●
choose the Company's governance model;
appoint and dismiss the Chairman, any Senior Independent Director, the CEO and, upon the CEO's proposal, any Deputy CEOs and the persons who effectively run the Company; determine the strategic orientations of the Company's activities and ensure their implementation; notably the multi‑year strategic orientations in terms of social and environmental responsibility. In conjunction with these strategic orientations, it regularly monitors opportunities and risks such as financial, legal, operational, social and environmental risks as well as measures taken in relation thereto; prepare the Company's annual financial statements, the Group's consolidated financial statements and half‑year financial statements, and the management report, including in particular sustainability‑related information; convene Shareholders' Meetings and present a report on corporate governance to the shareholders; fulfill its obligations under applicable laws and regulations (including Solvency II and the Digital Operational Resilience Act (DORA)); set the risk appetite and overall risk tolerance limits for the Company; determine the compensation of the corporate officers and the granting of long term incentives; authorize regulated agreements (conventions réglementées).
In addition, certain significant transactions are subject to the prior authorization of the Board, including in particular: disposals or acquisitions (exceeding €500 million per transaction or €2 billion in aggregate over one year); reinsurance transactions entered into with a third party and resulting in a transfer of reserves for €10 billion or more; share buy‑backs other than those intended to hedge employee shareholding and share allocations to employees or corporate officers; sureties, guarantees, endorsements or warranties provided in favor of third parties (exceeding €200 million per transaction executed in order to guarantee obligations of the Company's subsidiaries or sureties put up to guarantee its own obligations, or €100 million for other guarantees, endorsements and warranties, and, up to a limit of €1 billion in aggregate over one year); and financial transactions that may substantially change the Company's financial structure or fall outside the strategy previously announced by the Company. 3

 3.3 Organization and activities of the Board of Directors and its Committees 3.3.2.2 Executive sessions In accordance with the Board's Terms of Reference, the Chairman convenes the directors at least once a year without the presence of the CEO. In addition, the Chair of the Compensation, Governance & Sustainability Committee shall convene the members of the Board of Directors, at least once a year, without the presence of the Chairman of the Board of Directors and Executive Management. These meetings are an opportunity to assess the performance of the Chairman of the Board of Directors, to review his/her compensation and prepare his/her succession plan. In 2024, the directors met three times without the presence of the CEO to discuss, in particular, the evaluation of his performance, his compensation and his succession. The Board also met once without the presence of the Chairman (and the CEO) to discuss his performance in the context of the Board assessment, as well as his compensation and succession. 3.3.2.3 Training In 2024, directors received training on the CSRD, the double materiality assessment and ESRS (European Sustainability Reporting Standards). Directors received training on the reduction of the carbon intensity of the Group's investments, the decarbonization of the Group's own operations and the alignment with Group incentives. Prior to this training, the directors were also invited to take the "Low carbon transition" course on the AXA Climate platform dedicated to the Board. The Board of Directors received a presentation on sustainable and responsible forest management during its visit to one of the forests managed by the Group. The Board also participated in a solidarity event organized by AXA Atout Cœur and received a presentation on volunteering activities carried out within the Group. Directors received a training on risks related to ICT, including cybersecurity. The training plan for 2025, approved by the Board of Directors upon recommendation from its Compensation, Governance & Sustainability Committee, includes the following: ● ● ● ●
transition underwriting;
internal model and natural catastrophes;
nature, biodiversity and oceans;
the fight against financial crime, including corruption.
In addition, directors will continue to benefit from AXA Climate's training program, particularly on planet boundaries and the impact of climate change on human society. 3.3.2.4 Shareholder dialogue As part of its dialogue with shareholders, AXA regularly organizes meetings with investors and proxy advisors prior to the Shareholders' Meeting on topics related to governance, compensation and sustainability issues (governance roadshows). In 2024, AXA met with many institutional investors and proxy advisors to present the Group's new strategic plan, the composition, the organization and activity of the Board, executive compensation and the Group's sustainability strategy. The Chairman of the Board took part in a number of these meetings, alongside representatives of the relevant teams of the Company. The main questions and comments from investors and proxy advisors as well as a proposed action plan were presented to the Compensation, Governance & Sustainability Committee, which reported to the Board on this matter. 3

 3.3.3.4 Joint sessions of the Committees In 2024, several joint sessions between Board Committees were organized. Joint sessions of the Audit Committee and the Compensation, Governance & Sustainability Committee were held on three occasions, notably to discuss (i) SNFP, (ii) the climate and biodiversity reports, (iii) the double materiality assessment and (iv) the structure of the future Sustainability Statement. 3.3 Organization and activities of the Board of Directors and its Committees Joint sessions of the Audit Committee and the Finance & Risk Committee were also held on three occasions, notably to review: (i) the risk sections of the financial statements and management report, as well as the main off‑balance sheet commitments, (ii) the Group and Company ORSA, SFCR, and RSR reports, (iii) the Group and Company reinsurance strategy and (iv) the Solvency II policies. 3.3.4 Assessment of the Board of Directors and its Committees In accordance with the recommendations of the Afep‑Medef Code, the Board of Directors assesses and discusses its composition, organization, and activities, as well as those of its Committees, on an annual basis. In 2023, the Board's assessment was carried out by an external consultant (Russell Reynolds Associates), under the supervision of the Compensation, Governance & Sustainability Committee. This consultant was selected from among several providers following a tender process, based notably on the following criteria: (i) experience in Board assessments, (ii) independence, (iii) knowledge of the industry, etc. Each director completed an online questionnaire, and then met with the external consultant to assess, in particular (i) the progress made compared to the conclusions of the 2022 self‑assessment, (ii) the composition and the organization of the Board and its Committees, (iii) the role of the Chairman, (iv) the relationship between the Board and Management and (v) the alignment of the Board with the Group strategy, particularly in terms of sustainability. During the individual interviews, the directors were also asked to comment on other members' individual contributions to the Board's work. All results were anonymous, and the directors received individual feedback from the Chairman of the Board on their respective contributions. In 2024, the assessment exercise was conducted internally. In December 2024, the Compensation, Governance & Sustainability Committee conducted a review of the results of this assessment before submitting its conclusions to the Board of Directors, including identified areas for improvement and a proposed action plan. Corporate Governance 3
Assessment procedure implemented in 2024 Each director (with the exception of the Chairman of the Board): 1. replied in writing to an anonymized questionnaire; 2. met with the Chairman of the Board to give his/her opinion on the composition, organization and activities of the Board as well as the other members' individual contribution to the Board's work. Each director then received an individual feedback from the Chairman on his/her contribution; 3. met with the Chairman of the Compensation, Governance & Sustainability Committee to assess the Chairman's contribution to the Board's work. Results The assessment highlighted the positive perception by directors of the composition and organization of the Board and its Committees. The directors noted the progress made in those areas of improvement identified in the previous assessment, which notably led to (i) the appointment of Ewout Steenbergen, (ii) the implementation of a work plan including regular deep dives on business topics, (iii) the review of the M&A approval process and the succession processes, (iv) the review of the Board's Terms of Reference, and (v) the organization of several joint sessions of the Committees. The directors confirmed that, both individually and collectively, the Board of Directors had the relevant skills, expertise and experience to carry out its duties. Areas for improvement The Board identified several areas for improvement, in particular: 1. continue to operationalize the division of responsibilities for sustainability between the Committees and the Board; 2. discuss more regularly the competitive environment in which AXA operates and the risks to which the Group is exposed; 3. continue to refine the M&A process; 4. further discuss human resources developments and strategy at the Board; 5. provide more information on upcoming regulatory changes and their potential implications for the Group.  3.4 Agreements 3.4.1 Regulated agreements 3.4 Agreements During the financial year ended December 31, 2024, the Company did not enter into any agreements falling within the scope of "regulated agreements" (conventions réglementées) pursuant to French law which would require shareholder approval. 3.4.2 Assessment procedure for ordinary agreements entered into on arm's length terms and conditions The Board adopted a procedure to assess on a regular basis whether agreements relating to ordinary agreements entered into on arm's length terms and conditions actually meet these conditions. This procedure, which is reviewed by the Board annually, involves promptly informing the Group Legal Department before entering into any agreement falling within the scope of Article L.225‑38 of the French Commercial Code, regardless of the ordinary nature of the transaction or its arm's length terms. The Group Legal Department conducts a preliminary review of the agreement to determine whether it should be subject to the "regulated agreements" procedure set out in Articles L.225‑38 et seq. of the French Commercial Code, or if it is exempt. For this purpose, the Group Legal Department may seek the opinion of other relevant departments within the AXA Group and of the Statutory Auditors. Furthermore, the Group Legal Department annually assesses, in cooperation with AXA Group's relevant departments, and with the Statutory Auditors where necessary, whether the ongoing agreements entered into on arm's length terms and conditions continue to meet the conditions for such qualification. If the Group Legal Department considers that an agreement initially qualified as ordinary and entered into on arm's length terms and conditions constitutes a "regulated agreement", it will inform the Chairman of the Board. The Board will then decide on the qualification of the said agreement and on the actions to be taken in relation thereto according to the qualification adopted. This procedure was reviewed by the Board during its meeting of February 26, 2025. 3.4.3 Agreements entered into between corporate officers or certain shareholders of the Company and controlled companies For further information on related‑party transactions, please refer to Chapter 6 Note 25 - Related‑party transactions of this Document.  3.5 Executive compensation and share ownership 3.5 Executive compensation and share ownership Guiding principles of AXA's compensation policy AXA's compensation policy is designed to attract, develop and motivate critical skills and talents, encourage superior performance and align compensation levels with the results of the Company. To align the interests of the Group's executives and employees with those of the Company and its shareholders, the compensation policy is based on a pay‑for‑performance approach that (i) requires the achievement of challenging financial and operational targets that are defined and aligned with the Group's strategy, (ii) promotes long‑term sustainable performance while incorporating risk adjustment measures in performance metrics and (iii) determines the effective of the actual individual compensation on the basis of financial and extra‑financial results. ● competitiveness and market consistency of the remuneration practices while avoiding potential conflicts of interest that may lead to undue risk taking for short‑term gain; ● fairness based on individual and collective performance, in order to ensure remuneration is reflecting employees' individual quantitative and qualitative achievements and impact; ● internal equity based on remuneration policies and procedures designed to ensure that employees are paid equitably based on objective and justifiable professional criteria (such as, but not limited to, role, skills, contribution or impact), and do not discriminate on the basis of factors irrelevant to duties of the role; and ● achievement of the Group's overall financial and extra‑financial, as well as operational objectives over the short, medium, and long term and execution against medium and long‑term strategic objectives as a prerequisite to fund any mid‑to‑long term award. Thus, AXA's policy on executive compensation focuses on the variable part of compensation at risk for beneficiaries and encourages both individual and collective performance over the short, medium and long term while ensuring compliance with regulatory requirements. It follows four main guiding principles: The requirements set out in AXA's compensation policy may be supplemented in order to comply with local regulatory requirements. The Policy is reviewed and updated annually to mirror, as the case may be, changes in the internal organization and the nature, scale and complexity of the risks inherent to AXA's business. 3.5.1 Compensation policy for the Company's corporate officers (submitted to the Shareholders' Meeting on April 24, 2025) This policy was prepared in accordance with articles L.22‑10‑8 and R.22‑10‑14 of the French Commercial Code and presents the principles and criteria for determination, distribution, and allocation of all elements of compensation of the corporate officers (mandataires sociaux, i.e. Chief Executive Officer, Chairman of the Board and other directors) of the Company as approved by the Board of Directors on February 26, 2025, after recommendation from its Compensation, Governance & Sustainability Committee. AXA regularly engages with its shareholders to discuss governance, compensation and sustainability matters. Following the 2024 Shareholders' Meeting and after having analyzed investors' feedback and the results of the votes, the Board of Directors has decided to maintain the main terms of the compensation policy approved by shareholders on April 23, 2024, which were considered appropriate, fair and balanced. As part of the continuous shareholder engagement approach, the Board of Directors will keep monitoring investors' and proxy advisors' expectations, along with market practices. The Compensation, Governance & Sustainability Committee, which is mainly composed of independent members in accordance with the Afep‑Medef Code, is responsible for formulating proposals to the Board on the compensation and principles for the Company's corporate officers. The Committee meets frequently with the Group executives and the departments of the Company including Group human resources, Group Legal and Group Sustainability. It may also work with a compensation‑consulting firm in order to benefit from external technical expertise and an independent overview in order to compare AXA's variable compensation practices with market's.  3.5 Executive compensation and share ownership 3.5.1.1 Compensation policy for the Chief Executive Officer Amount, structure and criteria for determining the compensation of the Chief Executive Officer The Chief Executive Officer's compensation policy is based on practices of AXA's main European and international competitors (insurance, reinsurance companies and banks) and other CAC 40 companies. It is also in line with the recommendations of the Afep‑Medef Code, the G20, the European Commission and the Financial Stability Board (FSB). The overall amount, structure and criteria of the Chief Executive Officer's compensation are determined in accordance with the guiding principles of AXA's compensation policy described above. In application of the pay‑for‑performance approach, the at‑risk portion of the CEO's total compensation (variable cash compensation and share‑based compensation) represents a significant component of his compensation structure to align his compensation with the operation strategy of the Group and the interests of shareholders. Upon the renewal of Thomas Buberl's term as Chief Executive Officer in 2022, the Board of Directors, following the recommendation of the Compensation, Governance & Sustainability Committee, decided that his total compensation would remain unchanged for the duration of his term of office, i.e. until 2026.  The different components of the Chief Executive Officer's total compensation are presented hereafter: Annual fixed compensation of the Chief Executive Officer The determination of the amount of the Chief Executive Officer's annual fixed compensation, in 2022, was notably based on an in‑depth analysis of market practices carried out by an independent external advisory firm (Willis Towers Watson) as well as applicable regulations. It also took into consideration experience, skills, as well as the criticality and scarcity of such skills, and the Group's fairness principles or the individual's compensation history. Upon recommendation from its Compensation, Governance & Sustainability Committee, the Board confirmed that the Chief Executive Officer's annual fixed compensation, set in 2022, at €1.65 million will be maintained for 2025. Annual variable compensation of the Chief Executive Officer When it determined the Chief Executive Officer's target annual variable compensation, in 2022, following a comparative review of national, European, international and industry practices, the Board of Directors sought to establish a balanced structure between the fixed part and the variable part of his cash compensation. Upon recommendation from its Compensation, Governance & Sustainability Committee, the Board confirmed that the Chief Executive Officer's target annual variable compensation, set in 2022, at €1.75 million (i.e. 106% of the amount of his annual fixed compensation) will be maintained for 2025 and would continue to be subject in its entirety to challenging performance conditions and closely aligned with the Group's strategy. No minimum payment is guaranteed to the Chief Executive Officer and his total effective variable compensation may not exceed 130% of his target variable compensation (i.e. 142% of his annual fixed compensation). The evaluation of the Chief Executive Officer's annual performance for the 2025 financial year will be based on the following two components, each capped at a 130% achievement rate: ● The Group's performance, evaluated on the basis of (i) Underlying Earnings per share, (ii) cash remittance, (iii) the reduction in Group carbon footprint in General Account assets, and (iv) the Net Promoter Score (customer recommendation index). The relative weight of each indicator will remain unchanged (i.e. 45%, 25%, 15% and 15% respectively). The financial and extra‑financial indicators chosen to measure the Group performance are directly linked to the Group's strategic orientations for growth, capital management, sustainability and customer proximity and rely on the achievement of a predefined budget or target defined by the Board of Directors. Each of the indicators is associated with a floor threshold below which the performance condition is considered not to be met and will be capped at a 130% achievement rate. Some of these indicators (Underlying Earnings per share, cash remittance and the reduction in Group carbon ● 3.5 Executive compensation and share ownership footprint in General Account assets) are identical to share‑based compensation indicators in order to align part of the performance criteria of the short- and long‑term compensation with the objectives of the strategic plan and to measure them on different time scales through different acquisition schedules. The
 Individual performance, based on (i) various indicators and qualitative and quantifiable objectives determined by the Board of Directors in a target letter drawn up at the beginning of the year, and (ii) demonstrated leadership abilities by the Chief Executive Officer. The target letter includes detailed objectives related to the Group's progress in the implementation of its strategic plan as well as other performance indicators and objectives assessing the achievement of global strategic initiatives or related to certain geographic areas, as well as progress on investments that are expected to contribute to the development of the Group's operations. Each of the indicators will be capped at a 130% achievement rate. With respect to 2025, the Board of Directors, upon recommendation from its Compensation, Governance & Sustainability Committee, set the five following objectives and their respective weights: (i) successfully deliver on the second year of the strategic plan, especially on the financial targets (30%), (ii) further assess strategic opportunities and initiatives (20%), (iii) portfolio management: develop and decide M&A strategy and successfully close AXA investment Managers' sale (15%), (iv) begin reflection on the key challenges for the next strategic plan (2027‑2029) (10%), and (v) people: successfully drive the efficiency and cohesion of the Management Committee, and further develop the solid succession plans (25%). With respect to 2025, the determination of the actual amount of variable compensation to be paid to the Chief Executive Officer will continue to be based on the addition of two components: the Group performance for 70% and the individual performance for 30%. Each of these two components will be evaluated separately so that the Chief Executive Officer's overall variable pay‑out reflects his performance against two distinct components assessed independently. To ensure that AXA remains aligned with current market practice and regulations within the financial industry, both in France and abroad, the Board of Directors decided to continue to use a deferral mechanism with respect to the Chief Executive Officer's annual variable compensation. Under this mechanism, the payment of 30% of his actual annual variable compensation will be deferred over a three‑year period paid out in three equal tranches and will remain subject to performance conditions. The deferred amount effectively paid out for each tranche will vary accordingly depending on the performance of the AXA share price over the deferral period within the limit of a cap at 130% of the deferred amount but without a floor. No deferred variable compensation would be paid (i) in the event that the Group's Underlying Earnings are negative for the year ending immediately prior to the year of the scheduled payout, or (ii) in case of resignation or dismissal, for gross or willful misconduct prior to the payout date.  3.5 Executive compensation and share ownership In case of significant change affecting the calculation of the Group's economic parameters (significant transaction approved by the Board of Directors, change in accounting standards…), the Board would be able to calculate the parameters mutatis mutandis, i.e. without taking into account extraordinary external elements, while ensuring that this adjustment does not compromise the demanding nature of the performance criteria set by this policy. The Board of Directors also reserves the right to exercise its discretionary power regarding the determination of the Chief Executive Officer's compensation pursuant to legal provisions and in accordance with articles L.22‑10‑8 and L.22‑10‑34 of the French Commercial Code, should any particular circumstance arise such as a major event impacting the markets, the economic environment, and/or the Group's sector of activity, and justify an exceptional adjustment, either upwards (within the limit of 130% of the target variable compensation) or downwards to ensure that the application of the abovementioned criteria fairly reflect the Chief Executive Officer's performance as well as that of the Group. Such adjustment would be decided by the Board of Directors, upon proposal from its Compensation, Governance & Sustainability Committee and would be made public. Payment of the Chief Executive Officer's variable cash compensation for 2025 is subject to the approval by the Shareholders' Meeting to be held in 2026 of the compensation elements paid during, or granted with respect to, the 2025 fiscal year to the Chief Executive Officer. 3
Corporate Governance Share‑based compensation granted to the Chief Executive Officer ‑
Each year, the Board of Directors, upon recommendation from its Compensation, Governance & Sustainability Committee, decides to grant Long‑Term Incentives (LTI) to the Chief Executive Officer in the form of performance shares. To give the Chief Executive Officer a stake in long‑term value creation, these performance shares represent an important part of his compensation. However, the value of the performance shares granted to the Chief Executive Officer, as determined in accordance with the IFRS standards, may not in any event exceed half of his total compensation. The Board of Directors also decided that the number of LTI granted to the Company's executive corporate officers, including the Chief Executive Officer, may not exceed 10% of the total number of LTI granted to all beneficiaries within the Group. The performance shares granted to the Chief Executive Officer are entirely subject to demanding internal and external performance conditions (the details of which are presented in Section 3.5.2 - Corporate officers' and executives' compensation of this Document), which are assessed over a period of three years (followed by a two‑year holding period), and do not guarantee a minimum grant or gain. Moreover, the performance shares plan rules provide that should the Chief Executive Officer leave his position (1) at any time before the end of the performance period, any performance shares initially granted with respect to such period would be irremediably lost, unless otherwise decided by the Board of Directors in a decision publicly disclosed at the time of the Chief Executive Officer's departure (in such case, all or part of the performance shares could be maintained, and the vesting calendar and performance conditions attached to the performance shares set at the grant date would remain unchanged). In order to align the Chief Executive Officer's compensation more directly with the Group's long‑term performance and the interests of shareholders, the Board of Directors, upon proposal from its Compensation, Governance & Sustainability Committee, has decided in 2022 that the total value of the performance shares to be granted to the Chief Executive Officer, shall not exceed 180% of the amount of his target annual variable compensation. Through this change, the Board of Directors sought to (i) give significant importance to the Chief Executive Officer' share‑based compensation, which will represent almost 50% of his total compensation, and (ii) strengthen the "at‑risk" portion of his total compensation (variable cash compensation and share‑based compensation) so that it represents the vast majority (approximately 75%) of his total compensation. The Board has decided to maintain this principle for 2025. The Board of Directors has decided to maintain for 2025 the principles according to which, as long as the Chief Executive Officer has not met his Minimum Shareholding Requirement (i.e. holding a number of shares representing his annual fixed compensation multiplied by three), all stock options and performance shares granted to him will be subject to the following restrictions: ● upon each exercise of these stock options granted, the Chief Executive Officer must continue to hold in registered form a number of shares obtained upon exercise equal in value to at least 25% of the pre‑tax capital gain realized upon exercise. These shares shall be held during his entire term of office as Chief Executive Officer; ●
for performance shares granted, the Chief Executive Officer must, at every performance share acquisition date, hold in registered form at least 25% of the performance shares acquired during his entire term of office as Chief Executive Officer. These restrictions do not apply if the Chief Executive Officer complies with his Minimum Shareholding Requirement. (1) Except in the event of death, invalidity or retirement.  Exceptional compensation of the Chief Executive Officer The Board of Directors does not contemplate granting any exceptional compensation to the Chief Executive Officer. Directors' compensation of the Chief Executive Officer The Chief Executive Officer, who is also a member of the Board of Directors of the Company, is not entitled to payment of any directors' compensation from the Company. Benefits in kind granted to the Chief Executive Officer The Chief Executive Officer benefits from the use of a company car. The Chief Executive Officer also benefits from social benefits (health expenses, provident funds, etc.) on terms equivalent to those of all other AXA Group management executives in France. Compensation elements related to the retirement of the Chief Executive Officer The Chief Executive Officer does not participate in any pension schemes with defined benefits. As all other executives of AXA Group entities in France, he participates in the retirement performance share plan, under which grants are made on an annual basis. The retirement performance shares granted under such plans are subject to (i) an acquisition period of three years, and (ii) an undertaking not to sell the retirement performance shares before the date on which the beneficiary retires, subject to the option offered to the beneficiaries, for diversification purposes, to sell their shares (after the end of the acquisition period of three years) as long as the proceeds of such sale are invested in a long‑term savings plan until the beneficiary retires. The acquisition of the retirement performance shares is subject to the satisfaction of a performance condition (the details of which are set out in Section 3.5 - Executive compensation and share ownership of this Document), linked to the average AXA Group Solvency II ratio calculated over the performance period. No minimum grant or gain is guaranteed to the Chief Executive Officer under this scheme. The Board of Directors, upon recommendation from its Compensation, Governance & Sustainability Committee, decided that the total value of the retirement performance shares to be granted to the Chief Executive Officer during 2025 shall not exceed 15% of his fixed and cash annual variable compensation. As all other employees of AXA Group entities in France, the Chief Executive Officer participates in a mandatory and collective supplementary pension scheme with defined contributions (collective insurance contract – contrat d'assurance de Groupe as defined in article L.141‑1 of the French Insurance Code). This AXA pension fund was implemented in 2011 and benefits to the employees of AXA Group entities in France falling within the Scope of the Collective Agreement of December 18, 2009. The individual 3.5 Executive compensation and share ownership employer contribution rate is set at 0.75% of the total gross compensation, which is not capped. The contribution base is the same as the one used for contributions related to the Social Security General Scheme. Contributions are subject to 9.7% social charges (9.2% for CSG and 0.5% for CRDS). Commitments made to the Chief Executive Officer upon termination of service In accordance with the recommendations of the Afep‑Medef Code, Thomas Buberl has decided to renounce his employment contract as of the date of his appointment as Chief Executive Officer. In connection with this decision, the Board of Directors upon recommendation from its Compensation, Governance & Sustainability Committee undertook a review of the consequences of this renunciation including with respect to the continuity of the social benefits (Health insurance, Life insurance, Disability insurance, etc.) to which Thomas Buberl was entitled as an employee. In this context, the Board of Directors was concerned that the decision of Thomas Buberl to renounce his employment contract in accordance with the recommendations of the Afep‑Medef Code would jeopardize the continuity of his accrued and future social benefits. As a result, on August 2, 2016, the Board of Directors decided to maintain social benefits and provide a contractual severance benefit for Thomas Buberl, in case of termination of service, designed to replicate the benefits to which he would have been entitled as AXA employee under the 1993 collective agreement covering director‑level employees of the insurance sector, but with the addition of performance conditions. A severance benefit would be applicable, except in the case of gross or willful misconduct, solely in the event of dismissal or non‑renewal further to the Board of Directors' decision. The payment of the severance benefit would be subject to the three following performance conditions as determined by the Board of Directors: (i) achievement, for at least 2 of the 3 preceding fiscal years, of the objectives set for the beneficiary's variable compensation and corresponding to the payment of at least 60% of his variable compensation target, (ii) evolution of the AXA share price at least equal to the stock reference index of the insurance sector (SXIP) (in percentage) over a 3‑year period preceding the termination of the term of office, and (iii) the average consolidated adjusted ROE over the 3 preceding years higher than or equal to 5%. The amount of severance benefit to be paid to the beneficiary would be adjusted in accordance with the level of achievement against these performance conditions: 100% of the severance benefit will be paid if at least two of the three performance conditions are met; 40% of the severance benefit will be paid if only one performance condition is met; and no severance benefit shall be paid if none of the performance conditions are met. Notwithstanding the foregoing, if only two of the three performance conditions are met, the amount of severance benefit will be reduced by 50% if performance condition (i) above is not met or if AXA's consolidated net income for the preceding fiscal year is negative. Under such circumstances, the compensation structure applicable to a new Chief Executive Officer would comply with this policy and the Board of Directors would perform a comprehensive review of the situation of the relevant executive, provided that: ● the amount and criteria of the executive's compensation would be determined in accordance with existing practices within the Company by reference to compensation practices for similar functions in AXA's main European and international competitors (insurance, reinsurance companies and banks) and other CAC 40 companies; and ● the experience, skills and individual compensation history of the executive would also be considered. Finally, should the Chief Executive Officer be recruited externally, the Board of Directors would retain the right to grant to the newly appointed executive a lump sum (in cash and/or in shares), the amount of which, in accordance with the recommendations set forth in the Afep‑Medef Code, may not under any circumstances exceed the amount of the benefits the executive would have had to forgo by resigning from his previous position. Structure and criteria for determining the Chairman of the Board of Directors' compensation The Board of Directors, upon recommendation from its Compensation, Governance & Sustainability Committee, and in accordance with the recommendations of the Afep‑Medef Code, has determined that the most appropriate compensation structure for the Chairman of the Board of Directors consists exclusively of fixed compensation. In determining the annual fixed compensation of its Chairman, the Board of Directors consulted an independent advisory firm (Willis Towers Watson) in 2022 to identify compensation practices for similar functions among a sample of CAC 40 companies and major European companies in the financial sector. It also considered Antoine Gosset‑Grainville's experience and expertise, as well as the extensive duties assigned to him as Chairman of the Board of Directors. These are detailed in the Terms of Reference of the Board of Directors and exceed the duties assigned to the Chairman of the Board under French law. This duties are detailed in Section 3.1 - AXA's governance of this Document. Accordingly, the Board of Directors, upon recommendation from its Compensation, Governance & Sustainability Committee, decided to maintain the Chairman's annual fixed compensation unchanged at €925,000 for 2025. Each year, AXA, with the assistance of specialized firms, conducts compensation reviews to ensure the competitiveness and consistency of executives' compensation. Three markets are used as a reference: ● a first market composed of 12 companies in the French CAC 40 index (companies selected to form the panel, which may vary from year‑to‑year, which are comparable to AXA in terms of stock capitalization, revenues, sector, number of employees and/or geographic coverage); ● a second market composed of international financial companies comparable in size and Scope to the AXA Group (mainly insurance, reinsurance companies and banks) principally based in the main European markets (France, Germany, Italy, the Netherlands, the United Kingdom, Spain and Switzerland); ●
a third market composed of international financial companies comparable in size and Scope to the AXA Group (mainly insurance and reinsurance companies and banks) principally based in the United States and Asia. Annual cash compensation Total target compensation The Board of Directors, upon recommendation from its Compensation, Governance & Sustainability Committee, has decided to maintain the Chief Executive Officer, Thomas Buberl's total annual cash compensation target at €3,400,000 for 2024. The Chief Executive Officer's total target compensation is composed of a annual fixed compensation and a target annual variable compensation. The annual fixed compensation of the Chief Executive Officer amounts to €1,650,000 and his target annual variable compensation amounts to €1,750,000, i.e. 106% of his annual fixed compensation. Annual variable compensation and performance conditions The annual variable compensation is entirely subject to performance conditions and no minimum payment is guaranteed. The determination of the variable compensation to be paid to Thomas Buberl as Chief Executive Officer in respect of 2024 was based on the following two metrics: ● Group performance for 70% is measured based on (i) Underlying Earnings Per Share, (ii) cash remittance, (iii) a sustainability indicator (reduction in Group carbon footprint in General Account assets), and (iv) the Net Promoter Score (customer recommendation index) with relative weights of 45%, 25%, 15% and 15% respectively. These indicators have been defined at the beginning of the year with (i) a predefined target, aligned with the strategic plan (budget), the completion of which will result in 100% achievement, (ii) a floor (at a pay‑out of 70%), which defines the threshold below which no variable compensation for that component will be paid, and (iii) a cap (at a pay‑out of 130%), which defines the threshold above which the variable compensation for that component is capped. To evaluate Thomas Buberl's individual performance in respect of 2024, the Board of Directors assessed the achievement of the objectives set in his target letter: Finalizing and launching the strategic plan (achievement rate: 125%) The Group finalized and successfully launched its new strategic plan including financial targets and a new capital management policy. The plan was well received by the market as shown by AXA's share price which outperformed the market following the Investor Day, and which increased by 24% (dividends reinvested) in 2024. Implementing the strategic plan and delivering 2024 performance (achievement rate: 120%) Despite difficult political headwinds, the Group delivered a strong performance in 2024 (Revenue at +8%, Underlying Earnings (UE) reached €8.1bn, up 8% and UEPS +8% and Solvency II ratio at 216%) and is well on track to deliver on its key financial plan targets. Moody's upgraded AXA's rating outlook to positive. The impact of hurricanes Helene and Milton has been limited and the Group ended 2024 below the CAT budget, which demonstrates the impact of the portfolio re‑underwriting discipline undertaken over the past years. Significant turnarounds have been completed in the three businesses that faced difficulties in 2023 (UK Health, UK Motor, Germany Retail). On Technical Excellence, the new Group Underwriting function had been implemented and has successfully supported local teams. The Group began implementing a new Health business unit and strategy. The Group executed the rollout of Secure GPT across the Group. 3.5 Executive compensation and share ownership The Group developed and began implementing priority Artificial Intelligence (AI) use cases as part of its Tech, Data and Operation (TDO) program. Managing continued stability in Management Committee, with solid succession plans and strong collaboration while continuing to drive progress on diversity and inclusion amongst leadership teams (achievement rate: 105%) Management has built a solid succession pipeline for each member of the Management Committee. Management continued to focus on gender diversity, and the Group continued to compare favorably with its peers in the insurance industry and other CAC 40 companies. In 2024, the Group maintained gender diversity at all levels: the Management Committee (38% women), the Partners Group (48% women), and the "Global Leadership Network" (GLN) (41% women). Progress has also been made among executives (Top 2000) with an increase from 35.4% women in 2023 to 37.5%. Across the Group's global employee population, engagement remains at a high level. The most recent Pulse results show that engagement remained strong at 85%. Since 2017, the Group has observed a steady increase in employee engagement levels and this trend appears to be continuing. Navigating the Group through political, regulatory, and reputation risks in the context of a turbulent political and geopolitical environment (achievement rate: 100%) The Group executed the first year of its plan and achieved strong results overall in spite of the political uncertainties across several of its core European markets, uncertainties stemming from the US election, the continuing impact of the Ukraine war and increased regulatory / reporting obligations across many of its markets. Continuing to assess opportunities to optimize the perimeter of the Group (achievement rate: 125%) The Group took the strategic decision to exit Asset Management and to enter into a long‑term investment management partnership with BNP Paribas in line with its strategy to focus on insurance business: Life & Savings, Property & Casualty and Health. The Group announced the acquisition of Nobis in Italy. This acquisition will further diversify the Group distribution channels, through long‑term agreements with car dealerships. Each of these two metrics (Group Performance and Individual Performance) is evaluated separately so that the overall variable payout reflects performance against two distinct components assessed independently.  Annual deferred variable compensation The Board of Directors implements a deferral mechanism for 30% of the executive officers' variable compensation. As of 2022, the Board of Directors, upon recommendation from its Compensation, Governance & Sustainability Committee, decided to extend the deferral period from two years to three years to align executive officers' variable compensation with shareholder interests. The Board of Directors also decided to (i) eliminate the payout floor (previously set at 80% of the deferred amount) to emphasize the "at risk" nature of this component, and (ii) increase the payout cap (from 120% to 130%) in the event of superior share price performance. Under this mechanism, the Chief Executive Officer's deferred amount of actual annual variable compensation for the 2024 fiscal year will be paid in three tranches, in 2026, 2027 and 2028. Each tranche will be subject to performance conditions as described in Section 3.5.1.1. At the end of February 2025, (i) the second tranche of Thomas Buberl's deferred variable compensation for the 2022 fiscal year i.e., an amount of €225,381, and (ii) the first tranche of Thomas Buberl's deferred variable compensation for the 2023 fiscal year i.e., an amount of €233,758, were paid. These amounts reflect the evolution of the AXA share price and were set at 123% of the deferred variable compensation granted for the 2022 fiscal year and 118% for the 2023 fiscal year. All the amounts presented in this Section 3.5 are gross amounts and before taxation. Annual grant of Long‑Term Incentive (LTI) 3.5.2.2
Compensation of the other members of the Management Committee on December 31, 2024 3.5 Executive compensation and share ownership Corporate Governance 3
In 2024, the Board of Directors, upon recommendation from its Compensation, Governance & Sustainability Committee, granted Long‑Term Incentives (LTI) to the Chief Executive Officer in the form of performance shares. They ensured that the Performance Shares valued in accordance with IFRS standards are not disproportionate compared to the aggregate compensation granted (as described in following tables). The Performance Shares granted to the Chief Executive Officer are acquired at the end of a three‑year acquisition period, with the entirety of the acquired shares restricted from sale during a two‑year holding period. These Performance Shares are integrally subject to performance conditions as described in Section 3.5.3. and therefore do not guarantee any grant or minimal gain. In the context of the 2024‑2026 strategic plan, a new approach to compensation has been designed for the other members of the Management Committee to deliver strong financial and non‑financial performance. Their fixed remuneration is completed by a total variable compensation consisting of the annual cash variable compensation and the annual Long Term Incentive grant (AXA Performance Shares). The total actual variable compensation payout equals the mechanical business performance score subject to a possible Individual Performance modifier while remaining overall capped at 130%. The business performance score is calculated as follows: ● for Management Committee members with Corporate responsibilities: (50% Group Performance score +50% Average of Operating Business Performance scores), subject to a possible Individual Performance Modifier; ● for Management Committee members with Operating Business responsibilities: (50% Group Performance score +50% Operating Business Performance score), subject to a possible Individual Performance Modifier. The measurement of Group performance is described in Section 3.5.1 - Compensation policy for the Company's corporate officers (submitted to the Shareholders' Meeting on April 24, 2025). The performance of Operating Businesses is notably determined based on the following performance indicators: Underlying Earnings, cash remittance, Property & Casualty Commercial lines gross written premiums, Health and Protection gross written premiums; and New Business Contractual Service Margin, net promoter score and non‑commission expenses. The individual performance modifier is determined based on (i) the achievement of predetermined strategic objectives and (ii) qualitative factors, including demonstrated leadership skills, quality of earnings/execution, collaboration, delivery on human resources objectives. In the tables in this section, compensation not paid in Euro was converted into Euro using the 2024 yearly average exchange rate: 1 USD = 0.9242 EUR.  3.5 Executive compensation and share ownership 3.5.3 Shares subject to performance conditions (Performance shares and international performance shares, restricted shares and international restricted shares) Shares subject to performance conditions are designed to recognize and motivate the Group's best talents and core skills by aligning the individuals' interests with the overall performance of the AXA Group, and the corresponding operational entity/business unit as well as with the stock performance of the AXA share over the medium‑long term. Shares subject to performance conditions generally result in low shareholder dilution due to the possibility to deliver existing shares, this choice being the one made up to this date. Since 2022, the Board of Directors, upon recommendation from its Compensation, Governance & Sustainability Committee, may grant Performance Shares to Senior Management (1), including members of the Management Committee and Restricted Shares to other beneficiaries. Performance Shares and Restricted Shares are usually granted to beneficiaries residing in France, while International Performance Shares and International Restricted Shares are generally granted to beneficiaries residing outside of France. 3.5.3.1 Grant procedure Within the multi‑annual cap authorized by the Shareholders' Meeting, the Board of Directors approves all programs of shares subject to performance conditions prior to their implementation. Each year, the Board of Directors, acting upon recommendation from its Compensation, Governance & Sustainability Committee, approves a pool of global performance shares to be granted subject to performance. The recommendations for grants of shares subject to performance conditions are made by the management of each operating business and by the Group's functional department heads. These recommendations are reviewed by the Executive Management to ensure a global coherence and respect of the Group's internal equity principles. The amount and nature of individual share grants (in the form of Performance Shares or Restricted Shares) subject to performance conditions are then decided by the Board of Directors, provided that individual grants to the Chief Executive Officer and other members of the Management Committee are preceded by a proposal of the Compensation, Governance & Sustainability Committee of the Board of Directors, taking into consideration their aggregate compensation elements as well as the market studies carried out by the Group together with an independent compensation‑consulting firm. Furthermore, the grant to AXA executive officers shall depend on the level of achievement of the strategic objectives previously defined by the Board of Directors. The Company, in line with market best practices, applies a regular and consistent formula to determine the number of shares to be granted each year to the beneficiaries. The Board of Directors also decided that the total number of performance shares granted to the Company's executive officers each year may not exceed 10% of the aggregate number of shares subject to performance conditions (Performance Shares and Restricted Shares) granted during the same year, to avoid an excessive concentration of performance shares granted to the executive officers. Since 2020, the annual grant of shares subject to performance conditions occurs in March in order to simplify the structure of the senior executives' total variable compensation, so that they are granted (i) a variable compensation in the form of cash (annual bonus), and (ii) shares during the same month. 3.5.3.2 Rules regarding shares subject to performance conditions Each beneficiary receives an initial preliminary allocation of shares subject to performance conditions (Performance Shares or Restricted Shares) which is then used as a reference to calculate the number of shares that will be definitely granted at the end of a 3‑year performance period. During the performance period, all shares subject to performance conditions initially granted are integrally subject to performance criteria regardless of the beneficiary's status. (1) Performance Shares are granted to approximately 250 senior managers as well as employees who are subject to specific local regulations on the date of grant. 3

 3.5 Executive compensation and share ownership The Performance Shares and International Performance Shares granted to the Management Committee members (except for the Chief Executive Officer) will be acquired after a three‑year acquisition period, and 50% of the shares acquired will be subject to a further two‑year holding period. The Performance Shares granted to the Chief Executive Officer will remain acquired at the end of a three‑year acquisition period, with the entirety of the acquired shares restricted from sale during a two‑year holding period. The amounts corresponding to International Performance Shares and International Restricted Shares are operating expenses during the vesting period. They do not create any dilution for shareholders as no new shares are issued. The payment is made in shares. Should this type of payment not be possible for legal, tax or any other reason, the payment could be made in cash. Restricted Shares Since 2022, the Board of Directors, upon the recommendation from its Compensation, Governance & Sustainability Committee, may grant Restricted Shares to Group employees who are not senior managers. For Restricted Shares beneficiaries, the sole performance condition is the achievement of an average result of AXA's score in the S&P Global Corporate Sustainability Assessment (DJSI), calculated over the performance period, greater than or equal to the 75th percentile. Should AXA's average score, calculated over the performance period, be equal to the 50th percentile, only half of the shares initially granted would be acquired. Finally, should AXA's average score, calculated over the performance period, be lower than the 50th percentile, no shares would be granted. This scheme does not guarantee a minimum gain to the beneficiaries. Among the different performance levels, the number of shares definitively granted is calculated on a linear basis depending on the achieved performance. For Performance Shares and Restricted Shares, the achievement rate of the performance criterion (performance rate) is used to determine the number of AXA shares that will be definitively acquired by the beneficiaries at the end of the acquisition period, under the condition that the beneficiary is still employed by the AXA Group. The number of AXA shares definitively granted shall therefore be equal to the number of rights to AXA shares initially granted multiplied by the performance rate, which may not exceed 130% for the Performance Shares and 100% for the Restricted Shares. Furthermore, for the Performances Shares and Restricted Shares, should no dividend be proposed for payment (outside regulatory constraints) by the Board of Directors to the Company shareholders for any of the fiscal years of the performance period, the number of shares definitively acquired would be automatically divided by two. 3

The purpose of these grants was to associate them with AXA's share price performance and encourage their performance over the long term. In 2019, after having progressively reduced the number of stock options beneficiaries over the past few years, AXA's Board of Directors, upon recommendation from its Compensation, Governance & Sustainability Committee, in order to simplify AXA's compensation policy and in line with market practice and feedback received from a number of institutional investors, decided to cease awarding stock options to corporate officers and AXA employees. Previous plans are still valid until their expiration (maximum duration period of 10 years). They were granted at market value, with no discount, and become exercisable by tranches, generally in thirds between 3 and 5 years following the grant date. Pursuant to the stock option plan rules, beneficiaries who resign from the Group lose the right to exercise their options. On December 31, 2024, 68 AXA employees held a total of 2,475,227 outstanding options, representing 0.11% of the Company's share capital on the same date. Performance conditions All options granted to all members of the Management Committee until 2018 were subject to a performance condition. This performance condition also applied to the last tranche of each option grant (i.e., the last third of the options granted) for all other beneficiaries of options. Pursuant to this performance condition, the options become fully exercisable only if the AXA share price outperformed the stock reference index of the insurance sector (1). No option subject to such performance condition can be exercised as long as this criterion has not been reached. This external performance condition subjects the acquisition of the right to exercise the options to the achievement of a fully objective and public performance and allows the measure of the relative performance of AXA compared to its main European competitors over a period of at least three years. If the performance condition has not been met at the expiry date of the options, these options are automatically cancelled. Furthermore, the Board of Directors decided that a second performance condition would apply to all options granted in 2017 and 2018, pursuant to which beneficiaries would not be able to exercise their stock options in the event the net income of the Group is negative, and for as long as it remains so. (1) SXIP index (STOXX Insurance Index): a capitalization‑weighted index, which includes European companies that are involved in the insurance sector. As at December 31, 2024, the index included 31 companies of the sector. AXA | Universal Registration Document 2024

This policy requires each executive to hold, during the entire duration of his/her functions, a minimum number of AXA shares (the "Minimum Shareholding Requirement") representing a multiple of their annual fixed compensation during their tenure: ● The Chief Executive Officer is required to hold shares equivalent to three times his annual fixed compensation ; ● Management Committee members are required to hold shares equivalent to two times their annual fixed compensation ; ● Partners Group members are required to hold shares equivalent to their annual fixed compensation. AXA shares, ADS AXA or shares of listed Group subsidiaries, held directly or indirectly through Mutual funds or similar investment vehicles, regardless of their acquisition procedure, are considered for calculating the Minimum Shareholding Requirement. Each concerned senior executive must meet the Minimum Shareholding Requirement within a period of 5 years as from the date of their first appointment. Pursuant to articles L.22‑10‑59 II and L.22‑10‑57 of the French Commercial Code, the Board of Directors has decided that, until the Chief Executive Officer meets his Minimum Shareholding Requirement, all Stock Options and Performance Shares granted to him will be subject to the following restrictions: ● Upon each exercise of these stock options, the Chief Executive Officer must hold in registered form a number of shares equal to at least 25% of the pre‑tax capital gain realized upon exercise. These shares shall be held during his entire term of office as Chief Executive Officer; and ●
For Performance Shares, the Chief Executive Officer must, at every performance share acquisition date, hold in registered form at least 25% of the acquired shares throughout the entire term as Chief Executive Officer. These restrictions do not apply if the Chief Executive Officer complies with his Minimum Shareholding Requirement. AXA | Universal Registration Document 2024
