It provides beneficiaries with portable pension benefits until retirement in line with Directive 2014/50/UE/of the European Parliament and of the Council of April 16, 2014, on enhancing worker mobility by improving the acquisition and preservation of supplementary pension rights. These performance shares are subject to (i) a three‑year acquisition period, and (ii) an obligation to hold the shares until retirement. Beneficiaries may sell their shares for diversification purposes after the three‑year period), provided the sale proceeds are invested in a long‑term savings plan until retirement. The definitive acquisition of these performance shares is subject to (i) the beneficiary's presence in the AXA Group on December 31 of the year during which the grant is decided, and (ii) the achievement of a performance condition related to the average AXA Group Solvency II ratio during the performance period. To acquire the totality of performance shares initially granted, the average AXA Group Solvency II ratio during the performance period must be at least 170%. However, if the average ratio is 150%, only half of the shares initially granted will be acquired. No shares will be acquired if the average ratio is below 150%. No minimum number of shares is guaranteed. Between these performance levels, the number of shares acquired is calculated on a linear basis depending on the achieved performance. In the event that no dividend would be proposed for payment (outside regulatory constraint) by the Board of Directors to the Company shareholders for any of the fiscal years of the performance period, the number of shares acquired shall be automatically divided by two. The Board of Directors also decided that no more than 10% of the total number of shares granted in any fiscal year should be granted to AXA's executive officers, to avoid an excessive concentration of retirement performance shares amongst them. On December 11, 2024, the Board of Directors approved a grant of retirement performance shares. Accordingly, 520,615 retirement performance shares were granted to 482 officers, representing 0.024% of the outstanding share capital on the date of the grant, of which 16,433 retirement performance shares were granted to AXA's Chief Executive Officer representing 3.156% of the total grant. All retirement performance shares granted are subject to the performance conditions described above and calculated over a three‑year period beginning on January 1, 2024 and ending on December 31, 2026. In addition, Group's executives, as well as all other employees of AXA Group entities in France, participate in a mandatory and collective supplementary pension scheme with defined contributions (collective insurance contract – contrat d'assurance de Groupe as defined in article L.141‑1 of the French Insurance Code). This AXA pension fund was implemented in 2011 and benefits to the employees of AXA Group entities in France falling within the scope of the Collective Agreement of December 18, 2009. The individual employer contribution rate is set at 0.75% of the total gross compensation, which is not capped. The contribution base is the same as the one used for contributions related to the Social Security General Scheme. Monthly contributions are subject to 9.7% social charges (9.2% for CSG and 0.5% for CRDS). AXA | Universal Registration Document 2024

 4.1 General information 4.1 General information AXA has published the following sustainability statement for the financial year ended December 31, 2024 (this "Sustainability Statement"), on the basis of Directive (EU) 2022/2464 of the European Parliament and the Council of December 14, 2022, concerning the Corporate Sustainability Reporting Directive (the "CSRD") and in accordance with Commission Delegated Regulation (EU) 2023/2772 of July 31, 2023, setting out the European Sustainability Reporting Standards (the "ESRS"). The CSRD, which was transposed into French law following the publication of Ordinance 2023‑1142 of December 6, 2023, published in the French official journal on December 7, 2023, applies to financial years beginning on or after January 1, 2024. As a result, this Sustainability Statement comprises AXA's initial disclosures regarding the impacts, risks and opportunities ("IRO") related to environmental, social and governance ("ESG") sustainability matters that are material to the Group's activities and operations. AXA has identified the material IROs through a double‑materiality assessment ("DMA") of its risks and opportunities (i.e. an outside‑in perspective) and its positive and negative impacts (i.e. an inside‑out perspective) based on the ESRS. The results of the double‑materiality assessment are disclosed in Section 4.1.3.2 - AXA's material impacts, risks and opportunities. 4.1.1 Certain preliminary information about the Sustainability Statement This Sustainability Statement includes statements with respect to future events, trends, plans, expectations, targets, objectives and other forward‑looking statements relating to AXA's future business, financial condition, results of operations, performance and strategy. These statements, including AXA's sustainability‑related plans, objectives and targets, are subject to a number of factors, risks and uncertainties (many of which are outside of AXA's control) including, without limitation: general economic and political conditions, overall transition of the world economy, society and corporate entities to net‑zero, which itself depends on a variety of political, economic, regulatory (including through government incentives and funding), societal and scientific developments; AXA's ability to effectively implement its initiatives and achieve its sustainability‑related initiatives, as well as the likelihood that the changes introduced as part of these initiatives will deliver the expected outcomes; risks related to climate change (such as global temperature increases and the growing frequency and severity of extreme weather events and natural disasters) that may adversely affect AXA's operations, financial performance, or overall stability; the risk that legal, regulatory, or market‑driven measures designed to combat climate change could negatively impact AXA's business and financial results; the exposure of AXA's operational sites, as well as those of its clients, suppliers and subcontractors, to natural disaster‑prone areas (e.g., floods, earthquakes, hurricanes); the heightened scrutiny from governmental and non‑governmental organizations, consumer advocacy groups, investors, customers, employees, and other stakeholders, whose expectations regarding AXA's sustainability‑related practices and performance are evolving rapidly; and the increasing stringent nature of global ESG/sustainability‑related regulatory requirements, including emerging and unharmonized standards for sustainability reporting and disclosures, which continue to develop. Where reference is made to a website in this Sustainability Statement, the contents of such website do not form part of this Sustainability Statement. 4.1.2 Sustainability strategy and business model 4.1.2.1 Sustainability strategy AXA's stated purpose is to "act for human progress by protecting what matters", which embodies the social purpose deeply embedded in AXAs core business of insurance (mutualizing risk to protect its customers which underpins commerce and promotes economic development) and its ambition to create sustainable value for the environment and for society. As society and the economy are undergoing significant transitions driven by demographics, technology and climate change, our customers and the communities we operate in are facing new threats and challenges, raising the fundamental question of the insurability of these new risks. In the face of multiple crises, AXA's sustainability strategy aims to align and leverage AXA's assets to serve the purpose of protection and insurability. These assets include: its ability to invest in the global economy and to provide protection and prevention services to individual and corporate customers; its expertise in improving resilience against risks; but also the responsible management of its different stakeholders' interests. AXA leverages its understanding of risks to help better protect the basic needs of individuals and communities, such as environmental protection, access to healthcare, prosperity, and quality of life. (1) Refer to Section 4.2.1.4 "Addressing climate change as an insurer" Acknowledging AXA's achievements and defining next steps Recognizing the critical importance of sustainability for both the Group's business and its customers, AXA's Management Committee dedicated a pillar of its former strategic plan "Driving Progress 2023" to the Group's sustainability ambition: "Sustain our Climate leadership position". In 2023, AXA successfully delivered on several strategic actions including: (i) reaffirming the Group's objective to align its business with the COP21 Paris Agreement, (ii) announcing new 2030 targets to drive the decarbonization for both its insurance and investments portfolios, and (iii) supporting climate transition, through transition underwriting (1), transition investments (2) and climate training (e.g. AXA Climate Academy). 4

This includes engagement with key stakeholders to help identify and assess both impact materiality and financial materiality, focusing on potential and actual impacts on people and the environment and the financial risks and opportunities influenced by sustainability issues. The engagement process with affected stakeholders through AXA's activities and its direct and indirect business relationships across its value chain, is at the heart of the sustainability materiality assessment. 4.1.3.1 Key stakeholders Different stakeholder groups have been identified across AXA's activities and direct and indirect business relationships across its value chain.  While engagement allows departments to directly interact with stakeholders for a better understanding of their needs, some insights have a broader or cross‑functional purpose. These insights are linked to strategic issues for the Group and inform the discussions of the Role in Society Steering Committee ("RISSC"), a cross‑regional/ functional colleague Committee which is responsible for overseeing AXA's sustainability strategy. The RISSC consider the revisions of policies and guidelines and the launch of initiatives at Group level and the development of sustainability strategic plans as part of their objectives. Fostering positive communications and actively seeking opportunities to promote the sustainability strategy goals and impacts, to internal and external stakeholders, is also an essential part of the role of the RISCC. In addition, the Management Committee reviewed the definition of the Group's value chain and stakeholder mapping as part of the double materiality results validation process and was regularly informed of the outcome of the engagement with stakeholders through dedicated information sessions. For further information, please refer to Section 4.1.4 - Governance of sustainability matters. The views of the different stakeholders presented below have been integrated into the DMA, with particular attention to respect of the human rights of customers, employees and workers in the value chain. The additional opportunities for engagement initiated as part of the DMA are expected to be extended to a larger set of stakeholders in future. Investees As an investor, AXA regularly engages with the management of the companies it invests in to assess the resilience of their business models and discuss the performance and commitments on material ESG factors. Those factors are selected based on a top‑down sector approach. Regarding climate in particular, the objectives of this engagement are to: ● assess investees' commitments, strategies, and performance in achieving their 2030 and 2050 carbon intensity targets; and ● align AXA Group's investments with its intermediate and 2050 carbon intensity targets. For further information on the engagement process with investees refer to Section 4.2.1.3 - Addressing climate change as an investor.  4.1 General information Employees AXA's own workforce is another group of relevant stakeholders. Social dialogue at the local and Group level has always been considered as essential to AXA's culture. At Group level, the European Works Council ("EWC") serves as an important forum to engage with employee representatives. It meets twice a year in plenary sessions chaired by the Group CEO, and seven times in bureau sessions, which are more restricted. Together, members of the EWC serve as representatives of employees' views and interests. The Group's sustainability strategy and sustainability‑related initiatives are presented at least once a year to employee representatives at the European level. These exchanges are helping to roll out successful initiatives more broadly across the Group, as illustrated by the Group's commitment to enhancing the role of senior employees. To effectively engage with all employees, AXA conducts regular, voluntary and anonymous surveys to collect feedback about their working experience at AXA. The results of the Pulse and Inclusion surveys described below contribute to the development of the People strategy and, consequently, are leveraged to adapt human resources (HR) policies, such as "Smart Working", inclusive recruitment guidelines and action plans related to multigeneration management and disability inclusion. AXA's Global Pulse surveys are short and focused employee surveys that measure a few components at a time to evaluate the impact of AXA's strategy and business model on its own workforce and facilitate the identification of areas for improvement. These surveys are conducted biannually and address topics relevant to AXA's cultural and organizational strategy to check how employees feel about AXA – both its business and its corporate culture. For example, the surveys measure how confident employees are in AXA's strategic direction, and how connected they feel to their teams. A range of key metrics, notably covering participation, engagement, strategic alignment and team connection, have been measured over time, allowing monitoring of historical trends over the past five years. With the launch of AXA's new 2024‑2026 strategic plan "Unlock the Future" and its People strategy: Care & Dare, Pulse surveys have been enhanced in order to reflect up‑to‑date market practices for assessing employee satisfaction. AXA believes that employees should feel welcomed and fairly treated. To learn about the experiences of its employees and seek ways to continuously improve, AXA conducts the Inclusion survey annually. The survey includes optional self‑identification questions on age, disability, gender, sexual orientation, ethnicity minority and socio‑economic background to help AXA track progress, spot patterns and identify challenges faced by these specific groups. This enables the implementation of targeted actions to support their inclusion and well‑being. For further information on these surveys refer to Section 4.3.1.3 - Taking care of AXA's workforce / Engaging our people. Additionally, in furtherance of the People strategy: Care & Dare, focus groups with employees from different entities have been organized to obtain feedback on their expectations, as well as AXA's strengths and areas for improvement. Business partners and suppliers The relationship between an insurance company and its various business partners is crucial to AXA's operations and success. AXA works with a wide variety of distribution channels. Depending on the market, AXA relies on salaried agents, general agents, insurance brokers, distributors or other types of partners to distribute its products and services. Other service providers are involved in the management of insurance policies and claims. AXA also works with reinsurers to adjust its exposure to various risks. In the context of its internal operations, AXA entities work with numerous suppliers of goods and services, including technology, office supplies, and professional services for which the Group has specific sustainable engagement processes. For information on the engagement process with the Group's business partners and suppliers, including the use of the sustainability clause and ESG screening in accordance with AXA's ESG procurement guidelines, refer to Section 4.7.3 - Procedures for regular assessment of subsidiaries, sub‑contractors or suppliers in the vigilance plan. Other institutions and technical bodies The Group cooperates with relevant supervisory authorities on matters relating to the protection of customers and policyholders or as part of their supervisory procedures. AXA implements processes to enhance its compliance with the recommendations of these authorities, in accordance with the applicable regulatory and legislative framework. For further information on the regulatory environment in which the AXA Group operates, please refer to paragraph "Regulatory‑related risks" in Section 5.1 - Risk factors In addition, AXA participates in more than 50 cross‑sectoral initiatives and working groups, involving NGOs, the private and public sectors, on matters including climate change, biodiversity and inclusion. External stakeholders are also regularly invited to dialogue sessions with the members of the different governance bodies. These several touch points enable Group management to integrate major and emerging issues into its strategy. 4

 4.1.3.2 AXA's material impacts, risks and opportunities The following section gives an overview of the methodologies, assumptions, and key steps involved in identifying and assessing the material IROs that AXA has included in this Sustainability Statement. Methodologies and assumptions This Sustainability Statement reports on sustainability matters based on the double materiality principle and this is the first year that such a statement has been prepared. In the absence of specific judgement or guidance AXA has exercised its judgement based on its own facts and circumstances. As this is the first year of reporting for the Sustainability Statement, there can be no comparison with prior reporting years. The DMA process follows the ESRS and the guidance provided by the European Financial Reporting Advisory Group ("EFRAG"). The assessment considers both impact materiality and financial materiality, focusing on potential and actual impacts on people and the environment and the financial risks and opportunities arising from sustainability issues. AXA adopted a phased approach to identify material IROs related to sustainability matters. This approach involved breaking down the process into manageable stages, ensuring a consistent identification of material IROs. This enabled AXA to prioritize and focus on the most significant matters in its sustainability reporting. Data sources include internal reporting, external research, and engagement with affected stakeholders. The DMA covered all of AXA's operations, that is its own operations as well as upstream and downstream value chains. Identification of IROs related to sustainability matters As a first step, the assessment involved gaining a comprehensive understanding of AXA's business activities, business relationships, and key affected stakeholders, which set the foundation for the subsequent analysis. Building on the five main categories of stakeholders introduced previously (see Section 4.1.3.1), and considering distinctly insurance and investment activities, eleven groups of key affected stakeholders have been mapped across AXA's activities and business relationships, which are depicted in the chart in Section 4.1.2.2. 4.1 General information Then, AXA identified 16 relevant sustainability matters by considering ESRS guidance, previous sustainability risk assessments, and stakeholder engagement. A macro‑level analysis of each topic was carried out and documented. The analysis and the subsequent steps of the assessment were supported by external publications and reports from international organizations (such as the United Nations Environmental Program or the World Health Organization), European Union agencies (such as the European Environment Agency or the European Insurance and Occupational Pensions Authority), global research organizations (such as the World Resources Institute or the Global Real Estate Sustainability Benchmark), data providers (such as MSCI or ENCORE Partnership) and other organizations (such as the WWF or the Banque de France). The relevance of the sustainability matters was evaluated during a consultation phase that mobilized 125 internal experts across various functions and entities, as well as 11 external stakeholders using individual interviews and collective workshops. The analysis eventually helped to confirm the list of relevant sustainability matters to be assessed and more specifically the 98 sustainability subtopics which required further analysis. The internal experts consulted were selected based on their experience and ties to certain stakeholder groups, as well as their expertise on sustainability issues. This allowed for a broad and representative coverage of the Group's activities and the integration of perspectives from entities that are more specifically exposed to certain issues or stakeholders. Employees from the following entities of the Group were also consulted in the process: AXA XL, AXA Banque, AXA Partners, AXA Japan, AXA China, AXA IM, AXA Climate, AXA Group Operations, AXA Next, AXA European Markets, AXA Emerging Customers, AXA International Markets, ALHIS, Healthanea, One Health, Keralty, AXA France, AXA UK&I, AXA Switzerland, AXA Spain and AXA GIE. In parallel, a representative and diverse selection of external stakeholders was interviewed to obtain their respective views on the material impacts of AXA. The consultation sought to gather insights from institutional investors, distributors, corporate and individual clients, suppliers and non‑governmental organizations.  4.4.1 - Business Conduct impacts AXA has mapped relevant sustainability topics to key affected stakeholder groups and value chains to identify actual and potential IROs. AXA then assessed impacts deriving from its operations and business relationships. Leveraging information gathered through the previous steps and information about key stakeholders' areas of priority, AXA initially identified 1,030 individual IROs relevant to the materiality assessment. The review process focused on specific activities, business relationships, geographies, and factors that give rise to heightened risk of adverse impacts. The long list of individual IROs was then reduced to 128 aggregated IROs after verifying that the grouping of stakeholders and/or sustainability topics would not obscure the conclusions of the detailed assessment. Assessment and determination of the material impacts related to sustainability matters To determine the material impacts, AXA assessed their severity, likelihood, and connection, while also considering the time horizon. In the absence of specific guidance and benchmarking, AXA exercised judgment based on its own facts and circumstances and its own evaluation of the identified impacts in relation to each other. As the information available at this stage is insufficient and at times inconclusive to set specific quantitative thresholds, the approach followed was to rank the various impacts on each of the dimensions of the analysis. Positive and negative impacts were analyzed according to their scale and scope. In addition, negative impacts were assessed according to their irremediable character. Five‑point scales were systematically used to assess the different criteria. The overall impact materiality score was calculated as the average of the scores related to the severity, the likelihood, time‑horizon and the connection with the impact. Based on the results of the initial phase of the DMA, the significance of a matter and its priority for AXA also impacted the scoring. Where a score was equal to or above 4, the materiality assessment concluded that the impact is material. After grouping, 10 positive impacts and 13 negative impacts have been identified as material.  Assessment and determination of the material risks and opportunities related to sustainability matters The detailed assessment and scoring process for financial materiality focused on evaluating the risks and opportunities related to sustainability matters, but followed a similar approach to the one used for the assessment of impact materiality. In this case, the objective was to understand how these factors may affect the financial performance of the organization. For financial materiality, it was necessary to assess the magnitude of potential financial impacts and the likelihood of these risks or opportunities occurring. The assessment also took into consideration the time‑horizon. Judgement was applied to assess how significantly the risks and opportunities would affect the organization's performance, including financial, operational, and reputational aspects. Similarly to its assessment of impact materiality, AXA exercised judgment based on its own facts and circumstances, evaluating the risks and opportunities in relation to each other, due to the lack of specific guidance and benchmarks. The approach also involved ranking the various risks and opportunities on each of the dimensions of the analysis and systematically positioning them on five‑point scales, with comments to support the ratings. The overall financial materiality score was calculated as an average of the scores in relation to magnitude, likelihood and time‑horizon. Based on the results of the initial phase of the DMA, the significance of the risk or opportunity to AXA also affected the scoring. Whenever the score was equal to or above four, the risk or the opportunity was deemed material, resulting in six individual material risks and two individual material opportunities. AXA determined it was appropriate to aggregate several risks for purposes of the financial materiality assessment, resulting in two material risks and two material opportunities. 4.1 General information Sustainability 4

 4.1 General information 4.1.4 Governance of sustainability matters 4.1.4.1 Sustainability governance at AXA AXA has established a dedicated governance framework to develop, implement and monitor the implementation of its sustainability strategy in which the Board of Directors and its Committees play a major role. At the level of AXA's Board of Directors and its Committees Composition of the Board of Directors and its Committees As of December 31, 2024, the Board comprised 15 members, including one executive member (the Chief Executive Officer) and 14 non‑executive members, including one director representing employee shareholders and two directors representing employees. In 2024, the average ratio of women on the Board was 59% (including directors representing the employees and employee shareholders) and 41% of men. 8 nationalities were represented on the Board. For detailed information on the composition of the Board of Directors, diversity of the members of the Board, the representation of employees and employee shareholders, and the Board of Directors' independence, please refer to Section 3.2 - Composition of the Board of Directors and its Committees of this Document. Role of the Board of Directors and its Committees Since 2022, the Board of Directors has been conducting a comprehensive review of its governance on sustainability matters in an effort to clarify the duties of each Committee and ensure that sustainability matters are regularly included in the meeting agendas of the Board and its Committees. As a result of this work, the Board of Directors notably decided to rename the "Compensation & Governance Committee" the "Compensation, Governance & Sustainability Committee" in order to emphasize its role on sustainability matters, and specify each Committee's duties in respect of these matters. In 2024, the Board's Terms of Reference were further amended, notably to take into consideration the requirements of CSRD and further clarify how duties among the Committees in connection with sustainability matters, including sustainability reporting, are allocated. 4

 At the Executive level The Management Committee oversees material sustainability‑related initiatives across the Group. The Management Committee is supported by the Role in Society Steering Committee ("RISSC"). The RISSC is charged with developing and overseeing the operational implementation of the Group's sustainability strategy and reviewing material sustainability‑related issues faced by the Group as well as monitoring material sustainability‑related initiatives across the Group. The RISSC oversees the setting of targets on key sustainability matters, including material IROs, and is regularly updated on their progress. The RISSC meets monthly and is co‑chaired by the Group Chief Underwriting and Pricing Officer, Group Chief Investment Officer, and Group Chief Communication, Brand and Sustainability Officer. The RISSC reports back to the Management Committee on a regular basis concerning material sustainability‑related decisions taken or to be taken and issues considered in respect of which Management Committee guidance and/or decisions are needed. Furthermore, the Audit Risk and Compliance Committee ("ARCC") is charged with reviewing all material audit, risk (including sustainability‑related risks) and compliance issues faced by the Group. The ARCC also reviews mandatory sustainability reporting (including this Chapter 4). The ARCC meets monthly and is chaired by the Group General Counsel, and reports back to the Management Committee on a regular basis on such topics.  4.1 General information 4.1.4.3 Risk Management and Internal Control over sustainability reporting AXA regularly communicates information related to its sustainability strategy, objectives and risks notably through Annual Reports and updates, whether prepared to comply with regulatory requirements (such as this Sustainability Statement) or on a voluntary basis (e.g.: Climate & Biodiversity Report). The extra‑financial reports involve several professional families and benefit from risk management and internal control frameworks that were primarily designed for financial reporting. The preparation of this Sustainability Statement follows the same approach and diligence process, as further described below. The governance framework applicable to external reporting is set by the Group Standards, as supplemented by internal guidelines, manual and procedures. Regular trainings are available to staff in charge of reporting across the Group. As the level of non‑financial disclosures increases and evolves, AXA regularly updates the controls and processes applicable to non‑financial reporting. The guidelines and procedures are designed to mitigate risks related to data collection and reporting and securing data accuracy, coherence, and consistency. These measures of control also aim to reduce the compliance risk (e.g.: liability risk related to sustainability information disclosure) which is also subject to the operational risk management framework. This framework is deployed throughout the Group and the main risks identified are regularly presented to the management via established governance structures. For more details on Risk Management and Internal Control framework, please refer to respectively Section 5.2 - Internal control and Risk Management and Section 5.7 - Operational risks . From an operational standpoint, sustainability statements must be prepared on an annual basis and are coordinated by the Planning, Budget, Results, Central ("PBRC") Department. Given this Sustainability Statement was the inaugural publication, a special operational governance framework was established, which included a dedicated steering committee that was co‑chaired by Group Finance and Group Sustainability teams to coordinate and supervise contributions from all professional families across the Group. Professional families also developed and maintained a list of manuals (e.g.: the Sustainability Reporting Manual, the Environmental reporting Methodology & Process Handbook, the Digital Sustainability Environmental Reporting Protocol and the Social Data Reporting (SDR) User Guide) and controls to support the contributors across AXA's entities. The respective heads of departments signed off on their contributions before this Sustainability Statement was presented to the ARCC, GAC, CGSC and Board of Directors. In addition, during the preparation of this first Sustainability Statement, PBRC organized a dry‑run exercise and regularly presented outputs of their controls (e.g.: inconsistency or incoherences in preliminary data) and related action plans via established governance structures. Prior to its communication to the Board of Directors or external stakeholders (e.g.: communication to administrative or supervisory bodies) this Sustainability Statement, which is included in the Board's Management Report, was reviewed by the ARCC and by the GAC and the CGSC during a joint meeting. The overall Risk Management and Internal Control frameworks contribute to providing the Group's Senior Management and Board of Directors with reliable sustainability reporting. 4

The metrics disclosed in this report are measured on this scope, unless otherwise indicated. The scope of AXA's information disclosed in this Sustainability Statement covers AXA's own operations and, where relevant, value chain information (see Sections 4.1.2.2 - Business model and value chain and 4.1.3.2 - AXA's material impacts, risks and opportunities for more details). Frameworks used This Sustainability Statement follows the requirements set out in the CSRD and is in line with the ESRS. To develop its sustainability strategy and this Sustainability Statement, AXA voluntarily uses certain international benchmarks, as described in this chapter. These include, for example, the Greenhouse Gas ("GHG") Protocol (https:// ghgprotocol.org) for the calculation of CO2 emissions. In particular, the concept of Scope 1, Scope 2 and Scope 3 as part of the computation of the Group's GHG emissions is key to understanding the information presented in the Sustainability Statement. ● Scope 1 refers to emissions related to fuel combustion on the Group's sites (e.g. gas, heating oil…) as well as fuel combustion by vehicle fleet. ● Scope 2 refers to emissions from combustion of fuels to generate electricity, steam, heating and cooling purchased and consumed by the Group's buildings and data centers. ●
Scope 3 refers to emissions from business travel, Information Technologies (IT) equipment manufacturing and purchased services, and emissions related to the production of fuels and energy purchased and consumed by the Group as well as emissions related to investments. In addition, AXA refers to the Science Based Target Initiative ("SBTi") (https://sciencebasedtargets.org) for the reduction taregt setting of its own operation carbon footprint and to the Partnership for Carbon Accounting Financials ("PCAF") for the calculation of its underwriting and investment carbon footprint. Other benchmarks and frameworks are highlighted in the relevant notes or presented in Section 4.5.2 - Reporting methodologies. Time horizons As part of the DMA performed by the Group, the time horizons that were considered when identifying actual or potential IROs are aligned with the ESRS requirements: (i) short term horizon is defined as equal to or less than one year, (ii) medium term as between one and five years and (iii) long term as over five years.  AXA has identified transition risks, in respect of its investment portfolio, and physical climate risks, in respect of its insurance portfolio: ● physical risk refers to the direct impacts of climate change on people (life and health) and property, such as those arising from rising temperatures, an increased frequency and severity of extreme weather events, fires and rising sea levels, and changes in exposure to vector‑borne diseases. ● transition risk refers to the risks linked to the transition to a low‑carbon economy, including those related to changes in the behavior and strategies of industrial actors, market participants and customers in response to climate change as well as related policy, regulatory and technological developments including cross‑sectoral structural changes stemming from the transition towards a low‑carbon economy. Transition risk can impair the value of investments held by insurers and reinsurers. AXA's resilience analysis is presented in Section 4.2.1.6 - Addressing climate change risks. The main observable financial impact related to the natural catastrophe insurance activities is presented in Note 4 - Financial and insurance Risk Management of the Group's Consolidated Financial Statements. 4.2.1.2 Developing AXA's Climate Transition Plan AXA's CTP is embedded in and aligned with AXA's overall business strategy and financial planning. It is integrated at the highest level with the Group strategic plan "Unlock the Future" featuring a pillar "Expanding AXA's role in society". One focus of this pillar is "Climate Transition" in order to: (i) expand prevention and consulting services for adaptation, and (ii) increase transition underwriting in core activities. The CTP decarbonization targets, key levers and actions cover AXA's investment, underwriting, and operational activities (1), and were approved by the Board on March 12, 2025. 4.2 Environmental information AXA's climate change disclosures are organized into three subsections reflecting AXA's activities: as an insurer, as an investor and as a company. Each subsection addresses certain ESRS E1 disclosure requirements. AXA's CTP decarbonization targets As an investor, upon joining the NZAOA in 2019, AXA engaged in transitioning its investment portfolio to net‑zero GHG emissions by 2050 through investment‑related intermediate targets set every five years. After an initial intermediate decarbonization target for 2025, AXA announced in June 2023 a second target (2) aiming for a 50% reduction in carbon intensity of its General Account by 2030 compared to 2019. The target encompasses listed corporate debt, equity, and real estate equity (3), covering investee's Scopes 1 and 2 GHG emissions according to the GHG Protocol, and is based on carbon intensity per Enterprise Value including Cash ("EVIC") for listed corporate assets and carbon intensity per Gross Asset Value ("GAV") for real estate equity. This target on AXA's investment emissions (Scope 3.15) has been determined on a bottom‑up approach relying on investment opportunities assumptions and portfolio composition and constraints and is in line with NZAOA (4) recommendation and interpretation from the IPCC Sixth Assessment Report (AR6) for a +1.5°C pathways. As an insurer, in 2023, AXA extended its decarbonization commitments to its insurance business by announcing three underwriting portfolio decarbonization targets (5) on AXA's insured emissions: (i) reducing the Insurance‑Associated Emissions ("IAE") for AXA's largest corporate clients (6) by 30% in absolute value by 2030 compared to 2021. This target is in line with the modelled scenarios from the IPCC Sixth Assessment Report (AR6) WGIII for the +1.5°C pathway with no or limited overshoot (7); (ii) reducing IAE intensity (8) for all other corporate clients within AXA's largest markets by 20% by 2030 compared to 2021; and (iii) reducing the IAE intensity (9) for retail motor vehicles insured within AXA's largest markets by 20% by 2030 compared to 2019. The targets are science‑based and set in accordance with SBTi guidelines, applying the "Absolute Contraction Approach" in alignment with the Paris Agreement.  4.2 Environmental information Target setting and monitoring 4
Sustainability Transitioning investment portfolio to net‑zero GHG emissions by 2050 ‑
To support its net‑zero target, AXA is measuring the carbon intensity within its General Account, allowing the Group to track the GHG emissions arising from its investment activities. The initial focus was on investments in listed corporate debts and equities, as well as real estate equities. In 2024, AXA continued to expand its GHG measurement scope to include direct infrastructure and indirect infrastructure equity, alternative credit, CLOs (1), indirect real estate debt and indirect private debt investments. As of December 31, 2024, 72% of the General Account's asset classes were covered by a GHG emissions measurement methodology. The majority of unaccounted assets are sub‑sovereign debt, mortgages, cash, and private equity. This is primarily due to missing data from AXA's asset manager, with whom the Group is currently engaging, or the absence of a mutually agreed methodological approach. AXA is aiming to be in a position to measure the carbon intensity of its entire General Account by 2030, assuming the PCAF (2) and NZAOA standards are available. This target covers the listed corporate debt and equities, and real estate equity assets (3) from AXA Group's General Account. The selection of 2019 as the baseline year allows for the exclusion of impacts resulting from the COVID‑19 pandemic and ensures a coherent representation of investment portfolios, particularly following the integration of AXA XL. From 2019 to 2024, the carbon intensity was reduced by 50%, from 64.5 tCO2 eq/€m to 32.4 tCO2 eq/€m. This reduction is largely due to active portfolio management and investment selection, including assets financing climate transition. Consequently, AXA has already reached the 50% reduction in carbon intensity and is on track to meet the 2030 decarbonization target. The Group is currently developing an updated target. Mitigating climate change AXA's investment policy AXA has defined the AXA 2023 Energy Policy (6) to manage the material IROs within its investment portfolio. Over time, AXA has put in place restrictions to address certain activities in sectors that may pose certain risks to AXA as an investor, such as the risk of having an investment portfolio not adapted to climate change and exposed to both physical and transition risks. AXA also aims to contribute to the transition toward a more sustainable and less carbon‑intensive economy. Since 2015 AXA has implemented several fossil fuel‑related restrictions, including restrictions on investments in thermal coal and oil & gas. An updated version of these restrictions is part of a global Energy Policy published in 2023.  4.2 Environmental information Sustainability 4
With regards to AXA's contribution to climate‑change mitigation through its investment activities, AXA believes it is critical to accelerate the transition of the energy sector towards a more sustainable model, consistent with net‑zero trajectories. This transition can only happen through regular engagement with companies to implement ambitious climate transition plans. AXA's ambition, as an investor, is to focus its support for companies engaged in the climate‑transition path. The Responsible Investment Committee ("RIC") defines, implements and monitors AXA's Energy Policy applicable to all investment decisions. This policy is made available to stakeholders, including investment managers, and is communicated through AXA's investment guidelines, regular stakeholder meetings, and published on the AXA website to facilitate accessibility and implementation across all investment‑related activities. With respect to this long‑term exit target from the thermal coal industry, AXA has achieved the following performance: As at the end of 2024, the Group's General Account exposure to coal assets (1) amounted to €1.2 billion, representing 0.3% of the General Account. Exposure to coal decreased by 35% in 2024 and by 74% compared to the end of 2019. In line with the AXA 2023 Energy Policy, the remaining exposure is mostly concentrated on corporates with a low share of coal in their activities, i.e. below the Group's current 15% exclusion threshold. Exposure to coal (General Account assets) 2024 2023 Coal exposure: AUM (in Euro millions) 1,222 1,872 Coal exposure % of AUM 0.3% 0.4% To support the implementation of this policy, AXA takes the following actions for General Account and in Unit‑Linked assets with controlled mandates: On thermal coal, AXA prohibits investments in companies that have the following characteristics: ● mining companies with a coal revenue share exceeding 15% or with an annual coal production over 20 million tons or engaged in thermal coal exploration or planning to develop new thermal coal mines; ● power generation companies with a coal share of power production (energy mix) exceeding 15% or with over 10 GW of coal‑based power installed capacity or planning to develop new thermal coal‑fired power capacity; ● certain coal industry partners, defined as manufacturers (e.g. equipment suppliers) and infrastructure players (e.g. port terminals, dedicated railways) developing significant new coal assets. In addition, AXA is committed to a long‑term "exit" strategy of reducing exposure to the thermal coal industry to zero by 2030 in respect of companies established or operating primarily in the European Union and OECD countries, and by 2040 in respect of companies in the rest of the world, as recommended by certain climate scenarios (such as the IEA "Beyond +2°C" scenario). On oil & gas, Since 2017, the Group has divested from oil sands‑related businesses (defined as companies deriving more than 20% of their revenue from oil sands, including pipeline operators). As an asset owner, AXA has ceased new investment in oil and gas companies in developed markets across upstream, oilfield services, downstream subsectors, as well as midstream companies. On a case‑by‑case basis, AXA may invest in integrated oil and gas companies which are the most proactive in transitioning towards a low carbon economy. For details on the Group's investment restrictions related to conventional oil & gas, please refer to the AXA Group Energy Policy available on the AXA website. In respect of unconventional oil and gas exploration and production, the AXA Group Energy Policy stipulates: ● ● ●
Tar Oil Sands: AXA no longer directly invests in companies deriving more than 5% of their production from tar oil sands and in companies producing more than 5% of the worldwide volume of tar oil sands; Fracking/shale Oil and Gas: AXA no longer directly invests in companies deriving more than 30% of their production from fracking/shale Oil and Gas; Ultra‑Deepwater: AXA no longer directly invests in companies deriving more than 20% of their production from Ultra Deepwater Oil and Gas projects. Sustainability ● Arctic: AXA no longer directly invests in companies deriving more than 10% of their production from the AMAP Region (relative threshold) or producing more than 5% of the worldwide volume of total AMAP -based Oil and Gas (absolute threshold). Indeed, several industries, particularly capital‑intensive businesses with low geographical diversification are exposed to material physical risks, which may lead to reduced demand, increased compliance costs and lower returns on investments. Climate change also poses increasing risks associated with physical risks which can damage physical assets and disrupt business operations. Based on an overall assessment of environmental materiality, AXA's Credit Research Team assesses which issuers are eligible for Fixed Income investments (5), assigns internal credit ratings (ICRs) along with investment maturity constraints, and classifies industries into three Resilience categories - high, medium, or low. This assessment is also performed on the social and governance pillars; For more information, please refer to Section 4.3.2.2 - AXA's impact as an investor. Supporting the transition Financing the transition, including the development of renewable energy sector Aligning with net‑zero goals requires significant investments. AXA is capitalizing on climate transition investment opportunities to redirect its investments toward low‑carbon and transition activities. AXA established a transition financing target starting in 2024. The objective consists of investing at least €5 billion per year through 2030 in climate transition initiatives. This target encompasses all asset classes from AXA's General Account, including green and transition bonds, as well as capital expenditures that facilitate the transition with respect to real estate assets. This target, part of the "AXA for Progress Index", was validated by the RISSC and RIC. This objective is based on ESG market opportunities and assumptions on AXA's investment capacity. Additionally, the RIC monitors this target on a monthly basis. Engage with companies to encourage them to adopt ambitious climate strategies As an investor, AXA aims to conduct yearly outreach with top management of issuers in its investment portfolio. These meetings provide an opportunity to discuss long‑term strategy, risk appetite and ESG commitments. In the context of transitioning its investment portfolio to net‑zero ("NZ") GHG emissions by 2050, AXA Group has specifically initiated engagement with the largest contributors to its investment portfolio carbon footprint since 2023. The NZ engagement objectives include assessing issuers' commitments, strategy, and performance in achieving their 2030 and 2050 NZ targets, as well as aligning the Group's investments with its intermediate and 2050 NZ targets. The engagement outcomes are presented on a yearly basis to the RIC. Based on these recommendations, AXA can decide to restrict or ban investments on issuers deemed unaligned with AXA's ambitions. In the context of its NZ engagement, AXA selected 59 issuers in 2024, representing roughly half of its listed corporate portfolio's carbon footprint (1). Most issuers are part of the utilities, energy, industrial and materials industries. In 2025, AXA plans to continue engagement with companies representing circa half of its listed corporate carbon footprint and expand its engagement effort to include banks. Following its engagement in 2024, AXA has drawn conclusions about the ability of the targeted sectors to align their businesses with the energy transition. For instance, AXA considers the utilities sector to be at the forefront of the energy transition, with leading companies embracing new business. Key strategic drivers include coal exit, investments in low carbon technologies and transmission grid upgrade to manage renewable power integration. Investment constraints are currently in place for some of utilities lacking a clear coal exit policy. Conversely, AXA estimates that the energy sector faces significant challenges in aligning their business models with a NZ trajectory. As a result, AXA restricts its investments in this sector to only a few energy companies which it identifies as the most proactive in addressing the energy transition. See the AXA Group Energy Policy mentioned above in this document. Separately, AXA IM, as AXA's asset manager, engages directly with investee companies, and as part of a coalition of investors, with companies in key sectors. AXA IM has a thematic approach to engagement on which it applies two different strategies: "engagement with objectives" and "sustainability dialogue." For more information on AXA IM, AXA's asset manager's voting/ engagement strategy with investee companies, please refer to AXA IM Corporate Governance & Voting Policy and AXA IM's article 29 LEC Report.  4.2 Environmental information 4.2.1.4 Addressing climate change as an insurer As an insurer, AXA supports individual and commercial insureds with risk transfer products and accompanying services to address and adapt to the impacts of climate change. In 2021, AXA Group developed a Green Business Program to deploy Property and Casualty (P&C) products (insurance coverage or services) that contribute to climate change mitigation, climate change adaptation, transition to a circular economy and limitation of biodiversity loss and pollution. Please refer to the Green Business Report (1) for more details and examples. In 2023, AXA published targets to drive the decarbonization of some P&C insurance portfolios as part of a net‑zero ambition. In 2024, following the identification of material IROs as part of the DMA, AXA announced Transition Insurance objectives, replacing the Green Business ambition and expanding the 2023 net‑zero ambition for renewable energy (2).
AXA organized its sustainability action under three pillars of focus for the P&C Underwriting businesses: climate change mitigation, climate change adaptation and supporting the climate transition. The efforts are viewed in terms of both "what" AXA insures, i.e. the activities and assets which contribute to sustainability objectives, and "how" AXA insures, i.e. the insurance products and services which support customers sustainable behaviors and choices. 4

To mitigate the negative impact of significant GHG emissions from insurance underwriting activities, AXA extended its investment target of net‑zero to its insurance business in June 2023 by announcing the first underwriting portfolio near‑term decarbonization targets. The specific portfolio targets are complemented by concrete actions to support the transition, with new services, insurance products, and sustainable claims options for insureds (specified in 2023 for retail motor claims), and specific engagement targets with AXA's largest commercial clients (set at 200 of AXA's largest clients to encourage insured transition efforts). AXA used the following methodologies, protocols, and frameworks at the time of the target setting development in 2022 and 2023: ● ● ● ●
Methodology for associating insured emissions to insurers developed by the PCAF, namely the PCAF Standard Part C (2) published in 2023 which outlines the Insurance‑Associated Emissions ("IAE") methodologies for most, but not all, Commercial lines of business and for the retail motor line of business; Target Setting Protocol version 1 (3) developed by the former net‑zero Insurance Alliance and published in 2023; and EU Taxonomy Environmental Objectives, to classify sustainability aligned activities and assets. The initial decarbonization targets were based on calculation methodologies developed by the insurance sector. AXA expects these methodologies to evolve with sector usage and refinement, with the application of various global regulatory definitions and requirements, and as data availability improves. These initial decarbonization targets are a first step to better steer the carbon footprint of AXA's insurance portfolios. The emissions scope coverage of the IAE targets for the Commercial lines calculation is Scopes 1 & 2 of commercial insureds. Following the PCAF recommendation regarding Scope 3, AXA will consider the inclusion of Scope 3 emissions of commercial insureds where significant and data allows. AXA will continue to monitor the availability and quality of Scope 3 emissions of the largest insureds, for future inclusion. The retail motor calculation follows the boundary of emissions of the PCAF methodology, which considers the insurance influence to be only during the use of the vehicle. As mentioned above, the initial decarbonization targets, in both absolute and intensity values, have been set on AXA's most material underwriting portfolios both in terms of Gross Written Premium and the related IAE values. Specifically, the Commercial lines IAE targets represent 24% of the total Commercial primary GWP (excluding reinsurance) of the three specific entities for the year 2024. The in‑scope premium versus AXA total Commercial GWP is 16%; and (1) Policies, actions, targets and metrics associated with the risk of higher frequency and intensity of climate related perils impact AXA's business model are presented in Section 4.2.1.6 / Addressing climate change risks.  4.2 Environmental information ● the retail motor IAE target represents 83% of the total Retail Motor GWP for the four specific entities for the year 2024. The in‑scope premium versus AXA total retail motor GWP is 41%; The Commercial lines decarbonization targets comprises an absolute IAE reduction in respect of 88 of the largest clients based on IAE and GWP (1). Other significant clients to AXA XL, which did not report actual emissions, have also been included in the engagement target. An IAE intensity reduction for a subset of all other Commercial lines accounts beyond the identified 88 clients was determined based on several factors, namely the: ● ● ● GWP size of the in‑scope commercial portfolio versus all other portfolios; present and future availability of Scopes 1 and 2 emissions data of the commercial insureds; and commercial insureds operating in sectors most significant for the transition and for which credible transition pathways exist. This in‑scope subset was thus initially focused on the portfolios within France, Germany, and further AXA XL portfolios within the US, Canada, Australia, and UK and the industry sectors of agriculture (2), transport, manufacturing (heavy), manufacturing (chemicals), utilities, and mining. The target setting process involved the use of industry sector decarbonization pathways (3) within the in‑scope territories, projections of energy decarbonization trends for the in‑scope nation states, and assumptions about changes to the underlying portfolios (4). A baseline year of 2021 was chosen due to greater availability of quality emissions and revenue information for use in the calculation. The retail Motor decarbonization target setting exercise followed a similar approach, namely: the retail motor portfolios most significant to AXA on a GWP basis were chosen, these being in France, Germany, Switzerland, and the UK. And decarbonization pathways for individual motor transport for the in‑scope geographies along with expected changes to the underlying retail motor portfolios were then used to perform the target setting exercise. A baseline year of 2019 was chosen as the best representation of the underlying portfolios compositions and driving behaviors pre‑pandemic as driving distances declined significantly during the pandemic. Progress on the 2030 targets is expected to be monitored annually at Group level. Given the long‑term nature of the decarbonization targets and the possibility for changes that impact the chosen scenarios or alter the pathways (e.g. geopolitical risks, pandemic, etc.), and may invalidate assumptions made for the purposes of future projections (e.g. introduction of a new technology, failure of a nascent technology, etc.), and/or business decisions that differ from assumptions (e.g. acquisition or sale of a business unit, etc.), year over year changes will serve as a monitoring opportunity, with progress evaluated on a three- or five‑year timescale from the baseline year. Any re‑baselining or modifications to the target would therefore also be done on this timescale, the first opportunity being after 2026 reporting for the Commercial lines target and after 2024 reporting for the retail motor target. 4
Sustainability (1) AXA identified approximately 200 top clients by GWP (Gross Written Premiums) and IAE (Insurance Associated Emissions) contributions, and were able to verify Scope 1 and 2 reported emissions and emissions reduction ambition for the 88 clients. Thermal coal restrictions To support the implementation of the AXA Group Energy Policy and to achieve the objective of reducing exposure to the thermal coal industry, AXA has taken, and planned to take, certain actions described below. for mining: any company with an annual thermal coal share of revenues greater than or equal to 15%; and for power generation: any company with plans to develop any new thermal coal‑fired power capacity, or any power generation company with an annual thermal coal share of power production greater than or equal to 15%. Oil and gas restrictions Since 2021, AXA no longer underwrites new upstream oil exploration projects (greenfield) and extended in July 2023 its existing restrictions to cover new gas exploration and development so AXA will no longer directly insure new oil & gas exploration and development projects. The restrictions for new oil exploration and development came into force on January 1, 2024. The restrictions for new gas exploration and development will come into force on September 1, 2025. Exemptions may be granted, on a case‑by‑case basis, for new standalone site‑specific insurance policies to the extent they satisfy the following criteria: companies with sufficient financial capacity to invest in low‑carbon activities (i.e., large capital expenditures) and the willingness to have a material impact in the transition; qualitative assessments to monitor developments/ implementation of strategy, key risks and positioning relative to peers. AXA focuses on the following factors and key performance indicators: (i) energy transition strategy; and (ii) emissions management based on GHG intensity, methane emissions and emissions reduction targets; the relevant oil and gas company's safety management and environmental management system ; and company's pollution prevention plans and oil spill response certification with respect to oil and gas obtained through methods other than traditional extraction. In respect to unconventional oil and gas, as outlined in the Group Energy Policy, AXA reduced its insurance coverage as follows: Tar Oil Sands: as of January 1, 2024, AXA no longer provides insurance coverage (for new or existing business) for tar oil sands activities for companies with (a) 50% of production generated from tar oil sands activities; or (b) 50% of revenues generated from tar oil sands production and oil sands‑related transportation (pipelines); Arctic: as of January 1, 2024, AXA no longer issues new stand‑alone, site‑specific insurance policies and facultative reinsurance (excluding Employee Benefits and treaty reinsurance) for greenfield oil and gas exploration and development projects in the AMAP (Arctic Monitoring and Assessment Program) Region (excluding Norwegian operations) licensed after December 31, 2021; Ultra‑Deepwater: as of January 1, 2024, AXA no longer provides new stand‑alone, site‑specific insurance policies and facultative reinsurance (excluding Employee Benefits and treaty reinsurance) for greenfield Ultra‑Deep‑Water oil & gas exploration and development projects licensed after December 31, 2021 (defined as drilling at least 1,500 meters below sea level); and Shale oil and gas: as of January 1, 2024, AXA no longer provides insurance coverage (either for new or existing businesses) for shale oil and gas projects carried out by companies deriving more than 30% of their total oil and gas production from shale oil and gas. Supporting the transition As mentioned above, AXA organized the sustainability action under three pillars of focus for the P&C Underwriting businesses: climate change mitigation, climate change adaptation and supporting the climate transition. To support insureds with their transition efforts and consequently aid AXA underwriting portfolio decarbonization, accompanying the decarbonization targets are targets for engagement and support to the transition with sustainable insurance solutions, namely sustainable retail motor claims. Engaging with corporate clients The initial engagement target was set for 200 of AXA's largest corporate insureds, with an objective to engage and support them with their transition and mitigation efforts between 2023 and 2026. AXA implemented a comprehensive strategy in 2023 to identify engagement leads for each of the original targeted clients, define the types of engagements possible (1), and train all engagement leads for the engagement conversation. The first set of engagements began in 2024 with 15 of the original 200 participating in at least one form of the qualifying engagement activities. After introduction in 2024, the program was extended to clients not among the original 200, a reflection of the value of the dialogues. An additional 52 Commercial lines clients participated in climate transition engagements in 2024 at AXA for a total of 67 engagements. The Group as an insurance underwriter aims to play a role in accelerating the transition to a resilient, net‑zero emissions economy, and engagement with insureds is one of the efforts deployed. The Group P&C Underwriting has also structured the client engagement process based on three types of engagement: 1. Sustainability dialogue: specific meetings with corporate clients focusing on Decarbonization and Biodiversity & Nature strategy; 2. Engagement with objectives, firstly engagement with incentives to encourage behavioral change; 3. Engagement with objectives linked with AXA's sustainability underwriting policies.  4.2 Environmental information In addition, a business referral process is in place to monitor and control the implementation of the Group's sector policies focusing on the restrictions for AXA's insurance activities. This involves any sensitive business risks being identified and escalated to Group Underwriting Office ("GUO"). Such sensitive business risks are assessed by sustainability underwriting experts. Each case that is referred requires a prior engagement with the broker and the client to gather all information necessary to prove that the case will meet the Group's underwriting sustainability guidelines. For each case approved, annual monitoring and client engagement is compulsory. Sustainable claims For further information on sustainable claims refer to Section 4.2.2.3 - Addressing other environmental issues as an insurer. Transition insurance While the original transition insurance target focused narrowly on support for renewable energies, as part of its new strategic plan, "Unlock the Future", AXA introduced a transition objective to reach a cumulative €6 billion GWP from 2024 to 2026 that encompasses assets and activities beyond renewable energy. AXA intends to develop a range of insurance products contributing to environmental objectives such as climate change mitigation, circular economy, pollution prevention and protection of biodiversity and nature, including nature‑based solutions. In 2021, AXA developed a Green Business Program to encourage P&C products (insurance coverage or services) that contribute to these environmental objectives (for example, low‑emission energy infrastructure/vehicles, environmental risks insurance, and pay‑how‑you‑drive programs). In 2024, AXA announced transition insurance objectives for the P&C business that contribute to one of the environmental objectives as listed above (to replace the Green Business Program), for both "how we insure", meaning the risk transfer product and accompanying coverage terms, and "what we insure", as in the "assets" or "activities" of the insured. For example: ● ● transition to a circular economy – by supporting circular economy principles in industries such as building construction; and ● limitation of biodiversity loss and pollution – by protecting and restoring a natural site to its original state, prior to suffering the effects of a peril, e.g. environmental risk insurance in case of an accidental pollution. Adaptation to climate change Helping our clients with adaptation solutions A fundamental role of insurers is to help insureds adapt to the effects of climate change through pre‑event education and risk mitigation, and post‑event recovery through indemnification and crisis support. As part of AXA's ambition on climate adaptation, a goal of providing 9,000 solutions and services to commercial insureds was set in 2024 within the "Unlock the Future" strategic plan. This objective addresses the opportunity to develop innovative risk prevention services and solutions for commercial insureds, increasing their resilience to the effects of climate change protecting them against climate‑related risks. These adaptation solutions and services include, but are not limited to: ● ● ● ● ●
risk consulting services like flood and wind studies;
remote and on‑site natural catastrophe surveys;
climate adaptation studies and field surveys; severe weather alerts with recommended actions; and
training, such as through engagements with local government, authorities, and standards makers to tackle climate change adaptation at the community level. For example, the AXA UK team has engaged extensively with the UK Government on flood resilience and preparedness. In 2023, following AXA's Building for the Future report, the then‑Government accepted our recommendations to mandate sustainable drainage systems for new developments in England aimed at reducing surface water and sewerage flooding. AXA continues to support public policymakers seeking to future‑proof the built‑environment, with the publication of a follow up report on extreme weather risks in England in 2024. climate change mitigation – by enabling or encouraging the reduction of GHG emissions, e.g. low‑emission energy infrastructure and vehicles and pay‑how‑you‑drive insurance programs; 4

 4.2 Environmental information Target setting and monitoring Own operations decarbonization ambition As stated in the AXA for Progress Index, AXA targets achieving a 50% reduction in its GHG emissions related to energy (Scopes 1 and 2), car fleet and business travel (Scope 3.6) between 2019 and 2030, while financing carbon credits corresponding to the amount of the residual emissions (1). 2019 has been chosen as a baseline year for this target in order to remove the COVID‑19 effect and to ensure a consistent geographic scope following the integration of AXA XL. This target is science‑based and set in accordance with SBTi guidelines (2) to be compatible with the 1.5°C pathway on Scope 1 and Scope 2, and with a well‑below 2°C pathway on Scope 3.6. Scopes 1 & 2 | Car Fleet and Energy To effectively track the reduction of its Scope 1 (fuel combustion on AXA' sites and car fleet) and Scope 2 (purchased electricity, heat, or steam consumed in buildings and data centers) emissions, AXA has established different targets (market‑based value (3)) with two‑time horizons: ● ● ● ● ● ●
by the end of 2025, AXA aims to reduce its Scope 1 and Scope 2 emissions (in tons of CO2 equivalent) by respectively 25% and 35% compared to 2019 (4); by the end of 2030, AXA aims to reduce its Scope 1 and Scope 2 emissions (in tons of CO2 equivalent) by 60% compared to 2019. These targets apply to 100% of Scope 1 and Scope 2 emissions. Scope 3.6 | Business Travel To effectively track the reduction of its GHG‑emissions related to business travel, AXA has set two targets, each associated with distinct time horizons: by the end of 2025, AXA aims to reduce its GHG‑emissions related to business travel (in tons of CO2 equivalent) by 18% compared to 2019; by the end of 2030, AXA aims to reduce its GHG‑emissions related to business travel (in tons of CO2 equivalent) by 40% compared to 2019. Please refer to "Reporting on AXA's footprint" below for Scopes 1 & 2, and Scope 3.6 decarbonization target 2024 results. For further information on the other decarbonization targets AXA has set on its Scope 3 GHG emissions, please refer to the sections 4.2.1.3 - Addressing climate change as an investor, and 4.2.1.4 - Addressing climate change as an insurer. Mitigating climate change AXA's operations contribute to significant GHG emissions (Scopes 1, 2 and 3). AXA has not implemented a global decarbonization policy for its operational activities due to its decentralized organizational structure. However, depending on the categories of emissions, the Group issues guidelines that may be transposed into local policies. To support Group entities in reducing their own operations' emissions, the Group has developed an operational handbook. This document provides a non‑exhaustive list of possible climate actions and levers to address the obstacles to the implementation of these actions. The decarbonization initiatives and actions have been defined according to AXA's market‑based targets. This approach ensures consistency across the various regions where AXA operates. The following section details the different actions and initiatives implemented either globally or locally to reduce AXA's negative impact on climate change. As of the date of this Sustainability Statement, AXA is not in the position to quantify the contribution of each decarbonization action. The Group is working to be in a position to assess these contributions in the future. Energy (Scopes 1 & 2) To achieve its objective of contributing to net zero and reducing carbon emissions related to energy Scopes 1 and 2 by 2030 compared to 2019 levels, AXA has taken the following actions: AXA aims to reach 100% renewable electricity sourcing for its operations by the end of 2025. In alignment with this objective, AXA has signed a Europe‑wide Virtual Power Purchase Agreement ('VPPA') in 2023 which is expected to cover the electricity consumption of all the Company's buildings and data centers in Europe from 2025 and onward in addition to supplying the grid with renewable energy; additionally, AXA is dedicated to achieving high levels of energy efficiency in all owned buildings. Regarding sites not owned by AXA and not meeting the environmental standards satisfactory to the Group, various measures are being taken, including the transition to more innovative and energy‑efficient buildings. AXA Belgium, for instance, plans to close an energy‑inefficient site by 2025 and relocate to a significantly more environmentally friendly building, while also implementing sustainable practices, such as solar panels. 4

 Car fleet (Scope 1) As part of the Group's efforts to reduce its car fleet emissions, AXA Procurement team has taken the lead on the Request for Proposals (RFP) for car fleets across all entities in Europe. The RFP took into account European regulations on CO₂ emissions along with legislative changes related to ecological penalties. However, the unsustainable environmental impact of Information and Communications Technology (ICT) has been acknowledged by certain global institutions, including the United Nations in their "Digital Economy Report" and this underscores the urgent need to "embrace a new mindset that considers sustainability at every stage of the digital life cycle" (2). This observation led Group IT to launch the "Digital Sustainability" program in 2020. This program aims to measure the carbon footprint of AXA's digital activities within a scope that includes data centers, workplace equipment and digital services purchases like cloud computing and network services from main AXA's providers (3). It considers both the impact of hardware manufacturing phase and of energy consumption linked to its usage. The program's goal is to identify levers to reduce this environmental footprint and to support the transformation of AXA's organization to better manage its emissions. in anticipation of upcoming legislation related to company car fleets, AXA Belgium has been exclusively procuring electric vehicles since 2024, with the goal of achieving a fully electric vehicles fleet by 2026 (1);
 similarly, AXA France is implementing initiatives such as transitioning to 100% electric vehicles for executives and offering financial compensations to employees eligible for a company car who choose alternative commuting options. validate its strategy by the Digital Sustainability program Steering Committee chaired by the Group Chief Information Officer and composed of entity Chief Information Officers with the support of Digital Sustainability Leaders in AXA entities; To achieve the decarbonization target of -40% by 2030 compared to 2019 for the business travel category, the Group revised its global travel framework in 2024. This framework incorporates sustainability guidelines that are applicable to all AXA entities, with the possibility for local travel policies to be more restrictive while maintaining the established standards. Combining the Group's ambitious Tech, Data & Operations strategy while setting environmental targets is a challenge, which AXA aims at answering with the following actions: ● ● ● ●
The sustainability guidelines are centered on proactively managing travel volumes to reduce the overall carbon footprint of the Group. These guidelines encompass measures focused on reducing AXA's GHG emissions, with specific recommendations for alternative transportation options. These recommendations include utilizing trains for trips under 3 hours instead of air travel, opting for public transport over taxis (excluding high‑risk countries), choosing green taxis (Hybrid or Electric Vehicles) over conventional vehicles, and prioritizing the rental of low GHG emission cars. set internal GHG digital targets for the Group Information Technology Department and cascade them across AXA entities; further reduce the demand for new equipment by extending its lifespan and minimizing the number of devices per employee; secure timely cloud migration, decommissioning replaced servers and assess the environmental impact of new projects, services and technologies, such as Generative Artificial Intelligence (4);

Supporting the transition Supply chain decarbonization program AXA has developed an internal program for supply chain decarbonization to address the adverse impact of AXA's operations on climate change. The primary objectives of this program are: ● to enhance the precision of GHG emissions measurement from goods and services procured from suppliers; and ● Through this program, AXA is expecting a reduction in its own emissions related to the procurement of goods and services. This program specifically focuses on the General Expenses ("GenEx") and IT supply chain, excluding Insurance Procurement (Scope 3, category 1). AXA also signed a Virtual Power Purchase Agreement (VPPA) to support the development of renewable energy and is intended to cover the electricity consumption of all buildings and data centers in Europe from 2025 onwards. 4
