 4.2 Environmental information GHG emissions related to energy (Scopes 1 and 2), car fleet and business travel decreased by 38% between 2019 and 2024, and amounted to 101,033 tons equivalent CO2 (tCO2 eq) in 2024. Those emissions remained flat as compared to 2023, as the resumption of business travel has been offset by a decrease in vehicle fleet and energy emissions. The target of decrease by 20% between 2025 and 2019 on the GHG emissions related to energy, car fleet, business travel and IT was already reached in 2024 with a reduction of 30%. Analysis by Scope GHG emissions related to Scope 1 was 21,054 tCO2 eq (1) in 2024, decreasing by 4% as compared to 2023 mainly due to the switch to hybrid and electrical vehicles in several countries this year. In 2024, 22% of the km travelled by AXA's Group's vehicle fleet were covered by hybrid and electric cars versus 11% last year. The decrease in GHG emissions from vehicle fleet reach 26% versus 2019 this year. GHG emissions from Scope 2 Market‑based was 23,146 tCO2 eq (2) in 2024, decreasing by 11% as compared to 2023 driven by a lower energy consumption and an increase in the share of renewable energy. GHG emissions from Scope 3.6‑Business travelling increased by 7%. Despite the measures taken at entity level to reduce their impacts, several inter‑continent events occurred in 2024, leading to an increase in the number of km travelled this year. Yet, the emissions associated to business travel decreased by 29% compared to 2019, on track regarding the 2030 target of 40% reduction and well beyond the 2025 target of 18% reduction, confirming sustained changes of habit. GHG emission from Scope 3.1‑Purchased goods and services remained stable, however the portion related to cloud computing and data centers services increased by 23% due to the transfer of services from AXA data centers to the cloud purchased services, and to the organic growth. AXA expect better performance from cloud hyperscalers compared to data centers it operates directly. While progress has already been made, more transparency from cloud services providers is needed to confirm this assumption, for instance in electricity and water consumption, manufacturing footprint of digital equipment, digital waste and equipment lifecycle policy. GHG emissions from Scope 3.2‑Capital goods increased by 5% as compared to 2023, due to the increase in number of terminals tracked (+7%) that is partly compensated by the lifespan extension levers activated by AXA entities. Furthermore the latest hardware generation used in AXA's data centers, though more energy‑efficient, happened to have a higher manufacturing footprint. GHG emissions from Scope 3.3‑Fuel and energy related activities decreased by 10%, due to the decrease of energy consumption and the decrease in the life cycle upstream emissions factor provided by the International Energy Agency. GHG emissions from Scope 3.9 - Downstream transportation and distribution related to the emissions from energy used by AXA Agencies. The measurement has been introduced this year, retroactively since 2019, with no material difference versus 2023 due to a stable number of agencies. GHG emissions from Scope 3.7 employee commuting decreased by 29% as compared to 2023, due to lower number of kilometers travelled for commuting reported by AXA employees that answer to the AXA's Transport Survey in 2024. The transport survey has been simplified to improve the data quality, however there is still high volatility due to low level of answers collected (around 20% of employees). GHG emissions from digital increased by 4% market based compared to 2023, from the increase in emissions of capital goods (explained above) and the emissions from electricity of terminal due to higher number in inventories and the consequence of the winter 2023 European energy crisis, who caused the IEA's electricity emission factors to inflate in 2024. This result is to compare to the global IT sector GHG footprint that grows at an average rate of 6% per year according to The Shift Project 2021 report .

Contributing to net zero As provided in its AXA for Progress Index 2024‑2026, AXA targets achieving a 50% reduction in its GHG emissions related to energy Scopes 1 and 2, car fleet and business travel, between 2019 and 2030, while financing GHG emission reduction projects outside the value chain by purchasing carbon credits (1), corresponding to the amount of residual emissions (2). AXA's selection process is based on specific criteria, such as: (i) projects must have a significant positive environmental impact; (ii) they must be located in countries where AXA operates; and (iii) they must meet high quality standards through certification (including Gold Standard for the Global Goals, Verified Carbon Standard - Verra, Climate Action Reserve Standard, ISO 14064‑2 standard and CERCARBONO Certified Carbon Standard). For 2024, the GHG emissions considered were aligned with the scope covered by the 2025 targets (3). It seeks to conserve local biodiversity and strengthen resilience to climate change and natural disturbances while also addressing fires and illegal logging. It is necessary to keep monitoring these risks to identify material changes in underlying trends whose magnitude may challenge the insurance business model over the medium and long term. The material risks together with other IROs outlined in the context of CSR are consistent with the priorities of AXA's sustainability strategy as presented in the Group's strategic plan "Unlock the Future" and in the 2024 AXA Group Climate and Biodiversity Report. These publications detail AXA's objectives and commitments to address both risks and opportunities in relation to climate change but also illustrate how the climate risk analysis requests inputs from various expert teams to better gauge physical and transition risks. The key elements of this analysis are further investigated in the context of the Own Risk & Solvency Assessment ("ORSA") which is prepared in line with the Solvency II directive guidelines and discussed with the Autorité de contrôle prudentiel et de résolution ("ACPR"). This assessment is also complemented by the stress test exercises which are launched by ACPR (e.g.: Climate Pilot Stress test in 2020, Climate Stress Test in 2023).  The exchanges with the ACPR (or other supervisors) around climate‑related physical and transition risks are opportunities to evaluate the impact of long‑term stressed scenarios on AXA's profitability and financial strength (e.g.: adverse impact on reserves and claim payments, deterioration of combined ratio, loss in fair value of real assets) and more generally speaking to consider challenges such as insurability issues and the resilience of insurers' business model. AXA also conducted a resilience analysis in 2024 whose main steps and outcomes are summarized below. 4.2 Environmental information Scenario analysis The quantitative estimate of the financial impact of the main ESG risks are evaluated based on available climate change scenarios offering high‑level narratives to illustrate the potential trajectories of the economy under different assumptions. The scenarios employed in the Group's ORSA are as follow: Sustainability 4
Corresponding temperature rise Aspects covered Scenario Time Frame Scope Source 1.9 (2050 median) Physical Risks SSP2-RCP4.5 IPCC Medium term (2040-2060) Residential, industrial and commercial Lobs 2.4 (2050 median) Physical Risks SSP5-RCP8.5 IPCC Medium term (2040-2060) Residential, industrial and commercial Lobs 1.8 (2050 median) Transition Risks Disorderly transition NGFS Short-term (instantaneous shock) General Account Assets The Group built its business resilience analysis based on reference scenarios released by the Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change ("IPCC") and the Network for Greening the Financial System ("NGFS") which are widely used by economic players across the insurance industry and sectors. As part of the ORSA and the stress‑tests exercises, AXA conducts an analysis of its physical risk and adopts different time horizons depending on the nature of the considered business or underlying risk factors. These exercises typically include a short‑term time horizon (3‑5 years) but may also cover a medium (10 years) or longer‑term view (30‑50 years). Regarding the transition risk assessment, it was decided to follow the scenario selected by the European Insurance and Occupational Pensions Authority ("EIOPA") in the context of the 2022 climate stress test required by the Institutions for Occupational Retirement Provision ("IORP II") Directive. This scenario assumes that new climate policies are not introduced until 2030 and assesses the impact of a delayed and yet, firm policy action towards transition to a low‑carbon economy so that the global temperature remains below 2°C by 2100. This scenario aims at showing the shocks that would suddenly impact financial markets, across asset classes and sectors. Lastly, AXA uses the Climate Value at Risk (CVaR) developed by MSCI to analyze the physical and transition risks (see dedicated sub‑section below) under two temperature rise scenarios including one limiting global warming to +1.5°C. Physical risks to AXA's insurance businesses As identified in the DMA, the risk for AXA of having an insurance portfolio not adapted to more frequent extreme natural events and more impactful climate related perils is reflected through the physical risks to AXA's Property & Casualty ("P&C") business and AXA's Life and Health business. Physical risks to AXA's P&C businesses Over the past risk assessment exercises (1), P&C physical risks have been evaluated through modular approaches (from sophisticated to simple modeling were used to represent the three drivers of natural risks (changes in hazard, exposure, and vulnerability) and model the evolution of Average Annual Losses (2) ("AAL") according to forward‑looking scenarios (pessimistic versus optimistic views). (1) ACPR 2020 Pilot and 2023 Stress test exercises, Group ORSA report (2) AAL represent our expected incurred losses resulting from modeled hazards over a one‑year time horizon as per current climate parameters.  4.2 Environmental information The materiality assessment highlighted that for the P&C business, the most significant physical risks from climate change by 2050, under a pessimistic scenario (RCP 8.5), would be rising temperatures, increasing sea levels, and extreme precipitation. The assessment also reviewed risks such as extra‑tropical cyclones, severe convective storms, droughts, hail, or wildfires focusing on the perils that could primarily impact AXA's financial indicators through its insurance business. In this perspective, there is either limited claim experience in the Group's past business exposure or insufficient scientific consensus on other hazards mentioned in the EU Taxonomy to adequately assess these risks. Due to the Group's worldwide exposure, which affords a high level of intrinsic diversification, the possible evolution of future P&C natural catastrophe ("NatCat") claims remains driven mainly by changes in future exposure (demographic changes and wealth growth), rather than an increase in the climate hazard itself. Based on the 2020 ACPR Climate Pilot Stress test which provided more details on the French market, the Group's current studies show that the fluvial and pluvial flood risk would increase by 1.5% per year up to 2050 in France (80% due to evolving exposure and 20% due to changing physical flood characteristics) under a pessimistic climate scenario (RCP 8.5). Such a change could be managed in a timely manner with no significant impact on AXA by adapting AXA's underwriting, pricing, reserving or reinsurance strategy and fostering prevention initiatives. The findings of the 2023 ACPR climate stress test align with previously identified trends: the overall impacts are limited and primarily result from the combined effects of inflation and changes in exposure, outweighing the isolated impact of climate change on natural disasters. Physical risks to AXA's Life and Health businesses The potential physical risks to the Life and Health business are related to indirect effects of global warming temperature and primarily impacts Protection & Health products. Generally speaking for Life business, climate change could impact the underlying trend of the future mortality and morbidity rates. This risk is relevant for insurance products which pay out contingent on death of the insured person (Protection business) or on survival (for example, "annuities") of the insured person. The risk of increased morbidity and medical expenses is limited given that Health policies are often renewed annually. The Group participated in recent ACPR stress tests and launched its own study to further investigate on the potential impacts by 2050. During the 2020 Climate Pilot Exercise and the 2023 Climate Stress test, the ACPR focused on two physical scenarios (vector‑borne diseases and pollution in urban areas) and provided data for metropolitan France with shocks by age‑band – the elderly being the most affected population. In 2020, based on the results of ACPR Pilot exercise, AXA estimated that AXA's Protection & Health business had the potential to withstand climate change impacts over a 30‑year period. The timing of the impact depends on the pace of the temperature increase, which remains highly uncertain. The main financial impact would come from the deaths induced by the pollution scenario that would mainly hit the largest metropoles, although expected to be limited under a conservative approach. In 2023, the conclusions of the most recent ACPR Climate Stress test were derived from a less pessimistic scenario (RCP 4.5 instead of RCP 8.5 (1), and confirmed the limited impacts observed during the ACPR Pilot Exercise. In 2021, AXA also conducted a six‑month study on the impact of climate change on its health and protection business by deriving AXA's own air pollution mortality shocks more adapted to its portfolio. Without applying any reactive management actions such as repricing, AXA's internal study further evidenced the relative resilience of the AXA Protection & Health business to climate change in general and the air pollution scenario in particular. The results of these studies and stress tests support the conclusion that climate change physical risks may have a long‑term financial impact on Life & Health businesses, although with low materiality and not in an abrupt way. AXA current assessment is that potential changes would occur at a speed that would allow for adequate management actions such as repricing. Physical and transition risks to AXA's investments As identified in the DMA, the risk for AXA of having an investment portfolio not adapted to climate‑change and exposed to both physical and transition risks (with the risk of holding stranded assets in its investment portfolio) is reflected through physical risks to the Group's real estate assets and transition risks to the Group's investments. 4

 Physical risks to AXA's real estate assets Extreme weather events may impact the Group's direct property assets, which are subject to both physical and transition risks in TCFD terminology. AXA has a significant amount of claims‑related data to conduct a physical risks analysis of its real estate portfolio. The underlying climate mechanisms overlap with property insurance and reinsurance, but the impacts differ significantly when looking at such assets from an investor's perspective. AXA studies the effects of climate change on its real‑estate holdings and provides an updated estimate of the Average Annual losses ("AAL") on a yearly basis (1). This analysis covered a range of direct property investments totaling c. €46billion in 2023 General Account. AXA's Responsible Investment and Risk Management teams analyzed the financial impact of floods, windstorms and hail on these properties in a selection of 20 nations that make up close to 85% of its portfolio. According to AXA's research on the 2023 real‑estate portfolio, the total AAL were estimated at €5.4 million which is small in comparison to the value of all assets. The AXA Group's real‑estate exposure is global, with the majority of its portfolio located in Europe (80%). The portfolio's highest risk exposure was to floodings (36% of the AAL), followed by windstorm (35%) and hail (29%). This figure was calculated in proportion with AXA's stake in each property. Furthermore, Germany led the AAL in terms of the risk of flooding (44%), followed by the United States and Switzerland (20%). Japan, the United States and France accounted for 61% of the average annual loss due to windstorms. The United States, France and Australia make up 75% of the AAL for hail hazards. Transition risks to AXA's investments The ORSA or the stress‑tests exercises illustrated that most of the Group's transition risk may stem from its invested assets which AXA assesses with a sectorial approach - summarized in the next two paragraphs below - to limit its exposures to potential stranded assets. Based on the CPRS classification (2), AXA assessed the corporate bond (3) and listed equities that are part of its General Account and exposed to industries potentially affected by transition risk. The CPRS‑related assets represented 23% of the overall 4.2 Environmental information considered invested assets portfolio as end of 2023 (4). Of note, 67% of AXA's investments in corporate bonds and exposed to a CPRS sector were expected to mature by 2030. As part of the 2022 climate stress test required by the IORP II Directive, the EIOPA provided market shocks reflecting a sudden, disorderly transition to carbon neutrality due to delayed policy action. This scenario explores high transition risks compared to an orderly transition scenario and provided sectoral shocks based on NACE codes. For its annual ORSA, the Group simplified the EIOPA IORP scenario by focusing its analysis on corporate bonds and listed equities in General Account to assess how portfolios could be exposed to and impacted by the most penalized sectors and stranded assets. For this purpose, AXA considered the credit spread shocks and equity price changes by sector (5), while ignoring the other shocks on interest rates and other asset classes. The overall impact at the Group level of transition risks based on this EIOPA scenario (IORP) is equivalent to approximately -9% of the shocked assets' market value as end of 2023. Forecasting future climate‑related financial impacts ‑
Sustainability 4
AXA uses the Climate Value at Risk ("CVaR") metric to assess the potential future financial impacts of climate change on its investments in listed corporate assets. This metric, different from the traditional Value‑at‑Risk ("VaR") used in Risk Management, covers the physical risk by providing an estimate of the impact of extreme climate events on company valuation, according to various climate change scenarios. The result is expressed as a percentage of the current company valuation. For more information on CVaR methodology, please refer to Section 4.5.2. AXA has been analyzing the Physical CVaR for its listed corporate debt and listed equities portfolios (6) since 2021. In the +1.5°C Orderly and 3°C NGFS scenarios, the physical CVaR reduced compared to 2023 levels. The physical CVaR went from -2.5 % in 2023 to -1.7% in 2024 under the 1.5°C Orderly scenario. This reduction is mainly a consequence of updates made to the underlying model, particularly the flooding model, with improved flood hazard data. The current methodology does not account for systemic risk, climate change tipping points (1), or second‑order effects from natural disasters. Due to the developing nature of CVaR, AXA has not set targets on this metric, or integrated such metric into its investment decisions. AXA's business resilience to climate‑related risks ‑
Based on AXA's resilience analysis to climate related risks presented above, there is no significant risk of a material financial effect within the next annual reporting period to the carrying amounts of assets and liabilities reported in the related financial statements. The strategic plan and capital allocation exercises involve all professional families and particularly business, finance and risk functions to ensure the consistency of the projections. These financial projections are regularly presented to the Board of Directors as well as to AXA's investors and tend to focus on shorter‑term time horizons. For medium- and longer‑term horizons, AXA has a dedicated Emerging Risks Steering Board attended by Group and local senior management members which meets bi‑annually to follow‑up on the Emerging Risks action plan. AXA's Climate Transition Plan (2) illustrates the Group's commitment to accelerating the transition of the energy sector towards a more sustainable model, consistent with net‑zero trajectories. AXA has implemented actions to address physical or transition risks. On the one hand, restrictions on certain activities are defined by AXA's Energy Policy, and on the other hand business objectives toward a more sustainable and less carbon‑intensive economy (3) were communicated to external stakeholders (4). Lastly, AXA is also committed to initiatives intended to upskill and develop all AXA's employees with training material on transition matters notably via the AXA Climate Academy. AXA uses natural hazard models that rely on data obtained from models driven by current climate conditions, the frequency and severity they capture are characteristic of current climate. These models simulate a large set of climate events over a typical period of 10,000 years that could occur in the year following the current one. The climate variations over a 3–5‑year horizon are expected to be covered in the 10,000‑year simulation set. The natural catastrophe models are typically revised every 5 years to remain representative of current climate conditions. For longer‑term horizons such models are not suitable to accurately assess climate change and require further developments. Going forward, AXA will keep monitoring developments in the climate science as well as in the modelling of the climate‑related perils to progressively better account for their specificities and derive financial impacts at a more granular level. AXA will continue to analyze its resilience to climate‑related risks by taking a more comprehensive approach notably in terms of underwriting, investment, or risk management and thus addressing concerns from customers, regulators and governments. The adoption of new competencies in areas such as engineering and green technologies is also embedded in the Group's strategic plan "Unlock the Future" with specific objectives shared with external stakeholders. Lastly, AXA is also committed to fostering a multidisciplinary approach notably through the AXA Research Fund or partnerships with public authorities which further strengthens its ability to anticipate future risks. Biodiversity loss endangers ecosystem services and poses risks to society and businesses that depend on them, and in turn investors and insurers that rely on a well‑functioning economy. Furthermore, the Intergovernmental Science‑Policy Platform on Biodiversity and Ecosystem Services ("IPBES"), the Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change ("IPCC") and the Taskforce on Nature‑related Financial Disclosures ("TNFD") all identify climate change as a key driver of changes to nature and by extension, biodiversity. As a result, the Group implemented a series of initiatives designed to contribute positively to the protection of ecosystems and to act on the nexus between climate and nature, along with ongoing efforts to improve quantitative analyses relevant to nature‑related issues to support future target setting. 4.2 Environmental information As a result of the DMA, AXA has identified the material impact of its investment and insurance activities on nature and ecosystem services (including issues related to biodiversity, water and marine resources, and pollutions). AXA's policies, actions, and targets regarding other environmental issues are presented in relation to each activity for which the topics are material, i.e. investment activities and insurance activities.  4.2 Environmental information Unlike companies operating in primary and secondary sectors, AXA's activities primarily consist of financial services, which have limited direct impacts and dependencies on environmental issues other than climate change. Moreover, the review of the business model, value chains, and relationships did not identify any pressures or dependencies that would be relatively more severe for the Group compared to others. Considering the high level of interconnection between different pressures and dependencies, AXA adopted a common process to identify and assess material impacts, risks, dependencies and opportunities related to pollution (ESRS E2), water and marine resources (ESRS E3), biodiversity and ecosystems (ESRS E4), resource use and circular economy (ESRS E5). Consequently, to ease readability and unless otherwise specified, the following disclosures apply equally to the various environmental topics previously mentioned. For instance, the measurement, policies and actions presented below generally address most of the factors of biodiversity erosion identified by the IPBES: change in land, fresh water and sea use; exploitation of resources; and pollution. Only the impacts related to invasive alien species has not been assessed as being material for AXA. Identification of nature‑related impacts and dependencies ‑
4
Sustainability AXA has screened its assets and activities to identify actual and potential pressures and dependencies on nature and ecosystem services in own operations, as well as upstream and downstream value chains. The analysis specifically considered the impacts and dependencies related to own sites, insurance clients and investee companies. It was informed by previous initiatives, notably the findings of a study concluded in early 2024 to better assess the impacts and dependencies of AXA France's activities (i.e., as a company, an insurer and an investor) on nature and ecosystem services. Two initiatives with distinct approaches have enabled an initial assessment of the impacts and dependencies of AXA's own operations on nature and ecosystem services. In 2022, the Group determined a Global Biodiversity Score, which focused on purchased goods, notably office furniture and supplies, IT equipment, corporate car fleet, catering food. The study assessed the indirect impacts of the sectors that are producing such goods. According to its results, the main impacts are linked to food supply and car fleet production, with pressures on land use, water consumption and pollution, but these indirect impacts remain limited in their scale. Dependencies in AXA's own operations have been evaluated as limited, as they are not directly relying on natural resources, while activities within its value chain that are exposed to significant dependencies are not critical to its functioning and perturbation of these activities would not result in significant financial risks for AXA. In addition, a study conducted by AXA France and concluded in early 2024 analyzed more than 3,000 sites in France to determine their proximity to biodiversity issues and their possible contribution to biodiversity erosion. This macro‑analysis of AXA France's portfolio of sites revealed limited risks of impact on nature or dependence on ecosystem services. Consequently, no material impacts or dependencies have been identified in AXA's own operations and sites. However, through the companies it finances or insures, AXA contributes to impacts on terrestrial, freshwater and marine resources and can indirectly accentuate the decline of nature, which ensure the functioning of ecosystem services. The assessment of the potential impact on nature through the activities of its investees and policyholders was based on geographical data to specify the interfaces between nature and financed or insured assets, on a sectoral materiality of the impacts and dependencies, and on the weight of the financed or insured assets in the overall activity portfolio. It was supported by scientific publications review, sectoral research and interviews with internal experts. While no external consultation was planned as part of this analysis, information sources included the Global Biodiversity Information Facility (GBIF), the World Resources Institute, the Biodiversity Risk Filter and others. The absence of precise geographical data and the lack of transparency regarding the activities ultimately financed make it impossible to carry out an in‑depth diagnosis of the physical and transition risks. Consideration to these risks in insurance activities was also challenged by the access to detailed information at asset level. Nevertheless, this analysis has enabled AXA to identify sectors, such as construction and real estate, which are associated with major impacts on nature and highly dependent on ecosystem services. Farming has also been identified as a high‑stakes sector, given its dependence on and interaction with nature. Accurate quantification of impacts and risks in Personal lines remains also a challenge, but the manufacturing of insured vehicles has an impact on biodiversity, insured homes have an impact on land artificialization, and biodiversity loss can lead to increase in claims from extreme events on insured properties. AXA's analysis has confirmed that potential impacts on nature and dependencies on ecosystem services through investment and insurance activities are indirect, but material. They have encouraged the Group to implement policies and actions to mitigate or prevent their adverse effects. AXA | Universal Registration Document 2024

The indicators that AXA discloses are the percentage of its listed portfolio invested in sectors which are highly or very highly impacting or depending on nature through at least one of the seven selected pressures or six selected ecosystem services, respectively. Potential impacts As of December 2024, 16% of AXA's listed corporate debt and equity portfolio is in sectors potentially highly or very highly adversely impacting nature through at least one pressure. Potential dependencies As of December 2024, 15% of AXA's listed corporate debt and equity portfolio is in sectors which are potentially highly or very highly (1) dependent on at least one ecosystem service. The above percentages are calculated by including financial companies in the portfolio scope. As the ENCORE tool solely accounts for direct impacts and dependencies, which result from companies' direct operations only, the significant indirect impacts and dependencies of financial companies, which have a substantial allocation in AXA's portfolio, are not reflected in the metrics' results, representing a limitation in assessing the overall impacts and dependencies of AXA's investments. An analysis conducted by the Banque de France in 2022 (2) found that 42% of the French financial institutions' portfolio is highly or very highly dependent on at least one ecosystem service. This study excluded the investments in financial companies from the portfolio of listed assets. When performing the same analysis (i.e. excluding financials from AXA's listed portfolio), AXA obtains 32%. The 10 points gap between AXA's and Banque de France figures might be explained by the difference in asset allocation. Indeed, the investment portfolio considered in Banque de France's paper gathers portfolios from financial institutions such as banks and asset managers, and their asset allocations might be partly different from insurers. (1) ENCORE materiality ratings use a five‑point rating scale of Very High (VH), High (H), Medium (M), Low (L) and Very Low (VL). In some cases, a blended approach was adopted, with a combination of quantitative and a qualitative assessment of pressures and dependencies. Acting for biodiversity and ecosystems preservation Ecosystem Conversion and Deforestation Policy Deforestation leads to severe consequences, including climate change, biodiversity loss, and the destruction of carbon sinks, further contributing to greenhouse gas emissions. Recognizing the urgency of these challenges, AXA has adopted a comprehensive approach to address deforestation and promote sustainable practices. To tackle the key drivers of deforestation and ecosystem conversion linked to commodities within the Group's activities, AXA introduced a Group Ecosystem Conversion and Deforestation Policy (2) in 2021. Building on previous efforts to mitigate deforestation, this policy reflects AXA's objective of protecting forests. It incorporates three screening methods: assessing exposure to high‑risk commodities, monitoring controversies related to biodiversity and land use, and evaluating the impact of corporate activities on forests. The Deforestation Policy is reviewed annually by the RISSC and monitored by the RIC, ensuring an integration across all investment‑related activities. Stakeholders, including investment managers, are informed through AXA's investment guidelines, regular meetings, and AXA's website. As part of this policy, AXA bans investment in companies linked to unsustainable palm oil production, those facing "high" or "severe" biodiversity and land‑use controversies, or those with 4.2 Environmental information activities posing a "critical" impact on forests by the Carbon Disclosure Project ("CDP"). These exclusions apply to AXA's General Account and Unit‑Linked assets in fully controlled mandates. Additionally, AXA engages with companies through direct dialogue and coalitions. Biodiversity actions for mitigation and adaptation In December 2022 at the Conference of the Parties ("COP15") in Montreal, parties to the Convention on Biological Diversity adopted the Kunming Montreal Global Biodiversity Framework. This framework includes 23 new targets. Not all of them are quantitative, and ongoing work is necessary to translate them into quantifiable and standardized objectives for implementation by governments and eventually voluntary adoption by non‑state actors, including the private sector. AXA has started taking concrete actions to meet the above targets, with a focus on: ● ● ●
contributing to target 1: Loss of highly important biodiverse areas close to zero by 2030; contributing to target 10: Sustainable management of agriculture; contributing to target 11: Restore and enhance ecosystems through nature‑based solutions. In this context and to mitigate the negative impacts on nature and ecosystems, the Group has taken concrete actions: Natural Capital Fund Launched at the end of 2022, AXA IM Alts' Natural Capital Fund, with a size of $400 million, will principally finance projects that aim to protect and restore natural capital, for example in forests, mangroves and peatlands. In addition, the fund will invest in project developers to enhance their capacity to develop and deliver natural capital projects, solutions that enable faster and more accurate measurement of nature‑based projects, and marketplace solutions for carbon. Sustainable forestry investments Forests are virtuous invested assets due to their high biodiversity value and role in capturing carbon, while offering sustainable long‑term returns resilient to economic cycles. Therefore, AXA is engaged in responsible forestry, working to ensure that its investments foster both environmental preservation and sustainable growth, aligning with AXA's broader sustainability objectives. https://www.axa.com/en/ commitments/axa‑and‑forests Sustainability 4

Engaging in voluntary initiatives To support its action towards mitigation of and adaptation to biodiversity loss, AXA is engaged in voluntary initiatives such as Finance for Biodiversity ("FFB") and the Taskforce on Nature‑Related Financial Disclosures ("TNFD"). AXA is an early adopter of the TNFD and has committed to making its TNFD disclosure by end‑2025. TNFD and FFB are structured around principles, giving investors a framework for their commitment to protect nature through their financing activities. Both initiatives propose to follow a roadmap with very similar objectives to achieve over the short, medium and long horizon, especially for setting targets related to biodiversity. For example, FFB expects from its signatories to set and achieve 3 types of targets, within 5 years, from early 2025 to end of 2030: ● ● ● initiation targets to be achieved by the end of 2025 are designed to demonstrate the capacity to assess nature‑related impacts and dependencies, risks and opportunities of the portfolio; monitoring targets to be achieved by the end of 2026 aims to monitor sectoral KPIs, deploy actions to tackle impact drivers on nature and report on the progress; portfolio targets to be achieved by the end of 2030 finally aim to set reduction targets on the negative impact of portfolios. Taking into account these three dimensions, the continuous enforcement of the rules already established by this policy, along with their regular review by experts, enables AXA to minimize the indirect contribution that the Group may have through its insurance activities to the erosion of nature and ecosystem services. AXA Group Natural World Heritage Sites policy In line with the UN PSI‑UNESCO classification, AXA supports the protection of Natural World Heritage Sites ("NWHS") by ensuring it does not support, through Property and Construction insurance underwriting, businesses in sensitive sectors that are developing activities incompatible with ecosystem preservation in these vital sites. NWHS are classified by UNESCO as containing both "exceptional natural beauty" and "the most important and significant natural habitats for conservation." Examples include the Galapagos Islands of Ecuador, and the Ivindo National Park in Gabon. They provide both key biodiversity benefits, such as fauna and flora protection, and environmental benefits, including soil stabilization, flood prevention, and carbon capture. They also contribute to economies through jobs, ecotourism and recreation. To date, there are 266 natural sites inscribed on the UNESCO World Heritage List and NWHS account for around 8% of the total surface area covered by all 280,000+ terrestrial and marine protected areas worldwide. Yet they are under increasing pressure. (1) AXA has identified the insured assets located in NWHS. The NWHS policy restricts project‑specific construction and property covers for oil and gas, mining, large scale hydropower and large‑scale infrastructure projects located in NWHS. This policy has been implemented by all AXA entities. Full details of the current AXA Group Natural World Heritage Sites policy are available at https://www.axa.com/en/ commitments/axa‑and‑forests. A specific questionnaire has been developed for high‑risk projects (e.g. hydropower projects) on biodiversity and natural capital, to engage with clients and ensure that appropriate due diligence, prevention, and mitigation measures are in place to avoid impacts on nature and biodiversity. Specific questions address the rights of indigenous people and local communities to Free, Prior and Informed Consent ("FPIC") in accordance with the United Nations Declaration on the Rights of Indigenous Peoples. With a particular focus on water and oceans, and as a signatory to the Poseidon Principles for Marine Insurance, AXA XL has implemented since 2022 a framework for measuring and reporting alignment of insurers' shipping portfolios with climate goals. The aim of this framework is to promote environmental stewardship throughout the maritime value chain, foster collaboration with clients and gain insights to enhance strategic decision‑making. AXA XL has also put in place additional safeguards to support the detection of Illegal, Unreported, and Unregulated ("IUU") fishing by requiring International Maritime Organization ("IMO") numbers for all fishing vessels and refrigerated cargo vessels that it insures and adding further explicit checks on IUU fishing to its Marine Underwriting Rules and Guidelines. The monitoring of those policies is presented along with the referral process in Section 4.2.1.4 - Addressing climate change as an insurer. Action 2 | Deliver products and services that limit pollution, restore biodiversity and protect natural ecosystems To steer and support Group entities to develop insurance products that contribute to the environmental objective of biodiversity protection and pollution prevention, all premiums contributing to this objective are included in the transition insurance definition with the €6 billion GWP target by end of 2026. 4

In addition to insurance products, AXA seeks to extend the provision of prevention services. For instance, AXA XL developed an "Environmental Sensitivity assessment" enabling companies to identify and mitigate pollution and environmental risks in Europe and in the UK. The tool uses comprehensive datasets and applies machine learning algorithms to assess the environmental liability risks of individual sites and facilities. The outputs include environmental sensitivities scores and high‑resolution maps for each location. When combined with customer‑specific information like site location, industry type, and occupancy, companies can use this tool to assess their environmental risks exposures at individual facilities or across an entire portfolio of sites. The overall goal is to prevent and mitigate environmental risks including impacts on biodiversity. Currently the tool is being used by clients and underwriters in Europe and the UK, with expansion to more markets in 2025. AXA XL and AXA France have also partnered with emergency pollution response specialists to offer companies with environmental risk insurance policies access to tailored recommendations through a 24/7 hotline when an incident occurs. Action 3 | Increase biodiversity awareness thanks to clients and brokers engagement and dedicated trainings AXA is increasingly learning how biodiversity loss and climate change compound and reinforce each other. This is the reason why the Group has enriched its original engagement strategy focused on climate mitigation (i.e. decarbonization) and broaden these engagements to include nature and biodiversity. 4.2 Environmental information AXA Climate has also developed specific trainings on nature and biodiversity in addition to climate change mitigation, climate change adaptation via the Climate school (4) and Climate briefs (5).
Targets and metrics AXA has not yet defined any target or metrics due to insufficient quality of data and data availability. However, in the next reporting years, the number of environmental sensitivity assessments done for corporate clients will be reported to demonstrate AXA's ambition to avoid and prevent biodiversity loss and pollution. Acting for sustainable resources use and circular economy Policies and actions AXA aims to participate in the transition towards a less resource‑intensive economy. While no policy has been adopted to manage the potential negative impact of its insurance activities on resources use and circular economy, AXA is exploring solutions to limit the use of raw materials in its claims management and to provide insurance coverage that are suited for circular practices. To test those initiatives, AXA has set up two dedicated working groups, also within the PlanNet expert network mentioned earlier, involving various entities to address topics related to resources use and circular economy: ● to improve the understanding of the risks associated with emerging circular practices and business models, to drive their insurability, and thereby facilitating their development (see actions 1 and 2 below); and ●
to develop circular practices in AXA's claims management processes to limit the use of new raw material (see action 3 below). The working group dedicated to the insurability of circular practices and business models has identified two priority actions for the 2024‑2026 plan period: Sustainability 4

 4.2 Environmental information Action 1 | Enhance AXA's understanding of risks associated with the use of reclaimed and bio‑sourced materials in the build environment ‑
4
Sustainability Since reclaimed materials may come from various sources and have a history that is not easily traceable, there is a higher risk of potential defects compared to new materials. This uncertainty around quality and risk levels can potentially lead to higher premiums or even lack of insurance coverage. Therefore, a dedicated workstream, gathering representatives of the "Construction" and "Property" lines of business in the entities, aims at enhancing AXA's understanding of the unique risks associated with these materials in order to facilitate their insurability. Action 2 | Enhance AXA's understanding of risks associated with the insurance of recycling plants Some recycling companies, especially smaller operators, struggle having insurance coverage due to their higher risk exposure to fire, thus triggering multiple adverse effects: their financials are affected by higher insurance costs (if any), investors cannot finance recyclers as too risky, companies struggle to source recycled materials since unavailable and/or too expensive. On the other hand, insurers – particularly in Property business - seek opportunities to improve their performance on this market. The aim of the workstream is to develop guidelines and prevention measures and engage with clients and partners (e.g. reinsurance) on innovative solutions. The workstream dedicated to "sustainable claims" aims at developing practices with reduced environmental impacts. Action 3 | Develop circular practices in motor claims management The workstream will prioritize the delivery of the +10% "sustainable claims" target (1) set for retail motor (see below metrics and target). The workstream will leverage existing initiatives already in place in several European countries, to promote (i) the use of remote expertise for claims adjusting, (ii) the repair of damaged parts rather than their replacement, and (iii) whenever replacement is necessary, the use of second‑hand parts, to limit the use of raw material and prevent waste generation in downstream value chain. This action will be implemented over the 2024‑2026 plan period. Targets and metrics With respect to AXA's potential negative impact on resources use and circular economy, which could occur through claims management, AXA has set a voluntary target to increase its "green and sustainable claims" (2) by 10% for its retail motor business by 2026 (compared to the 2021 baseline). "Green and Sustainable Claims" correspond to claims involving (i) repair over replace (incl. windscreen repair, paintless dent repair for hail, smart repairs), (ii) replacement with second‑hand spare parts, or (iii) remote assessments. Note that it is possible for a claim event to have more than one sustainable claim solution used, for example the use of both remote assessment and a second hand part, such that the sustainable claims utilization could be higher than the number of claims overall. The geographical scope of this target covers AXA France, AXA Germany, AXA Switzerland, and AXA UK, and the target has been defined by involving claims experts from those entities. The EU Taxonomy Regulation and the Commission Delegated Regulation (EU) 2021/2178 of July 6, 2021 (the "Disclosure Delegated Act") require companies such as AXA to disclose how and to what extent their activities are associated with economic activities that qualify as "environmentally sustainable" under articles 3 and 9 of the EU Taxonomy Regulation. Pursuant to Article 3 of the EU Taxonomy Regulation, an economic activity must meet the following conditions to be considered "environmentally sustainable" (i.e. a Taxonomy‑aligned economic activity): 4.2 Environmental information As an insurance and reinsurance Group, AXA must disclose the Key Performance Indicator ("KPI") related to underwriting activities, which represents the proportion of Taxonomy‑aligned "gross written premiums" ("GWP") for Non‑Life insurance and reinsurance services related to the underwriting of climate‑related perils relative to the total non‑Life insurance and reinsurance GWP. Under the Climate Delegated Act Non‑Life insurance activities through the underwriting of climate‑related perils can indeed qualify as contributing substantially to climate change adaptation. AXA also separately discloses the KPI related to investments by insurance or reinsurance undertakings, which present the weighted average of those investments that are directed at funding or are associated with Taxonomy‑aligned economic activities. Sustainability The methodology deployed in the Group to identify premiums eligible or aligned to EU Taxonomy is described in detail in Appendices in Section 4.5.3.5 - Additional information related to the EU Taxonomy. Based on the criteria set out in Article 3 of the EU Taxonomy Regulation, AXA recognizes only 1% of its Non‑Life GWP as being Taxonomy‑aligned. For now, the Taxonomy‑aligned share of Non‑Life GWP only covers the following policies underwritten by AXA France and AXA XL: ● ● insurance policies covering natural catastrophes for cars and homes underwritten by AXA France; insurance policies covering commercial property damage underwritten by AXA XL, where pricing and recommendations offered to the clients are dependent on the assessment of a loss prevention report analysis which is influenced by the degrees of prevention against climate‑related events. Non‑Life insurance products covering climate‑related perils distributed by AXA entities generally contain features that help customers to better adapt to the consequences of climate change, including incentives to prevent its effects on property and people. However, as they do not strictly meet all the conditions in order to qualify as "environmentally sustainable", under Articles 3 and 9 of the EU Taxonomy Regulation, these products cannot technically be considered as a Taxonomy‑aligned economic activity. AXA considers that the complex and restrictive framework defined by the EU Taxonomy Regulation does not reflect appropriately the role that Non‑Life insurance sector in general should have in helping its clients to adapt to climate change. Integration of the EU Taxonomy Regulation in the business strategy and product design process Insurance products and features are developed locally at market or entity level. All new P&C insurance products and any major product feature changes must follow the guidelines of an approval process, which includes instructions for the consideration of sustainability features in the design of a new insurance product. If an insurance coverage or service delivers a positive impact on the environment by contributing to at least one of the following objectives: climate change mitigation; climate change adaptation; transition to circular economy; protection of biodiversity and nature (that includes water and marine resource) and pollution prevention, Group entities should fill in the "Insuring the Transition Index" template. Regarding the climate change adaptation objective, the "Insuring the Transition Index" template enquires as to the availability of risk prevention services within the new product. Such risk prevention services may encompass two dimensions: (i) impact of an insured's activity on the environment or climate, and how to reduce any negative impacts, and (ii) the risks to the insured related to the consequences of climate change and advice or encouragement to reduce their vulnerability. The features of the service are described and scored with the relative significance the service has on the climate change adaptation dimension, either minor or significant. The final score will classify whether this new product may be classified as a "sustainability value added product", "standard product", or "not recommended as a sustainable classified product". To further support the development of Taxonomy‑aligned products, a network across entities covering all P&C product lines has been set at Group level to support and set clear sustainability underwriting objectives. This network should facilitate the development and introduction of additional products and/or features which align to the EU Taxonomy Regulation insurance product criteria for climate change adaptation. At this stage, AXA has not made any global commitment regarding Taxonomy regulation alignment for its insurance portfolio. AXA | Universal Registration Document 2024

The Covered Assets correspond to all assets invested, including cash and excluding exposures to central governments, central banks, and supranational issuers. The Covered Assets thus include exposures to investments in properties, equity securities, debt instruments (excluding sovereign exposures), non‑consolidated investments funds, derivatives, loans, cash and cash equivalents. Assets backing contracts where the financial risk is borne by policyholders are also included. In 2024, the Covered assets represented 74.6% of the total investments. Based exclusively on data published by companies subject to the reporting requirement under the EU Taxonomy Regulation and collected through ESG data provider, AXA has identified €4.6 billion investments exposed to Taxonomy‑aligned activities, representing 1.1% of the Covered Assets (turnover‑based). These assets correspond mainly to debt or equity instruments in European non‑financial companies held directly by AXA. AXA also identified €52.5 billion of other assets exposed to eligible activities but not Taxonomy‑aligned, representing 12.6% of the Covered Assets. This corresponds mainly to investments in real estate properties that have been recognized as Taxonomy‑eligible based on actual information. A review is underway to determine their alignment with the EU Taxonomy Regulation. Due to the lack of data, the results at this stage remains inconclusive to classify the assets reliably and to disclose any alignment for this asset class. AXA's real estate manager is currently enhancing its capabilities to improve the sourcing of underlying data with the aim to identify the alignment of real estate assets in future reporting periods. More information on the reporting methodology applied at AXA and the detailed tables related to investments (breakdown of numerator, denominator and exposure to nuclear and gas activities) are presented in Section 4.5.3.5 - Additional information related to the EU Taxonomy. Integration of the EU Taxonomy Regulation in the business strategy At this stage, AXA has not made any global commitment regarding Taxonomy regulation alignment for its investment portfolio, but has begun to set alignment targets at the level of a few financial products, with low alignment expectations, mainly for green bond strategies. The expected improvements in terms of understanding of the regulatory framework, data availability and quality of information may eventually enable AXA to refine and raise its ambitions regarding the proportion of its investments in economic activities aligned with the Taxonomy. Only then, AXA would be in a position to integrate this dimension into its shareholder engagement, at least for undertakings that fall within the scope of the Taxonomy Regulation.  The policies and actions described in this section can be grouped into two major priorities: ● ● supporting inclusion and diversity; and
taking care of its own workforce. 4.3 Social information In this way, the policies are expressions of AXA's people strategy and culture. A good example of such a policy is the "We Care and Dare for Progress" strategy, launched in 2024. "Care" reflects our commitment to protect & demonstrate empathy for our customers and employees. "Dare" is our call to action to confront challenges and foster a growth mindset. AXA's culture is based on four core values of (i) Customer First, (ii) Integrity, (iii) Courage and (iv) ONE AXA. Care and Dare lies at the heart of the Group's people strategy, driving a strong focus on the behaviors and mindsets of collaboration, feedback, agility and innovation. AXA's policies apply to all employees. AXA operates under a decentralized framework, where each entity applies the Group policies according to local specificities and is encouraged to adapt the Group policies to their unique context. The responsibility for implementing these Group policies ultimately belongs to AXA's Chief Executive Officers at entity‑level. Progress is monitored at the Group level by the Group HR team - comprising Group Culture, Inclusion and Diversity team, Group Compensation and Benefits team and Group Talent and Learning team. Two Pulse surveys and one Inclusion survey are conducted annually to track the impact of Group policies' and gather insights from employees about their experiences. These insights are used by leaders to develop plans at both local and global levels. Other yearly reports describe the reach of AXA's programs. The Group measures the utilization and adoption of policies and standards that derive from the Healthy You and We Care programs. Results from the Pulse and Inclusion surveys are recorded anonymously. They are aggregated before they are shared, and that output goes to Partners, Human Resources Directors, and the Culture, Inclusion, and Diversity community. Local entities access their own results and share them as needed. Local actions plans are developed according to these insights, taking global recommendations into account. This approach ensures that feedback influences decisions at both global and local levels. At Group level, specific targets for material IROs are not set, as they are often best determined at local level, enabling a more tailored and effective approach. The policies and actions outlined below are already in place and, while AXA keeps enhancing its understanding of and response to social issues, they do not require material expenditure as yet.  4.3 Social information Sustainability 4.3.1.2 Supporting inclusion and diversity Preventing human rights abuses AXA adheres to international standards AXA has adopted the "Group Human Rights Policy" (1) to manage the "negative impact on workers' human rights and critical work‑related rights in investee companies" and address the "negative impact on own workforce from unequal treatment or opportunities linked to the lack of diversity or inclusion" (i.e. work‑life balance, health and safety, gender equality, training, diversity)". The general objective of this policy is to prevent human rights abuses and reflect AXA's adherence to general and sectoral international standards. This policy covers the AXA Group's activities as an employer, business partner, insurer, and investor. The policy applies to all AXA entities, with the aim of ensuring that human rights are respected in all regions where AXA operates. As an employer, AXA promotes inclusion and diversity. All employees should be treated with dignity and respect, so AXA aims to offer equal opportunities, and prohibits discrimination. In this regard, AXA is guided by the Group Human Rights Policy, which also includes ambitions to: ● ● ● ● ● ● ensure pay is consistent and free from discrimination; provide a decent and living wage to all AXA employees;
provide a safe and healthy working environment;
uphold gender parity;
prevent discrimination and harassment;
support working parents. Through the implementation of this policy, AXA has committed to respecting third‑party standards such as the United Nations Universal Declaration of Human Rights, the core standards of the International Labour Organization (ILO), the UN Global Compact, and the OECD recommendations. The Group Human Rights Policy has been set by considering the interests of key stakeholders, including employees, customers, suppliers, and investors, through continuous dialogue and engagement. This policy is made available to potentially affected stakeholders and those who need to implement it through several channels, such as the AXA Group website, the Global Edition of the intranet, and local Whistleblowing policies. Additionally, AXA provides training and resources to ensure that all employees understand and adhere to the policy. Building the right culture AXA fosters a culture of inclusion The Group Inclusion and Diversity Policy ensures that every part of the organization contributes to a welcoming, diverse environment, reflecting AXA's values and its dedication to positive change. The policy sets standards for the workforce, emphasizing the organization's expectation that every employee and contractor will treat their colleagues, customers, and partners with respect. The policy also commits AXA to fairness and equal access for employees to opportunity. To accelerate progress, AXA focuses on key areas like gender, sexual orientation and gender identity or expression (LGBT+), disability, origins, different age groups, and mental health. Taken together, these policies led AXA to take actions on areas detailed in the following sections. Rewarding people fairly AXA's employees must be paid equitably The AXA Group Remuneration Policy addresses equitable remuneration. Pay should be based on roles, skills, contributions and impact and remuneration must not be influenced by gender or other personal characteristics. The guiding principles of the policy are competitiveness, fairness, internal equity, and achievement. The policy ties remuneration to performance over the short, medium and long term. By adhering to this policy, AXA seeks to comply with regulations, offer competitive compensation, and avoid conflicts of interest. The way remuneration is calculated drives sustainable business performance without encouraging excessive risk‑taking. AXA employees are paid at or above the adequate wage For reporting purposes, AXA uses the terms "adequate wage" and "living wage" interchangeably. A living wage goes beyond any current statutory minimum wage: it is more comprehensive and defined as the wage needed for employees and their families to cover basic needs including decent housing, food, and access to healthcare and education (depending on their working location). AXA's Fair Pay Framework includes the "We Care" program next to other local benefits and forms of remuneration. The organization has reinforced its approach by considering that employees' pay should not only be fair and competitive, but also at a level determined sufficient for the location they work in. In preparation, AXA contracted with the Fair Wage Network to conduct an analysis in all of the Group's countries of operation to assess employees' pay against local living wages. Since 2023, AXA has been certified as a Living Wage employer by the provider Fair Wage Network. The living wage assessment at AXA is done annually. The local living wage thresholds provided by the Fair Wage Network may vary year on year depending on the local socio‑economic and/ or inflationary environment which may lead to a few employees identified below these ever‑evolving local living wage thresholds, for which AXA entities commit to close any gap to local living wage in the subsequent pay review cycle. Across the Group, AXA aims to offer all employees a total target cash remuneration of at least 110% of local living wage by the end of 2026. In addition, and in respect of the living wage, representatives from AXA have signed the B4IG pledge, part of the UN Global Compact "Forward Faster" initiative.  4.3 Social information Pay equity is a strong requirement of AXA inclusion policy AXA monitors through regression analysis its adjusted pay gap which compares the average pay of the women with that of the men after accounting for a selected set of professional characteristics (role, seniority, level, or location). The residual gap may still be justifiable based on other relevant professional characteristics – at AXA, the global residual adjusted pay gap is currently assessed at 1.3% on fixed pay and 1.4% on total compensation i.e. below the 5% threshold set by the European Union Pay Transparency Directive (1). Entities are committed to ensuring that unjustified pay gaps are not recreated. The unadjusted pay gap (also called Gender Pay Gap) measures the difference between the average pay of all women and all men regardless of their professional characteristics, and mainly reflects gender balance within the organization. At AXA, the unadjusted pay gap is currently assessed at 24% on fixed pay and 29% on total compensation. 4

Entities have further policies, commitments and partnerships In 2019, AXA Spain signed the Protection Protocol for cases of gender‑based violence. The protocol includes emotional, financial and legal support that goes beyond legal obligations. For example, an employee who has experienced sexual violence may reduce their working hours by up to 50% for three months, without any reduction in salary. In 2021, AXA Turkey joined the Business Against Domestic Violence Project. The project is led by the Sabancı University Corporate Governance Forum, with the support of the Sabancı Foundation, United Nations Population Fund, and Turkish Industry and Business Association. In 2024, AXA UK updated its Domestic Abuse Policy to include harmful practices such as honor killing and forced marriage. Throughout the years, leaders have received training on their role in offering support, and with the introduction of this new policy, 15 employees were able to be supported with additional leave. Additionally, the Group is a member of The Action Coalition on Gender‑Based Violence and a partner to the NO MORE Foundation. The foundation aims to set up helplines in more than 200 territories and so help people who have experienced domestic or sexual violence. Upskilling employees The Management Committee updated AXA's Performance Management Policy In May 2024, AXA's Management Committee revised the Performance Management Policy, outlining the annual performance management process from goal setting to year‑end review for all employees. The policy also establishes a common framework for assessing individual employee performance. The primary goal of this policy is to harmonize performance management practices and foster an environment where every employee can achieve their full potential. It is structured around promoting a growth mindset and a culture of feedback, supporting employees in their accomplishments, and addressing performance challenges. Additionally, new elements have been introduced across the Group, such as an annual career discussion separate from the performance conversation, providing employees and managers with the opportunity to consider long‑term development priorities. AXA is committed to investing in the learning and development of its employees AXA continues its investment in training and development for its employees, as it is a central priority across all AXA entities. In 2024, for instance, AXA Belgium set an annual objective for all employees to set aside at least one day to develop soft skills, promoting a variety of topics to inspire them. The Tech, Data and AI Academy added learning journeys Data and AI are key parts of AXA's strategy. The organization realizes that it must build new capabilities to remain competitive. To that end, AXA aims to further upskill its experts and lay a foundational level of AI literacy for most employees. The AXA Tech, Data & AI academy targets three distinct employee groups with tailored training programs: leaders, experts, employees. Trainings include "Data & AI for leaders", Data Management Fundamentals for experts" and "GenAI For Everyone" launched by AXA Group Operations in 2024 to support notably the roll‑out of a generative AI tool developed specifically for AXA. This online course served as an introduction for employees: it gave them a shared language about AI, an appreciation of ethical and security considerations, and hands‑on experience with the tool. The approach aims to foster a culture of continuous learning and skill development, to enable employees to remain adept at leveraging technological advancements to drive value and innovation within the organization. Over 13,000 employees completed this training in 2024. These efforts were complemented by local initiatives. For example, AXA UK established an Academy site for Data and AI skills and ran a "Data and AI Discovery" week which invited everyone from the Executive Committee to new‑starters to experiment with AI‑augmented workflows. Technical skills will always be core to AXA's business The Cyber Academy is managed by AXA's Cyber Centre of Expertise. The academy provides training paths, industry certifications and access to a community of learners. In 2024, the program held 17 sessions with 166 enrolments, enabling participants to become Certified Cyber Insurance Specialists. Beyond these group‑wide initiatives, local entities acted to upskill the workforce. AXA France launched the "PEPSI" program, for new and experimented underwriters. It combines a structured learning path with a "pick‑and‑choose" approach for more technical modules. PEPSI was launched in October 2024 with a goal of upskilling 900 underwriters. AXA Germany also implemented an underwriting training program with modular approach. It was taken by 126 underwriters in 2024. 4

 4.3.2 Workers in the value chain AXA recognizes that respect for social and human rights extends beyond its own workforce, and therefore considers to which extent the workers in the supply chain, including workers in outsourced services, and the workers in downstream companies purchasing products and services from AXA could potentially or actually have their social and human rights 4.3 Social information severely affected by its activities. In addition, workers in investee companies may also be exposed to actual and potential adverse social and human rights impacts. The policies and actions associated with the impact of AXA on workers in its value chain are detailed below. 4.3.2.1 AXA's material IROs associated to workers in the value chain Sustainability 4
AXA's Impacts, risks and opportunities As an investor
 ESRS Topic (Yes/No) (1) WORKERS IN THE VALUE CHAIN Potential negative impact on social issues through the activities of the companies invested by AXA Negative impacts
Yes I- Negative impacts I+ Positive impacts c Opportunities R Risks The Group Vigilance Plan (see Section 4.7) and the human rights risks assessment are the due diligence processes by which AXA identifies, prevents, mitigates and accounts for material actual and potential negative impacts on people with AXA's own operations and value chain, including through its products or services, as well as through its business relationships. AXA does not publish metrics in relation to workers in the value chain and has not set any target to drive its progress in addressing material negative impacts related to workers in the value chain. Nonetheless, the above due diligence processes allow AXA to actively monitor the actual and potential impacts that could materially affect all types of workers in the value chain, including the workers in the investment value chain of the Group or the employees of joint‑ventures involving AXA. AXA considers unlikely that its operations and suppliers have direct business relationships with companies causing or contributing to severe human rights impacts and incidents, particularly those in the most at‑risk sectors or countries. For instance, AXA's operations and suppliers are deemed not at significant risk of incidents of child, forced or compulsory labor. Conversely, exposure to potential negative social and human rights impacts, including previously mentioned incidents, is more likely through investment activities, even if limited to individual relationships with specific investees. AXA's impacts related to workers in the value chain do not currently generate any significant risk for its strategy or business model. Nevertheless, a better understanding of the Group's relationship with investee companies through stewardship and engagement with our investees, enables AXA to encourage high standards of corporate governance as well as effective management of environmental and social risks. By progressively enhancing its assessment of impacts, AXA will then be able to appreciate the actual and anticipated effects of this impact, and the need to further integrate these issues into its strategy and business model. Additionally, AXA has not conducted a resilience analysis of its strategy and business model regarding its capacity to address workers in value chain impact. (1) IROs flagged as "Yes" refer to sustainability topics identified in the ESRS standards and addressed by disclosure requirements set by the regulation.  4.3 Social information Human Rights Risk Assessment The "Human Rights Risk Assessment 2022 Report," conducted by an independent third party (KSAPA), outlines identified risks and measures taken to mitigate them. Available on AXA's website, the report presents how human rights risks are assessed, how the Group contributes to the protection of human rights, monitors human rights risks, and implements actions to promote respect for human rights and mitigate risks. The analysis reviews the relevant policies, processes, and management systems in place across different regions. The human rights risk assessment is undertaken regularly by the Group, and at least every three years. The last assessment performed in 2022 identified risks in the following areas: forced labor, working conditions, discrimination and vulnerable populations. Further consideration to the issues specific to some geographies or commodities will be given in the next human rights risk assessment, which will be carried out in 2025. The Human Rights Risk Assessment 2022 Report identifies and prioritizes several human rights as material considerations for AXA's activities due to the associated risks. The prioritized human rights include the right to safe and healthy living and working conditions, the right to be free from modern slavery and child labor, the right to protection from climate‑related events, and the right to be protected from challenges related to raw material extraction. They are defined in the following table and mitigated by the enforcement of AXA's Human Rights Policy. 4
Sustainability Potential human rights risk Definition Right to safe & healthy living & working conditions ● ● ● ● ●
Risk that failure to ensure adequate health and safety measures may infringe on the ability for beneficiaries to enjoy 'favorable working & living conditions' -- inclusive of a proactive policy on mental health and the prevention of psycho‑social risks. Right to be free from modern slavery, forced labor and human trafficking Risk that employees may be subject to work that is performed involuntarily and under the menace of any penalty. It refers to situations in which persons are coerced to work through the use of violence or intimidation, or by more subtle means such as manipulated debt, retention of identity papers or threats of denunciation to immigration authorities. Right to be free from child labor Risk that supplier policies and practices permit and/or encourage child exploitation, slavery, poor working conditions. Risk that the company may employ workers under the legal minimum age and/or under 15 years old. Right to be protected from climate‑related events
Risk that extreme weather situations (exacerbated by climate change) cause greater risk of resulting in severe harm or loss of life. Right to be protected from challenges related to sensitive/ energy‑intensive raw material extraction Risk that extraction of sensitive or energy‑intensive raw materials may cause environmental degradation and social conflicts. Further details on the human rights risk assessment are available in Section 4.7.2.2 of the Vigilance Plan. Additionally, since June 2022, human rights risk assessments have been integrated into the due diligence process implemented in the context of potential mergers, acquisitions and joint ventures. Human Rights Policy AXA has a policy commitment to respect human rights, contribute to the abolition of child labor and uphold the elimination of all forms of forced and compulsory labor. The Group Human Rights Policy aims to prevent or mitigate adverse human rights impacts that could arise from AXA's activities, and explicitly addresses human trafficking, forced or compulsory labor, and child labor. Its application in the context of investment activities is further detailed below in Section 4.3.2.2.
