 Engagement and channels to raise concerns AXA engages in continuous dialogue with stakeholders to ensure ongoing improvement and develops grievance mechanisms for all stakeholders in relevant countries and entities. The investments made by AXA do not provide privileged access to the workforce of investee companies and has no established channel to communicate directly with their workers on these topics. Nonetheless, concerns can be raised either locally or directly to the Group through the dedicated whistleblowing process through its hotline (speak‑up@AXA.com) which is publicly available on the AXA website. It is however acknowledged that AXA cannot assess whether value chain workers of its investee companies are aware of and trust the dedicated Whistleblowing process as a way to raise their concerns or needs and have them addressed. 4.3.2.2 AXA's impact as an investor Consideration of social criteria through the ESG credit assessment and investment processes Since 2015, AXA has made ESG a cornerstone of its investment policy by incorporating ESG considerations into its credit assessment and investment processes. AXA's Credit Research Team assesses which issuers are eligible for Fixed Income investments and assigns internal credit ratings (ICRs) along with investment maturity constraints. In addition to the Credit Research Team's assessment of creditworthiness, all investments must comply with AXA's Responsible Investment ("RI") policies. AXA's credit review combines top‑down and bottom‑up approaches - which are essential for assessing credit risk - and helps to identify and capture ESG risks and opportunities at both the industry and issuer levels. During a credit review, several factors related to an issuer's business and financial profiles are assessed. This includes E, S and G factors that may impact credit performance, expressed through the "Resilience & Transparency" factor. The relevance of this factor depends on the nature of the industry and is also often implicitly considered in various other aspects of the business risk profile such as the issuer's strategy and the competitive position. AXA's Credit Research Team classifies industries into three Resilience categories - high, medium, or low - based on an overall assessment of the materiality of E, S and G factors. For each industry, AXA defines the credit material E and S factors, 4.3 Social information while considering Governance as crucial and core to the credit assessment across all sectors. Governance is an overriding factor; poor governance - such as inadequate reporting, inappropriate company structure or weak Risk Management - can significantly impact credit performance, although all companies remain exposed to idiosyncratic events. Social factors, such as labor management can also be credit material, especially for business segments facing labor shortage and/or whose workforce is exposed to challenging working conditions. Inadequate health and safety practices can negatively impact a company's operating performance (e.g. accidents, injuries, operational disruptions) and may damage its license to operate, leading to legal liabilities. The impact of the Resilience and Transparency factor may vary from short term to long term. It can be credit material within the typical Credit rating horizon (approximatively 2 years) and thus affect the issuer's Internal Credit Rating ("ICR"). The risk may also have a longer time horizon, for instance, when integrating transition risks into credit assessment. In such cases, AXA's Credit Research Team may use other levers, such as proposing to cease investing or imposing maturity constraints. These decisions are then implemented by asset managers investing on behalf of AXA in fully controlled mandates. AXA's Human Rights Policy as an investor As an investor, with respect to human rights risks, AXA has implemented various responsible investment policies (including, as an example, a policy on controversial weapons) and built an analytical framework to identify potential indirect impacts on human rights. In its General Account, AXA seeks to avoid any adverse human rights impact that might be associated with its investments activities by applying the exclusion list derived from the AXA Human Rights policy. AXA Human Rights Policy aims at preventing Human Rights abuses and reflects AXA's commitment to general and sectoral international standards by incorporating the following human rights‑related inputs: (i) fundamental principles such as those of the United Nations Global Compact, the International Labor Organization (ILO) as well as OECD recommendations, and (ii) the reputation and potential controversies regarding these companies. The Human Rights Policy is reviewed and updated on a regular basis and applied in respect of the AXA's General Account. AXA does not disclose, if any, the cases of non‑respect of human rights in investee companies but addresses the questionable human‑rights practices during engagement meetings.  For instance, the content of the POG Policy focuses on several aspects with a view to preserving customer trust and financial well‑being by offering our customers suitable products and delivering them the right information: offering suitable products to our consumers: the POG Policy requires entities in scope to define a target market for products that are designed or reviewed. This includes considering vulnerable customers when relevant. Once the target market is defined, AXA entities need to test whether the product is suitable for the defined target market and adapt it if necessary. Products are also reviewed regularly to ensure that the product stays suitable for its target market during its whole lifecycle. During product design or review, AXA entities should also aim to ensure that the remuneration of the distributor does not create any conflict of interests between the distributor and its customers; delivering the right information to our customers: the POG Policy requires AXA entities to check that the distribution channel selected for the product is adequate, including that distributors are themselves suitable to sell the products. AXA entities should also provide distributors with all the relevant information to be able to sell the product in a right manner to ultimately provide customers with clear, understandable, accurate and non‑misleading information on the product they are buying. Accountability for the implementation of the POG Policy is with a first line business teams, to ensure that conduct and customer protection is well embedded. Second line of defense, both at Group and local levels, are responsible for monitoring its implementation and compliance through appropriate second line controls. No I- Negative impacts I+ Positive impacts O Opportunities R Risks According to the Geneva Association, the insurance protection gap is defined as "the difference between the amount of insurance that is economically beneficial, and the amount of coverage actually purchased". Under this definition, lack of insurance among financially vulnerable populations might lead to negative coping mechanisms (e.g., recurring to emergency loans, selling productive assets). Recognizing the importance of insurance in economic empowerment and progress, AXA has identified actions to address the global protection gap, by extending the reach of insurance to financially vulnerable populations, i.e. low‑income to mass‑market customers in emerging markets and modest income customers in mature markets, that are not served or under‑served by traditional insurance. In turn, this represents a business opportunity to strengthen AXA's positioning as a universal brand and build trust and insurance awareness among relevant interest groups. These actions include: ● ● ● the creation of a dedicated business unit to deliver on AXA's Inclusive Insurance strategy, AXA EssentiALL (formerly known as AXA Emerging Customers)- reporting to the Deputy CEO of AXA; the creation of the AXA EssentiALL community of practice to ensure implementation across key markets; oversight by the Inclusive Insurance Steering Committee composed of -among others members of AXA's Management Committee. In addition, the Compensation, Governance & Sustainability Committee reviews, at least once a year, the Group's sustainability strategy including AXA EssentiALL's ambition and performance. Inclusive insurance by AXA EssentiALL AXA EssentiALL, the Inclusive Insurance business of AXA, was founded with the mission of "providing affordable, accessible and attractive products (insurance and services) that reach underserved communities, enabling better risk mitigation and overall financial health." The AXA EssentiALL business unit is composed of 17 FTEs with diverse backgrounds in insurance, financial inclusion, and international development. The multidisciplinary nature of this business unit aims to respond to the multiple barriers behind the protection gap that underserved communities and small businesses in the low‑income to mass‑market segments in emerging markets, and modest income segments in mature markets face daily. The end goal is that, taking a customer‑centric approach, AXA EssentiALL drives the development of new, or adaptation of existing, insurance products and services that address the unmet needs of the target population (attractiveness), at a price they can sustainably pay over time (affordability), and that can be bought through convenient means (accessibility). This business unit has the mandate to: ● ● ● ●
define AXA's Inclusive Insurance strategy encompassing emerging and mature markets, including the principles of inclusive insurance underpinning the target set for 2026 in response to the positive impact of AXA; work with AXA's entities in emerging and mature markets to define the target segments for inclusive insurance and conduct relevant market research to gather relevant customer insights around their needs from an insurance perspective; define, in collaboration with relevant AXA's entities, their local Inclusive Insurance strategy, and accompany them in its execution; establish and manage the financial and social performance framework for the business, as well as conduct frequent business reviews on each market, to make sure that the delivery of AXA EssentiALL's 2026 ambition takes place successfully. This roadmap then led to a bottom‑up target setting process that quantified the ambition set for 2026. These targets consider historical information -whenever available-, as well as an estimation of what new initiatives could bring in terms of people covered during the defined time horizon. All targets were validated by management at entity‑level, and the overall global target of 20 million customers was validated by the Inclusive Insurance Steering Committee at Group Level. The perimeter of subsidiaries contributing to this ambition and defined target for 2026, includes AXA consolidated entities as well as associated entities under the AXA EssentiALL scope, that are located in key markets where insurance penetration remains significantly low and where these subsidiaries have a strong strategic position to provide inclusive insurance to the defined groups of interest in the markets where they operate. Business conduct and corporate culture at AXA Compliance and Ethics Code AXA has implemented a Group Compliance & Ethics Code to address the following IROs: "the positive impact on society and business environment through a corporate culture that fosters ethical behavior and trust, notably by protecting whistleblowers". This Code seeks to establish Group‑wide rules to ensure that all AXA employees worldwide share a common understanding of the compliance, corporate culture and ethical standards the Group requires. It applies to all AXA directors, officers, and employees, as well as other personnel with employee status (i.e. trainees, temporary employees and other personnel who are considered employees for purposes of applicable employment laws and regulations and/or for tax purposes) and tied agents. The Code is focused on AXA's core values: Customer First, Integrity, Courage and One AXA. The Code also covers a variety of matters and topics, such as specific rules concerning conflicts of interest, anti‑bribery and corruption, insider trading, management of confidential information, etc. The Code is available on the Group's website. Further information on corporate culture can also be found on AXA's websites on Our vision, purpose and values (1) and on AXA's Adventure (2). In 2024, AXA launched its revised Compliance and Ethics Code. The Code offers clear guidance on what employees should focus on and also includes concrete examples designed to help employees apply it to their daily professional activities. Anti‑Bribery and Corruption prevention ‑
In order to prevent and mitigate the risk of bribery and corruption, AXA has a Group Anti‑
‑Bribery and Corruption ("ABC") Policy that establishes the minimum standards for anti‑bribery and corruption that must be implemented by AXA entities. This policy is updated annually, notably to incorporate applicable regulations (for example Sapin II Law and FCPA). A Group anti‑bribery officer sets up the Global ABC Policy including the Group requirements and monitors its implementation across AXA entities. through trainings, policies, processes and associated controls. A local ABC program must be reviewed and updated annually. The program must cover, as a minimum, the following five requirements: (i) actions to mitigate risks identified during the ABC Risk Assessment exercise, (ii) a communication / awareness / training plan, (iii) actions to mitigate any recommendations raised during Group ABC Technical Reviews, (iv) major ABC activities and projects, and (v) the local control testing plan for the ABC program. Regarding training, the AXA companies must formalize and implement a training and awareness plan regarding their Anti‑Corruption Code of Conduct and anti‑bribery policies and procedures. Specific training must be given to staff identified in their Anti‑Bribery and Corruption Risk Assessment as being most exposed to ABC risk (such as procurement functions, distribution functions, claims functions, underwriting functions, and finance functions) and Senior Management. Training must be conducted on a periodic basis and can be included in other compliance and/or ethics training. AXA employs a multi‑channel approach to communicate its anti‑corruption policies, including intranet publications, internal newsletters, and employee workshops. In addition, AXA Group Standards set out a certification by entities' CEOs that includes an "anti‑bribery and fight against corruption" section. As a result, for all the entities in scope of the AXA Group Standards, CEOs are required to certify annually to the Group CEO that their entities comply with the Standards. Monitoring corruption and bribery AXA aims to ensure that investigations related to corruption or bribery are conducted by independent teams ultimately reporting to or supervised by Internal Audit. This structure minimizes conflicts of interest and maintains the impartiality of the investigation process. In 2024, AXA has not been convicted of violating anti‑corruption and anti‑bribery laws and the Company has not been fined on these matters. Whistleblowing procedure AXA has zero tolerance to any misconduct relate to a behavior or a process believed to be inappropriate, unethical or illegal which may cause detriment to (an) individual(s) or AXA. Group Standards set out that Whistleblowing is dealt with, reported, and investigated in a consistent and appropriate manner across the Group. All entities must have a documented Whistleblowing procedure that defines Whistleblowing, associated protections, as well as roles and responsibilities, aligns with local legal and regulatory requirements and allows any suspicion of inappropriate, unethical or illegal behavior by a member of staff or an external party to be escalated either locally or to the Group. Allegations can be raised through various channels, for example line management, a trusted colleague, the dedicated local Whistleblowing hotline, local Audit, or Group Audit directly. Speak‑up@axa.com is accessible internally via local intranets and externally via "axa.com" that is globally reachable to employees, suppliers, business partners, and external stakeholders. Whatever the channels, when a concern is submitted, its content is evaluated and triaged by independent Investigations teams, based on pre‑established criteria, including categories and impact severity of the reported concern. All allegations received will be acknowledged to the whistleblower promptly (target response time is within 7 working days from receipt) and monitored either by the relevant professional family or directly by the Investigation teams. An update to the whistleblower will be provided within 3 months. All allegations which fall within AXA's Whistleblowing & Investigations Policy must be logged in AXA's global internal audit tool. If at any point it becomes clear that the allegation is not admissible under the Whistleblowing & Investigations Policy, the reasons will be provided in writing to the whistleblower. Independent Investigation teams across the Group, ultimately reporting to or supervised by Internal Audit, investigate the allegations. All Investigation outcomes are presented and To maintain the integrity and effectiveness of these policies, AXA regularly checks its alignment to local regulations, ensuring the process evolve and are updated in line with regulatory requirements. Periodic audits and reviews are conducted to identify and address any weaknesses. AXA supplements these efforts with regular internal communication campaigns and an e‑learning module to raise awareness about the importance of reporting unethical conduct. The Group's commitment to transparency is reflected in its public disclosures about the number of allegations received, the types of outcomes of investigations, and the corrective actions taken. AXA does not tolerate retaliation against whistleblowers and any such incidents should be reported. Retaliation may lead to disciplinary action, up to and including termination. In addition, malicious allegations made in bad faith are considered a disciplinary offence. Whistleblowing and investigations are confidential. Legal Requirements for Protection of Whistleblowers In Whistleblowing, everyone, anonymous or otherwise, can be confident that they will be treated fairly. Any disclosure made to Investigation teams will be taken seriously and handled confidentially, with strict adherence to data privacy regulations. Any leakage of confidential information will be subject to appropriate disciplinary action. The whistleblower's identity will be known only to those involved in the independent investigation unless there is a specific legal requirement, or the whistleblower decides otherwise. In addition, any person facilitating the reporting of a Whistleblowing is protected under the AXA's Whistleblowing and Investigations policy. Retaliation against whistleblowers or anyone who is part of the process, or reporting allegations in bad faith, are not tolerated by AXA and will be subject to appropriate disciplinary action. Wording in employment contracts and settlement agreements for employees leaving AXA must not prohibit those employees from reporting a Whistleblowing disclosure, regardless of whether such a prohibition is permitted by local law. Reporting Misconduct Misconduct case tracking and, for the most material incidents, detailed investigation results are presented and discussed with Executive Management, as well as with the Board members that attend the Audit Committee. 4.4.1.3 Responsible procurement practices, including payment practices Following the DMA results, AXA has identified as a material IRO related to business conduct topics the potential negative impact on suppliers (unfair treatment, financial instability) due to non‑responsible procurement practices, including the Group's payment practices. Payment practices at AXA Translating AXA's sustainability strategy and commitments into its management of vendors is a key issue for the Group. Policies, description of approaches in relation with 4.4 Governance information relationships with suppliers, supply chain risks, inclusion of social criteria in the selection of contractual partners, and key performance indicators on sustainable procurement are presented in the Group's Vigilance Plan (Section 4.7 - Vigilance Plan). This IRO is mainly addressed at local entity level. For example, payment practices to manage negative impact on suppliers are managed locally by entities to better fit the regulations and best practices. However, due to the identification of this material risk, AXA's Finance and Procurement Department will collaborate with entities to confirm the feasibility of a Group policy. Moreover, following the potential implementation of this policy, AXA will aim to plan actions to reduce the negative impact as well as define potential targets to measure those actions. However, in order to monitor the negative impact on suppliers, AXA started to measure in 2024 metrics regarding payment practice. AXA Group Procurement is working with a third‑party provider to get a classification of suppliers, in order to report indicators by main category of suppliers identified, i.e. SMEs and large undertaking. Employee volunteering at AXA Group Sustainability Team oversees the coordination and promotion of the volunteering program, AXA Hearts In Action (AHIA) across the different entities. This program aims at promoting a culture of volunteering enabling AXA employees to give back to society through several activities organized by local entities and the AXA Week For Good (AWfG) that is coordinated by Group Sustainability, Group Communication and operationalized by local entities. The AHIA guidelines document is the policy that frames the international volunteering program for AXA employees. This program has been locally implemented in more than 35 entities.  4.4 Governance information Philanthropy and volunteering activities are complemented with two additional topics described in the policy: humanitarian aid and arts & culture. The Group Head of Sustainability is the most senior level in the organization that is accountable for this policy. Group Sustainability team monitors and adjusts the policy every year if needed. At Group level, the International Sponsorship Committee, the Group Chief Communication, Brand and Sustainability Officer and the Group Philanthropy and Volunteering Officer work together to ensure the functioning of the program globally. This policy is accessible to local entities through a dedicated online platform (SharePoint) and updated on a regular basis. The Group sustainability team engages with two communities, with meetings every two months: the community of the sustainability managers and the community with the persons in charge of the AHIA program in their entities. In addition, in the context of the AWfG, Group Sustainability and Group Communication organize a series of deep‑dive calls to align common objectives and share best practices. AXA offers its employees a wide range of activities with various levels of engagement, such as individual volunteering, charitable team building, or employee fundraising. Key actions include but are not limited to: ecosystem cleanups, meals preparation and distribution for people in need, clothes or hygiene products collections, tree planting, charity sport challenges etc. The entities select the charities and projects they want to help, following AHIA in action guidelines and in alignment with the two pillars of the strategy. Over the last years, AHIA has moved toward skills‑based volunteering, leveraging professional and personal skills of AXA's employees, to further increase the positive impact on society and the planet. The main action is the AWfG, which is organized every year as a one full week dedicated to sustainability and volunteering. The operational expenditures for the AWfG is €673,838 in 2024 (this figure includes the salaries, benefits and other overheads of community affairs staff along with research/communications spend if used to help the community engage with the company). Each year, AXA expects to on‑board more employees in volunteering thanks to the AWfG and local programs. In 2024, 23,433 AXA employees took part in at least one volunteering activity of more than 30 minutes during the AWfG. In 2024, 573 volunteering activities have been organized in 36 AXA entities for a total of 90,000 hours of volunteering. The actions take place during office hours but also during evenings or the weekend depending on the activity. The common point is that all the actions are organized by AXA even when it is not during the office hours. Monitoring employee volunteering AXA set a quantitative target to track the effectiveness of AHIA. The defined target consists of reaching 50% of employees engaged in at least one volunteering activity per year by 2026 and is relative (i.e. measuring the proportion of the employees engaged in volunteering versus the total number of employees). The Group target extends to local AXA entities who have developed local volunteering programs i.e. AXA Hearts in Action, representing more than 95% of the AXA Group's employees. This target was first discussed within the International Sponsorship Committee of French non‑profit "AXA Atout Coeur ". This target has then been integrated in the AXA for Progress Index validated by the RISSC. It is defined with reference to the baseline year 2022 whose value is 20% of employees committed to volunteering from which progress is measured. This target covers 2024 to 2026. This target, which is a component of the strategic plan, is monitored and reviewed annually. AXA has not involved stakeholders in setting this target. 4

 4.4.1.5 Data privacy, responsible data use and data security Data privacy is an existing subtopic in social standards, however, since data security and data privacy are managed together at AXA, the Group has included its disclosure on data security/privacy in the governance section below rather than the associated social standards. Following the DMA, AXA has identified as material IROs, the potential impact on individuals' privacy and trust in data systems due to unaccountable or insecure use of data through: ● ● a potential negative impact on individuals and society due to non‑responsible use of data (e.g. privacy breaches, potential for discrimination, risk of misuse for harmful purposes); a potential negative impact on individuals and society due to non‑secure use of data (e.g. data leakage, identity theft, financial fraud, privacy violations). The below policies, actions, targets and metrics refers to these IROs. Data privacy and data security at AXA Group data privacy policy AXA adopted a Group Data Privacy Policy to manage negative impact on individuals and society due to non‑responsible use of data (e.g. privacy breaches, potential for discrimination, risk of misuse for harmful purposes). The policy's primary objectives are to ensure that AXA entities adequately protect the personal and sensitive data of customers and other persons obtained during their business activities, in order to minimize the risk of AXA entities breaching applicable data privacy and protection laws (e.g. EU General Data Protection Regulation - GDPR) and reducing the potential for penalties or reputational damage. The Group Data Privacy Officer (GDPO) monitors the policy, while the Group Chief Compliance Officer approves it to ensure alignment with regulatory requirements and Group requirements. This policy applies to all AXA companies. While it sets the minimum requirements for all AXA entities, many entities may need to exceed these standards to comply with local legal and regulatory requirements and incorporate local market best practices. 4.4 Governance information The CEO of each AXA entity, is ultimately responsible for ensuring that the entity establishes policies and procedures consistent with the Group Data Privacy Policy within their business and that the entity aims to meet applicable legal, regulatory, or contractual requirements. Through the implementation of this policy, AXA aims to respect EU GDPR and other applicable Data Protection laws and considering the recommendations and guidelines provided by the European Data Protection Board and the relevant authorities responsible for the protection of personal data. This policy objective is to ensure that AXA entities adequately protect the personal and sensitive data of clients, employees and other persons obtained during their business activities. It is communicated to AXA entities and their DPOs through the AXA Standard communication process and the Binding Corporate Rules community (BCR call). Binding Corporate Rules (BCR) AXA has adopted Binding Corporate Rules (BCR) to manage negative impact on individuals and society due to non‑responsible use of data (e.g. privacy breaches, potential for discrimination, risk of misuse for harmful purposes). These rules represent an internationally recognized standard for protection of personal data. They have been approved by the French Data Protection Authority (CNIL) and 15 other European data protection authorities. The general objective of this policy is to set up adequate safeguards to ensure that personal data is protected while transferred within the Group from an AXA entity based in a regulated jurisdiction, e.g. the European Economic Area, to an AXA Company located in another jurisdiction where that transfer is not otherwise permitted by applicable law, and any subsequent onward transfer of that data that is not otherwise permitted by applicable law. These rules and the Group's Data Privacy Declaration, whereby the Group undertakes not to sell the personal data of its customers, are available on the AXA website (1).
This policy is monitored by the GDPO and the DPO Community to ensure that it is aligned with the regulatory environment and Group requirements. AXA's Data Privacy Standard (including GDPR and other regulations) implementation is monitored notably through on‑site or on‑line assurance reviews. This policy covers all signatory AXA local entities. This policy is updated as local entities are integrated or sold. The CEOs of AXA entities are accountable for the implementation of this policy, as well as GDPR and other applicable regulations. Sustainability 4

 4.4 Governance information The Group has implemented regulations recently adopted in countries outside the European Union similar to GDPR. AXA was an early adopter of BCR, and aims to lead Data Privacy and GDPR compliance. Furthermore, AXA has set a maturity level questionnaire process in order to track the effectiveness of AXA BCR. Data security The implementation of a Security Standard across AXA is one of the key components in place to manage the potential negative impact on individuals and society due to non‑secure use of data (e.g. data leakage, identity theft, financial fraud, privacy violations). The general objective of this policy is to describe how entities' security should be structured, managed and operated, and consequently, strengthen and homogenize the security maturity of AXA entities. This policy is monitored through the entities' respective Boards, Executive and Risk Committees and non‑compliance with the policy are escalated at Group level through the Board of Directors. This Security Standard is to be applied by all the Group's entities and covers holistically the four security disciplines: Information Security, Physical Security, Operational Resilience and Health & Safety. Its implementation falls under the responsibility of the entity's CEO, under the supervision of AXA's Board of Directors through the Group Audit Committee. This policy is made available to all entities security officers by communicating it through the Security Community of 1200+ people across the Group. To support the implementation of Security Standards and to enable a homogenous and continuous increase of security maturity, AXA has designed a risk‑based security strategy, reviewed, challenged and approved by the Board of Directors, focusing on strengthening security foundations across the Group and developing resilience capabilities. For instance, AXA aims at strengthening its security foundations through automation of detection and response processes, as well as enhancing AXA's readiness to face certain attack scenarios such as ransomware and ultimately develop faster reaction and recovery capabilities. Underlying actions are structured under a centrally managed program, "One Trust", which organizes the delivery around the strategic ambition. One Trust program is a 3‑year program aiming to centrally manage security activities to maximize return on investment, while providing flexibility for reprioritization to ensure agility. The program is organized around 7 projects, the strengthening of security controls, data protection, new technologies such as cloud and artificial intelligence (AI) protection, as well as the strengthening of security expertise and people awareness. A centralized vulnerability management system, a security shared service center, the release of strengthened internal security frameworks based on ISO 27001, are examples of what the program can deliver. With these actions, AXA expects to bring further homogenized security capabilities to its entities and embed end‑to‑end resilience into the organization. One Trust program represents a forecasted investment of €30 million from 2024 to 2026, with targets and budgets defined on a yearly basis. Monitoring data security and data privacy and Responsible AI Usage Data privacy AXA has set a target regarding the reception of customers' complaints with respect to data privacy in order to track the effectiveness of the Group Data Privacy Policy and BCR, with respect to the management of negative impact on individuals and society due to non‑responsible use of data (e.g. privacy breaches, potential for discrimination, risk of misuse for harmful purposes). This target enables AXA to ensure that customers' data is used in a responsible manner that safeguards clients' privacy, in accordance with its Data Privacy Declarations and internal corporate rules for the protection of personal data. The defined target consists of achieving a yearly target of 0,01% of complaints received with respect to data privacy in the Group (which means less than 9,500 complaints for 95,000,000 clients) and is absolute. This target covers all activities of AXA with respect to Data Privacy. This target has been defined with reference to complaints lodged by natural persons about any unlawful or inappropriate processing of their personal data that is incompatible with the BCR. This target covers the number of complaints received during a calendar year. AXA has defined this target using the number of complaints with respect to data privacy received by the Group, compared to the total number of AXA customers, to evaluate and challenge the number of customers' complaints. This target considers and respects of the freedoms and rights of the individuals, i.e. data subjects, thanks to close collaboration between all the local entities' DPOs, through the management of complaints received in all AXA entities. With respect to the first disclosed target, AXA has achieved the following performance: in 2024, out of 95 million customers, the Group received 509 customer's complaints with respect to data privacy, equivalent to 0.0005% customers. Compared with what had been initially planned, the progress is as follows: the ratio for 2023 is under the yearly target of 0.01%. In 2023, AXA received 497 complaints, equivalent to 0.0005% of customers, and in 2022, 471 complaints, equivalent to 0.0005% of customers. This performance is explained by the implementation of various initiatives to promote the responsible use of data and AI, to address societal challenges and to ensure the protection of customer data through a strong security strategy. 4

These targets aim to ensure that both individuals and the organization are equipped with tools and knowledge to anticipate and mitigate risks of a negative impact on individuals and society due to non‑secure use of data (e.g., data leakage, identity theft, financial fraud, privacy violations.). The defined targets consist of reaching a 3.0 ISO Maturity score, and 100% of staff trained about security and data privacy across the Group. As part of One Trust program described in the previous section, AXA is currently designing an internal framework, based on several security norms (ISO 27001, NIST, …) to better drive the improvement of entities' security posture. This framework aims at evaluating the entities via a security score considering entity's maturity with both technical standards (called Minimum Technical Security Baseline) and controls maturity (called the Security Assurance Framework). In this framework, this security score, illustrating the Group strengthened requirements against its entities, becomes the main indicator to drive security within AXA, and entities are split into two groups (Tier A and Tier B) and are provided with specific targets based on their size, branding and connection. The security and data privacy training are updated on a yearly basis, always with a target to reach 100% of staff trained. ISO maturity target is defined to be reached at the end of the strategic cycle, in 2026, and is driven and monitored on a yearly basis through the One Trust program described before. The maturity level of AXA entities is assessed based on the ISO 27001 norms. In 2024, AXA successfully maintained a maturity score of 3.45 out of 5 (Scope of the Tier A entities, i.e. the biggest and more connected entities), contributing to the goal of remaining among the safest organizations in AXA's sector. An external assessment, from Control Risks Group, confirmed that those 20 entities are in the top quartile of the most mature organizations in the financial sector. On the training side, the mandatory training on security and data privacy deployed across the Group in 2024, reached the 100% of salaried employees certified. Sustainability 4

 4.4 Governance information Responsible Usage of AI AXA recognizes the significant potential of artificial intelligence (AI) to provide value for customers and shareholders, but also understands that its use requires careful governance and risk management measures. AXA is implementing a risk‑based approach to AI management, founded on internal AI principles, such as Responsible AI Principles, and guided by relevant policies and governance frameworks. These measures aim to ensure that AI is developed and used in a human‑centered, responsible, safe, compliant and beneficial way, with the intent of minimizing risks and enabling the achievement of strategic business objectives. To ensure the implementation and adoption of AI principles and policies across entities, AXA established the AI Governance Forum. Its mission is to guide and support the development, deployment, and use of responsible AI systems while encouraging knowledge sharing and best practice dissemination. To support the development of skills internally and to familiarize employees with the opportunities offered by Data & AI, AXA launched a Data & AI Academy in 2022. This set of learning programs offers role‑appropriate training modules for the responsible use of data & AI, to support skills development. In 2023, a "Generative AI for Everyone at AXA" module was added to promote responsible usage of GenAI. A new awareness program, "Tech Data & AI Essentials", targeting all employees will be rolled out in March 2025, in which the importance of ethical and sustainable AI is reinforced. AXA also works closely with regulators and leading research centers (Sorbonne University, Lausanne EPFL, Stanford University), on Responsible AI and effective AI governance in the insurance industry. AXA has also signed a three‑year partnership with the HAI lab in Stanford focused on six research streams on Large Language Model agents and the business impact of the AI revolution. In 2024, AXA published or presented eight academic papers in top international conferences and journals, three of which were co‑authored through its academic partnerships with Sorbonne University and EPFL. AXA sponsored two PhDs on Large Language Models in collaboration with Sorbonne University. Alongside other stakeholders (industrial partners, consultancies, schools, and technology companies), AXA is also a contributor to Impact AI, a French think tank dedicated to ethics and Responsible AI. 4

 4.5.2 Reporting methodologies 4.5.2.1 Environmental methodologies Unless stated otherwise, all targets were set without external stakeholders' consultation and are not based on scientific evidence and no quantification of capital expenditures or operational expenditures in relation to any actions plans have been estimated. Investments associated metrics Carbon intensity on Listed Equities and Listed Corporate Debt Carbon intensity in relation to listed equities and listed corporate debt (within AXA's General Account investment portfolio) takes into account Scopes 1 and 2 GHG emissions, measured in accordance with the GHG Protocol. The targets are expressed in tons of CO2-equivalent per € million invested (tCO2e/ Euro millions). AXA's carbon intensity takes into account global GHG emissions from corporates but does not include any avoided emissions. In line with NZAOA's recommended approach, AXA elected to use a corporate's Enterprise Value Including Cash (EVIC) as the denominator over which to measure the carbon intensity of AXA's investment in that corporate. AXA uses this normalized carbon intensity as the measure for its decarbonization targets for 2025 and 2030. This approach, in AXA's view, provides a better comparison among all asset classes in its portfolio and facilitates comparability with peers, as EVIC remains a commonly used denominator among AXA's peers. However, this methodology is applied only to the extent the relevant corporate data is available (i.e. both GHG emissions and EVIC available). Scope 3 emissions are not taken into account by the calculation of carbon intensity, mostly because of the lack of reported information pertaining to these kinds of emissions. AXA continues to engage with its data provider to obtain the Scope 3 emissions (upstream and downstream) in all regions, with a high corporate coverage, and with a high‑quality confidence level. Coal exposure AXA is continuously evaluating and monitoring its exposure to coal assets. It is calculated by using exclusively the companies listed in the Global Coal Exit List (GCEL 2024) provided by Urgewald. The exposure to these companies is assumed to be 100%, i.e. AXA does not apply a pro‑rata factor based on the percentage of coal‑related activities for these corporates. The amount is communicated in absolute value (Euro billion), and relatively to the total AUM of AXA's General Account. Oil & Gas exposure AXA is tracking its exposure, without any explicit target to the Oil & Gas sector by identifying the portion of its portfolio invested in corporates classified under the following Bloomberg sectors: Oil & Gas, Oil & Gas Services, and Pipelines. The total exposure to the Oil & Gas sector is calculated as the total AUM for this subset of corporates. AXA reports this figure in absolute terms (Euro billion) and relative to the total AUM of its General Account. To further analyze its exposure to unconventional oil and gas activities, AXA uses the Global Oil & Gas Exit List (GOGEL 2024) from Urgewald, which provides data on the share of unconventional production for each company. The exposure to unconventional activities is then determined by multiplying the AUM invested in each corporate by its corresponding share of unconventional production, as reported in the GOGEL dataset. The global exposure to these industries covers both listed and private assets from the General Account.  4.5 Appendices Transition financing investments To qualify investments as an asset financing the transition, AXA applies its standards to each of the following asset classes: Physical Climate Value at Risk Climate VaR (CVaR) is the ratio between the net present value of future cash flows and the current company valuation. It is expressed as a negative number, representing the potential maximum loss for a company under a specific climate scenario. Physical Risk Climate VaR metric assesses the level of exposure and vulnerability of companies to increasingly frequent and severe extreme weather events. This metric combines chronic climate risks, which refer to long‑term shifts in climate patterns such as extreme heat, extreme cold, heavy precipitation, heavy snowfall and strong winds, and acute climate risks, which refer to event‑driven physical risks such as coastal flooding, river flooding, tropical cyclones, low river flows and wildfires. This metric thus evaluates the potential economic losses in a changing climate environment based on a given climate scenario. The main challenge when applying the Physical Risk metric lies in capturing all possible extreme weather events. In addition, this metric focuses only on the assets owned by a particular company, as identified by MSCI, and does not account for the sustainability of the infrastructure, grids or other necessary components that enable the assets to operate and generate revenue for the Company. 4

Where they differ is in the use of low-carbon technologies, with the disorderly scenario using more low-carbon sources of technology in various sectors and the orderly scenario using slightly more carbon sequestration. The REMIND NGFS 2°C scenarios are similar to the 1.5°C scenarios in terms of electricity generation fuel mix in 2050 and, for the Orderly 2°C scenario, in terms of carbon sequestration use. Where they differ is how fast the transition happens, the year emissions reach net-zero emissions and the projected carbon prices needed to reach the temperature target. The 3°C scenario assumes a slower pace of decarbonization than more ambitious scenarios and is based solely on current nationally determined contributions (NDC) of each country. ●

ENCORE assigns a materiality rating to each identified dependency link between an economic activity (of which there are 271) and an ecosystem service (of which there are 25). Additionally, a materiality rating is assigned for each identified link between an economic activity and pressure (or impact drivers, of which there are 13). AXA reflects the carbon footprint of its insurance underwriting portfolios using both absolute and intensity metrics, specifically: ● ● Insurance‑Associated Emissions (IAE) is the standard measurement provided by the Partnership for Carbon Accounting Financials (PCAF). This measurement provides the absolute CO2 equivalent associated with P&C insurance underwriting portfolios for in‑scope lines of business (1). For the Retail Motor IAE calculation, the attribution factor of 6.99% provided by PCAF was used; IAE intensity is IAE divided by a financial (e.g. premium) or physical value (e.g. insured vehicles) as a means of normalizing across portfolios of differing sizes. The absolute IAE measurement is appropriate for underwriting portfolios comprising insured clients that measure and disclose their GHGs. AXA has therefore applied the IAE measurement solely in respect of its largest commercial clients. In respect of commercial underwriting portfolios for which IAE intensity was used, AXA used IAE per premium as the relevant intensity metric. The Retail Motor metric is also expressed as an intensity, namely the retail motor absolute IAE per vehicle. The commercial IAE calculation is based on Scopes 1 and 2 GHG emissions of commercial insureds. Additionally for the commercial IAE intensity calculation, an in‑scope subset focused on the portfolios within France, Germany, and further AXA XL portfolios within the US, Canada, Australia, and UK and the industry sectors of agriculture , transport, manufacturing (heavy), manufacturing (chemicals), utilities, and mining. The only deviation to this industry sector approach is AXA XL included all sectors for the specific lines of business of marine and aviation, in recognition of the significance of the emissions from these lines of businesses. The Retail Motor intensity IAE calculation also used a subset of the total portfolio, namely a focus on passenger cars. AXA used third‑party data provider S&P Global Trucost for the carbon emissions information of commercial insureds. Emissions factors for those commercial insureds for which actual Scopes 1 and 2 emissions are unavailable came from EXIOBASE. The retail motor vehicle emissions and driving distance data comes from sources which best represent the geographies in‑scope, and include Agence de l'environnement et de la maîtrise de l'énergie (ADEME), Confederation Suisse, and Schwacke vehicle emissions data. Engagement with Insureds The initial engagement target with 200 of AXA's largest corporate insureds is an objective to engage and support insureds with their transition and mitigation efforts. AXA followed this target up with a comprehensive strategy in 2023 and 2024 to define the actions and topics that qualify as engagement for this specific objective. The commercial client engagement on the subject of climate mitigation and transition may be any one of these three possible options: 1. sustainability dialogue: bespoke meetings, training/ materials, seminars/webinars, and/or the facilitating of insured connection to solutions; all focusing on decarbonization and/or biodiversity & nature strategy; 2. engagement with objectives, such as engagement with incentives to encourage behavioral change or requests for additional climate disclosures (such as emissions information); 3. engagement with objectives linked with AXA's sustainability underwriting policies, such as a reduction in non‑compliant activity of the insured. If the eligible revenue value cannot be confirmed or is less than 70% then no GWP may be recorded as transition insurance for this insurance contract. Climate adaptation solutions and services AXA defines the number of climate adaptation solutions and services delivered to commercial clients as the number of trainings, risk assessment or awareness, gap analysis prevention or solution, crisis management or remediation response provided to commercial clients that create awareness on climate adaptation or that are specifically related to climate adaptation. Sustainable claims A sustainable claim solution is a claim service or solution that involves less CO2 equivalent emissions or makes use of circular economy to minimize waste. For the purposes of reporting, AXA categorizes sustainable claims within the three below categories: ● Remote assessment, when a claim adjustment process involves the application of technology or video usage to assess and remediate the claims; ● ● Repair (versus replace), when a damage claim is adjusted by fixing or correcting the damage instead of replacing the damaged piece (for example windshield reparation); and Secondhand parts, when a replacement component is from an after‑market provider versus from an original equipment manufacturer. A claim can benefit one or several of the above solutions, but a claim can only count for one solution in each category. The percentage of sustainable retail motor claims versus all motor claims is calculated as the ratio of claims providing sustainable solution by the total number of retail motor claims of entities in scope of the target. Data for the sustainable claims solutions is sourced from the repair garages in the case of the use of the repair and secondhand parts solutions, and from AXA Assistance or external suppliers regarding the remote assessment. Deforestation policy The Deforestation policy incorporates three screening methods: assessing exposure to high‑risk commodities, monitoring controversies related to biodiversity and land use, and evaluating the impact of corporate activities on forests. AXA uses external data providers such as Sustainalytics and CDP Forest. The Deforestation policy works with the "Sustainalytics Controversies" database and focuses on biodiversity and land use category, levels 3, 4 and 5 corresponding to "Significant", "High" and "Severe". Own Operations associated metrics Scope of emissions measured AXA measures certain Scopes 1, 2 and 3 GHG emissions in respect of its own operations: ● ● ●
Scope 1: emissions related to fuel combustion on the Group's sites (such as gas and heating oil) and its vehicle fleet; Scope 2: emissions from combustion of fuels to generate electricity, steam, heating and cooling purchased and consumed by AXA buildings and data centers; and Scope 3: emissions from business travel (category 6), Information Technologies (IT) equipment manufacturing (category 2) and purchased services (category 1), emissions related to the production of fuels and energy purchased and consumed by AXA (category 3), and emissions related to employee commuting (category 7). Additional categories of indirect emissions are included this year, such as those related to the purchase of goods and services beyond emissions from IT equipment and services (category 1), and distribution emissions generated by the AXA's agencies' energy usage (category 9). 4

 Overall Scope 3 emissions includes not only those from AXA's own operations but also financed emissions described in Section 4.5.2.1 - Environmental methodologies - Financed carbon emissions. Calculation methodology AXA's calculation methodology applies GHG protocol, namely the GHG Protocol Scope 2 Guidance and the GHG Protocol Corporate Value Chain. In respect of Scope 3 emissions, the methodology does not take into account any removals, purchases, sales or transfers of carbon credits or GHG allowances. GHG emissions related to own operations are calculated using third‑party software (from Tennaxia) that collects data from entities within scope including distance travelled and electricity purchased. GHG emissions related to primary energy consumption for buildings are calculated using consumption in kilowatt‑hours (kWh) multiplied by ADEME emission factors or those communicated by the supplier for renewable energies such as biogas. AXA does not have Scope 1 GHG emissions from regulated emission trading schemes neither biogenic emissions from the combustion or bio‑degradation of biomass. 4.5 Appendices For car fleet emissions, an emission factor per kilometer is applied based on the vehicle's emission range. To consider regulatory changes, vehicles acquired or leased from 2020 onwards are classified according to the WLTP (World harmonized Light‑duty vehicles Test Procedure) standard. The WLTP is a global harmonized standard for determining the levels of pollutants, GHG emissions and fuel consumption of traditional and hybrid cars, as well as the range of fully electric vehicles. GHG emissions related to electricity consumption are calculated on the basis of consumption in kWh which is based on two different methods (1):
● ●
a market‑based method which reflects emissions from electricity that companies have purposefully chosen and where certification of origin can be used. This method is the one used in the target; and a location‑based method which reflects the average emissions intensity of grids on which energy consumption occurs (using mostly grid‑average emission factor data). Market‑based GHG emissions for Scope 2 use ADEME (2) emissions factors when the primary source of energy is known. If unavailable, AXA uses either the emission factor provided by the electricity suppliers, or the average emission factor for residual electricity in the country or region concerned (sourced from AIB for European countries, Green‑e for United States and production mix of IEA for others). Location‑based emissions use IEA production mix in relation to electricity consumption. For other secondary energy sources (heating and cooling networks), consumption is multiplied by the emission factors provided by ADEME or directly by the supplier when the energy is produced from a renewable primary source. Scope 3 emissions in category 3 (energy‑related) are calculated based on the location‑based methodology. GHG emissions related to business travel are calculated multiplying the distance travelled by AXA's employees for the different transport category by emission factors. For planes, AXA uses emission factors provided by the UK Department for Environment, Food and Rural Affairs (DEFRA), including contrails impacts and the upstream emissions. For business travels by train, AXA uses emission factors provided by ADEME. https://ghgprotocol.org/ Sustainability 4

 Work‑related accidents ‑
4.5 Appendices Sustainability 4
Work‑related accidents are not uniformly defined across jurisdictions, but generally comprise the following: an unexpected event that occurs in the working environment (accident in the building: e.g. falling down the stairs) or during a work‑related activity (journey from home to work/work to home; business trip), which may cause an injury/illness and require medical treatment or lead to a disability. All the indicators of this category are measured on contractual working days and not calendar days. Employees who were considered absent due to injury/illness during the reporting year are included, even if they are no longer employed as of December 31, 2024, are included. The absenteeism rate (based on absences due to sickness and work‑related accidents) provide AXA with a measure that evaluates the impact of the Group's local health & wellbeing initiatives, together with SDR indicators, taking into account short‑term / long‑term absences. The absenteeism rate of salaried workforce The absenteeism rate of salaried workforce is the sum of the number of absence days in a year due to work‑related accidents and illness, divided by the number of days worked per year per employee, the result of which is multiplied by the average FTE of salaried workforce. Only open‑ended contracts are included. This rate does not take into account non‑salaried workforce. Pulse and Inclusion surveys In 2024, AXA transitioned from an 11‑point eNPS measurement scale (promoters minus detractors) to a 5‑point favorability scale for measuring its employee engagement. The answer options are: Strongly Agree, Agree, Neither Agree Nor Disagree, Disagree, Strongly Disagree. Engagement (Advocacy) question is "I would recommend AXA as a great place to work." Inclusion Advocacy question: "I would recommend AXA as an inclusive and diverse workplace". The Engagement favorability score is the percentage of the number of respondents who answered "Strongly Agree or Agree", out of the number of respondents who answered to this question. AXA's gender parity amongst Top Senior Executives (women representation): represents the share of women who are part of the Global Leadership Network (GLN), which comprises the CEO and Executive Committees of AXA's largest markets and major transversal entities, as well as senior leaders from the corporate functions, markets, and some country CEOs. The numerator for this calculation is the number of women, while the denominator is the number of women plus the number of men. The scope for this calculation concerns all entities having GLN members. The GLN definition is used as the definition of AXA's top management. Remuneration (gender pay gap and total remuneration) The assessment is based on Annual Target Total Direct Compensation which includes the Fixed Pay, Target Cash Variable Pay and Target LTI. Assessing pay gaps on a target basis ensures monitoring of our progress in closing the gaps year on year and avoids potential volatility linked to AXA's business. For the vast majority of AXA entities, compensation data is only available on an annual basis and not hourly basis due to the nature of financial services activities. Gender Pay Gap is calculated as the difference between the average Target Total Direct Compensation of men and women. The Remuneration Ratio measures the pay gap between the highest‑paid individual and the median employee in terms of annual total remuneration, excluding the highest‑paid individual from the calculation of the median. The reporting scope covers all AXA entities under the CSRD scope and excludes AXA TianPing (China) due to data transfer restrictions, and Roland (Germany) as it lacked adequate preparation time for the assessment due to its recent inclusion in the scope. Other excluded entities (missed from reporting) are GACM SEGUROS GENERALES COMPAÑÍA DE SEGUROS Y REASEGUROS , S.A.U. and EMERMEDICA S.A. SERVICIOS DE AMBULANCIA PREPAGADOS which will be included from the upcoming year's assessment. Adequate Wages The wage analysis is based on Actual Total Cash Compensation which includes the Fixed Pay and Actual Cash variable pay. AXA's wage analysis is conducted to ensure that employees are paid at or above adequate wages, which are defined as living wage thresholds obtained from the external provider, Fairwage Network. These living wage thresholds are based on a robust methodology of the provider, whereby the living wage is defined as a level of income that allows an individual worker to meet his/her basic needs and those of his/her family at decent standards (in terms of food, housing, education, health, etc.). The living wage thresholds used by AXA take into account the statistical number of income earners per household (average income earners) and family size according to the national fertility rate in each country. The analysis is conducted annually by the Fairwage Network, and this reporting is based on full year 2023 data to meet the reporting timeline. The reporting scope is the same as for the section "Remuneration (gender pay gap and total remuneration)" above. AXA | Universal Registration Document 2024

 4.5 Appendices Incidents of discrimination The number of people related incidents reported (discrimination, harassment, sexual harassment, inappropriate behavior) corresponds to incidents reported to Investigation/ Internal audit during the reporting year. Definitions of social indicators An employee is an individual who is in an employment relationship with the undertaking according to national laws or practices. It includes active employees who have a labor contract with permanent and temporary, full and part‑time contract. It excludes employees on "suspended or unpaid" leave, employees with a "garden leave" status/contract and people not having labor contract with (trainees on internship without labor contract, third party agency people, contractors and subcontractors). A permanent employee is a permanent workforce under open‑ended employment contract with AXA, full‑time or part‑time. In some countries (e.g. China), renewable contracts planned for a long period (several years) are considered as open‑ended contracts. This indicator is reported in headcount as of December 31 of the reporting year. A temporary employee is a temporary workforce under fixed‑term employment contract with AXA, full‑time or part‑time. They are salaried employees but with a limited duration of employment. This indicator is reported in headcount as of December 31 of the reporting year. The percentage of entitled employees that took family‑
‑related leave is calculated as the percentage of salaried workforce, corresponding to the ratio of number of headcounts of salaried workforce who took family‑related leaves (including maternity, paternity, parental leaves) during the reporting year divided by the average headcount of salaried workforce during the reporting year. Salaried workforce who took family‑related leave during the reporting year but left AXA and no longer are part of the workforce as of December 31 are included. It concerns only salaried workforce with open‑ended contract. 4
Sustainability The contingent labor or temporary non‑salaried workforce includes contractors, consultants or those having a contract with a job agency and freelancers. It excludes trainees, interns, apprentices, outsourcing agreements, third‑party suppliers or Strategic Consulting specific services. This indicator is reported in Average Full Time Equivalent as of December 31 of the reporting year. Workers in the value chain AXA does not publish metrics associated with social standards in relation to workers in the value chain IRO. Consumers and end‑users ‑
In order to reflect the social component of the AXA EssentiALL's business, the indicator selected for this IRO corresponds to the number of customers served by inclusive insurance solutions. In 2024, the strategic decision to enlarge the scope of AXA EssentiALL was made in recognition of the global nature of the protection gap. In that regard, AXA EssentiALL is now working on developing inclusive insurance solutions across mature European Markets, with the objective of better serving modest income households and small businesses with insurance solutions. As a consequence of this enlarged geographic scope with increased rates of insurance penetration (compared to the initial scope covering emerging markets), the methodology has been adjusted to reflect the possibility of multiple contracts per customer. As a consequence a customer adjustment factor has been incorporated into the formula in an effort to eliminate double counting. The formula is as follow: No. of Customers = (A + B)/C (A) Number of individual contracts in‑force at the end of the period (B) Number of certificates within a group contract in‑force at the end of the period (C) The customer adjustment factor The primary assumptions are: 1. that in Emerging Markets the customer adjustment factor is equal to "1" to reflect the low‑insurance penetration environment and the low likelihood that customers have more than one contract with AXA; 2. that in Mature Markets, the customer adjustment factor is computed by each contributing entity based on its own local customer data, and is applied to avoid double counting of customers, 3. that a majority of policyholders or insurance beneficiaries (i.e. the customers having bought or receiving an inclusive insurance product from AXA EssentiALL) will belong to the defined segments in the local inclusive insurance strategy (i.e., low‑income to mass‑market customers in emerging markets and modest‑income customers in mature markets). Reported data is collected from AXA entities as part of the AXA EssentiALL Performance Management Process or, in cases where the AXA EssentiALL solutions are distributed through partners or third parties, data is collected directly from these business partners, which is then reviewed for consistency with local reporting by AXA entities and submitted to AXA Group (the AXA EssentiALL business unit) for consolidation purposes.  4.5.2.3 Governance methodologies Volunteering The percentage of employees engaged in volunteering activities is calculated as the number of employees (headcount) that participate at least to a volunteering activity during the year divided by the total average headcount of AXA's salaried workforce. A volunteering activity is an active participation in an activity with a charitable purpose/for the benefit of a charitable organization, of at least 30 minutes. Data‑privacy and data security ‑
4.5 Appendices Sustainability 4
Data privacy The number of complaints with respect to data privacy received by the Group, compared to the total number of AXA customers, is used to evaluate the potential negative impact on individuals' privacy and trust in data systems resulting from unaccountable or insecure use of data. The percentage of the Group's revenues represented by the Group entities that apply the Binding Corporate Rules is used to evaluate performance and effectiveness of the Group's Data Protection strategy. It is calculated based on previous year Group's revenues and list of entities that have signed the Binding Corporate Rules. Data security The One Trust Program yearly progress is used to evaluate performance and effectiveness of One Trust Program regarding the potential negative impact on individuals' privacy and trust in data systems due to unaccountable or insecure use of data. It is calculated based on the individual progress of the underlying program initiatives in an effort to ensure they remain on time, on scope and on budget. Management and relationships with suppliers The average standard time of payment in number of days according to contractual terms is calculated as the average across all the invoices paid during the reporting year. The average time of payment is calculated as the average time, in number of days, the undertaking takes to pay an invoice from the date when the contractual or statutory term of payment starts to be calculated. For most countries the contractual or statutory term of payment starts when the invoice has been issued by the vendor. Under some regulation, the contractual term of payment starts to when the invoice is received. In that case the entity uses the invoice receiving date as starting point for the calculation of time of payment. Business conduct and Anti Bribery and Corruption metrics The different cases of violation of anti‑corruption and anti‑bribery laws occurring at local entity level during the year is reported at Group level through the Group Audit Department. GHG emissions related to real estate investments (Scope 3 vs Scopes 1‑2): Uncertainties in standards interpretations led AXA to choose to categorize real estate investments in category 15 of the Group's carbon footprint related to Scope 3, although this includes invested properties over which AXA has operational control. Sustainability ● Quality of data: Information obtained from, or established on the basis of third‑party sources, including, but not limited to ADEME, S&P Global Trucost, EXIOBASE, World Bank, PRIMAP and ENCORE, is subject to measurement uncertainties resulting from limitations inherent in the nature and the methods used to determine them. This information will be disclosed as part of the progressive enhancements of the transition plan. The description of standard payment terms in number of days by main category of suppliers is unavailable, but procurement tools are currently enhanced to distinguish large companies from smaller undertakings. In addition, AXA has used phase‑in provisions for the Disclosure Requirements or datapoints of Disclosure Requirements in ESRS that may be omitted or that are not applicable in the first year(s) of preparation of the sustainability statement under the ESRS. The list of omitted disclosure is available in Section 4.5.3.2 - Cross‑cutting standards. In accordance with the Climate Delegated Act, as amended, insurance services classified in eight of the non‑Life insurance and reinsurance Solvency II lines of business are considered as EU Taxonomy‑eligible, to the extent that they include policy terms related to the underwriting of climate‑related perils. In this regard, AXA has identified three lines of business composed of insurance or reinsurance products generally including coverage of risks related to climate‑related perils: (i) motor insurance (other than third party liability insurance); (ii) marine, aviation and transport insurance; and (iii) fire and other property damage insurance. The share of gross written premiums covering climate‑related events reported in those three lines of business have been recognized as EU Taxonomy‑eligible, excluding premiums of products for which climate‑related perils are explicitly excluded from the insurance policy terms or for which climate‑related perils cannot trigger any claims. On other potentially eligible lines of business (i.e. medical expense insurance, income protection insurance, workers' compensation insurance, motor vehicle liability insurance and assistance), only the share of the gross written premiums covering climate‑related perils in products for which such cover is explicitly mentioned in the insurance policy terms could also be considered as EU Taxonomy‑eligible. The analysis has been done on direct lines of business, and reinsurance assumed. In the case of insurance coverage for natural disasters, the full premium was considered eligible. In other cases, the share of premium covering climate‑related perils can be determined in the pricing tool at the time of the subscription; otherwise it was estimated generally through historical losses recorded in respect of climate‑related perils. Alignment analysis To determine the amount of premiums related to Non‑Life insurance and reinsurance underwriting EU Taxonomy‑aligned activities, AXA analyzed the EU Taxonomy‑eligible products and identified those that satisfied each of the substantial contribution criteria of the Technical Screening Criteria (TSC) for insurance and reinsurance activities: leadership in modelling and pricing of climate risks, product design, innovative insurance coverage solutions, data sharing and high level of service in post‑disaster situations. Some criteria were covered at Group level and considered as satisfied for all portfolios. It notably concerns the fact that AXA uses state‑of‑the‑art climate risks modelling techniques (TSC 1.1), using Natural Hazard models to estimate the damage and losses to individual risks or insured and reinsured portfolios. Natural Hazards comprise geoscience‑based models for hazard intensity and frequency in formation, adding in engineering (vulnerability) and economic (financial) modules, along with reinsurance terms. Future climate risk is also assessed through forward‑looking scenarios analysis performed by Risk Management. For details relating to how the climate change risk is considered in the insurance activity (TSC 1.2), refer to Section 4.2.1 - Climate change - 4.2.1.4 - Addressing climate change as an insurer.  4.5 Appendices The criteria providing for risk‑based rewards for preventative actions (TSC 1.3) was the most restrictive such that the EU Taxonomy‑aligned share of Non‑Life gross premiums only covers the following guarantees underwritten by AXA France and AXA XL: Regarding the data sharing criteria (TSC 4), AXA France already shares loss data with the CCR (Caisse centrale de réassurance), a public‑sector reinsurer wholly owned by the French State. In addition, if a public authority or non‑profit research organization is carrying out analyses related to natural hazards, AXA will make available to them, free of charge, a significant portion of loss data related to climate‑related event. Any authority or research organization must submit a written request to AXA, specifying the purpose of the research and the scope of the data requested. Where appropriate, the data will be provided in a granular manner to enable analytical research but aggregated and anonymized, in order to comply with the provisions of Regulation (EU) 2016/679. insurance policies covering damage to cars and homes from natural catastrophes underwritten by AXA France, given the following characteristics: in France, insurance coverage for natural catastrophes is compulsory and regulated, including in respect of the level of premium. TSC 1.3 and TSC 2.1 on incentives for risk reduction and risk‑based reward are de facto complied with due to this legal restriction. The compulsory requirement also satisfies TSC 1.4 and TSC 3.1 on renewal condition and coverage for climate related premiums, ● ● high level of service in post‑disaster situation (TSC 5), characterized by adapting responses to individual situations, and evidenced by the absence of complaints. ● ● ● preventive measures takes the form of alerts sent to policyholders' mobile phone in case of extreme weather events (TSC 2.1), some specific solutions are included in commercial contracts, for example protection against business interruption (TSC 3.2), high level of service in post‑disaster situation (TSC 5) is ensured by the activation of specific measures in the event of large‑scale events, such as the systematic deployment of a national crisis unit to support policyholders in securing their property, speeding up processing times and adapting responses to individual situations, or the provision of emergency funds for the payment of upfront advances; insurance policies covering commercial property damage underwritten by AXA XL, where pricing and recommendations offered to the clients are dependent of the assessment of a loss prevention report analysis which is influenced by the degrees of prevention against climate‑related events, aligned due to the following characteristics: ● ● ● risk engineering reports provide location‑level risk assessments but more importantly risk prevention actions that could be taken to reduce the risk and ultimately reduce their insurance premiums (TSC 1.1, TSC 2.1 and TSC 2.2); the insurance coverage solution is client specific, and the various needs of the insured are established through a combination of the insured's risk manager, broker and AXA XL (TSC 3.1 and TSC 3.2); after a climate‑risk peril, the AXA XL provides information on the conditions under which coverage could be renewed or maintained, either with its in‑house claims team or with its third‑party adjustors (TSC 1.2); In order to comply with the DNSH criteria, the premiums from policies covering extraction, storage, transport or manufacture of fossil fuels or vehicles, property or other assets dedicated to such purposes, were excluded from the scope of EU Taxonomy‑aligned premiums. In order to qualify as "environmentally sustainable" in compliance with the EU Taxonomy Regulation, an economic activity must also comply with the minimum safeguards, laid down in article 18 of the EU Taxonomy Regulation. According to the European Commission, the central requirement is the implementation of appropriate procedures to identify, prevent, mitigate or remedy, on an ongoing basis, actual or potential negative impacts related to its operations, value chain and contractual relationships. This ensures that the entity's activities are carried out in compliance with such minimum safeguards. In France, the market consensus among French insurance companies is that the Loi de vigilance is based, at a high‑level, on the principles and standards mentioned in article 18 of the EU Taxonomy Regulation. To comply with the Loi de vigilance, AXA publishes a vigilance plan that covers AXA's activities and subsidiaries controlled directly or indirectly by AXA as well as the activities of subcontractors and suppliers with whom the group maintains an established business relationship, insofar as the activities are linked to that relationship. AXA's vigilance plan covers insurance activities and details how risks are identified, analyzed and prioritized (see 4.7.2.2). It also explains the procedures for regular assessment (see 4.7.3.3), the actions to reduce risks and prevent serious damage (see 4.7.4.2), and the monitoring of their effective implementation (see 4.7.6.3).
