Taxonomy alignment and eligibility of nuclear and fossil gas related activities AXA has taken into consideration the additional requirements of the Delegated Act 2022/1214 pertaining to Taxonomy eligibility of nuclear and fossil gas related activities, as well as guidance published by the European Commission (1).
On the basis of information provided by the relevant investees concerning the levels of safety, innovation and waste production from the fuel cycle and collected through an ESG data provider, AXA considers that it is exposed through its investments to all activities related to the production of electricity, heat or cold from gaseous fossil fuels (2) nuclear energy (3), as presented in Template 1 in Annex XII of Disclosure Delegated Act, as amended by the First Complementary Climate Delegated Act. The following tables present information on exposure to fossil gas and nuclear energy activities in accordance with templates 1 to 5 of Annex XII of Disclosure Delegated Act, as amended by the First Complementary Climate Delegated Act. However, in the interests of readability, as the six economic activities covered in this section are by definition recognized as Taxonomy‑eligible, no Taxonomy non‑eligible amounts are attributable to them and the table corresponding to template 5 would not provide any additional information other than the Total taxonomy‑non‑eligible economic activities in the denominator of the investment KPI, already presented in the table 'Investments directed at funding Taxonomy‑aligned activities‑Breakdown of denominator' above. It was intentionally decided not to disclose it. In some instances, the exposure to economic activities that have been reported as either Taxonomy‑aligned or Taxonomy‑eligible but not Taxonomy‑aligned, could not be allocated to either of the two climate change objectives, which explains why amounts reported in the total columns do not systematically reconcile with the sum of the amounts for climate change mitigation and climate change adaptation. These differences are not significant, in AXA's view. The Delegated Regulation on fossil gas and nuclear energy specifies in its Recital 15 the need for non‑financial and financial undertakings to provide investors with a high degree of transparency concerning their investments in fossil gas and nuclear energy generation activities. AXA's interpretation is that the fossil gas and nuclear energy exposure only apply to investments (meaning the Taxonomy investment KPI). As such, the tables intentionally do not present the Taxonomy underwriting KPI. 4

 4.5 Appendices Other methodological considerations and limitations To assist companies, the European Commission regularly publishes Taxonomy FAQs and notices that aim at further clarifying the disclosure requirements under the EU Taxonomy Regulation and the Disclosures Delegated Act. AXA acknowledges the interpretative and implementation guidance provided by the EU Commission, notably the specific guidance to financial undertakings in the Third Commission Notice released on November 8, 2024. Given that the legal framework for the EU Taxonomy reporting is new and still in the process of development, the requirements are subject to uncertainty in interpretation and the underlying data that are necessary to produce the KPIs may not always be immediately available. Against this background, AXA has opted for a prudent approach and will progressively enhance the completeness and quality of the Taxonomy reporting. Furthermore, the information included in the section on EU Taxonomy Disclosures provides only a partial view of how AXA contributes to climate‑change adaptation and should be read in conjunction with all other information provided in Section 4 that gives further examples of such contribution. 4.5.3.6 Other tables Climate The EU Taxonomy's regulatory framework identifies the dual role of insurers in the environmental transition, i.e.: Given that these two roles are measured on a different basis, applying the current definition and supporting guidance to calculate the combined KPI for insurance undertakings and the consolidated Group‑level KPI would lead to blurred information. Therefore, AXA intentionally decided not to disclose these indicators. Finally, the KPIs applicable to AXA presented in Sections 4.2.3 and 4.5.3.5, in accordance with the EU Taxonomy Regulation and the supplementing Commission Delegated Regulations, for the purpose of the financial year ended December 31, 2024, were based on financial data available as of December 31, 2024. 4

 4.6 Sustainability information certification report 4.6 Sustainability information certification report Sustainability 4
This is a free translation into English of the statutory auditors' report on the certification of sustainability information and verification of the disclosure requirements under Article 8 of Regulation (EU) 2020/852 issued in French and it is provided solely for the convenience of English‑speaking users. This report should be read in conjunction with, and construed in accordance with, French law and the H2A guidelines on Limited assurance engagement - Certification of sustainability reporting and verification of disclosure requirements set out in Article 8 of Regulation (EU) 2020/852. Report on the certification of sustainability information and verification of the disclosure requirements under Article 8 of Regulation (EU) 2020/852, relating to the year ended December 31, 2024 To the Annual General Meeting of AXA, This report is issued in our capacity as statutory auditors of AXA. It covers the sustainability information and the information required by Article 8 of Regulation (EU) 2020/852, relating to the year ended December 31, 2024 and included in the Group management report and presented in sections 4.1 to 4.5 of the universal registration document (called "the Sustainability Statement" hereafter). Pursuant to Article L. 233‑28‑4 of the French Commercial Code, AXA is required to include the above‑mentioned information in a separate section of the Group management report. This information has been prepared in the context of the first‑time application of the aforementioned Articles, a context characterized by uncertainties regarding the interpretation of the laws and regulations, the use of significant estimates, the absence of established practices and frameworks in particular for the double‑materiality assessment, and an evolving internal control system. This information enables an understanding of the impact of the activity of the Group on sustainability matters, as well as the way in which these matters influence the development of the business of the Group, its performance and position. Sustainability matters include environmental, social and corporate governance matters. Pursuant to Article L. 821‑54 paragraph II of the aforementioned Code our responsibility is to carry out the procedures necessary to issue a conclusion, expressing limited assurance, on: ● compliance with the sustainability reporting standards adopted pursuant to Article 29 b of Directive (EU) 2013/34 of the European Parliament and of the Council of 14 December 2022 (hereinafter ESRS for European Sustainability Reporting Standards) of the process implemented by AXA to determine the information reported; ● ● compliance of the sustainability information included in the Sustainability Statement with the requirements of Article L. 233‑28‑4 of the French Commercial Code, including the ESRS; and compliance with the reporting requirements set out in Article 8 of Regulation (EU) 2020/852. This engagement is carried out in compliance with the ethical rules, including independence, and quality control rules prescribed by the French Commercial Code. It is also governed by the H2A guidelines on Limited assurance engagement - Certification of sustainability reporting and verification of disclosure requirements set out in Article 8 of Regulation (EU) 2020/852. In the three separate sections of the report that follow, we present, for each of the sections of our engagement, the nature of the procedures that we carried out, the conclusions that we drew from these procedures and, in support of these conclusions, the elements to which we paid particular attention and the procedures that we carried out with regard to these elements. We draw your attention to the fact that we do not express a conclusion on any of these elements taken individually and that the procedures described should be considered in the overall context of the formation of the conclusions issued in respect of each of the three sections of our engagement. Finally, where deemed necessary to draw your attention to one or more disclosures of sustainability information provided by AXA in the Sustainability Statement, we have included emphasis of matter paragraphs hereafter. Limits of our engagement As the purpose of our engagement is to express limited assurance, the nature (choice of techniques), extent (scope) and timing of the procedures are less than those required to obtain reasonable assurance.  4.6 Sustainability information certification report Furthermore, this engagement does not provide guarantee regarding the viability or the quality of the management of AXA, in particular it does not provide an assessment of the relevance of the choices made by AXA in terms of action plans, targets, policies, scenario analyses and transition plans, which would go beyond compliance with the ESRS reporting requirements. It does, however, allow us to express conclusions regarding the Entity's process for determining the sustainability information to be reported, the sustainability information itself, and the information reported pursuant to Article 8 of Regulation (EU) 2020/852, as to the absence of identification or, on the contrary, the identification of errors, omissions or inconsistencies of such importance that they would be likely to influence the decisions that readers of the information subject to this engagement might make. Any comparative information that would be included in the Sustainability Statement is not covered by our engagement. Compliance with the ESRS of the process implemented by AXA to determine the information reported Nature of procedures carried out Our procedures consisted in verifying that: ● ● the process defined and implemented by AXA has enabled it, in accordance with the ESRS, to identify and assess its impacts, risks and opportunities related to sustainability matters, and to identify the material impacts, risks and opportunities, that lead to the publication of information disclosed in the Sustainability Statement; and the information provided on this process also complies with the ESRS. Conclusion of the procedures carried out On the basis of the procedures we have carried out, we have not identified any material errors, omissions or inconsistencies regarding the compliance of the process implemented by AXA with the ESRS. Elements that received particular attention Below we present the elements that we paid a particular attention to regarding the compliance with the ESRS of the disclosure process implemented by AXA. Concerning the identification of stakeholders Information on the identification of stakeholders is mentioned in section 4.1.3.2 of the Sustainability Statement entitled "AXA's material impacts, risks and opportunities". We have obtained an understanding of the analysis carried out by the entity to identify: ● stakeholders, who may affect or be affected by entities within the scope of information, through their activities and direct or indirect relationships across the value chain; ● We made inquiries with operational departments and individuals we deemed appropriate and inspected available documentation. Our procedures consisted primarily in: Primary users of the sustainability statement (including primary users of financial statements) ● ●
evaluating the consistency of the main stakeholders identified by the entity in view of the nature of its activities and its geographical location, taking into account its business relationships and value chain; evaluating the appropriateness of the description given in 4.1.3.2 of the Sustainability Statement, in particular with regard to the processes put in place by the entity to collect stakeholder interests and views. Concerning the identification of impacts, risks and opportunities Information relating to the identification of impacts, risks and opportunities is provided in section 4.1.3.2 of the Sustainability Statement entitled "AXA's material impacts, risks and opportunities". We have obtained an understanding of the process implemented by the entity regarding the identification of impacts (negative or positive), risks and opportunities ("IRO"), actual or potential, in relation to the sustainability matters set out in paragraph AR 16 of the "Application Requirements" of the ESRS 1 standard and those specific to the entity, as presented in aforementioned section of the Sustainability Statement. We also evaluated the completeness of the activities included within the scope used to identify IROs. We have obtained an understanding of the entity's mapping of identified IROs, especially including the description of their distribution within the entity's own operations and value chain, as well as their time horizon (short, medium or long term), and evaluated the consistency of this mapping with our knowledge of the entity and, where applicable, with risk analyses carried out by the group. 4

 4.7 Vigilance Plan 4.7 Vigilance Plan To comply with applicable French law requirements (1), AXA has (i) adopted a vigilance plan that sets out the measures established and implemented by the Group in order to identify the risks relating to, and prevent, violations of human rights, fundamental freedoms and health and safety of persons and adverse impacts on the environment resulting from the Group's activities and from its own operations (the "Vigilance Plan"), and (ii) prepared a report, included in this section, on the application of the Vigilance Plan during the financial year 2024. For the avoidance of doubt, "ESG" and "sustainability", as used in the context of describing criteria, risks, impacts or objectives, refer to environmental, social (including human rights and fundamental freedoms as well as health and safety) and governance matters; and "suppliers and subcontractors" mean the Group's direct suppliers and subcontractors for its procurement activities. 4.7.1 Scope In accordance with the requirements of French law on the duty of vigilance, the Group's Vigilance Plan, covers the activities of AXA and those of the companies controlled, directly or indirectly, by AXA within the meaning of article L.233‑16 II of the French Commercial Code, as well as the activities of subcontractors and suppliers with whom the Group maintains an established business relationship, insofar as the activities are linked to that relationship. The Vigilance Plan includes: ● ● ● ● ● a risk mapping to identify, analyze and prioritize risks;
procedures for the regular assessment of subsidiaries as well as of subcontractors and suppliers with whom an established business relationship is maintained; appropriate actions to mitigate risks and prevent serious harm;
an alert mechanism ("Whistleblowing") to report potential violations established in consultation with representative trade unions; and a system for monitoring the implementation of measures and assessing their effectiveness.
Given the diversity of the activities of AXA's subsidiaries and the Group's subcontractors and suppliers, the Vigilance Plan sets forth the Group's guiding principles and policies designed to ensure that they have a common understanding of, and operate in accordance with, the Group's standards in terms of sustainability, Whistleblowing and personal data protection.  4.7.2 Identification, analysis and prioritization of risks 4.7.2.1 Risk mapping of material sustainability risks The Group carries out three levels of risk assessment to identify, analyze and prioritize risks: ● ● ● firstly, the double‑materiality assessment ("DMA"), which became applicable to the Group from 2024 following the transposition of Directive (EU) 2022/2464 of the European Parliament and of the Council of December 14, 2022 as regards corporate sustainability reporting (the "Corporate Sustainability Reporting Directive" or "CSRD") into French law (1), as described in the section "Double Materiality Assessment" below; secondly, a human rights risk assessment which identifies and prioritizes the human rights risks for the Group and its stakeholders, across all its activities, as described in the section "Human Rights Risk Assessment" below; and thirdly, an annual ESG assessment campaign for screening general expenses and IT suppliers and subcontractors in respect of the Group's own operations, as described in the section "Risk mapping of AXA's supply chain through ESG screening" below. Double materiality assessment In 2024, for the first time, AXA carried out its DMA to identify and assess the sustainability matters which are most material to the Group's activities and its own operations. This involved a comprehensive understanding of its business activities and value chain. The DMA extends beyond identifying sustainability risks to also include material impacts (i.e. negative and positive impacts) and opportunities related to sustainability matters (Risks, Impacts and Opportunities - "IROs"). It adopts a dual perspective: (1) examining how the Group's activities affect the environment and society ("impact materiality") and (2) assessing how sustainability issues influence the Group's development, performance, and position ("financial materiality"). The focus of the Vigilance Plan is on the "impact materiality" perspective. The full list of material IROs and further details on the process of the DMA performed in 2024 are presented in Section 4.1.3.2 - AXA's material impacts, risks and opportunities. 4.7 Vigilance Plan Sustainability AXA adopts the following key steps to identify the material IROs as part of its DMA: Identifying Relevant Sustainability Topics IRO identification combines stakeholder engagement, analysis of business activities and relationships and the mapping of material sustainability matters across the value chain. This begins with the ten sustainability topics identified which are based on the Commission Delegated Regulation (EU) 2023/2772 of July 31, 2023 as regards sustainability reporting standards (the "European Sustainability Reporting Standards" or "ESRS") which were then refined through stakeholder consultations and insurance sector‑specific considerations. The identification process also considers prior risk assessments notably, AXA's internal sustainability risk assessment (performed in 2023) and the human rights risk assessment (performed in 2022). The process resulted in a list of 16 sustainability topics, giving rise to 98 sub‑topics for further analysis, with the end result of 27 IROs. The process of prioritization of the IROs is described in detail in Section 4.1.3.2 - AXA's material impacts, risks and opportunities. Mapping Actual and Potential IROs The actual and potential IROs were then mapped, connecting them with the Group's activities and stakeholders. For the purpose of identifying risks under the French law on the duty of vigilance (2), AXA considers the concept of risk to include both the risks and the negative impacts derived from the DMA exercise. Analyzing material IROs For the purposes of the "impact materiality" assessment, materiality was determined using a threshold matrix combining severity (e.g. scale, scope, irreversibility) and likelihood (e.g. probability of occurrence across time horizons). 4

For this reason, these topics were not identified as "material" IROs for AXA this year. Please refer to Section 4.2.1.5 - Addressing climate change as a company for further information. To address these specific issues, the Group conducts a human rights risk assessment every three years to identify its key human rights risks in conducting its business, as an insurer, an investor, a business partner and a company (in respect of its own operations). 4

business process outsourcing (BPO) services (particularly in geographies considered to be of "high criticality" (1)). This assessment process helps companies demonstrate their commitment to sustainability and gain valuable insights for ongoing improvement efforts. The Group's human rights policy, available on the Group's website (1), aims to prevent human rights violations across its activities and operations and to reflect the Group's adherence to internationally recognized human rights principles and standards, including those enshrined in: ● ● ● ● ● ● ● the United Nations Universal Declaration of Human Rights; the core standards of the International Labour Organization (ILO); the Guiding Principles on Business and Human Rights (implementation of the United Nations "Protect, Respect and Remedy" Framework or "Ruggie Principles"); the United Nations Global Compact;

The Group's human rights policy underpins its commitment to conducting business responsibly (as an employer, a business partner, an insurer and an investor) while promoting and protecting human rights, specifically the principles of freedom of association and the rights to fair and favorable working conditions, equal treatment and a living wage. Specific actions include: ● ● promoting the 10 guiding principles of the United Nations Global Compact (particularly principles 1 and 2 (human rights) and 3 to 6 (labor standards) and encouraging the reporting of potential or actual severe violations of human rights; setting inclusion and diversity targets and initiatives. requiring local CEOs to certify annually the compliance of their activity with the AXA Compliance and Ethics Code, available on the AXA website. As detailed in Section 4.3.1.2 - Supporting inclusion and diversity, the Group engaged with the Fair Wage Network in 2024 and successfully renewed its certification by recognizing fair wage practices in the Group for the second year running. The living wage assessment at AXA is done annually. The local living wage thresholds provided by the Fair Wage Network may vary year on year depending on the local socio‑economic and/ or inflationary environment which may lead to a few employees identified being below the local living wage thresholds. For these employees, AXA entities endeavor to close any gap to local living wage in the subsequent pay review cycle. Across the AXA Group, AXA aims to offer all employees a total target cash remuneration of at least 110% of the local living wage by the end of 2026. In addition, representatives from AXA have signed the "Business For Inclusive Growth" (B4IG) pledge, part of the UN Global Compact "Forward Faster" initiative. For more details about the living wage methodology see Section 4.5.2.2 - Social methodologies. In 2024, AXA launched a revised Compliance and Ethics Code, which includes a new section on "Protection of the Environment and Human Rights". All AXA employees are encouraged to: (1) reduce the carbon footprint of our day‑to‑day business activities; (2) know, understand and respect AXA's human rights policy, (3) know and apply AXA's sustainable procurement requirements, and (4) complete the AXA Climate Academy (as defined in Section 4.1.2.1 - Sustainability strategy).  Protection of Employee Safety, Health and Security As a company, AXA aims to be a "responsible employer" with respect to health and safety, which means creating a workplace that fosters diversity and equal opportunity for all, promotes engagement, encourages professional development and supports employee well‑being by providing a safe environment to work. The Group's Safety Management System requires AXA entities to regularly, and in the case of a serious incident or changes in regulation, conduct risk assessments in accordance with applicable local laws. The assessment involves evaluating risk based on severity (potential harm or impact) and probability (likelihood of occurrence) of a particular job or task and prioritizing the risk accordingly. This approach enables Group entities to address critical hazards effectively and efficiently, ultimately improving overall health and safety. Group training programs, hazard identification workshops and template checklists are also available to employees for conducting risk assessments to ensure a safe working environment and consistent standards of protection of health and safety in the workplace. "We Care & Dare for Progress" is the flagship of AXA's 2024‑26 People Strategy in respect of employee well‑being. "Care" reflects our commitment to protect and demonstrate empathy for our customers and employees. "Dare" is our call to action to confront challenges and foster a growth mindset. The "Care & Dare" people ambition lies at the heart of the Group's people strategy, driving a strong focus on the behaviors and mindsets of collaboration, feedback, agility and innovation; it is built around three key pillars: reinforcing our culture DNA of "Caring" for individuals, customers and society while "daring" to challenge ourselves and our business to grow. We rely on our teams and people managers to drive a "daring" and "growth" mindset, empowering individuals to take responsibility and lead initiatives to grow our business; developing the capabilities of our teams, prioritizing technical excellence and preparing our multigeneration workforce for the future through learning, mobility and career opportunities; and placing care and inclusion as the cornerstone of what we do and how we do it, delivering caring support for our people and driving progress in diversity and inclusion to endeavor to ensure that everyone feels they belong. 4.7 Vigilance Plan As regards employee health, the "Healthy You" innovative global program which was launched by the Group in November 2020 to improve the health and wellbeing of its employees around the world, continued running in 2024 within all AXA entities globally. To further care for employees, AXA also launched a financial wellbeing program named "Invest in You" focusing on educating employees to better manage their finances and leveraging the opportunities existing at AXA. As a result, AXA UK received an award for inclusion through financial education. In 2023, the AXA Group announced the launch of its "We Care" program as part of AXA's 2024‑26 People Strategy, which is also one of the Group's key milestones under the current "Unlock the future" strategic plan. This program aims to provide all employees with the time, support, and resources to make personal and professional decisions with confidence and enables further progress on its long‑term commitment towards an inclusive and supportive global workplace culture. In addition, the "We Care" program provides support to its workforce particularly where health and safety is at issue through the implementation of policies focused on providing support and protection to caregivers, parents and victims of domestic and sexual violence. The "We Care" program was deployed across all AXA entities in 2024 through four main pillars: (i) parental leave, including support for employees receiving and recovering from in‑vitro fertilization or pregnancy loss, (ii) caregiver leave, (iii) support for employees if they experience domestic and/or sexual violence and (iv) an expansion of the "Healthy You" program to include a supportive working environment for employees experiencing menstrual health conditions, menopause or andropause. In 2024, to reinforce the third pillar of the "We Care" program, AXA formed a working group on domestic and sexual violence. The group was comprised of diverse employees and an independent researcher/expert. Through this working group, AXA's entities now have additional policies, commitments, and partnerships in place to strengthen this pillar, which are further elaborated in Section 4.3.1.3 - Taking care of AXA's workforce - Looking after health and wellbeing. Beyond the "We Care" program, the Group has also established health, safety and security guidelines in respect of which all AXA entities shall strive to comply.  4.7.5 Alert mechanism AXA is committed to upholding its vigilance law, requirements which necessitate the establishment of a risk reporting and alert mechanism. In 2018, AXA established an alert mechanism to respond to the specific requirements of the loi de vigilance for its French entities in consultation with the trade unions. This alert mechanism is known internally as the Group's Whistleblowing procedure. AXA's Whistleblowing and Investigations Policy mandate that all Whistleblowing incidents be handled, reported, and investigated consistently and appropriately across the Group. Every entity is required to have a documented Whistleblowing procedure that defines Whistleblowing, provides associated protections, outlines roles and responsibilities, and complies with local legal and regulatory requirements. This procedure enables any suspicion of inappropriate, unethical, or illegal behavior by a staff member or external party to be escalated, either locally or to the Group, as AXA maintains a zero‑tolerance policy towards any misconduct deemed inappropriate, unethical, or illegal that could potentially harm individuals or AXA. Concerns can be reported through various channels, including line management, trusted colleagues, the dedicated whistleblowing hotline, local Audit or Group Audit directly (speak‑up@axa.com). Once a concern is submitted, its content is evaluated and triaged by independent Investigations teams based on pre‑established criteria. All allegations are acknowledged promptly to the whistleblower, with a target response time of seven working days from receipt. An update to the whistleblower is provided within three months. All allegations falling under AXA's Whistleblowing & Investigations Policy are logged in AXA's global internal audit tool. If at any point it becomes clear that the allegation is not admissible under this policy, the reasons will be communicated to the whistleblower in writing. 4.7 Vigilance Plan Independent Investigation teams, ultimately reporting to or supervised by Internal Audit, investigate the allegations. The outcomes of these investigations are presented and discussed with the CEO and/or top Executive Management. In "very high" founded (material) cases, the chair of the relevant Audit Committee(s) is made aware. All AXA investigations adhere to the strict protocols and procedures for conducting the investigation which are outlined in an Investigation Manual. Global compliance with these procedures across all AXA entities is mandated in the Whistleblowing and Investigations Policy. Each investigation aims to confirm or refute each element of the allegation, using methods such as interviews, documentary evidence reviews, and advanced data analysis tools. External specialists may be engaged for forensic investigations where specific expertise is required. To ensure the effectiveness and integrity of these policies, AXA regularly checks its alignment with local regulations and evolves its policies and protocols in line with regulatory requirements. Periodic audits and reviews are conducted to address any weaknesses. AXA promotes awareness about reporting unethical conduct through internal communication campaigns and e‑learning modules. The Group's commitment to transparency is reflected in its public disclosures about the number of reports received, the types of investigation outcomes, and corrective actions taken. AXA does not tolerate retaliation against whistleblowers, and any such incidents should be reported. Making malicious allegations in bad faith is a disciplinary offence. Whistleblowing and investigations are confidential, and any disclosure made to Investigation teams will be handled confidentially, with strict adherence to data privacy regulations. The whistleblower's identity will be known only to those involved in the independent investigation unless there is a specific legal requirement or the whistleblower chooses otherwise. Any person facilitating the reporting of a Whistleblowing incident is also protected under AXA's Whistleblowing & Investigations Policy. Sustainability AXA | Universal Registration Document 2024

 4.7 Vigilance Plan 4.7.6 Monitoring the effective implementation of the vigilance measures 4.7.6.1 As a company Reporting process AXA has put in place a comprehensive system of internal control and risk management (please refer to Section 4.1.4.3 - Risk Management and Internal Control over sustainability reporting) to support the enforcement of Group standards and policies across its operating entities. This system aims to ensure compliance with applicable regulations and to monitor the implementation of vigilance measures (such as sustainable procurement and fair treatment of customers) to ensure effectiveness. In addition, AXA relies on the Group's Sustainability team's annual review and reporting process for AXA entities, known as the AXA Entities Sustainability Index (the "AESI"). Conducted each year, the AESI is an internal questionnaire which assists in promoting and assessing the implementation of the Group's Sustainability guidelines and standards. Evaluation criteria are shared in June allowing entities to focus on any required remediation actions prior to year‑end. In 2024, the AESI was completed by 32 AXA entities (compared to 29 in 2023). The AESI questionnaire was reviewed and enhanced in 2024 based on the improvements identified in 2023, with questions focused on the Group's sustainability strategy implementation. Regarding the French duty of vigilance in particular, questions were included this year which allowed entities to self‑assess their compliance against the 27 requirements split across the themes below: ● sustainable procurement: inclusion of sustainability criteria in the RFPs, inclusion of sustainability clause in contracts, procurement associates' compliance with the Buyer Code of Ethics by procurement associates, implementation of sustainability awareness sessions in local procurement teams, sustainability evaluation of suppliers by independent third parties, compliance with the Sustainable Procurement Guidelines; ● ● ● ● ● ●
employee human rights: LGBT+ inclusion policies, provisions for employees with temporary or permanent disabilities, implementation of a social dialogue framework, compliance with AXA Standards on Employee Benefits and Remuneration, compliance with Group Policy on Harassment, compliance with AXA Global I&D Policy; Whistleblowing policy: existence of a local policy, communication of this policy, definition of main roles and responsibilities, the coverage of the speak‑up procedure and its accessibility for employees and external stakeholders, compliance with Global Whistleblowing and Investigations Policy; safety, health and security of employees: processes to identify risks, proactive and reactive measures and performance measurement, compliance with the Group incident reporting framework; personal data protection: application of the Binding Corporate Rules; environment and human rights as an insurer and as an investor: compliance with the Group standards and guidelines (such as P&C underwriting guidelines – Group life, savings, health underwriting sustainability guidelines and Group responsible investment guidelines); and fair treatment of customers: ensure fair treatment of customers and offer products designed to meet the needs and expectations of customers. 4

For example, the criteria grid may involve a third‑party ESG rating, the carbon emissions of suppliers' and subcontractors' products and services, or their responsible procurement policy. More than 85% (2) of RFPs launched in 2024 contained the sustainability criteria from the ESG criteria grid. In addition, the Group implemented an assessment of the extra‑financial risks of IT and general expenses suppliers, identified from an internal risk matrix by procurement category (please refer to Section 4.7.2.3 - Risk mapping of AXA's supply chain through ESG screening for further information). This requires 4.7 Vigilance Plan Sustainability any sensitive business risks identified being escalated to the GUO to the attention of the Head of Sustainability Underwriting. Each case that is referred requires a prior engagement with the broker and the client to gather all information necessary to prove that the case is not in breach of the controls referred to above. Specific engagement meetings can be organized together with the local entity involved, the underwriting sustainability experts at the GUO, the client and broker. In 2024, 94 business cases were referred and/or escalated to the GUO, compared to 100 in 2023. These restrictions apply to all investments made by local entities as principal and, with respect to General Account assets and Unit‑Linked assets in fully dedicated mandates, notwithstanding the asset manager. To monitor our General Account investments and ensure compliance with these restrictions, AXA has developed a dedicated monitoring tool. This tool tracks the Group's General Account investments and provides timely alerts regarding potential breaches.  5.1 Risk factors 5.1 Risk factors You should carefully consider the following risk factors together with other information contained in this Document. Any of these risks could materially affect our business, financial condition, or results of operations, cause the trading price of our ordinary shares to decline materially or cause our actual results to differ materially from those expected or those expressed in any forward‑looking statements made by or on behalf of the Company. Management believes that these are the significant risks to which the AXA Group is exposed as of the filing date of this Document with the AMF, based on Management's assessment of their materiality and specificity to the AXA Group. These risks are presented below in order of their materiality according to that assessment, which takes into account the likelihood that they may materialize and the expected magnitude of their negative impact on the Group. The primary categories of risk are: ● ● ● ● ● ● market‑related risks;
credit and liquidity‑related risks;
insurance and reinsurance pricing, underwriting and reserve‑related risks; operational risks;
regulatory and litigation‑related risks; and
risks related to the ownership of the Company's shares.
The risks faced by the Group are increasingly interconnected. For example, risks related to climate change may affect our claims experience through increased natural catastrophe frequency and severity, while also increasing legal, regulatory and compliance risk. The risks identified in this Document are not the only risks the Group faces, and new information may change Management's assessment of these risks. In particular, Management's assessment may be impacted by the most significant risks identified in AXA's most recent Future Risks Report, including climate change, cyber‑security risks, geopolitical instability, risks related to artificial intelligence ("AI") and big data, and energy risks. Other risks that are currently unknown to us, not specific to us, or currently assessed as immaterial, may also materially adversely affect us. Management devotes substantial resources to risk management, but the Group's risk management activities, like all control systems, are inherently limited and cannot provide absolute protection from risks or losses. Where the risks herein have had quantifiable and material financial impacts on the Group and/or resulted in material contingent liabilities, that information is reflected in the Consolidated Financial Statements, in accordance with applicable accounting principles. The Company's risk management processes, procedures and controls are described in Section 5.2 - Internal control and Risk Management of this Document, which should be read together with this Section. You should also refer to Section 5.3 - Market risks, and the subsequent sections of this Document, for quantitative information on the material risks to which the Group is exposed (1).

 5.1 Risk factors Changes in interest rates and credit spreads may adversely affect the value of our investments, as well as our business, results of operations, solvency, liquidity and financial condition Fluctuations in interest rates directly impact (i) the value of our assets (particularly fixed‑income investments) and (ii) our ability to realize gains or avoid losses from asset sales, ultimately affecting our earnings. Increased volatility in these fluctuations reduces the predictability of our investment valuations and earnings. Periods of declining or low interest rates may negatively impact our net investment income and the profitability of our Life & Health and Property & Casualty businesses, as well as our Solvency II ratio. Earnings from fixed income investments entered into in a low‑rate environment compared to the rates we pay to policyholders and annuity contract owners may result in reduced profitability. In‑force life insurance and annuity products may become comparatively more attractive to customers when interest rates decline, increasing our liabilities (for both flexible- and regular‑premium products), asset‑liability duration mismatches (as more policies and contracts remain in‑force from year to year), and an increase in provisions for guarantees in life insurance and annuity products. Our Property & Casualty business profitability may also be negatively impacted, as periods of lower rates and corresponding reduced investment returns put pressure on underwriting margins and may result in a higher combined ratio for our Property & Casualty business. In addition, an extended period of low interest rates may increase the regulatory capital and amount of assets required to cover provisions, which could adversely impact our Solvency II ratio. During periods of increasing interest rates, the fair value of certain of our fixed‑income investments may decrease, which could negatively impact our shareholders' equity and Solvency II ratio, or could impact our net income in the event our fixed‑income assets were sold. Furthermore, surrenders and lapses of life insurance policies and fixed annuity contracts may accelerate as policyholders seek higher returns, which may cause us to liquidate fixed maturity investments to obtain liquidity to satisfy such obligations. This, in turn, may result in liquidity strains and realized investment losses. Lower assets under management would reduce the management fees of our Asset Management business. In addition, we may be required to pay higher interest rates on our debt securities, debt, and bank facilities. We may also face an increase in the costs of our repurchase agreements and derivative hedging transactions, increasing our interest expenses. Our exposure to changes in credit spreads (both corporate and sovereign) primarily relates to the impact of such changes on market prices and cash flow variability. Widening credit spreads will generally reduce the value of the fixed‑income securities we hold (including credit derivatives where we assume credit exposure) and increase investment income from purchases of new fixed‑income securities in our investment portfolios. Tightening credit spreads generally increase the value of fixed‑income securities we hold and reduce our investment income from new purchases of fixed‑income securities in our investment portfolios. Although we try to manage interest rate and credit spread risk, including hedging through derivative instruments, this may not mitigate effects of volatility in interest rates or credit spreads, which could have a material adverse effect on our solvency position, results of operations, financial condition or cash flows through realized losses, impairments and changes in unrealized gains and loss positions. For additional information on the sensitivity of our Eligible Own Funds ("EOF") and Solvency II ratio to changes in interest rates and credit spreads, please refer to Section 6.6 - Note 4.2 - Market risks (including sensitivity analysis) of this Document and the AXA Group's SFCR, available on AXA's website (www.axa.com) (1).
Fluctuations in currency exchange rates may significantly affect our results of operations, financial condition, liquidity, and solvency position The geographical diversity of our businesses and the location of significant Group subsidiaries in the United States, the United Kingdom, Switzerland, Japan, Hong Kong, and other international markets exposes us to exchange rate fluctuations. A significant portion of our shareholdings and investments, revenues and expenses are denominated in non‑Euro currencies, while our Consolidated Financial Statements are published in Euros. Likewise, the portion of our debt and other obligations (including certain of our reinsurance treaties and retrocession agreements) denominated in non‑Euro currencies is exposed to exchange rate fluctuations. 5

Inflation or deflation in our principal markets would have multiple impacts on the Group and may negatively affect our business, solvency position and results of operations We are exposed to inflation risk in certain of our principal markets (i) through our holdings of fixed interest rate and other instruments, and (ii) as a result of the potential for claim payments and expenses to rise faster than anticipated in our reserving and pricing assumptions. Elevated inflation could impact our assets via increases in interest rates by central banks, leading to a decrease in the market value of bonds held in our investment portfolio, which could adversely affect the profitability of our investment portfolio and our revenues. Increases in inflation could also have an impact on the creditworthiness of bond issuers and could result in an increase in credit spreads (see "Changes in interest rates and credit spreads may adversely affect the value of our investments, as well as our business, results of operations, solvency, liquidity and financial condition" above). Depending on the macroeconomic context, increases in 5.1 Risk factors inflation could also have a negative impact on risk premiums and share prices, which could cause a decline in the market value of the Group's equity portfolio. Both our Property & Casualty and Life & Health businesses are impacted by claims cost inflation, the size of damage awards resulting from litigation, labor costs, costs of energy and raw materials, medical costs, new/replacement parts and materials, construction costs and/or inflation due to disruptions in the global supply chain. The impact of inflation on claims costs could be more pronounced on claims indexed to inflation and long‑tail Property & Casualty lines of business, such as general Liability, Workers' Compensation and professional Liability, and other specialty lines of our AXA XL business, where finalizing and settling claims for a given accident year takes a relatively long time. In addition, lags in re‑pricing in our Property & Casualty business, when combined with inflation, could reduce our profitability, and our customers may refuse pricing actions to counteract inflation. Inflation driven in the short term by supply chain disruptions and shortages affecting raw materials and goods may negatively impact our results, especially for our short‑tail lines of business. Changes in the level of inflation could also result in an increased level of uncertainty in our estimation of claims reserves, particularly for long‑tail lines of business, which could have a significant adverse effect on our solvency, results, financial position or cash flows. Moreover, inflation may negatively impact the value of Life & Savings products (for example, those with fixed future payments), which may reduce new business and increase lapse rates, including as a result of policyholders being attracted to other financial products with comparatively higher rates of return. A significant decline in the market value of the Group's asset portfolio and an increase in claims costs could also lead to a decrease in the Group's shareholders' equity. We are also exposed to deflation risk, which has materialized in the Eurozone and parts of Asia in prior periods. Deflation may erode collateral values and diminish the quality of certain invested assets, and may otherwise negatively affect our business and results of operations.  5.1 Risk factors Adverse experience relative to the methodologies, estimates and assumptions used by Management in valuing investments and determining allowances and impairments may adversely affect our results of operations Certain of our invested assets do not have an active trading market or other observable market data and are valued using methodologies involving estimates, assumptions and significant Management judgment. During periods of market disruption, more of our invested assets may be valued using these methodologies as a result of reduced trading or observable market data for certain asset classes. Our resulting valuations may not represent the price for which a security may ultimately be sold or the effect on such valuations of external factors, including but not limited to climate‑related transition risks. These transition risks may cause the market and prices for certain investments with a high carbon footprint (also known as "stranded assets") to decrease over time. More generally, the foregoing risks may materially impact fair value amounts, with potential material adverse effects on our results of operations and financial condition. The determination of the allowances and impairments that we recognize for invested assets varies by investment type, and is based upon our periodic assessment of known and inherent risks associated with the respective asset class. Such assessments are revised as in light of changes to relevant conditions and available information. Management considers a wide range of factors for impairments, including those described in Section 6.6 - Note 1.9.2 - Financial investments of this Document, and uses its best judgment in evaluating the cause of the decline in the fair value of the invested assets and the prospects for near‑term recovery. For certain asset classes, particularly debt instruments, Management's evaluation involves various assumptions and estimates about the relevant issuer and its earnings potential. Management updates its evaluations regularly and makes any corresponding adjustments to allowances and impairments. However, Management may not have accurately assessed the level of impairments taken and allowances reflected in our financial statements. The need for and timing of any additional impairments and/or allowances may have a material adverse effect on our results of operations and financial condition. 5.1.1.2 Credit and liquidity‑related risks Adverse capital and credit market conditions may significantly affect our ability to meet liquidity needs, our access to capital and increase our cost of capital Capital and credit markets may experience high levels of volatility and disruption which, during certain periods of acute stress, limit the availability of additional market liquidity and credit capacity of issuers, including the Company. The Group needs liquidity to pay our operating expenses (including claims and surrenders) and dividends, buy back shares, pay interest on our debts, refinance certain maturing debts, meet collateral or margin calls and cover other liabilities. The availability of additional financing depends on market conditions, the availability of credit, our credit ratings and credit capacity. It also depends on customers' or lenders' perception of our long- or short‑term financial prospects. Our Liquidity Risk Management framework includes active monitoring of the Group's liquidity position and contingency plans for accessing liquidity, but prolonged liquidity constraints may have a material adverse effect on our business, results of operations and financial condition. For information about the contractual maturities of debt instruments held by the Group, please refer to Section 6.6 - Note 7.4.3 - Debt instruments and loans by contractual maturity and exposure to interest rate risk of this Document. 5

 5.1 Risk factors 5.1.2 Risks related to the Company and its business 5.1.2.1 Insurance and reinsurance pricing, underwriting and reserve‑related risks Adverse experience relative to the assumptions and judgment used in setting reserves, developing and pricing products and calculating industry measures of value may significantly affect our results of operations or performance indicators, which may have an adverse impact on the price of our securities The profitability of our businesses depends on a variety of factors including social, economic and demographic trends, natural catastrophe forecasts and budgeting for large losses, policyholder behavior, dispute/litigation settlements or court decisions, changes in laws and regulations, inflation, investment returns, the cost and availability of reinsurance, and underwriting expenses. We make assumptions about these factors in estimating our risk exposures, determining the pricing of our products, establishing our technical provisions and employee benefits reserves and reporting capital levels and business results. These assumptions are based on various modeling techniques (e.g., scenarios, predictive, stochastic and/or forecasting), using both proprietary and third‑party models, data analytics and related analyses. The model outputs and related analyses are subject to various assumptions, uncertainties, model errors and the inherent limitations of any statistical analysis (including the existence, availability, use, accuracy and relevance of historical, internal and industry data). In addition, they incorporate numerous assumptions and forecasts about market conditions (such as interest rates, inflation and currency exchange rates), capital requirements, loss frequency and severity and policyholder behavior. The use of such models can also be affected by operational risks, including cyber risks impacting us directly or indirectly via third parties, as well as input, data and human errors. Adverse experience relative to such assumptions (in particular in the context of the occurrence or timing of major natural and man‑made catastrophic events, pandemics, systemic risks, cyber risks, the use of and reliance on inaccurate or incomplete models and data, the inability to accurately model risks because of a lack of historical data, or inherent product defects) may result in an increase in the pricing of our insurance products and, as a consequence, an increased need to further strengthen our product reserves. This may in turn have an adverse effect on our results of operations and financial condition, or lead to litigation. In our businesses and for the Group's reinsurance activities, we establish reserves for claims (reported and unreported) and claims expenses in accordance with industry practices, as well as accounting, actuarial and regulatory requirements. Reserves do not represent an exact calculation of liability, but instead represent expectations of what the ultimate settlement and administration of claims would cost based on our assessment of available facts and circumstances, review of historical settlement patterns (if any), estimates of trends in claims severity and frequency, actuarial experience with respect to past events, legal liability and other factors. The amount of reserves net of reinsurance also depends on the availability and loss coverage of reinsurance/retrocessions, including, for example, our ability to aggregate losses in accordance with the relevant policy terms and recoverability of reinsurance receivables. In establishing such reserves, there is also a risk related to discounting, which may turn out to be erroneous or need updating, and which may in turn impact the Group's assessments and/or require that they be revised. Furthermore, given that our New Business Value ("NBV") and Contractual Service Margin ("CSM") calculations are done on a market‑consistent basis, changes in assumptions used in calculating these measures may have a material adverse effect on the level of our NBV and/or CSM. For example, our NBV is sensitive to interest rate movements and, consequently, an adverse evolution of interest rates may significantly impact our NBV and impact the trading price of our securities. In both our Property & Casualty and Life & Health businesses, we are exposed to primary insurance risks and our earnings depend significantly on our actual claims experience being consistent with our assumptions in pricing our products and establishing the liabilities for obligations for technical provisions and claims. Certain risks for each of those business, relating to actual claims experience, reserving and related potential losses, are set out below. 5

Other factors, such as social inflation (increased litigation, expanded theories of liability, higher jury awards and settlement expectations), as well as mass litigation claims concerning harmful products or substances (e.g., asbestos, opioids, talc, glyphosate, lead paint, PFAS), may affect the ultimate costs of claims in our Property & Casualty business in ways that are difficult to assess for reserve estimate purposes and could be material. For example, it may be difficult to estimate the losses resulting from major litigation claims due to uncertainties in the timing of claims, damage awards, or settlements. Our estimates may also be more difficult during times of adverse economic conditions due to unexpected changes in behavior of claimants and policyholders, increased fraudulent reporting of exposures and/or losses, reduced maintenance of insured properties or increased small claims frequency. Because establishing claims reserves is inherently uncertain and complex and involves numerous estimates and assumptions, ultimately losses may materially exceed our 5.1 Risk factors claims reserves and have a material adverse effect on our results of operations and solvency position. Our risk management programs and hedging strategies may be inadequate to protect us against the full extent of the exposure or losses we seek to mitigate and may leave us exposed to unidentified, unanticipated or incorrectly quantified risks that could result in significant losses We employ risk management strategies, including reinsurance for our own account and capital markets solutions (such as catastrophe bonds), to avoid or limit losses and liabilities. A failure or inadequacy of any of our risk management strategies could result in significant losses and have a material adverse impact on our financial condition, results of operations, and cash flows. We use hedging to mitigate risks under guarantees provided to our clients or stemming from our assets. We hedge a portion of those risks via derivatives (including equity futures, treasury bond futures, interest rate swaps and swaptions, equity options and foreign currency derivatives). In particular, to hedge against significant widening of credit spreads, we use credit swaps to mitigate our exposure to significant deterioration of the credit 5.1 Risk factors quality of our asset portfolio. Our hedging program is modeled using numerous estimates and Management judgments, including, among others, judgments about volatility and interest rates, and correlation among various market movements. However, we may not use (or be able to use) our hedging program to hedge our risks as intended or expected, for any number of reasons, which may result in higher realized losses and unanticipated cash needs to collateralize or settle transactions. Our hedging models or their underlying estimates and assumptions could prove inadequate for certain market environments, especially when financial markets experience high volatility. These models may also fail to function as expected for reasons including: the size or liquidity of the derivative markets in question; the financial cost of hedging; or the nature of the relevant risks themselves, which may limit our ability to effectively hedge them. Hedging counterparties may also fail to perform their obligations, creating unhedged exposures and losses on uncollateralized positions. Any such failure of our hedging strategies or models to perform as intended or expected may adversely affect our results of operations, financial position, liquidity and solvency. Reinsurance may be unavailable or too expensive and may not be adequate to protect us against losses, in particular in the context of cyclical Property & Casualty insurance and reinsurance businesses The Group's net exposure may increase if we are unable to obtain adequate types and amounts of reinsurance/ retrocession, whether as the result of pricing pressures (e.g., in a hardening market), limited market capacity, responses to specific natural catastrophes (including losses incurred by our ceding insurers, reinsurers and retrocessionaires), or otherwise. Any of these factors could adversely affect our ability to write future business, or may cause us to assume more risk from the policies we issue. In addition, reinsurance treaty terms may evolve, which may negatively impact our renewals or renewal terms or may limit our coverage for certain exposures under our policies, potentially resulting in a larger net risk retained by AXA or increased costs of coverage.  5.1 Risk factors We operate through arrangements with third parties, including delegation of underwriting and claims authority, which expose us to operational and regulatory risks and could materially adversely impact our business, results of operations and financial condition We have entered into contractual outsourcing arrangements with third‑party service providers for a wide variety of services required in connection with the day‑to‑day operation of our insurance, reinsurance and Asset Management businesses (including policy administration, claims‑related services, securities pricing, asset management and advisory services and other services) and our product distribution. Such arrangements expose us to operational and regulatory risks incurred by these third parties, including employee misconduct or negligence, fraud, internal control failure, contract error, disruption to distribution arrangements, ransomware and other forms of cyber‑attacks, failures in processing policies and handling claims, and non‑compliance with applicable laws and regulations. In particular, in the context of the contemplated sale of AXA Investment Managers to BNP Paribas, we expect to enter into a long‑term partnership agreement with BNP Paribas under which BNP Paribas's asset management subsidiaries (including AXA Investment Managers and its subsidiaries) would be our preferred asset manager and would continue to provide asset management and advisory services in respect of a significant portion of our assets, in line with the current share of our assets managed by AXA Investment Managers and its subsidiaries. In addition, part of our insurance and reinsurance businesses is underwritten by third parties under contractual arrangements, which typically authorize such third parties to bind us to new policies and to renew policies, within the terms of our licenses and subject to various contractual obligations and restrictions. We could be subject to fines, penalties, injunctions, or other restrictions for breach of outsourcing/licensing requirements and be liable under the policies issued by breaching third parties, if we do not delegate appropriately to such third parties. We may also suffer such consequences if these third parties: ● ● ● do not abide by the terms of our policies or licenses; amend material terms of the policies without our approval; or do not price appropriately or fail to reserve relevant assumptions or otherwise breach their contractual obligations to us. We may also be exposed to risks relating to legislative/ regulatory measures and judicial decisions that may impose adverse interpretations (including retroactively) of wording in policies to which we have been bound by third parties. Furthermore, as with other reinsurers, we do not separately evaluate the individual risks assumed under our reinsurance treaties, and we largely rely on original underwriting decisions made by ceding companies. As a result, if ceding companies do not adequately evaluate the risks insured, the premiums ceded to us may not compensate us for the risks we reinsure and the losses we may incur, which may adversely affect our results of operations and financial condition. Finally, we operate through a number of joint ventures and other partnership arrangements with third parties that we do not control. These arrangements expose us to the failure of any of our partners to meets its contractual obligations or to comply with applicable laws and regulations. Any of our contractual arrangements with third parties may expose us to operational, financial, and reputational risk, which could materially adversely impact our business, results of operations and financial condition. We may also not be fully indemnified for the contractual breaches of our third parties. Inadequate or failed processes or controls, human factors or external events may adversely affect our profitability, reputation, or operational effectiveness We are also exposed to risks arising from potential failures in, or non‑compliance with, Group and entity policies concerning such matters as internal controls and procedures and financial reporting policies, as well as from employee misconduct or negligence and fraud. We may also be impacted by external events, including pandemics, terrorist attacks, and disruptions arising from or related to geopolitical conflicts, social unrest, or acts of violence, whether from groups or individuals, which may target the insurance industry or the Company or our Group specifically. While we take measures to manage such risks, they may result in losses from time to time, as well as impairments to our liquidity, disruption of our businesses, deterioration of our solvency position, legal claims, regulatory sanctions, or damage to our reputation.  5.1 Risk factors AML and other compliance matters. There has been a significant increase in legislative and regulatory initiatives and enforcement actions in the areas of financial crime compliance, including Anti‑Money Laundering ("AML"), international trade sanctions and anti‑bribery laws and regulations (e.g., the US Foreign Corrupt Practices Act, the UK Bribery Act of 2010, and the French Law known as Sapin II), which require enhanced controls on transactions. Financial crime compliance programs of financial institutions remain a major focus for regulatory and law enforcement authorities, while regulatory measures in this area continue to be further amended, expanded, and strengthened. Most recently a regulation establishing a new, EU‑wide AML/CFT authority (AMLA) and implementing a sixth AML directive have been finalized, which will result in enhanced obligations in the future. Furthermore, in certain jurisdictions the relevant AML, anti‑corruption and sanctions rules may be enforced on an extraterritorial basis, which could expose our operations to additional or conflicting requirements. If we were to become subject to enforcement proceedings or alleged to be non‑compliant with these requirements, increased cooperation among authorities globally may result in more severe sanctions or monetary penalties. The regulatory sanctions for noncompliance may be significant and could have a material adverse effect on our business, results of operations and financial condition. Regulations for underwriting syndicates and related risks. We manage, through AXA XL Underwriting Agencies Limited, Syndicate 2003, a large underwriting syndicate at Lloyd's and, as a result, are exposed to a variety of Lloyd's‑related regulatory risks. For instance, the Council of Lloyd's has wide discretionary powers to regulate members of Lloyd's, and may vary the method by which the capital solvency ratio is calculated, or impose additional or special levies on members. In addition, if Lloyd's fails to satisfy the FCA's and the Prudential Regulation Authority's annual solvency test in any given year, Syndicate 2003 could be required to cease or reduce underwriting through Lloyd's. A downgrading of the Lloyd's market could also impair Syndicate 2003's ability to trade in certain classes of business at current levels. As a Lloyd's syndicate providing coverage to US policyholders and covering US risks, we could also be required by US regulators to increase the level of funding required as minimum deposits for the protection of US policyholders. Any Lloyd's‑related risks could have an adverse effect on our business, financial condition and results of operations. Changes to IFRS. Changes to IFRS, as required by the International Accounting Standards Board ("IASB"), may significantly impact insurers and other financial institutions, including AXA, that prepare their consolidated financial statements in accordance with IFRS. For more information on IFRS requirements adopted on January 1, 2024 and standards and amendments published but not yet effective, please refer to Section 6.6 - Note 1.2.2 - Standards and amendments published but not yet effective of this Document.  5.1 Risk factors The evolving and complex regulatory environment surrounding data and artificial intelligence could increase our costs and adversely impact our business Collection, transfer, and protection of significant amounts of sensitive data are critical to the operation of our business. Regulations in this area are evolving rapidly around the world, which could adversely affect our business if we fail to adapt our rules, internal controls, and strategy to the emerging regulatory environment. In the European Union, we are subject to GDPR restrictions on data usage/processing (including profiling) and requirements regarding disclosure to customers, as well as an enforcement regime under which competent data protection authorities are able to fine companies that do not comply with EU rules up to 4% of their global annual turnover. Similar data protection regulations to GDPR have entered into force in many other jurisdictions, including the United Kingdom, certain US states, China, Brazil, Algeria, Egypt, and Thailand. There is a risk that data collected by the Group and its third‑party service providers is not processed in accordance with notices provided to, or obligations imposed by, data subjects, regulators or other counterparties, or in compliance with applicable data‑privacy laws. Negligence or failure to implement and follow internal Group policies, in particular regarding encryption of data, or to fail to perform adequate internal data collection/processing controls, may also facilitate hacking and other intrusions and result in breaches of GDPR and other applicable laws. In addition, data transfer practices and mechanisms for data transfer may be subject to future challenge or to stricter regulatory scrutiny. Regulation with respect to AI is also at various stages of evolution around the world. In the European Union, the AI Act aims to foster responsible AI development and deployment, and establishes obligations for AI systems based on their potential risks and impact. Competent authorities may impose large fines for infringement of the AI Act requirements (e.g., up to 7% of the global annual turnover for infringements related to prohibited AI practices). AI regulations have entered into force, or will likely enter into force, in many other jurisdictions, including China. In the meantime, competent authorities may apply stringent GDPR or other data privacy laws to the use of AI involving personal data. Compliance with these laws and regulations involve significant costs. The AXA Group operates in Insurance, Reinsurance, and Asset Management globally, facing market, credit, insurance, operational, and other material risks detailed in Part 5 "Risk Factors and Risk Management" and Section 6.6 - Note 28 "Litigation" of this Document.  5.2 Internal control and Risk Management Group Actuarial function The Group Actuarial function is led by the Group Actuarial function holder, who reports to the Group Chief Financial Officer and to the Group Deputy Chief Executive Officer, responsible for Finance, Risk Management, Strategy, ceded Reinsurance, Underwriting, and Operations. The Actuarial function, in accordance with the Solvency II Directive: ● ● ● ● ● ● ● ● coordinates the calculation of technical provisions;
ensures the appropriateness of the methodologies, underlying models, and assumptions used in the calculation of technical provisions; assesses the sufficiency and quality of the data used in the calculation of technical provisions; compares best estimates against experience;

 5.2 Internal control and Risk Management Group Compliance function The Group Compliance function is responsible for advising Executive Management and the Board of Directors on applicable compliance laws, regulations, and administrative provisions, and on the impact of regulatory change on the AXA Group's operations. The Group Compliance function provides expertise, advice, and support to AXA entities to assess significant compliance matters, analyzes the major compliance risks and contributes to designing solutions to mitigate the risks to which the Group is exposed. The Group Compliance function manages a wide range of compliance related matters including (i) financial crime (which includes anti‑bribery and corruption, anti‑money laundering and international sanctions/embargo compliance), (ii) data privacy and artificial intelligence (AI) compliance, (iii) conduct and customer protection, (iv) compliance & ethics, (v) the monitoring of other major compliance and regulatory risks, and (vi) regular reporting of significant compliance and regulatory matters to Executive Management, the Board of Directors and regulators. The Group Chief Compliance Officer reports to the Group Deputy Chief Executive Officer & Group General Secretary. Compliance activities within the Group are set out in a number of standards and policies which set the minimum requirements expected to be achieved by AXA entities and their Compliance functions.
 The compliance section of the Group Standards contains standards and policies on significant risk areas affecting compliance activities and sets out the high‑level controls and monitoring principles to which the entities must adhere. Adherence to the Standards and Policies (Compliance Governance, Anti‑Money Laundering, Sanctions, Anti‑Bribery, Cross Border, Data Privacy, Conduct & Customer Protection) is mandatory. In 2024, the AXA Group Compliance and Ethics Code was updated to reinforce existing ethical and compliance commitments in various areas such as customer protection, the prevention of conflicts of interest as well as anti‑bribery and corruption. A new section around the environment and human rights has also been introduced. The new Code, applicable to all AXA employees was launched in November 2024. The Group Compliance function undertakes an annual Compliance Risk Assessment exercise which requires entities to identify the most significant compliance risks to which they are exposed. Based on this assessment an Annual Compliance Plan is developed for the following year. Using the same methodology and to comply with the French extraterritorial law (Sapin II) and ACPR requirements, separate Anti‑Bribery and Corruption Risk Assessment and Anti‑Money Laundering Risk Assessment were also implemented across AXA entities. These risk assessments enable entities to identify in a more granular manner the associated risks. The Group Compliance function has implemented a number of oversight and monitoring processes to assess AXA entities against the Group Compliance framework and regulatory requirements. For example, the Compliance In‑Depth Review (CIDR) which is an ongoing program of structured on‑site Compliance reviews conducted by the Group Compliance Department to AXA Group Companies worldwide. The main purpose of these reviews is to measure adherence to the Group Standards and Policies, to define improvement action plans, to provide experienced advice, including on compliance technical aspects, and to rate the entity's maturity level against other Group companies. This program was introduced in 2009 using a risk‑based approach. On a regular basis, the Group Compliance function reports to the Group Audit Risk & Compliance Committee, the Audit Committee, and the Board of Directors (as required) on significant compliance matters. These include major regulatory changes that have compliance implications, results of the Group oversight activities and the Compliance Risk Assessment, and any other significant issues that require escalation. Group Internal Control function To further strengthen its control environment, the AXA Group established in 2017 a centralized Internal Control function within the second line of defense independent from business operations. The Group Internal Control Department is responsible for the development of a standardized methodology and framework for internal control and for oversight of its implementation in the AXA Group's subsidiaries. The Internal Control framework of the AXA Group: ● ● ● ● ●
is based on the principle of proportionality and takes into account the nature, scale, and complexity of the entities' operations; is aligned with the "Internal Control – Integrated Framework" of the Committee of Sponsoring Organizations of the Treadway Commission (COSO); is an integral part of the Risk Management thus providing Executive Management with a view of the risk and control environment; is underpinned by the Group Risk Management Standard and Group Solvency II Internal Control and Operational Risk Policy, first approved by the Audit Committee and the Board of Directors of AXA SA in December 2017; encompasses Internal Financial Control, implemented since 2010 to provide reasonable assurance regarding the reliability of Internal Control Over Financial Reporting (ICOFR), and extended to Solvency II in 2014 to contribute to the Internal Model validation process. The Group Internal Control function regularly reports to the Group Audit Risk & Compliance Committee and to the Audit Committee of the Board of Directors. 5
