 Third line of defense: Group Internal Audit function The Group Internal Audit function provides the Group Audit Committee and the Group Management Committee with independent and objective assurance on the effectiveness of Risk Management and the overall control environment across the Group to help protect the assets and reputation of the organization and help improve its operations. Group Internal Audit sets an annual plan of work, approved and monitored by the Audit Committee, based on an assessment of both the inherent risk and the adequacy of controls as well as consideration of cyclical coverage. A report is issued at the conclusion of each audit assignment to relevant senior management, with the results and resolution status of internal audit issues presented regularly to the Audit Committee and Management. Organizational independence and objectivity are ensured through the Group Head of Internal Audit reporting to the Chair of the AXA Group Audit Committee and to the Group Deputy Chief Executive Officer & General Secretary. All Internal Audit teams across the Group report to the Group Head of Internal Audit and have a direct reporting line to their local Audit Committee Chair and an administrative reporting line within their local management structure. Risk Management, Internal Control, Compliance and Actuarial function at local level Governance: The Group Governance Standards mandate the establishment of an Audit Committee by the Boards of AXA's main subsidiaries. The Audit Committees are responsible for reviewing financial results, financial reporting and control processes, critical accounting policies, specific accounting issues, key risks, and systems of internal control. The Group has established Standards that apply to AXA SA and entities where AXA has the majority of the voting rights or exercises control. These Standards are mandatory for all entities within the scope and focus on critical requirements as part of the overall Risk Management framework, enabling a clear understanding of risks both locally and Group‑wide. Chief Executive Officers must annually certify to the Group Chief Executive Officer that their entities comply with the Standards. Entity Boards should be informed of the annual certifications, material breaches, non‑compliance areas, and mitigation plans to monitor remedial action progress. Please note that in the following paragraphs AXA SA is included within the scope of local entities. 5.2 Internal control and Risk Management Risk Management: Local entities are responsible for Risk Management, following GRM standards and guidelines. The roles of local Risk Management teams are validated by both the Executive Committees of local entities and the Group Chief Risk Officer, ensuring alignment between central and local interests. The minimum responsibilities of local Risk Management teams are: ● ● ● ● ● coordinating the second line of defense locally through a specific governance framework; maintaining local written policies aligned with Group Solvency II policies; ensuring the risk appetite is implemented consistently with the Group's risk appetite, with enhanced reporting, risk limits, and decision processes; providing a second opinion on key processes including strategic plan, investment decisions, local reserves, Mergers & Acquisitions projects, IT initiatives (strategic IT, information security, AI and data), reinsurance strategy, product strategy and underwriting strategy; checking the adequacy of the local risk profile with respect to the Internal Model, and implementing, testing, and validating the Internal Model. Local Chief Risk Officers head the local Risk Management teams within each operational entity, reporting both to their local Chief Executive Officer and to the Group Chief Risk Officer. They regularly report to the local Board of Directors (or to a sub‑Committee) on Risk Management matters. Their teams are responsible for controlling and managing risks within Group policies and limits, and for validating investment or underwriting decisions through local Risk Committees. The Group Chief Risk Officer chairs the Chief Risk Officers' meeting composed of the Chief Risk Officers of AXA's main subsidiaries, which meets on a quarterly basis. Internal Control: Internal Control is a local responsibility in accordance with the Internal Control Standard and Policy. Entities are expected to: ● ● ● define, document and implement their controls and control procedures covering all important risks and processes (first line responsibility); regularly verify and challenge the effectiveness of the control environment (second line responsibility); implement a comprehensive monitoring and reporting process for internal control deficiencies at a senior level of the organization to ensure that they are rectified in an appropriate and timely manner. Local Heads of Internal Control (or equivalent) are independent from operations and report preferably to their local Chief Risk Officer.  5.2.3 Internal Model (1) 5.2 Internal control and Risk Management For more information on regulatory capital and solvency requirements applicable to the Group, including Solvency II, please refer to Section 7.3 "General information - regulation and supervision" of this Document. The Group has developed a robust economic capital model since 2007, which is utilized in its Risk Management systems and decision‑making processes. The ACPR has approved the Group's use of its Internal Model for calculating its SCR under Solvency II regulations. This Internal Model incorporates the use of the AXA Group's economic capital model across all material entities. AXA regularly reviews the scope, methodologies, and assumptions of its Internal Model and adjusts its Solvency Capital Requirement (SCR) accordingly. The model may be revised to comply with regulations, and any significant changes require college of supervisors' approval, potentially affecting the SCR level. Additionally, the Group monitors EIOPA's work program which, through its objectives, is also expected to continue to review the consistency of European insurers' internal models. This could lead to regulatory changes enhancing convergence and oversight, impacting internal models and Solvency II capital requirements. The Group's primary objective in utilizing its Internal Model over the Solvency II standard formula is to more accurately represent its risk profile in the SCR. This goal is achieved by taking into account local specificities, allowing for a tailored approach to risk calibration and benefiting from diversification across various markets and products. Additionally, it addresses the limitations of the standard formula by leveraging the Group's expertise to develop models that more closely match local specificities and risk exposures. Furthermore, the flexibility of the internal model supports ongoing adaptation and improvement, reflecting the Group's expanding experience, entry into new markets, and the introduction of innovative products with new risk considerations. The Internal Model is based on a common definition of risks used consistently throughout the Group, aiming to ensure comprehensive risk mapping across the Company. It establishes efficient procedures and reporting, with clearly allocated roles and responsibilities for identifying, measuring, monitoring, managing, and reporting key risks. The Group risk grid (2) aims to identify all material risks applicable to the Company's insurance businesses, using the Internal Model to capture all material risks to which the Group is exposed. This process includes risk assessment at the sub‑risk level and the overall aggregation of risk categories. The underlying methodologies and assumptions used in the economic capital model are regularly reviewed to ensure they accurately reflect the AXA Group's risk profile, with new methods regularly developed and integrated in accordance with the Internal Model change policy. The Solvency Capital Requirement (SCR), calculated using the Internal Model, represents the capital necessary to withstand an extreme shock, occurring with a 0.5% probability (equivalent to a 1 in 200 years event), ensuring the Group's Eligible Own Funds (EOF) are protected at the 99.5th per
 centile over a one‑year horizon. This requirement encompasses all material quantifiable risks, including market, credit, insurance, and operational risks, and accounts for the Group's diversified risk profile. In addition to monitoring risks that impact the Solvency Capital Requirement (SCR) through the Internal Model calculation, the Group also oversees its liquidity risk, reputation risk, strategic risk, regulatory risks, and emerging threats. 5.2.3.1 AXA Group Solvency II ratio The AXA Group conducts sensitivity analyses of its Solvency II ratio to material risks and events, in addition to the SCR assessment that covers all quantifiable risks (insurance, financial, and operational risks) to which the Group is exposed. These analyses quantify the potential impact on the AXA Group's Solvency II ratio of financial shocks on corporate bond and sovereign spreads, interest rates, equity, and credit migration, a wide range of shocks reflecting historical stress events (such as the 2008‑2009 financial crisis and the 2011 financial crisis), and standardized shocks such as 1‑in‑200‑year pandemic spread on mortality exposures, a vicennial natural catastrophes shock, or a vicennial operational risk.

At the Group level, the Internal Model undergoes continuous review, testing, and approval by the Solvency II Committee, which is aided by risk technical working groups.  Internal Model validatio
n
 The Group has implemented and documented a validation process for its Internal Model to monitor performance and continued adequacy, with the process and governance detailed in the Group validation policy, endorsed by the ARCC. The Group validation policy is supplemented by local validation policies that specify the local validation activities and responsibilities. Validation encompasses both the quantitative aspects of the model, including input data quality and lineage, theory and methodology, parameters and assumptions, and results, and the qualitative aspects such as expert judgment, documentation, model governance, use test, and systems/IT. Risk Management conducts integrated validation activities as outlined in the Internal Model validation policy, focusing on the validation of the model structure, modeling choices, parameters, and assumptions, as well as the validation of the Solvency Capital Requirement calculation and results. The tasks are primarily conducted by the Risk Management Departments responsible for the model, using validation tools like sensitivity tests, back testing, scenario testing, and stability analysis, along with any other pertinent activities, to perform controls and validation. These validation procedures are complemented by independent challenge and validation of assumptions, key parameters, and results through Committees intended to provide an adequate level of expertise and seniority. Group Risk Management teams conduct independent testing of local model choices, parameters, assumptions, calibration, and results. Apart from this fully integrated validation, sanctioned by the Group Chief Risk Officer's review and sign‑off, a comprehensive independent review process has been defined and implemented to provide adequate comfort to the AXA Group Management and Board of Directors that the model and its outputs meet a "fit for purpose" standard. The independent reviews are performed by the following two internal teams: ● ●
IFC teams, at local and Group level, responsible for assessing the effectiveness of the Internal Control framework over Solvency II, based on the testing of processes and controls relating to EOF and the SCR, at least annually; and Internal Model Review (IMR) team, a Group team responsible for the in‑depth actuarial review of the model under local teams' responsibility, the design and methodology when locally developed, and the local implementation of Group principles where relevant. IMR controls are performed on a three‑year rolling basis, independently from the Group's year‑end procedures. Both IMR and IFC are fully independent from the development, the governance, and the processing of the Internal Model. 5

He is tasked with aligning AXA's investment strategy with the Group's overall strategy and its commitments to responsible investment. This includes fostering better cooperation between entities, enhancing investment strategies and methodologies, and guiding investment decisions. Local investment & ALM activities are steered by local CIOs who manage local portfolios with the goal of optimizing the risk‑return ratio. They maintain reporting lines to the Group and manage close relationships with asset managers and local stakeholders. Additionally, they are responsible for investment performance and for implementing and executing sound ALM. 5.2.4.2 Group and local governance bodies To efficiently coordinate local and global investment processes, decisions within the Group's investment community are made by two main governance bodies: the Group Investment Committee, chaired by the Group Chief Financial Officer, which defines the Group's investment strategy, steers asset allocation, evaluates new investment opportunities, and monitors the Group's investment performance; and the Group Financial Risk Committee, co‑chaired by the Group Chief Financial Officer and the Group Chief Risk Officer, with the Group Chief Investment Officer also a member. At entity level, each insurance company has a local Investment and ALM Committee, with terms of reference approved by the local Executive Committees. ALM focuses on matching assets with the liabilities from insurance policy sales, aiming to optimize asset allocation. This ensures liabilities can be met confidently while maximizing expected investment returns. ALM studies are conducted by the Investment & ALM Department, utilizing internal asset managers when suitable, and are reviewed for a second opinion by the Risk Management Department. These studies are based on methodologies and modeling tools to create deterministic and stochastic scenarios that take into account policyholders' behavior for liabilities and financial market evolution for assets, while also acknowledging the interaction between them. The aim is to maximize expected returns within a defined risk level, incorporating considerations such as the Solvency II economic capital model, earnings stability, solvency margin protection, liquidity preservation, and adherence to local and consolidated capital adequacy requirements. ALM constraints are taken into account during the design of new insurance products as part of the product approval process, as detailed in Section 5.6 "Insurance risks - Product approval" of this Document. At entity level, the strategic asset allocation resulting from the ALM study is reviewed by local Risk Management and verified against predefined risk appetite limits before approval by the local Investment and ALM Committee.  5.2 Internal control and Risk Management 5.2.4.4 Investment approval process Non‑standard investment opportunities, including private and illiquid investments, new strategies, or new structures, undergo an Investment Approval Process (IAP) consistent with the Group Risk Management principles, emphasizing a systematic second opinion on key processes. The IAP aims at building a comprehensive risk return assessment of the opportunity before investment. 5.2.4.5 Governance framework for derivatives Products involving hedging programs based on derivative instruments are designed and implemented with the support of dedicated teams at AXA Investment Managers and AXA SA. In a similar way, this set‑up ensures that all entities benefit from technical expertise, legal protection, and proper execution of such transactions within the following governance framework for derivatives. Derivative strategies are reviewed by local Investment and ALM Committees. In addition, there is a segregation of duties between those responsible for making investment decisions, executing transactions, processing trades, and instructing the custodian. This segregation of duties is aimed in particular at avoiding conflicts of interest. The market risks arising from derivatives are regularly monitored, taking into account the Group's various constraints (such as risk appetite and internal model). Monitoring is designed to ensure that market risks arising on cash or derivative instruments are properly controlled and remain within approved limits. Legal risk is managed through the establishment of a standardized master agreement, ensuring that derivatives trading by the Group's business units is conducted under legal documentation that adheres to Group standards. Changes to specific mandatory provisions within these standards require approval from the Group Risk Management (GRM). GRM has established a centralized counterparty risk policy that includes rules on authorized counterparties, minimum requirements regarding collateral, and counterparty exposure limits. The operational risk related to derivatives is measured and managed within the Group's global operational risk framework. Execution and management of derivatives are centralized within dedicated teams, which reduces the AXA Group's operational risk. Valuation risk is managed by dedicated teams within the Group's asset managers and banks, who independently assess derivatives positions to ensure accurate accounting, payment, and collateral management. These teams also scrutinize counterparty prices for initiating, terminating, or restructuring derivatives, relying on their expertise in market analysis and staying current with developments in new derivatives instruments. 5.2.4.6 Investment and Asset Management For a significant portion of its assets, the Group employs asset managers for market investments. Local AXA entities primarily entrust the management of their asset portfolios to AXA's Asset Management branch, AXA Investment Managers, with local CIOs actively overseeing the performance of these asset managers. Additionally, the Group occasionally calls on external asset managers to leverage specific asset or geographical expertise, conducting comprehensive due diligence and maintaining ongoing oversight at both Group and local level. 5

 5.3 Market risks 5.3.1 Market risks: definition and exposure 5.3.2 Risk control and risk mitigation Information in this section should be read in conjunction with Section 6.6 - Note 4 "Financial and insurance Risk Management" and the paragraph "Market risks" in Section 5.1 "Risk factors" of this Document. The AXA Group is exposed to financial market risks through its core business of financial protection and through the financing of its activities as part of its equity and debt management. The market risks to the Property & Casualty (P&C) and Life & Health portfolios include a decline in market returns that may lead to accelerate amortization of intangible assets, reduced investment margins or fees due to lower returns on assets, and adverse impacts on the Group's solvency or liquidity from changes in yields on fixed‑income investments. Additionally, a decline in the market value of investments such as equity, real estate, or alternatives could negatively affect the Group's solvency position and available surplus. While changes in foreign‑exchange rates have limited impact due to hedging, they could affect earnings in euros. Health and P&C activities face inflation risks, which may increase compensation payable to policyholders, managed through regular pricing adjustments or specific inflation protections. The AXA Group manages market risks through policies tailored to each product type and their related risks. The main market risks include interest‑rate risk, spread risk, and equity risk associated with the operating activities of Group subsidiaries, exchange‑rate risk from these activities, and risks from the management of holding companies' exposure to foreign currency exchange rate fluctuations and debt. The AXA Group's exposure to market risks is managed through its Solvency Capital Requirement metric, as outlined in the "Internal Model" paragraph of Section 5.2 "Internal control and risk management," and is incorporated into the Company's Liquidity Risk Management framework, as detailed in Section 5.5 "Liquidity risk" of this Document. 5.3 Market risks For more information on the AXA Group's risk controls and Risk Management processes, please refer to Section 5.2 "Internal control and risk management" of this Document. The AXA Group's investment strategy is primarily guided by ALM with the objective of fulfilling the Group's obligations to policyholders while safeguarding the solvency of the Group and its entities. This approach is detailed in the section entitled "Governance of investment strategy and Asset Liability Management (ALM)" within the "Internal control and risk management" section of this Document. Local operating units are primarily responsible for managing their market risks within the risk framework defined at Group level, which includes limits/thresholds and standards. This strategy enables a swift, accurate, and targeted response to changes in the financial markets, political, and economic environments where the Group operates. A wide variety of Risk Management techniques are used to control and mitigate the market risks to which the Group is exposed. These techniques include local risk appetite governance and processes, ALM for optimal strategic asset allocation, a disciplined investment process with formal analysis and a second opinion by Risk Management, hedging of financial risks exceeding tolerance levels, regular monitoring of financial risks on the Group Solvency II ratio, and reinsurance to mitigate certain financial risks. Regarding the management of holding companies' foreign exchange and interest rate exposure, specific Risk Management measures have been defined and implemented. To optimize financial management and control of financial risks linked to AXA SA and its sub‑holding companies, the Group Corporate Finance and Treasury Department has defined a methodology to monitor and assess financial risks.  5.4 Credit risk 5.4 Credit risk 5.4.1 Credit risk: definition and exposure This section should be read alongside Section 6.6 - Note 4 "Financial and insurance Risk Management" of the Annual Report, as only the information in Note 4 is covered by the Statutory Auditors' report on the Consolidated Financial Statements. Information in this section should be read in conjunction with the paragraph "Credit and liquidity risks" in Section 5.1 "Risk factors" of this Document. Credit risk is defined as the risk that a third party in a transaction will default on its commitments. The AXA Group monitors three major types of counterparties to manage this risk: investment portfolios held by the Group's insurance operations and by banks and holding companies; receivables from reinsurers resulting from reinsurance ceded by the AXA Group; and credit and credit‑sensitive exposures on third‑party obligors stemming from some Group insurance and assumed reinsurance businesses. The AXA Group's exposure to credit risk is managed through its Solvency Capital Requirement metric, as outlined in the "Internal Model" paragraph of Section 5.2 "Internal control and risk management," and is incorporated into AXA's liquidity Risk Management framework, as detailed in Section 5.5 "Liquidity risks" of this Document . 5.4.2 Risk control and risk mitigation For more information on the AXA Group's risk controls and Risk Management processes, please refer to Section 5.2 "Internal control and risk management" of this Annual Report. The AXA Group's investment strategy is primarily guided by ALM with the objective of fulfilling the Group's obligations to policyholders while safeguarding the solvency of the Group and its entities. This approach is elaborated in the section entitled "Governance of investment strategy and Asset Liability Management (ALM)" within Section 5.2 "Internal Control and Risk Management" in this Document. A specific set of limits considers all the AXA Group's exposures on a given ultimate shareholder through various financial instruments and commitments, including debt securities, cash, equity, derivatives, reinsurance receivables, insurance, and assumed reinsurance. These limits are designed to manage the overall default risk associated with a particular counterparty. 5.4.2.1 Invested assets: a central monitoring of counterparty exposure The AXA Group's concentration risk is monitored through analyses at the Group level by issuer and ultimate shareholder, complemented by local procedures, and managed within a framework of Group and local issuer limits. These limits are designed to manage the default risk associated with a particular issuer, taking into account its credit rating as well as the maturity and seniority of all bonds (corporate, government agency, and sub‑sovereign) issued by the issuer and held by the AXA Group. On sovereign exposures, specific limits have also been defined on government bonds and government‑guaranteed bonds. Compliance with the limits is ensured by the Group through defined governance. The Group Financial Risk Committee, which meets monthly, is regularly updated on the main credit risks and addresses issuer exposure breaches of the Group's limits and coordinates actions for excessive credit concentrations. A Group Alternative and Credit Team, under the Group Chief Investment Officer, conducts independent credit analyses alongside local CIO teams.  5.6 Insurance risks 5.6 Insurance risks 5.6.1 Insurance risks: definition and exposure This section should be read alongside Section 6.6 – Note 4 "Financial and Insurance Risk Management" of this Document, as only the information in Note 4 is covered by the Statutory Auditors' report on the Consolidated Financial Statements. Information in this section should be read in conjunction with the paragraph "Pricing and underwriting‑related risks" in Section 5.1 "Risk factors" of this Document. The Group's insurance subsidiaries manage their insurance risks related to underwriting, pricing, and reserving, and take appropriate actions in response to changes in insurance cycles and the political and economic environments. In the context of its Property & Casualty (P&C) and Protection & Health businesses, the AXA Group faces several main insurance risks as outlined in Section 1.2 "Business overview" of this Document. These risks include premium risk, which arises from fluctuations in the timing, frequency, and severity of insured events and can lead to insufficient liability coverage, mispricing of policies, and unexpected expense payments.
 Additionally, there is reserve risk, which involves fluctuations in the timing and amount of claim settlements due to failure to accurately estimate claim costs and statistical payment fluctuations. Catastrophe risks are also significant, encompassing natural events such as climatic or meteorologic phenomena (e.g., windstorms, hurricanes, floods, hailstorms), tectonic events (earthquakes, volcanoes), and man‑made events such as nuclear incidents, liability issues, cyber‑attacks, conflagrations, and terrorist attacks. Life risks encompass various factors including mortality, longevity, lapses, disability, and pandemics. Among these, the Life business primarily faces longevity risk, which pertains to the overestimation of mortality rates in longevity‑contingent business, and lapse risk. Lapse risk involves the potential for actual lapses to deviate from expectations either by being lower or higher on a permanent basis for businesses sensitive to changes in lapse rates, as well as the possibility of a mass lapse affecting all businesses in the short term. Both longevity and lapse risks are notably sensitive to interest rate fluctuations. The AXA Group's exposure to insurance risks is managed within its Solvency Capital Requirement and is taken into account in AXA's liquidity Risk Management framework, as detailed in the section entitled "Internal Model" in Section 5.2 "Internal Control and Risk Management" and Section 5.5 "Liquidity risk" in this Document. 5.6.2 Risk control and risk mitigation For more information on the AXA Group's risk controls and Risk Management processes, please refer to Section 5.2 "Internal Control and Risk Management" of this Document. The AXA Group's investment strategy is primarily guided by ALM to fulfill its obligations to policyholders while safeguarding the solvency of the Group and its entities, as detailed in the "Governance of investment strategy and Asset Liability Management (ALM)" section within Section 5.2 "Internal Control and Risk Management" in this Document. Insurance risks for the Life & Health and Property & Casualty businesses are managed through four key processes at Group level, involving both central and local teams. These include the implementation of a product approval process that involves risk controls and profitability analyses for new products, optimizing reinsurance strategies to limit peak exposures and protect solvency, reviewing insurance liabilities through roll forward analysis, and monitoring emerging risks to leverage shared expertise within the underwriting and risk communities. 5.6.2.1
 Product approval The AXA Group has established procedures for approving new product launches, adapted and implemented locally to encourage innovation while controlling risks. The validation framework, which includes assessments based on the AXA Group's Internal Model and the Solvency Capital Requirement, ensures a comprehensive approval process for new products before market introduction. In Life & Health insurance, the Product Approval Process for traditional products is managed locally, adhering to the Group's minimum requirements to ensure swift time‑to‑market launches. For sophisticated products, local entities must obtain the Group's approval before launch. This ensures that long‑term commitments are monitored at the Group level, leveraging the Group's expertise on enhanced or specific guarantees. 5

 In Property & Casualty, methods are tailored to risk underwriting while upholding local decision‑making based on a documented approval process and leveraging the internal model's output. The objectives are to ensure pre‑launch business risks are thoroughly evaluated before being offered to customers and to maintain appropriate profitability and risk control for post‑launch business, ensuring alignment with the Group's risk framework. This framework is integral to the Group's underwriting policy, ensuring adherence to Group tolerances and value creation through adequate risk pricing. 5.6.2.2 Exposure analysis and risk assessment The AXA Group regularly reviews its exposure to ensure risks are diversified geographically and by lines of business, managing concentration risk and verifying the adequacy of reinsurance. For the Life & Health and Property & Casualty activities, the AXA Group has developed and deployed common models and metrics to consistently measure risks throughout the Group. This enables the Group to verify that its exposure complies with consolidated risk appetite limits in terms of earnings, EOF, solvency, and liquidity. These tools also contribute substantially to monitoring the major risks, including accumulations, claims frequency deviation, claims severity, reinsurance, pricing consistency, natural and man‑made catastrophes, and biometric and behavior risks deviations. In the Life & Health businesses, the AXA Group utilizes tools for analyzing mortality/longevity risks across multiple countries, regularly monitoring its exposure to these risks, including mortality, longevity, and morbidity. The insights gained from this monitoring are used to optimize product design and reinsurance coverage, with these exposure analyses being supported by expert risk models in Life. Regarding Property & Casualty exposures, ad hoc concentration risk models, both internal and external, are used for natural and man‑made catastrophes to ensure that yearly volatility from events such as windstorms, earthquakes, hurricanes, typhoons, cyber incidents, and liability issues does not exceed the Group's set tolerance levels. Since 2015, the Group has strategically developed strong in‑house expertise in the collection and standardization of sum insured and financial terms and conditions underwritten by AXA entities and the development and validation of natural 5.6 Insurance risks catastrophe models to address their dynamic nature amid global climate change. This effort has been complemented by enhanced expertise in modelling systemic events, such as cyber, which are not constrained by time and space. On top of the Group risk appetite limits definition run annually and from which entities frame their underwriting capacities, the Group has developed a comprehensive approach to collecting, analyzing, and leveraging concentration and accumulation of natural catastrophes information by centralizing in a single tool the exposure (i.e. sum insured, deductible, (sub)limits, etc.) by geolocation and associated type of coverage from most entities around the world. These measures allow AXA to effectively monitor and manage its concentration and accumulation across the Group and enhance its decision making and reaction capabilities on a proactive basis. The AXA Group's standardized exposure is modelled through sophisticated stochastic physical natural peril software to assess the risk in terms of loss. 5.6.2.3 Ceded reinsurance The reinsurance structure responsible for Life & Health and Property & Casualty reinsurance operates under the supervision of the Group Chief Underwriting Officer. Its primary role is to enhance the Group's protection by centralizing the purchase of reinsurance. For the Life & Health and Property & Casualty operations, reinsurance programs are established in accordance with Group Standards and local risk appetite limits, based on prior risk assessments. Risks are analyzed through comprehensive actuarial studies. Specifically, Property & Casualty modeling aligns with the Group's economic capital model, utilizing both in‑house and external models for natural perils like storms, floods, and earthquakes, and in‑house models for man‑made risks, including Casualty and Cyber. Reinsurance strategy The centralization and harmonization of treaty reinsurance purchase follow identical procedures for both the Life & Health and Property & Casualty activities. To build adjusted and optimized protection, the Group's operating entities are reinsured by AXA SA, with exceptions for specific cases, notably products developed in partnership with reinsurers in Life & Health and some AXA XL treaties.  Insurance liabilities Operational entities specifically monitor their reserve risks, estimating and booking liability for incurred claims on a file‑by‑file basis by the claims handlers. Additional liabilities are also booked by the local entities. The additional liability calculations are performed locally through a process based on two independent opinions. Actuaries responsible for evaluating liabilities use a range of statistical and actuarial techniques. These assumptions are formulated after consultations with claims managers, pricing actuaries, underwriters, and other specialized departments. They notably ensure that a sufficient number of operating elements have been scrutinized, enough data is available and adequate for the reserving exercise, the technical assumptions and actuarial methodologies are in line with professional practices with sensitivity analyses performed for significant cases, a roll‑forward analysis of liabilities including merit‑rating back‑tests has been performed taking into account the regulatory and economic context with material deviations explained, the operational losses relating to the reserving process have been adequately quantified, and the best estimate liabilities have been calculated in accordance with the Solvency II Directive and Group guidelines. 5

 5.7 Operational risks Information in this section should be read in conjunction with the paragraph "Operational and business risks" in Section 5.1 "Risk factors" of this Document. The AXA Group has established a unified framework to identify, quantify, and monitor the primary operational risks stemming from organizational failures, system malfunctions, process inefficiencies, resource inadequacies, and external events. Operational risks include legal risks and exclude strategic decisions, as well as reputation risks. The AXA Group's operational risk framework provides a unified system, specialized operational risk teams, and a common operational risk typology to categorize operational risks into seven areas: internal fraud, external fraud, employment practices and workplace safety, customers, products and business practices, damages to physical assets, business disruption, and system failures and execution, delivery and process management. This framework is applied across all AXA entities, including insurance companies, banking activities, AXA Investment Managers, and internal service providers, in alignment with AXA's policy on operational Risk Management. ● Both quantitative and qualitative requirements are defined across the Group by identifying and assessing the most critical operational risks of each entity and a set of stress scenarios using a forward‑looking and expert‑opinion approach. These scenarios estimate the capital requirement needed to cover operational risks using advanced models based on Solvency II principles. The operational Risk Management process is integrated into local governance through senior management validation to ensure the risk assessment is adequate, appropriate, and comprehensive, and to define and implement corrective and preemptive actions for the main risks. Additionally, a loss data collection process within most Group companies tracks and mitigates actual operational risk losses, serving as a source to back‑test risk assessment assumptions. The AXA Group's operational risk economic capital model aims to understand and mitigate losses from operational failures 5.7 Operational risks and to establish a suitable risk response strategy for significant operational risk scenarios. Profiles of operational risk at both entity and group levels are reviewed by local and Group Risk Committees to determine necessary decisions and actions. In 2024, the Group Operational Risk Profile is well‑distributed across the seven operational risk categories, with the main risks identified as transaction capture, execution, and maintenance risk due to process error, failure, and/or misperformance; compliance risk, heightened by increasing legislation and regulation, closely monitored by Group Compliance; and external fraud & system security risk, with the AXA Group's high exposure to cyber risk as new technologies are integrated into its products and services. The AXA Group's exposure to operational risks is included in its Solvency Capital Requirement, as detailed in the "Internal Model" paragraph of Section 5.2 "Internal Control and Risk Management" in the Annual Report. Specific actions are identified at Group and local levels to mitigate these risks, and the implementation of the Internal Control framework will continue to better embed controls in activities and mitigate the risks. AXA has developed an Information Risk Management (IRM) framework to ensure consistent information risk decisions throughout the organization and to establish integrated and sustainable Risk Management capabilities. In the entities subject to the EU Digital Operational Resilience Act (DORA), documentation and practices against the DORA requirements have been anchored into their IRM framework, in close relationship with Security, IT and Procurement functions who altogether contribute to AXA's digital operational resilience. AXA relies on third‑party providers for outsourcing of services at different stages of the value chain, which is a strategic advantage. However, onboarding a vendor requires AXA to assess potential risks, including regulatory, compliance, and IT security risks.  5.8 Other material risks To protect both business and customers, AXA has established a dedicated Vendor Risk Framework program, co‑sponsored by Group Risk Management and Group Procurement, and supported by Group functions such as Compliance, Data Privacy, Information Security, Operational Resilience, Legal, Reputation, and Internal Control, along with local stakeholders including Chief Risk Officers, Chief Procurement Officers, and Insurance Procurement Directors. The Vendor Risk Framework employs a risk‑based approach to define requirements for both existing and potential new contracts, focusing on the criticality of each relationship through pre‑determined criteria. It ensures an appropriate level of due diligence, minimum requirements, and oversight is implemented for each. For more information on the AXA Group's risk controls and Risk Management processes, please refer to Section 5.2 "Internal control and risk management" of this Documen 5.8 Other material risks 5.8.1 Strategic risks A strategic risk is the risk of a negative impact on earnings or capital at Group level due to a lack of responsiveness to industry developments or changes in consumer needs, or adverse business decisions. This includes significant changes in footprint through disposals or acquisitions, changes in product offering and client segmentation, and changes in the distribution model, including channel mix, alliances/ partnerships, multi‑access, and digital distribution. Given the nature of strategic risks, there is no capital charge assessment but a strong strategic Risk Management framework to anticipate and mitigate these risks. Group Risk Management is involved at an early stage in major strategic projects, such as large M&A projects. The Group governance standards require a Risk Management second opinion on key processes, including significant transactions and strategic plans. Additionally, the ORSA report provides an assessment of the overall solvency needs, which includes the Solvency II ratio projection in line with the strategic plan timeframe and in stress scenarios, as detailed in Section 5.2 "Internal Control and Risk Management - Own Risk and Solvency Assessment (ORSA)" of this Document. 5.8.2 Reputation risk Reputation risk is the risk that an event, internal or external, will negatively influence stakeholders' perception and trust in the Company or the risk of a gap between stakeholder expectations and the Company's behaviors, attitudes, values, actions, or failures to act. The AXA Group has established a global framework to manage reputation risk, focusing on both reactive protection and proactive measures to identify, monitor, manage, and mitigate reputational issues. This approach aims to minimize value destruction while building and maintaining brand equity and trust among stakeholders, without the need for a capital charge assessment. The AXA Group's Global Reputation Network is designed to implement a local Reputation Risk Management framework, aligning with the Company's overall Enterprise Risk Management approach. This initiative aims to foster a culture of reputation risk awareness and intelligence, ensuring that Risk Management strategies are consistently applied across the organization. The Reputation Risk Management approach at AXA is driven by three main objectives: to proactively manage reputation risk by avoiding or minimizing negative issues and building trust among all stakeholders, to define accountability for reputation risk across the organization at both Group and local levels, and to implement a common Reputation Risk Management framework throughout the organization. The AXA Group monitors potential and existing issues in near real‑time and receives periodic top reputation risk reports from local entities. An informational report is submitted to the Group Audit, Risk and Compliance Committee every two months, and an internal report analyzing the main cases of reputation risk managed by AXA is produced annually. Regular dialogue with the Global Reputation Network facilitates the identification and dissemination of key topics that could negatively impact the Group's reputation. The implementation of the Reputation Risk Framework encompasses the AXA Group's main activities including insurance and Asset Management. 5

 5.8.3 Emerging risks Emerging risks are risks that may develop or already exist and are continuously evolving, characterized by a high degree of uncertainty, with some potentially never materializing. The AXA Group has established processes to monitor emerging risks, which could develop over time and become significant, without a capital charge assessment. The emerging risk framework includes a network of approximately 120 people across insurance, Asset Management, and support functions such as AXA Group Operations, facilitating expertise sharing within the business and risk communities and ensuring the definition of adequate underwriting policies. Emerging risks surveillance is organized through a detection process that includes regular monitoring of scientific publications and court decisions. Risks are monitored and classified within a risk mapping constituted of five sub‑groups: environment & energy, society, regulation & politics, economy, finance & business, health & medicine, and tech & data. After prioritization by senior management or after a warning from an entity, in‑depth internal studies are developed biannually by GRM to review a specific risk and its potential impact in terms of insurance. Since 2014, an annual Future Risks Survey collects the views of internal and external stakeholders on the most significant emerging risks for society. 5.8 Other material risks The AXA Group aims to enhance the understanding and forecasting of emerging risks and support sustainable development by developing new solutions, advising on Risk Management, and contributing to the broader discussion on these issues with other key market participants. 5.8.4 Regulatory risks For further information on the regulatory environment in which the AXA Group operates including regulatory risks, please refer to the paragraph "Regulatory‑related risks" in Section 5.1 "Risk factors" of this Document. 5.8.5 Sustainability risks For further information on the sustainability risks to which the AXA Group might be exposed, either from the impacts on AXA's business or from the impact of AXA's business operations, please refer to Section 4.1 "AXA Sustainability strategy" of this Document. Additionally, certain sustainability risks which are material to the AXA Group are referenced in "Risk Factors" in Section 5.1.  6.6 Notes to the Consolidated financial statements 6.6 Notes to the Consolidated financial statements Note 1 Accounting principles 1.1 General information AXA SA, a French Société Anonyme (the "Company" and, together with its consolidated subsidiaries, "AXA" or the "Group"), is the holding (parent) company and the internal reinsurer of an international financial services group focused on financial protection. The list of main entities included in the scope of consolidation is provided in Note 2 to the Consolidated Financial Statements. AXA is listed on Euronext Paris Compartiment A. The Consolidated Financial Statements for the year ended December 31, 2024, including all notes, were set by the Board of Directors on March 12, 2025. 1.2 General accounting principles AXA's Consolidated Financial Statements are prepared as of December 31, in compliance with International Financial Reporting Standards (IFRS) and interpretations of the IFRS Interpretations Committee that are endorsed by the European Union before the end of the reporting period with a compulsory date of January 1, 2024. Accordingly, the Group uses the option provided by the European Union which allows not to apply the annual cohort requirement under IFRS 17 - Insurance Contracts for determining the groups of insurance contracts meeting some criteria (refer to paragraph 1.14.2). 1.2.1 IFRS requirements adopted on January 1, 2024 The application of the amendments below as of January 1, 2024 had no material impact on the Group's Consolidated Financial Statements: ● ● ● amendments to IAS 1 - Presentation of Financial Statements: Classification of Liabilities as Current or Non‑current (including the deferral of its effective date) and Non‑current Liabilities with Covenants; amendments to IFRS 16 - Leases: Lease Liability in a Sale and Leaseback; and amendments to IAS 7 - Statement of Cash Flows and IFRS 7 - Financial Instruments: Disclosures: Supplier Finance Arrangements. 1.2.2
 Standards and amendments published
 but not yet effective
Amendments to the Classification and Measurement Requirements for Financial Instruments in IFRS 9 - Financial Instruments and IFRS 7 - Financial Instruments: Disclosures These amendments, issued on May 30, 2024, will be effective on January 1, 2026, with earlier application permitted. They have not yet been endorsed by the European Union. They result from the post‑implementation review of the classification and measurement requirements in IFRS 9 - Financial Instruments and related requirements in IFRS 7 - Financial Instruments: Disclosures. These amendments notably improve the requirements in IFRS 9 and IFRS 7 related to settling financial liabilities using an electronic payment system as well as to assessing contractual cash flow characteristics of financial assets with contingent features, including those with Environmental, Social and Governance (ESG)-linked features. The amendments also modify disclosure requirements relating to investments in equity instruments designated at fair value through other comprehensive income and add disclosure requirements for financial instruments with contingent features that do not relate directly to basic lending risks and costs. The assessment of their impact on the Group's Consolidated Financial Statements is in progress. IFRS 18 - Presentation and Disclosure in Financial Statements IFRS 18 - Presentation and Disclosure in Financial Statements, published on April 9, 2024, will be effective on January 1, 2027, with earlier application permitted. The standard has not yet been endorsed by the European Union. It is aimed at improving the quality and cross‑industry comparability of financial reporting, notably by introducing defined subtotals in the statement of profit or loss, adding new principles for aggregation and disaggregation of information and requiring disclosures about management‑defined performance measures. It will replace IAS 1 - Presentation of Financial Statements. The assessment of its impact on the Group's Consolidated Financial Statements is in progress. 6

 Other IFRS requirements not yet effective The following standards and amendments are not expected to have a material impact on the Group's Consolidated Financial Statements: ● ● ● ● IFRS 19 - Subsidiaries without Public Accountability: Disclosures, published on May 9, 2024, and effective for annual periods beginning on or after January 1, 2027; amendments to IAS 21 - The Effects of Changes in Foreign Exchange Rates: Lack of Exchangeability, published on August 15, 2023, and effective for annual periods beginning on or after January 1, 2025; annual Improvements to IFRS Accounting Standards - Volume 11: narrow amendments to IFRS 1, IFRS 7, IFRS 9, IFRS 10 and IAS 7, published on July 18, 2024, and effective for annual periods beginning on or after January 1, 2026; and amendments to IFRS 9 and IFRS 7 - Contracts Referencing Nature‑dependent Electricity, published on December 18, 2024, and effective for annual periods beginning on or after January 1, 2026. 1.2.3 Preparation of financial statements The preparation of financial statements in accordance with IFRS requires the use of estimates and assumptions, as well as a degree of judgment in the application of the Group accounting principles described below. In this respect, goodwill (in particular impairment tests described in paragraph 1.8.1), other intangible assets acquired in a business combination, certain assets accounted for at fair value, deferred tax assets, assets and liabilities relating to insurance and reinsurance contracts, pension benefit obligations and balances related to share‑based compensation, are the financial statements main items for which the measurement methods, along with key assumptions where required, are discussed in greater depth in the notes relating to the asset and liability items concerned where material and useful. 1.3 Principles and methods of consolidation 1.3.1 Scope and basis of consolidation Companies in which AXA exercises control are subsidiaries. They are fully consolidated from the date on which control is transferred to AXA. Under IFRS 10 - Consolidated Financial Statements, AXA controls an investee when it is exposed, or has rights, to variable returns from its involvement with the investee and has the ability to affect those returns through its power over the investee. Only substantive rights (i.e. the holder must have the practicability to exercise them) and rights that are not protective 6.6 Notes to the Consolidated financial statements shall be considered. AXA can have power with less than a majority of the voting rights of an investee, in particular through: ● ● ● ● ● the proportion of ownership with regards to the other investors; potential voting rights;
a contractual arrangement between the investor and other vote holders; rights arising from other contractual arrangements; or
a combination of these indicators.
Under IFRS 11 - Joint Arrangements, companies over which AXA exercises a joint control alongside one or more third parties are joint ventures and are accounted for using the equity method. Companies in which AXA exercises significant influence are accounted for using the equity method. Under IAS 28 - Investments in Associates and Joint Ventures, significant influence is presumed when AXA directly or indirectly holds 20% or more of the voting rights. Significant influence can also be exercised through an agreement with other shareholders. Under the equity method, AXA's share in equity investments' post‑acquisition profit or loss is recognized in the statement of profit or loss, and its share in post‑acquisition movements in reserves is stated under "Other reserves". Investment funds and real estate companies are either fully consolidated or accounted for using the equity method, depending on which conditions of IFRS 10/IFRS 11/IAS 28 listed above they satisfy. Fees received by internal asset managers are also taken into account in the assessment of the exposure to variability of returns. For investment funds managed by external asset managers, when group voting rights (including AXA Mutuelles) are less than 99%, the presumption is that the asset manager is not the agent of AXA only and acts on the best interest of all investors. This presumption can be rebutted based on other indicators such as the removal rights, the exit mechanism or existing contractual arrangements. For fully consolidated investment funds, non‑controlling interests are recognized at fair value as liabilities in the consolidated statement of financial position, if the companies' instruments can be redeemed at any time by the holder at fair value. Investment funds accounted for using the equity method are shown under the caption "Financial investments". Some companies in which AXA exercises control, joint control or significant influence, are excluded from the scope of consolidation as they are not material with respect to the Group's Consolidated Financial Statements. These are mainly investment funds. Consolidated financial statements AXA | Universal Registration Document 2024

 6.6 Notes to the Consolidated financial statements 1.3.2 Business combinations and subsequent changes in the Group ownership interest 1.3.2.1 Consideration transferred in a business combination Groups of insurance contracts acquired in their settlement period are, if material, valuated as if they were new insurance contracts issued by the acquirer as at the acquisition date: the LRC are recognized for such insurance contracts using the General Measurement Model described in paragraph 1.14.3, with the consideration received used as a proxy for the premiums received. Subsequently, the whole amount of the LRC recognized for the acquired claim settlement liabilities is recycled in the Insurance revenue over the coverage period applying the mechanism described in paragraph 1.19.1. The consideration transferred in a business combination is measured at fair value. Purchase consideration includes any contingent element (adjustment in the acquisition price conditional upon one or more events). Any change to the estimate of the contingent element between the acquisition date and the amount actually subsequently paid is recognized in the statement of profit or loss. Direct transaction costs related to a business combination are charged in the statement of profit or loss when incurred. 1.3.2.2 Valuation of assets acquired, and liabilities assumed in a business combination Upon first consolidation, all assets, liabilities and contingent liabilities (unless they are not present obligations) of the acquired company are estimated at their fair value. Insurance and investment contracts Insurance and investment contracts within the scope of IFRS 17 are measured as follows (refer to paragraph 1.14 relating to the classification and measurement of insurance contracts): ● ● ● ● the groups of contracts acquired in a business combination are identified as if AXA had entered into the contracts at the date of the acquisition, following the general guidance for the level of aggregation described in paragraph 1.14.2; these groups of contracts are measured using the consideration received or paid for the contracts as a proxy for the premiums received or paid; if those groups of contracts are profitable, and unless the premium allocation approach (PAA) for the Liability for Remaining Coverage (LRC) applies, the contractual service margin (CSM) is initially recognized applying the general valuation principles described in paragraph 1.14.3. The excess of the consideration paid or received over the fulfilment cash flows adjusts goodwill for the contracts measured using the PAA and the CSM for other contracts; in case of onerous groups of contracts, the loss component of the liability for remaining coverage is determined on initial recognition applying the approach described in paragraph 1.14.3, and the excess of the fulfilment cash flows over the consideration paid or received is recognized as a part of the goodwill; ● an asset for insurance acquisition cash flows is recognized as at the acquisition date. Contrary to direct participating insurance contracts and investment contracts with discretionary participation features, investment contracts with no discretionary participation features are financial liabilities within the scope of IFRS 9. These liabilities are measured at their fair value which cannot be lower than surrender value when those contracts contain a surrender option. Identifiable intangible assets Identifiable intangible assets are recognized separately from goodwill and may include: ● ●
marketing‑related intangible assets (trademarks, tradenames, Internet domain names, non‑competition agreements); the value of customer relationships relating to future insurance contracts, including (i) expected renewals of existing contracts (excluding any related asset for insurance acquisition cash flows to be separately recognized), considering cancellations by policyholders and a definite life due to the regular decline in renewals over the passage of time, (ii) expected cross‑selling with current policyholders, and (iii) expected other new contracts on customer lists (identified possible customers who have not yet a contract with the entity). These projections include assumptions regarding claims, expenses and financial revenues, or they can be estimated on the basis of the new business value. 6

According to a decision taken for each acquisition, non‑controlling interests may be measured at fair value or at its proportionate interest in the acquiree's identifiable net assets. In step acquisitions, previous non‑controlling interests held by the Group are measured at fair value and the resulting adjustment is recognized through profit or loss. Similarly, when an additional purchase changes the status from significant influence or joint control to control, any investment pre‑existing in a former associate/joint venture is re‑measured to its fair value with the gain or loss recognized through other comprehensive income (OCI) or profit or loss, depending on the previous classification (consequently also resulting in a new goodwill). Goodwill is measured as the excess of (i) the aggregate of the consideration transferred, the amount of any non‑controlling interest in the acquiree and, in a business combination achieved in stages, the fair value of the Group's previously held equity interest in the acquiree over (ii) the net amounts of the identifiable assets acquired and the liabilities assumed, as of the acquisition date. If the cost of acquisition is lower than the net amounts of the identifiable assets acquired and the liabilities assumed as of the acquisition date, the difference is directly recorded in the consolidated statement of profit or loss. Adjustments can be made to goodwill within twelve months of the acquisition date if new information becomes available to complete the initial accounting and relates to a situation existed on the acquisition date. Goodwill is allocated to cash generating units corresponding to (i) the companies acquired or portfolios of business acquired according to their expected profitability, and (ii) the entities already within the AXA Group that will benefit from the synergies of the combination with the activities acquired. This allocation of goodwill is used for impairment testing (refer to paragraph 1.8.1). Purchase and sale transactions of non‑controlling interests in a controlled subsidiary that do not change the conclusion of control are recorded through shareholders' equity (including direct acquisition costs). If control in a subsidiary is lost, any gain or loss is recognized in profit or loss. Furthermore, if an investment in the entity is retained by the Group, it is re‑measured to its fair value and any gain or loss is also recognized in profit or loss. Intra‑group transactions, including internal dividends, payables/receivables and gains/losses pertaining to these transactions, are eliminated in full for controlled subsidiaries and to the extent of AXA's interest for entities accounted for using the equity method. The effect on profit or loss of transactions between consolidated entities is always eliminated. However, in case of a loss, an impairment test is performed, in order to assess whether an impairment of the underlying item has to be booked. In the event of an internal sale of an asset that is not intended to be held on the long term by the Group, a deferred tax is recognized as the current tax calculated on the realized gain or loss is eliminated. In addition, the transfer of shares of a consolidated entity between two consolidated entities held with different ownership percentages should not impact the profit or loss. The only exception would be any related tax recorded in connection to the transaction, which is maintained in the Consolidated Financial Statements. Such a transfer also has an impact on Group shareholders' equity (with a balancing entry recorded in non‑controlling interests). This impact is identified in the "other" changes of the consolidated statement of changes in equity. The Consolidated Financial Statements are presented in Euro millions, the Euro being the Group's presentational currency. Thus, non‑monetary assets and liabilities of these entities which are not already expressed in terms of the measuring unit current at the end of the reporting period are restated to reflect the change in the consumer price index (CPI). The effect of inflation on monetary assets and liabilities relating to the reporting period is included in the consolidated statement of profit or loss of the reporting period as a gain or loss on net monetary position. The cumulated gains and losses on net monetary position for previous periods are included within the retained earnings. All items in the consolidated statement of comprehensive income need to be restated by applying the change in the CPI between the dates when those items were initially recorded in the financial statements and the end of the reporting period. To be included in the Group's Consolidated Financial statements, financial statements of hyperinflationary entities, restated for hyperinflation as described above, are translated in Euros at the closing exchange rate. In the consolidated statement of financial position as at the reporting date, the restatement effects on equity items are included in other comprehensive income, altogether with the translation differences, to the extent that the change in the CPI is correlated with movements in the exchange rate between the functional currency of the entity and Euro. 6

Principles below address mostly assets given the nature of the activities of the Group. 1.6.1 Assets and liabilities quoted in an active market An asset or a liability is considered as being quoted in an active market when quoted prices are readily and regularly available from a stock exchange, dealer, broker, pricing service or regulatory agency and those prices represent actual and regularly occurring market transactions on an arm's length basis between a willing seller and a willing buyer. The assets need to be liquid, meaning that AXA can dispose of them in the ordinary course of business within a certain limited time period at approximately the price at which the asset is valued. Liquidity for debt instruments is assessed using a multi‑criteria approach including the number of quotes available, the place of issuance and the evolution of the widening of bid ask spreads. The fair value of assets and liabilities traded on active markets is determined using quoted market prices when available. For financial instruments traded in active markets, quotes received from external pricing services represent consensus prices, i.e. using similar models and inputs resulting in a very limited dispersion. The fair value of assets and liabilities which is determined in whole directly by reference to an active market is disclosed as level 1 in the Notes to the Consolidated Financial Statements. 1.6.2 Assets and liabilities not quoted in an active market An asset or a liability is regarded as not quoted in an active market: ● if there is little observation of transaction prices as an inherent characteristic of the asset or the liability; 6.6 Notes to the Consolidated financial statements Consolidated financial statements ● ● ● when there is a significant decline in the volume and level of trading activity; in case of significant illiquidity;
if observable prices cannot be considered as representing fair value because of dislocated market conditions. Characteristics of inactive markets can therefore be very different in nature, inherent to the asset or the liability, or indicative of a change in the conditions prevailing in certain markets. The fair value of assets and liabilities that are not traded in an active market is estimated using: ● ● external and independent pricing services; or
valuation techniques.
The fair value of assets and liabilities that are not traded in an active market mainly based on observable market data is disclosed as level 2 in the Notes to the Consolidated Financial Statements. The fair value of assets and liabilities which is mainly not based on observable market data is disclosed as level 3. 1.6.2.1 No active market: use of external pricing services External pricing services may be fund asset managers in the case of non‑consolidated investments in funds or brokers. To the extent possible, the Group collects quotes from external pricing providers as inputs to measure fair value. Prices received may form tight clusters or dispersed quotes which may then lead to the use of valuation techniques. The dispersion of quotes received may be an indication of the large range of assumptions used by external pricing providers given the limited number of transactions to be observed or reflect the existence of distress transactions. 1.6.2.2 No active market: use of valuation techniques The objective of valuation techniques is to arrive at the price at which an orderly transaction would take place between market participants (a willing buyer and a willing seller) at the measurement date. Valuation techniques include: ● ● ● market approach: the consideration of recent prices and other relevant information generated by market transactions involving substantially similar assets or liabilities; income approach: use of discounted cash flow analysis, option pricing models, and other present value techniques to convert future amounts to a single current (i.e. discounted) amount; cost approach: the consideration of amounts that would currently be required to construct or replace the service capacity of an asset.  6.6 Notes to the Consolidated financial statements Valuation techniques are subjective in nature and significant judgment is involved in establishing fair values. They include recent arm's length transactions between knowledgeable willing parties on similar assets if available and representative of fair value and involve various assumptions regarding the underlying price, yield curve, correlations, volatility, default rates and other factors. Unlisted equity instruments valuation is based on cross checks using different methodologies such as discounted cash flows techniques, price‑earnings ratio multiples, adjusted net asset values, taking into account recent transactions on instruments which are substantially the same if concluded at arm's length between knowledgeable willing parties, if any. The use of valuation techniques and assumptions could produce different estimates of fair value. However, valuations are determined using generally accepted models (discounted cash flows, Black & Scholes models, etc.) based on quoted market prices for similar instruments or underlyings (index, credit spread, etc.) whenever such directly observable data are available, and valuations are adjusted for liquidity and credit risk. Valuation techniques may be used when there is little observation of transaction prices as an inherent characteristic of the instrument, when quotes made available by external pricing providers are too dispersed or when market conditions are so dislocated that observed data cannot be used or need significant adjustments. Therefore, internal mark to model valuations is either normal market practices for certain assets and liabilities inherently scarcely traded or exceptional processes implemented due to specific market conditions. When valuation techniques are used, the classification between levels 2 and 3 depends on the proportion of assumptions supported by observable market data used by external pricing services or, in very limited cases, by the Group. 1.6.2.3 Use of valuation techniques in dislocated markets The dislocation of certain markets may be evidenced by various factors. For example, a very large widening of bid ask spreads may be a helpful indicator in understanding whether market participants are willing to transact. The dislocation of markets may also be suspected in case of wide dispersion in the prices (over time or among market participants), small number of transactions, closing down of primary and/or secondary markets, forced transactions motivated by needs of liquidity or other difficult financial conditions with insufficient time to market the assets to be sold, and large bulk sales to exit such markets at all costs that may involve side arrangements (such as sellers providing finance for a sale to a buyer). In such cases, the Group uses valuation techniques including observable data whenever possible and relevant, adjusted if needed to develop the best estimate of fair value, including adequate risk premiums, or develops a valuation model based on unobservable data representing estimates of assumptions that willing market participants would use when prices are not current, relevant or available without undue costs and efforts: in inactive markets, transactions may be inputs when measuring fair value, but would likely not be determinative and unobservable data may be more appropriate than observable inputs. 1.7 Information by Segment Information by segment provided in AXA's Consolidated Financial Statements reflects six operating segments. Furthermore, at a geographical segmentation level that generally corresponds to a country, if the cumulated amount of unrealized losses under 15% (without offsetting with unrealized gains) represents more than 10% of the cumulated net cost of real estate assets, additional impairment is booked on a line‑by‑line approach until the 10% threshold is reached. If, in subsequent periods, the appraisal value rises to at least 15% more than the net carrying value, previously recorded impairment is reversed to the extent of the difference between (i) the net carrying value and (ii) the lower of the appraisal value and the depreciated cost (before impairment). The fair value of investments in real estate properties generally cannot be determined via reference to quotes of an active market from an exchange market or a service provider. Instead, AXA real estate properties are valued by independent appraisers with relevant professional qualification and experience in the locations and segments of the properties to be valued. These external real estate valuation experts are members of one of the professional organizations composing the Internal Valuation Standard Council (IVSC). External valuations are performed at least annually except for Switzerland, where 20% of real estate properties a revaluated externally each year, internal valuations being however performed for the remaining 80%; hence, the entire real estate portfolio is covered by external valuation over a 5‑year cycle. External real estate valuation experts generally use a primary valuation method to value real estate properties held by AXA, and additionally provide a value derived from a second method for verification. When relevant, an average value between the two valuation methods is retained. As the primary valuation method has to be applied consistently from one period to another, any change needs to be justified. Three main valuation methods may apply to determine the fair value of AXA real estate assets (the first two ones being the most used): ● the discounted cash flow method determines the value of the real estate property from its potential to generate future income. Thus, the value is estimated by compiling the net present value of the future cash flows. Main inputs for the valuation are: projected rental income, projected operating expenses, capital expenditures requirements, discount rate and exit yield which corresponds to the rate used to capitalize the exit rent to determine the exit value of an asset. The Group provides external appraisers with all relevant information (notably detailed rent rolls, budget, etc.) to enable them to determine future cash flows, to which they also apply their own assumptions. The discount rate and exit yield applied vary from one property to another since they are combination of the risk‑free rate and the risk premium attached to each property due to its location, quality, size, and technical specificities; ● ●
the income capitalization method determines the value of the real estate property by applying a capitalization rate at the net operating income at perpetuity. The income capitalization method can notably be used when cash flows are stable and relatively certain, mainly where the real estate asset is fully leased (with limited number of tenants) and/or no occupancy changes are expected. In that case, it may not be necessary to consider an explicit forecast period such as used under the discounted cash flow methodology and a terminal value may form the only basis for value; the Hardcore method is a variation of the income capitalization method and determines the value of the real estate property by applying a different capitalization rate at the net operating income depending on the type of income. Specific risks (such as climate, regulatory, legal risks...) on real estate property are reflected in the exit yield or discount rate used in the modelling by the external appraiser. 1.9.2 Financial investments 1.9.2.1 Classification of financial assets Financial assets held by AXA include notably debt instruments, equity instruments, loans, receivables and investments in non‑consolidated investments funds.
